subject: Physics
question_html: Three cells {tex}{A}, {B}{/tex} and C of emfs {tex}2 {~V}, 3 {~V}{/tex} and 5 V respectively are connected in parallel to each other. Their internal resistances are {tex}5 \Omega, 5 \Omega{/tex} and {tex}1 \Omega{/tex} respectively. Calculate the currents flowing through the cells {tex}{A}, {B}{/tex} and C.
question_text: Three cells {tex}{A}, {B}{/tex} and C of emfs {tex}2 {~V}, 3 {~V}{/tex} and 5 V respectively are connected in parallel to each other. Their internal resistances are {tex}5 \Omega, 5 \Omega{/tex} and {tex}1 \Omega{/tex} respectively. Calculate the currents flowing through the cells {tex}{A}, {B}{/tex} and C.
explanation: In loop ABEFA, {tex} 5-3-5 {I}_1-{I}=0 {/tex} {tex} 2=5 {I}_1+{I} {/tex} ...(1) In loop CBEDC, {tex} 2-3-5 {I}_1+5 {I}-5 {I}_1=0 {/tex} {tex} -1=10 {I}_1-5 {I} {/tex} ...(2) Solving equation (1) and (2) {tex} {I}=\frac{5}{7} {~A} {/tex} in arm AF/through the cell of 5V(A) {tex} {I}_1=\frac{9}{35} {~A} {/tex} in arm BE/through the cell of 3V(B) {tex} {I}-{I}_1=\frac{16}{35} {~A} {/tex} in arm CD/through the cell of 2V(C)
solution: In loop ABEFA, {tex} 5-3-5 {I}_1-{I}=0 {/tex} {tex} 2=5 {I}_1+{I} {/tex} ...(1) In loop CBEDC, {tex} 2-3-5 {I}_1+5 {I}-5 {I}_1=0 {/tex} {tex} -1=10 {I}_1-5 {I} {/tex} ...(2) Solving equation (1) and (2) {tex} {I}=\frac{5}{7} {~A} {/tex} in arm AF/through the cell of 5V(A) {tex} {I}_1=\frac{9}{35} {~A} {/tex} in arm BE/through the cell of 3V(B) {tex} {I}-{I}_1=\frac{16}{35} {~A} {/tex} in arm CD/through the cell of 2V(C)
solution_html:
In loop ABEFA,
{tex} 5-3-5 {I}_1-{I}=0 {/tex}
{tex} 2=5 {I}_1+{I} {/tex} ...(1)
In loop CBEDC,
{tex} 2-3-5 {I}_1+5 {I}-5 {I}_1=0 {/tex}
{tex} -1=10 {I}_1-5 {I} {/tex} ...(2)
Solving equation (1) and (2)
{tex} {I}=\frac{5}{7} {~A} {/tex} in arm AF/through the cell of 5V(A)
{tex} {I}_1=\frac{9}{35} {~A} {/tex} in arm BE/through the cell of 3V(B)
{tex} {I}-{I}_1=\frac{16}{35} {~A} {/tex} in arm CD/through the cell of 2V(C)

question_html: Two cells of emf 10 V each, two resistors of {tex}20 \Omega{/tex} and {tex}10 \Omega{/tex} and a bulb B of {tex}10 \Omega{/tex} resistance are connected together as shown in the figure. Find the current that flows through the bulb.
question_text: Two cells of emf 10 V each, two resistors of {tex}20 \Omega{/tex} and {tex}10 \Omega{/tex} and a bulb B of {tex}10 \Omega{/tex} resistance are connected together as shown in the figure. Find the current that flows through the bulb.
explanation: By applying Kirchhoff's loop rule to closed loops ABCFA and FCDEF {tex}2 {I}_{1}-3 {I}_{2}=1{/tex} ...(i) {tex}{I}_{1}+{I}_{2}=1{/tex} ...(ii) On solving, Current through the bulb, {tex}{I}_{2}=\frac{1}{5} {~A}{/tex}
solution: By applying Kirchhoff's loop rule to closed loops ABCFA and FCDEF {tex}2 {I}_{1}-3 {I}_{2}=1{/tex} ...(i) {tex}{I}_{1}+{I}_{2}=1{/tex} ...(ii) On solving, Current through the bulb, {tex}{I}_{2}=\frac{1}{5} {~A}{/tex}
solution_html:
By applying Kirchhoff's loop rule to closed loops ABCFA and FCDEF
{tex}2 {I}_{1}-3 {I}_{2}=1{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}{I}_{1}+{I}_{2}=1{/tex} ...(ii)
On solving,
Current through the bulb,
{tex}{I}_{2}=\frac{1}{5} {~A}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Three batteries {tex}{E}_{1}, {E}_{2}{/tex} and {tex}{E}_{3}{/tex} of emfs and internal resistances ({tex}4 {~V}, 2 \Omega{/tex}) , ({tex}2 {~V}, 4 \Omega{/tex}) and ({tex}6 {~V}, 2 \Omega{/tex}) respectively are connected as shown in the figure. Find the values of the currents passing through batteries {tex}{E}_{1}, {E}_{2}{/tex} and {tex}{E}_{3}{/tex}.
  2. The ends of six wires, each of resistance R (= {tex}10 \Omega{/tex}) are joined as shown in the figure. The points A and B of the arrangement are connected in a circuit. Find the value of the effective resistance offered by it to the circuit.
question_text: Three batteries {tex}{E}_{1}, {E}_{2}{/tex} and {tex}{E}_{3}{/tex} of emfs and internal resistances ( {tex}4 {~V}, 2 \Omega{/tex} ) , ( {tex}2 {~V}, 4 \Omega{/tex} ) and ( {tex}6 {~V}, 2 \Omega{/tex} ) respectively are connected as shown in the figure. Find the values of the currents passing through batteries {tex}{E}_{1}, {E}_{2}{/tex} and {tex}{E}_{3}{/tex} . The ends of six wires, each of resistance R (= {tex}10 \Omega{/tex} ) are joined as shown in the figure. The points A and B of the arrangement are connected in a circuit. Find the value of the effective resistance offered by it to the circuit.
explanation: In closed loop ABCD, using Kirchhoff's loop law {tex} 4 {I}_1+6 {I}_2=6 {/tex} ...(1) Similarly In closed loop CDFE {tex} 6 {I}_1+4 {I}_2=8 {/tex} ...(2) Solving eqn. (1) and (2) {tex} I_2=\frac{1}{5} A {/tex} {tex} I_1=\frac{6}{5} A {/tex} {tex} I_1+I_2=\frac{7}{5} A {/tex} Resistances {tex}{R}_{{AC}}, {R}_{{CB}}, {R}_{{AD}}{/tex} , and {tex}{R}_{{DB}}{/tex} form a balanced Wheatstone bridge Hence current through {tex}{R}_{{CD}}{/tex} is zero and will not contribute to equivalent resistance. The equivalent resistance of bridge is {tex}R{/tex} , is in parallel with {tex}R_{A B}{/tex} Series combinations of {tex}R_{A C} \ \& \ R_{C B}{/tex} and {tex}R_{A D} \ \& \ R_{D B}{/tex} is in parallel with {tex}R_{A B}{/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{R_{e q}}=\frac{1}{R}+\frac{1}{R} {/tex} {tex} R_{e q}=\frac{R}{2} {/tex} Given {tex}{R}=10 \Omega{/tex} , Therefore {tex}{R}_{{eq}}=5 \Omega{/tex}
solution: In closed loop ABCD, using Kirchhoff's loop law {tex} 4 {I}_1+6 {I}_2=6 {/tex} ...(1) Similarly In closed loop CDFE {tex} 6 {I}_1+4 {I}_2=8 {/tex} ...(2) Solving eqn. (1) and (2) {tex} I_2=\frac{1}{5} A {/tex} {tex} I_1=\frac{6}{5} A {/tex} {tex} I_1+I_2=\frac{7}{5} A {/tex} Resistances {tex}{R}_{{AC}}, {R}_{{CB}}, {R}_{{AD}}{/tex} , and {tex}{R}_{{DB}}{/tex} form a balanced Wheatstone bridge Hence current through {tex}{R}_{{CD}}{/tex} is zero and will not contribute to equivalent resistance. The equivalent resistance of bridge is {tex}R{/tex} , is in parallel with {tex}R_{A B}{/tex} Series combinations of {tex}R_{A C} \ \& \ R_{C B}{/tex} and {tex}R_{A D} \ \& \ R_{D B}{/tex} is in parallel with {tex}R_{A B}{/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{R_{e q}}=\frac{1}{R}+\frac{1}{R} {/tex} {tex} R_{e q}=\frac{R}{2} {/tex} Given {tex}{R}=10 \Omega{/tex} , Therefore {tex}{R}_{{eq}}=5 \Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
1. In closed loop ABCD, using Kirchhoff's loop law
    {tex} 4 {I}_1+6 {I}_2=6 {/tex} ...(1)
    Similarly In closed loop CDFE
    {tex} 6 {I}_1+4 {I}_2=8 {/tex} ...(2)
    Solving eqn. (1) and (2)
    {tex} I_2=\frac{1}{5} A {/tex}
    {tex} I_1=\frac{6}{5} A {/tex}
    {tex} I_1+I_2=\frac{7}{5} A {/tex}

  2. Resistances {tex}{R}_{{AC}}, {R}_{{CB}}, {R}_{{AD}}{/tex}, and {tex}{R}_{{DB}}{/tex} form a balanced Wheatstone bridge Hence current through {tex}{R}_{{CD}}{/tex} is zero and will not contribute to equivalent resistance.
    The equivalent resistance of bridge is {tex}R{/tex}, is in parallel with {tex}R_{A B}{/tex}
    Series combinations of {tex}R_{A C} \ \& \ R_{C B}{/tex} and {tex}R_{A D} \ \& \ R_{D B}{/tex} is in parallel with {tex}R_{A B}{/tex}
    {tex} \frac{1}{R_{e q}}=\frac{1}{R}+\frac{1}{R} {/tex}
    {tex} R_{e q}=\frac{R}{2} {/tex}
    Given {tex}{R}=10 \Omega{/tex},
    Therefore {tex}{R}_{{eq}}=5 \Omega{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
The resistance of a wire at {tex}25^{\circ} {C}{/tex} is {tex}10 \cdot 0 \Omega{/tex}. When heated to {tex}125^{\circ} {C}{/tex}, its resistance becomes {tex}10.5 \Omega{/tex}. Find

  1. the temperature coefficient of resistance of the wire, and
  2. the resistance of the wire at {tex}425^{\circ} {C}{/tex}.
question_text: The resistance of a wire at {tex}25^{\circ} {C}{/tex} is {tex}10 \cdot 0 \Omega{/tex} . When heated to {tex}125^{\circ} {C}{/tex} , its resistance becomes {tex}10.5 \Omega{/tex} . Find the temperature coefficient of resistance of the wire, and the resistance of the wire at {tex}425^{\circ} {C}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} {R}_2={R}_1\left(1+\alpha\left({t}_2-{t}_1\right)\right) {/tex} {tex} 10.5=10(1+\alpha \times 100) {/tex} {tex} \alpha=5 \times 10^{-4} /{ }^{\circ} {C} {/tex} {tex} R_{425} =R_{25}(1+\alpha(425-25)) {/tex} {tex} =10\left(1+5 \times 10^{-4} \times 400\right) {/tex} {tex} =12 \Omega {/tex}
solution: {tex} {R}_2={R}_1\left(1+\alpha\left({t}_2-{t}_1\right)\right) {/tex} {tex} 10.5=10(1+\alpha \times 100) {/tex} {tex} \alpha=5 \times 10^{-4} /{ }^{\circ} {C} {/tex} {tex} R_{425} =R_{25}(1+\alpha(425-25)) {/tex} {tex} =10\left(1+5 \times 10^{-4} \times 400\right) {/tex} {tex} =12 \Omega {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex} {R}_2={R}_1\left(1+\alpha\left({t}_2-{t}_1\right)\right) {/tex}
    {tex} 10.5=10(1+\alpha \times 100) {/tex}
    {tex} \alpha=5 \times 10^{-4} /{ }^{\circ} {C} {/tex}
  2. {tex} R_{425} =R_{25}(1+\alpha(425-25)) {/tex}
    {tex} =10\left(1+5 \times 10^{-4} \times 400\right) {/tex}
    {tex} =12 \Omega {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
What is the difference between 'emf' and 'terminal voltage' of a cell?
Two cells of emfs {tex}E_{1}{/tex} and {tex}E_{2}{/tex} and internal resistances {tex}r_{1}{/tex} and {tex}r_{2}{/tex} are connected in parallel. Derive an expression for the emf and internal resistance of the equivalent cell.
question_text: What is the difference between 'emf' and 'terminal voltage' of a cell? Two cells of emfs {tex}E_{1}{/tex} and {tex}E_{2}{/tex} and internal resistances {tex}r_{1}{/tex} and {tex}r_{2}{/tex} are connected in parallel. Derive an expression for the emf and internal resistance of the equivalent cell.
explanation: Potential difference between the terminals of a cell in open circuit is emf and in closed circuit it is terminal voltage. An emf does not depend on the external resistance, while terminal voltage depends on external resistance. emf is the cause and terminal voltage is the effect. {tex}V=E_{1}-I_{1} r_{1}{/tex} {tex}V=E_{2}-I_{2} r_{2}{/tex} {tex}I=I_{1}+I_{2}{/tex} {tex}I=\left(\frac{E_{1}-V}{r_{1}}\right)+\left(\frac{E_{2}-V}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} On comparing above equation with {tex}I=\frac{E_{e q}-V}{r_{e q}}{/tex} {tex}E_{e q}=\frac{E_{1} r_{2}+E_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex} {tex}r_{e q}=\frac{r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex}
solution: Potential difference between the terminals of a cell in open circuit is emf and in closed circuit it is terminal voltage. An emf does not depend on the external resistance, while terminal voltage depends on external resistance. emf is the cause and terminal voltage is the effect. {tex}V=E_{1}-I_{1} r_{1}{/tex} {tex}V=E_{2}-I_{2} r_{2}{/tex} {tex}I=I_{1}+I_{2}{/tex} {tex}I=\left(\frac{E_{1}-V}{r_{1}}\right)+\left(\frac{E_{2}-V}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} On comparing above equation with {tex}I=\frac{E_{e q}-V}{r_{e q}}{/tex} {tex}E_{e q}=\frac{E_{1} r_{2}+E_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex} {tex}r_{e q}=\frac{r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Potential difference between the terminals of a cell in open circuit is emf and in closed circuit it is terminal voltage.
  2. An emf does not depend on the external resistance, while terminal voltage depends on external resistance.
  3. emf is the cause and terminal voltage is the effect.

{tex}V=E_{1}-I_{1} r_{1}{/tex}
{tex}V=E_{2}-I_{2} r_{2}{/tex}
{tex}I=I_{1}+I_{2}{/tex}
{tex}I=\left(\frac{E_{1}-V}{r_{1}}\right)+\left(\frac{E_{2}-V}{r_{2}}\right){/tex}
On comparing above equation with {tex}I=\frac{E_{e q}-V}{r_{e q}}{/tex}
{tex}E_{e q}=\frac{E_{1} r_{2}+E_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}r_{e q}=\frac{r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A cell of e.m.f. E and internal resistance r is connected with a variable external resistance R and a voltmeter showing potential drop {tex}V{/tex} across R. Obtain the relationship between {tex}V, {E}, {R}{/tex} and r.
  2. Draw the shape of the graph showing the variation of terminal voltage {tex}V{/tex} of the cell as a function of current I drawn from it. How one can determine the e.m.f. of the cell and its internal resistance from this graph?
question_text: A cell of e.m.f. E and internal resistance r is connected with a variable external resistance R and a voltmeter showing potential drop {tex}V{/tex} across R. Obtain the relationship between {tex}V, {E}, {R}{/tex} and r. Draw the shape of the graph showing the variation of terminal voltage {tex}V{/tex} of the cell as a function of current I drawn from it. How one can determine the e.m.f. of the cell and its internal resistance from this graph?
explanation: Current drawn from the cell {tex}(I)=\frac{E}{r+R}{/tex} Potential difference {tex}({V})={IR}{/tex} {tex} V=\frac{E R}{r+R} {/tex} Slope of the graph is equal to internal resistance of the cell. The intercept on Y-axis gives the emf of the cell.
solution: Current drawn from the cell {tex}(I)=\frac{E}{r+R}{/tex} Potential difference {tex}({V})={IR}{/tex} {tex} V=\frac{E R}{r+R} {/tex} Slope of the graph is equal to internal resistance of the cell. The intercept on Y-axis gives the emf of the cell.
solution_html:
1. Current drawn from the cell {tex}(I)=\frac{E}{r+R}{/tex}
    Potential difference {tex}({V})={IR}{/tex}
    {tex} V=\frac{E R}{r+R} {/tex}

  2. Slope of the graph is equal to internal resistance of the cell. The intercept on Y-axis gives the emf of the cell.

subject: Physics
question_html: Find the effective resistance of the network of resistors between points {tex}A{/tex} and F as shown in the figure.
question_text: Find the effective resistance of the network of resistors between points {tex}A{/tex} and F as shown in the figure.
explanation: {tex} R_{B C D} =2+4=6 \Omega {/tex} {tex} R_{B D} =\frac{3 \times 6}{6+3} {/tex} {tex} =2 \Omega {/tex} {tex} R_{B D E} =2+8 {/tex} {tex} =10 \Omega {/tex} {tex} R_{B E} =\frac{40 \times 10}{40+10} {/tex} {tex} =8 \Omega {/tex} {tex} R_{A F} =7+8+5 {/tex} {tex} =20 \Omega {/tex}
solution: {tex} R_{B C D} =2+4=6 \Omega {/tex} {tex} R_{B D} =\frac{3 \times 6}{6+3} {/tex} {tex} =2 \Omega {/tex} {tex} R_{B D E} =2+8 {/tex} {tex} =10 \Omega {/tex} {tex} R_{B E} =\frac{40 \times 10}{40+10} {/tex} {tex} =8 \Omega {/tex} {tex} R_{A F} =7+8+5 {/tex} {tex} =20 \Omega {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} R_{B C D} =2+4=6 \Omega {/tex}
{tex} R_{B D} =\frac{3 \times 6}{6+3} {/tex}
{tex} =2 \Omega {/tex}
{tex} R_{B D E} =2+8 {/tex}
{tex} =10 \Omega {/tex}
{tex} R_{B E} =\frac{40 \times 10}{40+10} {/tex}
{tex} =8 \Omega {/tex}
{tex} R_{A F} =7+8+5 {/tex}
{tex} =20 \Omega {/tex}

question_html: Find the equivalent resistance between points A and B for the network shown in the figure.
question_text: Find the equivalent resistance between points A and B for the network shown in the figure.
explanation: Resistance between points C and B {tex}\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{R_{1}}+\frac{1}{R_{2}}+\frac{1}{R_{3}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{15}+\frac{1}{45}+\frac{1}{45}{/tex} {tex}{R}=9 \Omega{/tex} Equivalent resistance between points A and B {tex} R_{\text {eq }} =R_1+R_2 {/tex} {tex} {R}_{\text {eq }} =1+9 {/tex} {tex} =10 \Omega {/tex}
solution: Resistance between points C and B {tex}\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{R_{1}}+\frac{1}{R_{2}}+\frac{1}{R_{3}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{15}+\frac{1}{45}+\frac{1}{45}{/tex} {tex}{R}=9 \Omega{/tex} Equivalent resistance between points A and B {tex} R_{\text {eq }} =R_1+R_2 {/tex} {tex} {R}_{\text {eq }} =1+9 {/tex} {tex} =10 \Omega {/tex}
solution_html:
Resistance between points C and B
{tex}\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{R_{1}}+\frac{1}{R_{2}}+\frac{1}{R_{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{15}+\frac{1}{45}+\frac{1}{45}{/tex}
{tex}{R}=9 \Omega{/tex}
Equivalent resistance between points A and B

{tex} R_{\text {eq }} =R_1+R_2 {/tex}
{tex} {R}_{\text {eq }} =1+9 {/tex}
{tex} =10 \Omega {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Two wires of the same material and the same radius have their lengths in the ratio {tex}2: 3{/tex}. They are connected in parallel to a battery which supplies a current of 15 A. Find the current through the wires.
question_text: Two wires of the same material and the same radius have their lengths in the ratio {tex}2: 3{/tex} . They are connected in parallel to a battery which supplies a current of 15 A. Find the current through the wires.
explanation: {tex} {R}_1= \frac{\rho l_1}{{~A}} ; {R}_2=\frac{\rho l_2}{{~A}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{l_1}{l_2}=\frac{2}{3} \Rightarrow \frac{{R}_1}{{R}_2}=\frac{2}{3} {/tex} {tex} {I} \alpha \frac{1}{{R}} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{I}_1}{{I}_2}=\frac{3}{2} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {I}_1=\frac{3}{5} \times 15=9 {~A} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {I}_2=\frac{2}{5} \times 15=6 {~A} {/tex}
solution: {tex} {R}_1= \frac{\rho l_1}{{~A}} ; {R}_2=\frac{\rho l_2}{{~A}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{l_1}{l_2}=\frac{2}{3} \Rightarrow \frac{{R}_1}{{R}_2}=\frac{2}{3} {/tex} {tex} {I} \alpha \frac{1}{{R}} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{I}_1}{{I}_2}=\frac{3}{2} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {I}_1=\frac{3}{5} \times 15=9 {~A} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {I}_2=\frac{2}{5} \times 15=6 {~A} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} {R}_1= \frac{\rho l_1}{{~A}} ; {R}_2=\frac{\rho l_2}{{~A}} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{l_1}{l_2}=\frac{2}{3} \Rightarrow \frac{{R}_1}{{R}_2}=\frac{2}{3} {/tex}
{tex} {I} \alpha \frac{1}{{R}} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{I}_1}{{I}_2}=\frac{3}{2} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {I}_1=\frac{3}{5} \times 15=9 {~A} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {I}_2=\frac{2}{5} \times 15=6 {~A} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Define Electrical conductivity. Obtain the expression of electrical conductivity of a conductor in terms of number density and relaxation time of free electrons.
  2. Explain qualitative change in resistivity of a conductor with temperature using expression obtained in (a).
question_text: Define Electrical conductivity. Obtain the expression of electrical conductivity of a conductor in terms of number density and relaxation time of free electrons. Explain qualitative change in resistivity of a conductor with temperature using expression obtained in (a).
explanation: Electrical conductivity:- It is the reciprocal of the resistivity. {tex} {I} \Delta {t}=+{neA}\left|\overrightarrow{{v}}_{{d}}\right| \Delta {t} {/tex} {tex} \text { Substituting }\left|\overrightarrow{{v}}_{{d}}\right|=\frac{{e}|\overrightarrow{{E}}|}{{m}} \tau {/tex} {tex} {I} \Delta {t}=\frac{{e}^2 {~A}}{{~m}} \tau \ {n} \ \Delta {t}|\overrightarrow{{E}}| {/tex} {tex} {I}=|\overrightarrow{{j}}| {A} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{{j}}|=\frac{{ne}^2}{{~m}} \tau|\overrightarrow{{E}}| \ \ldots(1) {/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{{j}}=\sigma \overrightarrow{{E}} \ \ldots (2) {/tex} Comparing eq. (1) & (2):- {tex} \sigma=\frac{{ne}^2}{{~m}} \tau {/tex} On increasing the temperature, the value of {tex}\tau{/tex} decreases as a consequence conductivity decreases and hence resistivity increases.
solution: Electrical conductivity:- It is the reciprocal of the resistivity. {tex} {I} \Delta {t}=+{neA}\left|\overrightarrow{{v}}_{{d}}\right| \Delta {t} {/tex} {tex} \text { Substituting }\left|\overrightarrow{{v}}_{{d}}\right|=\frac{{e}|\overrightarrow{{E}}|}{{m}} \tau {/tex} {tex} {I} \Delta {t}=\frac{{e}^2 {~A}}{{~m}} \tau \ {n} \ \Delta {t}|\overrightarrow{{E}}| {/tex} {tex} {I}=|\overrightarrow{{j}}| {A} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{{j}}|=\frac{{ne}^2}{{~m}} \tau|\overrightarrow{{E}}| \ \ldots(1) {/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{{j}}=\sigma \overrightarrow{{E}} \ \ldots (2) {/tex} Comparing eq. (1) & (2):- {tex} \sigma=\frac{{ne}^2}{{~m}} \tau {/tex} On increasing the temperature, the value of {tex}\tau{/tex} decreases as a consequence conductivity decreases and hence resistivity increases.
solution_html:
1. Electrical conductivity:- It is the reciprocal of the resistivity.

    {tex} {I} \Delta {t}=+{neA}\left|\overrightarrow{{v}}_{{d}}\right| \Delta {t} {/tex}
    {tex} \text { Substituting }\left|\overrightarrow{{v}}_{{d}}\right|=\frac{{e}|\overrightarrow{{E}}|}{{m}} \tau {/tex}
    {tex} {I} \Delta {t}=\frac{{e}^2 {~A}}{{~m}} \tau \ {n} \ \Delta {t}|\overrightarrow{{E}}| {/tex}
    {tex} {I}=|\overrightarrow{{j}}| {A} {/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{{j}}|=\frac{{ne}^2}{{~m}} \tau|\overrightarrow{{E}}| \ \ldots(1) {/tex}
    {tex} \overrightarrow{{j}}=\sigma \overrightarrow{{E}} \ \ldots (2) {/tex}
    Comparing eq. (1) & (2):-
    {tex} \sigma=\frac{{ne}^2}{{~m}} \tau {/tex}
  2. On increasing the temperature, the value of {tex}\tau{/tex} decreases as a consequence conductivity decreases and hence resistivity increases.

subject: Physics
question_html: n identical cells, each of e.m.f. E and internal resistance r, are connected in series. Later on it was found out that two cells 'X' and 'Y' are connected in reverse polarities. Calculate the potential difference across the cell 'X'.
question_text: n identical cells, each of e.m.f. E and internal resistance r, are connected in series. Later on it was found out that two cells 'X' and 'Y' are connected in reverse polarities. Calculate the potential difference across the cell 'X'.
explanation: Net e.m.f {tex}=(n-4) {E}{/tex} {tex} \therefore {I}=\frac{({n}-4) {E}}{{nr}} {/tex} Potential difference across 'X' {tex} {V}={E}+{Ir} {/tex} {tex} =E+\frac{(n-4) E}{n r} \times r {/tex} {tex} =\frac{(2 n-4) E}{n} {/tex}
solution: Net e.m.f {tex}=(n-4) {E}{/tex} {tex} \therefore {I}=\frac{({n}-4) {E}}{{nr}} {/tex} Potential difference across 'X' {tex} {V}={E}+{Ir} {/tex} {tex} =E+\frac{(n-4) E}{n r} \times r {/tex} {tex} =\frac{(2 n-4) E}{n} {/tex}
solution_html:
Net e.m.f {tex}=(n-4) {E}{/tex}
{tex} \therefore {I}=\frac{({n}-4) {E}}{{nr}} {/tex}
Potential difference across 'X'
{tex} {V}={E}+{Ir} {/tex}
{tex} =E+\frac{(n-4) E}{n r} \times r {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{(2 n-4) E}{n} {/tex}

1. Define resistivity of a conductor. Discuss its dependence on temperature of the conductor and draw a plot of resistivity of copper as a function of temperature.
    1. "A low voltage battery from which high current is required must have low internal resistance." Justify.
    2. "A high voltage battery must have a large internal resistance." Justify.
question_text: Define resistivity of a conductor. Discuss its dependence on temperature of the conductor and draw a plot of resistivity of copper as a function of temperature. "A low voltage battery from which high current is required must have low internal resistance." Justify. "A high voltage battery must have a large internal resistance." Justify.
explanation: Resistivity is the resistance of a material of unit length having unit area of cross-section. On increasing the temperature of a conductor, the resistivity increases. A low internal resistance allows large current to be drawn even at a low voltage. To limit the current.
solution: Resistivity is the resistance of a material of unit length having unit area of cross-section. On increasing the temperature of a conductor, the resistivity increases. A low internal resistance allows large current to be drawn even at a low voltage. To limit the current.
solution_html:
1. Resistivity is the resistance of a material of unit length having unit area of cross-section.
    On increasing the temperature of a conductor, the resistivity increases.
    1. A low internal resistance allows large current to be drawn even at a low voltage.
    2. To limit the current.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A battery of emf E and internal resistance r is connected to a variable external resistance {tex}R{/tex}.
    1. Obtain the expression for current I in the circuit and the value of maximum current the battery can supply.
    2. Obtain the terminal voltage V across the battery and its maximum possible value.
  2. The above battery sends a current {tex}{I}_{1}{/tex} when {tex}{R}={R}_{1}{/tex} and a current {tex}I_{2}{/tex} when {tex}R=R_{2}{/tex}. Obtain the internal resistance of the battery in terms of {tex}{I}_{1}, {I}_{2}, {R}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{R}_{2}{/tex}.
question_text: A battery of emf E and internal resistance r is connected to a variable external resistance {tex}R{/tex} . Obtain the expression for current I in the circuit and the value of maximum current the battery can supply. Obtain the terminal voltage V across the battery and its maximum possible value. The above battery sends a current {tex}{I}_{1}{/tex} when {tex}{R}={R}_{1}{/tex} and a current {tex}I_{2}{/tex} when {tex}R=R_{2}{/tex} . Obtain the internal resistance of the battery in terms of {tex}{I}_{1}, {I}_{2}, {R}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{R}_{2}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}V=E-I r{/tex} {tex}I R=E-I r{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{E}{R+r}{/tex} For maximum value of current {tex}{R}=0{/tex} {tex}I_{\max }=\frac{E}{r}{/tex} {tex}{V}={V}_{+}+{V}_{-}-{Ir}{/tex} {tex}V=E-I r{/tex} {tex}V_{\max }=E , \text { when } {I}=0{/tex} {tex}{I}_{1} {R}_{1}+{I}_{1} {r}={I}_{2} {R}_{2}+{I}_{2} {r}{/tex} {tex}{r}=\frac{{I}_{2} {R}_{2}-{I}_{1} {R}_{1}}{{I}_{1}-{I}_{2}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}V=E-I r{/tex} {tex}I R=E-I r{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{E}{R+r}{/tex} For maximum value of current {tex}{R}=0{/tex} {tex}I_{\max }=\frac{E}{r}{/tex} {tex}{V}={V}_{+}+{V}_{-}-{Ir}{/tex} {tex}V=E-I r{/tex} {tex}V_{\max }=E , \text { when } {I}=0{/tex} {tex}{I}_{1} {R}_{1}+{I}_{1} {r}={I}_{2} {R}_{2}+{I}_{2} {r}{/tex} {tex}{r}=\frac{{I}_{2} {R}_{2}-{I}_{1} {R}_{1}}{{I}_{1}-{I}_{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}V=E-I r{/tex}
      {tex}I R=E-I r{/tex}
      {tex}I=\frac{E}{R+r}{/tex}
      For maximum value of current {tex}{R}=0{/tex}
      {tex}I_{\max }=\frac{E}{r}{/tex}
    2. {tex}{V}={V}_{+}+{V}_{-}-{Ir}{/tex}
      {tex}V=E-I r{/tex}
      {tex}V_{\max }=E , \text { when } {I}=0{/tex}
  2. {tex}{I}_{1} {R}_{1}+{I}_{1} {r}={I}_{2} {R}_{2}+{I}_{2} {r}{/tex}
    {tex}{r}=\frac{{I}_{2} {R}_{2}-{I}_{1} {R}_{1}}{{I}_{1}-{I}_{2}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Derive an expression for the resistivity of a conductor in terms of number density of free electrons and relaxation time.
  2. The figure shows the plot of current through a cross-section of wire over two different time intervals. Compare the charges ({tex}{Q}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{Q}_{2}{/tex}) that pass through the cross-section during these time intervals.
question_text: Derive an expression for the resistivity of a conductor in terms of number density of free electrons and relaxation time. The figure shows the plot of current through a cross-section of wire over two different time intervals. Compare the charges ( {tex}{Q}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{Q}_{2}{/tex} ) that pass through the cross-section during these time intervals.
explanation: Total charge transported along E is {tex}{I} \Delta {t}=\frac{{e}^{2} {~A}}{{~m}} \tau {n} \Delta {tE}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I}{A}=\frac{n e^{2}}{m} \tau E{/tex} {tex}{J}=\frac{1}{\rho} {E}{/tex} {tex}\rho=\frac{m}{n e^{2} \tau}{/tex} From given graph {tex} \frac{{Q}_1}{{Q}_2}=\frac{{A}_1(\text { Area of rectangle })}{{A}_2(\text { Area of triangle })} {/tex} {tex} \frac{{Q}_1}{{Q}_2}=\frac{2}{3 / 2} {/tex} {tex} Q_1>Q_2 {/tex}
solution: Total charge transported along E is {tex}{I} \Delta {t}=\frac{{e}^{2} {~A}}{{~m}} \tau {n} \Delta {tE}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I}{A}=\frac{n e^{2}}{m} \tau E{/tex} {tex}{J}=\frac{1}{\rho} {E}{/tex} {tex}\rho=\frac{m}{n e^{2} \tau}{/tex} From given graph {tex} \frac{{Q}_1}{{Q}_2}=\frac{{A}_1(\text { Area of rectangle })}{{A}_2(\text { Area of triangle })} {/tex} {tex} \frac{{Q}_1}{{Q}_2}=\frac{2}{3 / 2} {/tex} {tex} Q_1>Q_2 {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Total charge transported along E is
    {tex}{I} \Delta {t}=\frac{{e}^{2} {~A}}{{~m}} \tau {n} \Delta {tE}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{I}{A}=\frac{n e^{2}}{m} \tau E{/tex}
    {tex}{J}=\frac{1}{\rho} {E}{/tex}
    {tex}\rho=\frac{m}{n e^{2} \tau}{/tex}
  2. From given graph
    {tex} \frac{{Q}_1}{{Q}_2}=\frac{{A}_1(\text { Area of rectangle })}{{A}_2(\text { Area of triangle })} {/tex}
    {tex} \frac{{Q}_1}{{Q}_2}=\frac{2}{3 / 2} {/tex}
    {tex} Q_1>Q_2 {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A conductor of length {tex}l{/tex} is connected across an ideal cell of emf E. Keeping the cell connected, the length of the conductor is increased to {tex}2 l{/tex} by gradually stretching it. If {tex}R{/tex} and {tex}R^{\prime}{/tex} are initial and final values of resistance and {tex}v_{{d}}{/tex} and {tex}v_{{d}}{ }^{\prime}{/tex} are initial and final values of drift velocity, find the relation between (a) {tex}R^{\prime}{/tex} and {tex}R{/tex} and (b) {tex}v_{{d}}{ }^{\prime}{/tex} and {tex}v_{{d}}{/tex}.
  2. When electrons drift in a conductor from lower to higher potential, does it mean that all the 'free electrons' of the conductor are moving in the same direction?
question_text: A conductor of length {tex}l{/tex} is connected across an ideal cell of emf E. Keeping the cell connected, the length of the conductor is increased to {tex}2 l{/tex} by gradually stretching it. If {tex}R{/tex} and {tex}R^{\prime}{/tex} are initial and final values of resistance and {tex}v_{{d}}{/tex} and {tex}v_{{d}}{ }^{\prime}{/tex} are initial and final values of drift velocity, find the relation between (a) {tex}R^{\prime}{/tex} and {tex}R{/tex} and (b) {tex}v_{{d}}{ }^{\prime}{/tex} and {tex}v_{{d}}{/tex} . When electrons drift in a conductor from lower to higher potential, does it mean that all the 'free electrons' of the conductor are moving in the same direction?
explanation: {tex}l^{\prime} =2 l{/tex} {tex}A l =A^{\prime} l^{\prime}=\text { volume of the wire }{/tex} {tex}A l =A^{\prime}(2 l){/tex} {tex}\frac{A}{2} =A^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}{R} =\frac{\rho l}{A}{/tex} {tex}R^{\prime} =\frac{\rho l^{\prime}}{A^{\prime}}{/tex} {tex}R^{\prime}= \frac{\rho(2 l)}{A / 2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{R^{\prime}}{R} =4{/tex} {tex}{v}_{{d}}=\frac{{eE}}{{m}} \tau{/tex} {tex}{v}_{{d}}=\frac{{eV}}{{ml}} \tau{/tex} {tex}{v}_{{d}}^{\prime}=\frac{{eV}}{{ml}^{\prime}} \tau{/tex} {tex}\frac{{v}_{{d}}^{\prime}}{{v}_{{d}}}=\frac{l}{l^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} No
solution: {tex}l^{\prime} =2 l{/tex} {tex}A l =A^{\prime} l^{\prime}=\text { volume of the wire }{/tex} {tex}A l =A^{\prime}(2 l){/tex} {tex}\frac{A}{2} =A^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}{R} =\frac{\rho l}{A}{/tex} {tex}R^{\prime} =\frac{\rho l^{\prime}}{A^{\prime}}{/tex} {tex}R^{\prime}= \frac{\rho(2 l)}{A / 2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{R^{\prime}}{R} =4{/tex} {tex}{v}_{{d}}=\frac{{eE}}{{m}} \tau{/tex} {tex}{v}_{{d}}=\frac{{eV}}{{ml}} \tau{/tex} {tex}{v}_{{d}}^{\prime}=\frac{{eV}}{{ml}^{\prime}} \tau{/tex} {tex}\frac{{v}_{{d}}^{\prime}}{{v}_{{d}}}=\frac{l}{l^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} No
solution_html:
1. {tex}l^{\prime} =2 l{/tex}
      {tex}A l =A^{\prime} l^{\prime}=\text { volume of the wire }{/tex}
      {tex}A l =A^{\prime}(2 l){/tex}
      {tex}\frac{A}{2} =A^{\prime}{/tex}
      {tex}{R} =\frac{\rho l}{A}{/tex}
      {tex}R^{\prime} =\frac{\rho l^{\prime}}{A^{\prime}}{/tex}
      {tex}R^{\prime}= \frac{\rho(2 l)}{A / 2}{/tex}
      {tex}\frac{R^{\prime}}{R} =4{/tex}
    2. {tex}{v}_{{d}}=\frac{{eE}}{{m}} \tau{/tex}
      {tex}{v}_{{d}}=\frac{{eV}}{{ml}} \tau{/tex}
      {tex}{v}_{{d}}^{\prime}=\frac{{eV}}{{ml}^{\prime}} \tau{/tex}
      {tex}\frac{{v}_{{d}}^{\prime}}{{v}_{{d}}}=\frac{l}{l^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
  2. No

subject: Physics
question_html:
Two batteries of emf's {tex}3 {~V} \ \& \ 6 {~V}{/tex} and internal resistances {tex}0.2 \Omega \ \& \ 0.4 \Omega{/tex} are connected in parallel. This combination is connected to a {tex}4 \Omega{/tex} resistor. Find:

  1. the equivalent emf of the combination
  2. the equivalent internal resistance of the combination
  3. the current drawn from the combination
question_text: Two batteries of emf's {tex}3 {~V} \ \& \ 6 {~V}{/tex} and internal resistances {tex}0.2 \Omega \ \& \ 0.4 \Omega{/tex} are connected in parallel. This combination is connected to a {tex}4 \Omega{/tex} resistor. Find: the equivalent emf of the combination the equivalent internal resistance of the combination the current drawn from the combination
explanation: Because {tex}E_{\text {eq }}=\frac{E_{1} r_{2}+E_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex} {tex}E_{e q}=\frac{3 \times 0.4+6 \times 0.2}{0.6}=4 {~V}{/tex} {tex}r_{e q}=\frac{r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex} {tex}r_{e q}=\frac{0.2 \times 0.4}{0.2+0.4}=0.133 \Omega{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{E}{R+r_{e q}}{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{4}{4+0.13}=\frac{4}{4.13} {~A}{/tex} {tex}I=0.9 A{/tex}
solution: Because {tex}E_{\text {eq }}=\frac{E_{1} r_{2}+E_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex} {tex}E_{e q}=\frac{3 \times 0.4+6 \times 0.2}{0.6}=4 {~V}{/tex} {tex}r_{e q}=\frac{r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex} {tex}r_{e q}=\frac{0.2 \times 0.4}{0.2+0.4}=0.133 \Omega{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{E}{R+r_{e q}}{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{4}{4+0.13}=\frac{4}{4.13} {~A}{/tex} {tex}I=0.9 A{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Because {tex}E_{\text {eq }}=\frac{E_{1} r_{2}+E_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}E_{e q}=\frac{3 \times 0.4+6 \times 0.2}{0.6}=4 {~V}{/tex}
  2. {tex}r_{e q}=\frac{r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}r_{e q}=\frac{0.2 \times 0.4}{0.2+0.4}=0.133 \Omega{/tex}
  3. {tex}I=\frac{E}{R+r_{e q}}{/tex}
    {tex}I=\frac{4}{4+0.13}=\frac{4}{4.13} {~A}{/tex}
    {tex}I=0.9 A{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A battery of emf E and internal resistance r is connected to a rheostat. When a current of 2 A is dawn from the battery, the potential difference across the rheostat is {tex}5 V{/tex}. The potential difference becomes {tex}4 V{/tex} when a current of 4 A is drawn from the battery. Calculate the value of E and r.
question_text: A battery of emf E and internal resistance r is connected to a rheostat. When a current of 2 A is dawn from the battery, the potential difference across the rheostat is {tex}5 V{/tex} . The potential difference becomes {tex}4 V{/tex} when a current of 4 A is drawn from the battery. Calculate the value of E and r.
explanation: {tex} E=V+I r {/tex} In first case {tex}E=5+2 r{/tex} In second case {tex}{E}=4+4 {r}{/tex} After solving {tex}{E}=6 {~V}{/tex} {tex}{r}=0.5 \Omega{/tex}
solution: {tex} E=V+I r {/tex} In first case {tex}E=5+2 r{/tex} In second case {tex}{E}=4+4 {r}{/tex} After solving {tex}{E}=6 {~V}{/tex} {tex}{r}=0.5 \Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} E=V+I r {/tex}
In first case
{tex}E=5+2 r{/tex}
In second case
{tex}{E}=4+4 {r}{/tex}
After solving
{tex}{E}=6 {~V}{/tex}
{tex}{r}=0.5 \Omega{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Two cells are connected in parallel by connecting their positive terminals together and connecting their negative terminals together. Derive expression for the equivalent emf and the equivalent internal resistance of the combination.
question_text: Two cells are connected in parallel by connecting their positive terminals together and connecting their negative terminals together. Derive expression for the equivalent emf and the equivalent internal resistance of the combination.
explanation: {tex} {I}={I}_1+{I}_2 {/tex} ...(1) For the first cell, {tex} {V}={V}\left({~B}_1\right)-{V}\left({~B}_2\right)=\varepsilon_1-{I}_1 {r}_1 {/tex} ...(2) For the second cell, {tex} {V}={V}\left({~B}_1\right)-{V}\left({~B}_2\right)=\varepsilon_2-{I}_2 {r}_2 {/tex} ...(3) Combining equations (1), (2) and (3) {tex} I =\frac{\varepsilon_1-V}{r_1}+\frac{\varepsilon_2-V}{r_2} {/tex} {tex} =\left(\frac{\varepsilon_1}{r_1}+\frac{\varepsilon_2}{r_2}\right)-V\left(\frac{1}{r_1}+\frac{1}{r_2}\right) {/tex} {tex} V =\frac{\varepsilon_1 r_2+\varepsilon_2 r_1}{r_1+r_2}-I\left(\frac{r_1 r_2}{r_1+r_2}\right) {/tex} {tex} \varepsilon_{e q} =\frac{\varepsilon_1 r_2+\varepsilon_2 r_1}{r_1+r_2} {/tex} {tex} r_{e q} =\frac{r_1 r_2}{r_1+r_2} {/tex}
solution: {tex} {I}={I}_1+{I}_2 {/tex} ...(1) For the first cell, {tex} {V}={V}\left({~B}_1\right)-{V}\left({~B}_2\right)=\varepsilon_1-{I}_1 {r}_1 {/tex} ...(2) For the second cell, {tex} {V}={V}\left({~B}_1\right)-{V}\left({~B}_2\right)=\varepsilon_2-{I}_2 {r}_2 {/tex} ...(3) Combining equations (1), (2) and (3) {tex} I =\frac{\varepsilon_1-V}{r_1}+\frac{\varepsilon_2-V}{r_2} {/tex} {tex} =\left(\frac{\varepsilon_1}{r_1}+\frac{\varepsilon_2}{r_2}\right)-V\left(\frac{1}{r_1}+\frac{1}{r_2}\right) {/tex} {tex} V =\frac{\varepsilon_1 r_2+\varepsilon_2 r_1}{r_1+r_2}-I\left(\frac{r_1 r_2}{r_1+r_2}\right) {/tex} {tex} \varepsilon_{e q} =\frac{\varepsilon_1 r_2+\varepsilon_2 r_1}{r_1+r_2} {/tex} {tex} r_{e q} =\frac{r_1 r_2}{r_1+r_2} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} {I}={I}_1+{I}_2 {/tex} ...(1)
For the first cell,
{tex} {V}={V}\left({~B}_1\right)-{V}\left({~B}_2\right)=\varepsilon_1-{I}_1 {r}_1 {/tex} ...(2)
For the second cell,
{tex} {V}={V}\left({~B}_1\right)-{V}\left({~B}_2\right)=\varepsilon_2-{I}_2 {r}_2 {/tex} ...(3)
Combining equations (1), (2) and (3)
{tex} I =\frac{\varepsilon_1-V}{r_1}+\frac{\varepsilon_2-V}{r_2} {/tex}
{tex} =\left(\frac{\varepsilon_1}{r_1}+\frac{\varepsilon_2}{r_2}\right)-V\left(\frac{1}{r_1}+\frac{1}{r_2}\right) {/tex}
{tex} V =\frac{\varepsilon_1 r_2+\varepsilon_2 r_1}{r_1+r_2}-I\left(\frac{r_1 r_2}{r_1+r_2}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \varepsilon_{e q} =\frac{\varepsilon_1 r_2+\varepsilon_2 r_1}{r_1+r_2} {/tex}
{tex} r_{e q} =\frac{r_1 r_2}{r_1+r_2} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Define 'emf' of a cell. Derive expression for the equivalent emf and the equivalent internal resistance of two cells connected in series.
question_text: Define 'emf' of a cell. Derive expression for the equivalent emf and the equivalent internal resistance of two cells connected in series.
explanation: Emf of a cell is defined as the potential difference between the two electrodes of the cell when no current is drawn from it. {tex} V_A-V_B=\varepsilon_1-I r_1 {/tex} {tex} V_B-V_C=\varepsilon_2-I r_2 {/tex} {tex} V_A-V_C=\varepsilon_1+\varepsilon_2-I\left(r_1+r_2\right) {/tex} {tex} \because V_{A C}=\varepsilon_{e q}-I r_{e q} {/tex} {tex} \therefore r_{e q}=r_1+r_2 {/tex}
solution: Emf of a cell is defined as the potential difference between the two electrodes of the cell when no current is drawn from it. {tex} V_A-V_B=\varepsilon_1-I r_1 {/tex} {tex} V_B-V_C=\varepsilon_2-I r_2 {/tex} {tex} V_A-V_C=\varepsilon_1+\varepsilon_2-I\left(r_1+r_2\right) {/tex} {tex} \because V_{A C}=\varepsilon_{e q}-I r_{e q} {/tex} {tex} \therefore r_{e q}=r_1+r_2 {/tex}
solution_html:
• Emf of a cell is defined as the potential difference between the two electrodes of the cell when no current is drawn from it.

  • {tex} V_A-V_B=\varepsilon_1-I r_1 {/tex}
    {tex} V_B-V_C=\varepsilon_2-I r_2 {/tex}
    {tex} V_A-V_C=\varepsilon_1+\varepsilon_2-I\left(r_1+r_2\right) {/tex}
    {tex} \because V_{A C}=\varepsilon_{e q}-I r_{e q} {/tex}
    {tex} \therefore r_{e q}=r_1+r_2 {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)
In a metallic conductor, an electron, moving due to thermal motion, suffers collisions with the heavy fixed ions but after collision, it will emerge out with the same speed but in random directions. If we consider all the electrons, their average velocity will be zero. When an electric field is applied, electrons move with an average velocity, known as drift velocity ({tex}{v}_{{d}}{/tex}). The average time between successive collisions is known as relaxation time {tex}(\tau){/tex}. The magnitude of drift velocity per unit electric field is called mobility ({tex}\mu{/tex}).
An expression for current through the conductor can be obtained in terms of drift velocity, number of electrons per unit volume (n), electronic charge (-e), and the cross-sectional area of the conductor. This expression leads to an expression between current density {tex}(\hat{j}){/tex} and the electric field ({tex}\vec{E}{/tex}). Hence, an expression for resistivity ({tex}\rho{/tex}) of a metal is obtained. This expression helps us to understand increase in resistivity of a metal with increase in its temperature, in terms of change in the relaxation time {tex}(\tau){/tex} and change in the number density of electrons {tex}({n}){/tex}.

  1. Consider two cylindrical conductors A and B, made of the same metal connected in series to a battery. The length and the radius of B are twice that of A. If {tex}\mu_{{A}}{/tex} and {tex}\mu_{{B}}{/tex} are the mobility of electrons in A and {tex}B{/tex} respectively, then {tex}\frac{\mu_A}{\mu_B}{/tex} is:
    1. {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
    3. 2
    4. 1
  2. A wire of length 0.5 m and cross-sectional area {tex}1.0 \times 10^{-7} {~m}^2{/tex} is connected to a battery of 2 V that maintains a current of 1.5 A in it. The conductivity of the material of the wire (in {tex}\Omega^{-1} {~m}^{-1}{/tex}) is:
    1. {tex}2.5 \times 10^4{/tex}
    2. {tex}3.0 \times 10^5{/tex}
    3. {tex}3.75 \times 10^6{/tex}
    4. {tex}5.0 \times 10^7{/tex}
  3. The temperature coefficient of resistance of nichrome is {tex}1.70 \times 10^{-4}{ }^{\circ} {C}^{-1}{/tex}. In order to increase resistance of a nichrome wire by {tex}8.5 \%{/tex}, the temperature of the wire should be increased by:
    1. {tex}250^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
    2. {tex}500^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
    3. {tex}850^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
    4. {tex}1000^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
    1. Consider the contribution of the following two factors I and II in resistivity of a metal:
      1. Relaxation time of electrons
      2. Number of electrons per unit volume
        The resistivity of a metal increases with increase in its temperature because:
        1. I decreases and II increases.
        2. I increases and II is almost constant.
        3. Both I and II increase.
        4. I decreases and II is almost constant.

          OR

    2. A steady current flows in a copper wire of non-uniform cross-section. Consider the following three physical quantities:
      1. Electric field
      2. Current density
      3. Drift speed
        Then at the different points along the wire:
        1. II and III change, but I is constant.
        2. I and II change, but III is constant.
        3. I and III change, but II is constant.
        4. All I, II and III change.
question_text: Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4) In a metallic conductor, an electron, moving due to thermal motion, suffers collisions with the heavy fixed ions but after collision, it will emerge out with the same speed but in random directions. If we consider all the electrons, their average velocity will be zero. When an electric field is applied, electrons move with an average velocity, known as drift velocity ( {tex}{v}_{{d}}{/tex} ). The average time between successive collisions is known as relaxation time {tex}(\tau){/tex} . The magnitude of drift velocity per unit electric field is called mobility ( {tex}\mu{/tex} ). An expression for current through the conductor can be obtained in terms of drift velocity, number of electrons per unit volume (n), electronic charge (-e), and the cross-sectional area of the conductor. This expression leads to an expression between current density {tex}(\hat{j}){/tex} and the electric field ( {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} ). Hence, an expression for resistivity ( {tex}\rho{/tex} ) of a metal is obtained. This expression helps us to understand increase in resistivity of a metal with increase in its temperature, in terms of change in the relaxation time {tex}(\tau){/tex} and change in the number density of electrons {tex}({n}){/tex} . Consider two cylindrical conductors A and B, made of the same metal connected in series to a battery. The length and the radius of B are twice that of A. If {tex}\mu_{{A}}{/tex} and {tex}\mu_{{B}}{/tex} are the mobility of electrons in A and {tex}B{/tex} respectively, then {tex}\frac{\mu_A}{\mu_B}{/tex} is: {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} 2 1 A wire of length 0.5 m and cross-sectional area {tex}1.0 \times 10^{-7} {~m}^2{/tex} is connected to a battery of 2 V that maintains a current of 1.5 A in it. The conductivity of the material of the wire (in {tex}\Omega^{-1} {~m}^{-1}{/tex} ) is: {tex}2.5 \times 10^4{/tex} {tex}3.0 \times 10^5{/tex} {tex}3.75 \times 10^6{/tex} {tex}5.0 \times 10^7{/tex} The temperature coefficient of resistance of nichrome is {tex}1.70 \times 10^{-4}{ }^{\circ} {C}^{-1}{/tex} . In order to increase resistance of a nichrome wire by {tex}8.5 \%{/tex} , the temperature of the wire should be increased by: {tex}250^{\circ} {C}{/tex} {tex}500^{\circ} {C}{/tex} {tex}850^{\circ} {C}{/tex} {tex}1000^{\circ} {C}{/tex} Consider the contribution of the following two factors I and II in resistivity of a metal: Relaxation time of electrons Number of electrons per unit volume The resistivity of a metal increases with increase in its temperature because: I decreases and II increases. I increases and II is almost constant. Both I and II increase. I decreases and II is almost constant. OR A steady current flows in a copper wire of non-uniform cross-section. Consider the following three physical quantities: Electric field Current density Drift speed Then at the different points along the wire: II and III change, but I is constant. I and II change, but III is constant. I and III change, but II is constant. All I, II and III change.
explanation: (D) 1 (C) {tex}3.75 \times 10^6{/tex} (B) 500 o C (D) I decreases and II is almost constant OR (D) All I, II and III change
solution: (D) 1 (C) {tex}3.75 \times 10^6{/tex} (B) 500 o C (D) I decreases and II is almost constant OR (D) All I, II and III change
solution_html:
1. (D) 1
  2. (C) {tex}3.75 \times 10^6{/tex}
  3. (B) 500oC
    1. (D) I decreases and II is almost constant

      OR

    2. (D) All I, II and III change

subject: Physics
question_html: Define the term 'drift velocity' of electrons in a current carrying conductor and derive, in its term, the expression for current flowing through the conductor.
question_text: Define the term 'drift velocity' of electrons in a current carrying conductor and derive, in its term, the expression for current flowing through the conductor.
explanation: The drift velocity is defined as average velocity with which free electrons in a conductor get drifted under the influence of electric field applied across the conductor. Let us consider a conductor of length l and cross-sectional area A connected across the battery of voltage V. Let n be the electron density current flowing through the conductor is given by {tex} {I}=\frac{\text { Total charge }}{\text { time }} {/tex} ...(1) i.e {tex} {Q} ={nA} {~L} \times {e} {/tex} ...(2) {tex} {t} =\frac{{L}}{{v}_{{d}}} {/tex} ...(3) Using (2) and (3) in (1) {tex} {I} =\frac{{Q}}{{t}}=\frac{{nALe}}{{~L} / {v}_{{d}}} {/tex} {tex} {I} ={neAv}_{{d}} {/tex}
solution: The drift velocity is defined as average velocity with which free electrons in a conductor get drifted under the influence of electric field applied across the conductor. Let us consider a conductor of length l and cross-sectional area A connected across the battery of voltage V. Let n be the electron density current flowing through the conductor is given by {tex} {I}=\frac{\text { Total charge }}{\text { time }} {/tex} ...(1) i.e {tex} {Q} ={nA} {~L} \times {e} {/tex} ...(2) {tex} {t} =\frac{{L}}{{v}_{{d}}} {/tex} ...(3) Using (2) and (3) in (1) {tex} {I} =\frac{{Q}}{{t}}=\frac{{nALe}}{{~L} / {v}_{{d}}} {/tex} {tex} {I} ={neAv}_{{d}} {/tex}
solution_html:
The drift velocity is defined as average velocity with which free electrons in a conductor get drifted under the influence of electric field applied across the conductor.
Let us consider a conductor of length l and cross-sectional area A connected across the battery of voltage V.

Let n be the electron density current flowing through the conductor is given by
{tex} {I}=\frac{\text { Total charge }}{\text { time }} {/tex} ...(1)
i.e {tex} {Q} ={nA} {~L} \times {e} {/tex} ...(2)
{tex} {t} =\frac{{L}}{{v}_{{d}}} {/tex} ...(3)
Using (2) and (3) in (1)
{tex} {I} =\frac{{Q}}{{t}}=\frac{{nALe}}{{~L} / {v}_{{d}}} {/tex}
{tex} {I} ={neAv}_{{d}} {/tex}

question_html: The figure shows a network of resistors connected across a battery. Find the current supplied by the battery of emf 5 V and internal resistance {tex}\frac{2}{3} \Omega{/tex}.
question_text: The figure shows a network of resistors connected across a battery. Find the current supplied by the battery of emf 5 V and internal resistance {tex}\frac{2}{3} \Omega{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} \frac{{R}_{{AB}}}{{R}_{{BC}}}=\frac{{R}_{{AD}}}{{R}_{{DC}}}=\frac{4}{1} {/tex} The bridge is balanced. Resistance of the circuit between points A and C. {tex} \frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{(8+2)}+\frac{1}{(4+1)} {/tex} {tex} R=\frac{10}{3} \Omega {/tex} {tex} {I}=\frac{{E}}{{R}+{r}} {/tex} {tex} {I}=\frac{5}{\frac{10}{3}+\frac{2}{3}} {/tex} {tex} {I}=1.25 {~A} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \frac{{R}_{{AB}}}{{R}_{{BC}}}=\frac{{R}_{{AD}}}{{R}_{{DC}}}=\frac{4}{1} {/tex} The bridge is balanced. Resistance of the circuit between points A and C. {tex} \frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{(8+2)}+\frac{1}{(4+1)} {/tex} {tex} R=\frac{10}{3} \Omega {/tex} {tex} {I}=\frac{{E}}{{R}+{r}} {/tex} {tex} {I}=\frac{5}{\frac{10}{3}+\frac{2}{3}} {/tex} {tex} {I}=1.25 {~A} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \frac{{R}_{{AB}}}{{R}_{{BC}}}=\frac{{R}_{{AD}}}{{R}_{{DC}}}=\frac{4}{1} {/tex}
The bridge is balanced.
Resistance of the circuit between points A and C.
{tex} \frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{(8+2)}+\frac{1}{(4+1)} {/tex}
{tex} R=\frac{10}{3} \Omega {/tex}
{tex} {I}=\frac{{E}}{{R}+{r}} {/tex}
{tex} {I}=\frac{5}{\frac{10}{3}+\frac{2}{3}} {/tex}
{tex} {I}=1.25 {~A} {/tex}

question_html: State Kirchhoff's laws. Apply these laws to find the values of current flowing in the three branches of the given circuit.
question_text: State Kirchhoff's laws. Apply these laws to find the values of current flowing in the three branches of the given circuit.
explanation: Junction rule: At any junction, the sum of the currents entering the junction is equal to the sum of the currents leaving the junction. Loop rule: the algebraic sum of changes in potential around any closed loop involving resistors and cells in the loop is zero. In closed loop {tex}B C D E B \ 2 I_{1}+3 I_{2}=10{/tex} ... (1) In closed loop {tex}A B C D E F A \ 4 {I}_{1}+2 I_{2}=-5{/tex} ... (2) On solving eq. (1) and (2) {tex}I_{1}=-\frac{35}{8} {~A}{/tex} in arm {tex}A F{/tex} {tex}I_{2}=\frac{25}{4} {~A}{/tex} in arm {tex}B E{/tex} {tex}I_{1}+I_{2}=-\frac{15}{8} {~A}{/tex} in arm {tex}C D{/tex}
solution: Junction rule: At any junction, the sum of the currents entering the junction is equal to the sum of the currents leaving the junction. Loop rule: the algebraic sum of changes in potential around any closed loop involving resistors and cells in the loop is zero. In closed loop {tex}B C D E B \ 2 I_{1}+3 I_{2}=10{/tex} ... (1) In closed loop {tex}A B C D E F A \ 4 {I}_{1}+2 I_{2}=-5{/tex} ... (2) On solving eq. (1) and (2) {tex}I_{1}=-\frac{35}{8} {~A}{/tex} in arm {tex}A F{/tex} {tex}I_{2}=\frac{25}{4} {~A}{/tex} in arm {tex}B E{/tex} {tex}I_{1}+I_{2}=-\frac{15}{8} {~A}{/tex} in arm {tex}C D{/tex}
solution_html:
Junction rule: At any junction, the sum of the currents entering the junction is equal to the sum of the currents leaving the junction.
Loop rule: the algebraic sum of changes in potential around any closed loop involving resistors and cells in the loop is zero.
In closed loop {tex}B C D E B \ 2 I_{1}+3 I_{2}=10{/tex} ... (1)
In closed loop {tex}A B C D E F A \ 4 {I}_{1}+2 I_{2}=-5{/tex} ... (2)
On solving eq. (1) and (2)
{tex}I_{1}=-\frac{35}{8} {~A}{/tex} in arm {tex}A F{/tex}
{tex}I_{2}=\frac{25}{4} {~A}{/tex} in arm {tex}B E{/tex}
{tex}I_{1}+I_{2}=-\frac{15}{8} {~A}{/tex} in arm {tex}C D{/tex}

question_html:
1. What is the difference between 'velocity' and 'drift velocity' of electrons in a current-carrying conductor.
  2. A copper wire of uniform cross-sectional area carries a current of 3.4 A. The drift velocity of conduction electrons is {tex}0.2 {~mm} / {s}{/tex}. If the number density of electrons in copper is {tex}8.5 \times 10^{28} {m}^{-3}{/tex}, find the area of cross-section of the wire.
question_text: What is the difference between 'velocity' and 'drift velocity' of electrons in a current-carrying conductor. A copper wire of uniform cross-sectional area carries a current of 3.4 A. The drift velocity of conduction electrons is {tex}0.2 {~mm} / {s}{/tex} . If the number density of electrons in copper is {tex}8.5 \times 10^{28} {m}^{-3}{/tex} , find the area of cross-section of the wire.
explanation: Velocity is the rate of change of displacement with time. Drift velocity is the average velocity of free electrons under the influence of external electric field. {tex}I=n e A {v}_{d}{/tex} {tex}A=\frac{I}{n e {v}_{d}}{/tex} {tex}A=\frac{3.4}{8.5 \times 10^{28} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} {tex}A=1.25 \times 10^{-6} {~m}^{2}{/tex}
solution: Velocity is the rate of change of displacement with time. Drift velocity is the average velocity of free electrons under the influence of external electric field. {tex}I=n e A {v}_{d}{/tex} {tex}A=\frac{I}{n e {v}_{d}}{/tex} {tex}A=\frac{3.4}{8.5 \times 10^{28} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} {tex}A=1.25 \times 10^{-6} {~m}^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Velocity is the rate of change of displacement with time.
    Drift velocity is the average velocity of free electrons under the influence of external electric field.
  2. {tex}I=n e A {v}_{d}{/tex}
    {tex}A=\frac{I}{n e {v}_{d}}{/tex}
    {tex}A=\frac{3.4}{8.5 \times 10^{28} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
    {tex}A=1.25 \times 10^{-6} {~m}^{2}{/tex}

question_html: A steady current I is passed through a conductor at room temperature for time t. It is observed that its temperature rises by {tex}0 \cdot 5^{\circ} {C}{/tex}. If 2 I current is passed through the conductor (at room temperature) for the same duration, the rise in its temperature will be approximately:

question_html:
1. Define the term 'drift velocity' of conduction electrons in a conductor.
  2. A conductor of length {tex}l{/tex} and area of cross-section A is connected across an ideal battery of emf V. Derive the formula for the current density in terms of relaxation time {tex}\tau{/tex}.
question_text: Define the term 'drift velocity' of conduction electrons in a conductor. A conductor of length {tex}l{/tex} and area of cross-section A is connected across an ideal battery of emf V. Derive the formula for the current density in terms of relaxation time {tex}\tau{/tex} .
explanation: Drift velocity is the average velocity with which the free electrons move under external electric field in a conductor. Current density {tex}j =\frac{I}{A}{/tex} {tex}\because I =n e A {v}_{d}{/tex} {tex}\therefore j =n e {v}_{d}{/tex} {tex}\text { But } {v}_{d} =\frac{e E \tau}{m}{/tex} {tex}\therefore j =\frac{n e^{2} \tau E}{m}=\frac{n e^{2} \tau}{m}\left(\frac{V}{l}\right){/tex}
solution: Drift velocity is the average velocity with which the free electrons move under external electric field in a conductor. Current density {tex}j =\frac{I}{A}{/tex} {tex}\because I =n e A {v}_{d}{/tex} {tex}\therefore j =n e {v}_{d}{/tex} {tex}\text { But } {v}_{d} =\frac{e E \tau}{m}{/tex} {tex}\therefore j =\frac{n e^{2} \tau E}{m}=\frac{n e^{2} \tau}{m}\left(\frac{V}{l}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
1. Drift velocity is the average velocity with which the free electrons move under external electric field in a conductor.
  2. Current density
    {tex}j =\frac{I}{A}{/tex}
    {tex}\because I =n e A {v}_{d}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore j =n e {v}_{d}{/tex}
    {tex}\text { But } {v}_{d} =\frac{e E \tau}{m}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore j =\frac{n e^{2} \tau E}{m}=\frac{n e^{2} \tau}{m}\left(\frac{V}{l}\right){/tex}

question_html:
Two wires made of the same material have the same length (l) but different cross-sectional areas {tex}{A}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{A}_{2}{/tex}. They are connected together with a cell of voltage {tex}V{/tex}. Find the ratio of the drift velocities of free electrons in the two wires when they are joined in

  1. series, and
  2. parallel.
question_text: Two wires made of the same material have the same length (l) but different cross-sectional areas {tex}{A}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{A}_{2}{/tex} . They are connected together with a cell of voltage {tex}V{/tex} . Find the ratio of the drift velocities of free electrons in the two wires when they are joined in series, and parallel.
explanation: Series {tex} {I}={neAv}_{{d}}{/tex} {tex} \frac{{v}_{{d}_1}}{{v}_{{d}_2}}=\frac{{A}_2}{{~A}_1} {/tex} Parallel {tex} {v}_{{d}}=\frac{{V}}{\text { RneA }}=\frac{{V}}{\rho \ln {e}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{{v}_{{d}_1}}{{v}_{{d}_2}}=\frac{1}{1} {/tex}
solution: Series {tex} {I}={neAv}_{{d}}{/tex} {tex} \frac{{v}_{{d}_1}}{{v}_{{d}_2}}=\frac{{A}_2}{{~A}_1} {/tex} Parallel {tex} {v}_{{d}}=\frac{{V}}{\text { RneA }}=\frac{{V}}{\rho \ln {e}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{{v}_{{d}_1}}{{v}_{{d}_2}}=\frac{1}{1} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Series
    {tex} {I}={neAv}_{{d}}{/tex}
    {tex} \frac{{v}_{{d}_1}}{{v}_{{d}_2}}=\frac{{A}_2}{{~A}_1} {/tex}
  2. Parallel
    {tex} {v}_{{d}}=\frac{{V}}{\text { RneA }}=\frac{{V}}{\rho \ln {e}} {/tex}
    {tex} \frac{{v}_{{d}_1}}{{v}_{{d}_2}}=\frac{1}{1} {/tex}

question_html: What is a Wheatstone bridge? Derive the condition required for the Wheatstone bridge to be in a balanced state.
question_text: What is a Wheatstone bridge? Derive the condition required for the Wheatstone bridge to be in a balanced state.
explanation: The Wheatstone bridge is an arrangement of four resistances. In this bridge, four resistances are connected on four arms of a quadrilateral. Across one diagonal, a battery and key are connected. Across the second diagonal, a galvanometer is connected as shown in the figure. Consider P, Q, R, and S are four resistances connected on the sides AB, BC, AD, and DC of the quadrilateral respectively. Galvanometer G is connected between points B and D and battery B is connected between A and C' Now in the balance condition, when the deflection in the galvanometer is zero in the closed mesh ABDA, then by applying Kirchhoff's law, I 1 P - I 2 P = 0 or I 1 P = I 2 R ..... (i) In closed mesh CBDC, I 1 Q = I 2 S ..... (ii) Dividing (i) by (ii), {tex}\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{S}{/tex} This is the balanced condition for the Wheatstone bridge.
solution: The Wheatstone bridge is an arrangement of four resistances. In this bridge, four resistances are connected on four arms of a quadrilateral. Across one diagonal, a battery and key are connected. Across the second diagonal, a galvanometer is connected as shown in the figure. Consider P, Q, R, and S are four resistances connected on the sides AB, BC, AD, and DC of the quadrilateral respectively. Galvanometer G is connected between points B and D and battery B is connected between A and C' Now in the balance condition, when the deflection in the galvanometer is zero in the closed mesh ABDA, then by applying Kirchhoff's law, I 1 P - I 2 P = 0 or I 1 P = I 2 R ..... (i) In closed mesh CBDC, I 1 Q = I 2 S ..... (ii) Dividing (i) by (ii), {tex}\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{S}{/tex} This is the balanced condition for the Wheatstone bridge.
solution_html:
The Wheatstone bridge is an arrangement of four resistances. In this bridge, four resistances are connected on four arms of a quadrilateral. Across one diagonal, a battery and key are connected. Across the second diagonal, a galvanometer is connected as shown in the figure. Consider P, Q, R, and S are four resistances connected on the sides AB, BC, AD, and DC of the quadrilateral respectively.

Galvanometer G is connected between points B and D and battery B is connected between A and C' Now in the balance condition, when the deflection in the galvanometer is zero in the closed mesh ABDA, then by applying Kirchhoff's law,
I1P - I2P = 0 or I1P = I2R ..... (i)
In closed mesh CBDC,
I1Q = I2S ..... (ii)
Dividing (i) by (ii), {tex}\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{S}{/tex}
This is the balanced condition for the Wheatstone bridge.

subject: Physics
question_html: Define current density. Is it a scalar or a vector? An electric field {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}{/tex} is maintained in a metallic conductor. If n be the number of electrons (mass m, charge -e) per unit volume in the conductor and {tex}\tau{/tex} its relaxation time, show that the current density {tex}\vec{j}=\alpha \vec{E}{/tex}, where {tex}\alpha=\left(\frac{{ne}^2}{{~m}}\right) \tau{/tex}.
question_text: Define current density . Is it a scalar or a vector? An electric field {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}{/tex} is maintained in a metallic conductor. If n be the number of electrons (mass m, charge -e) per unit volume in the conductor and {tex}\tau{/tex} its relaxation time, show that the current density {tex}\vec{j}=\alpha \vec{E}{/tex} , where {tex}\alpha=\left(\frac{{ne}^2}{{~m}}\right) \tau{/tex} .
explanation: Current density is the amount of charge flowing per second per unit area normal to the flow. Alternatively: {tex}j=\frac{I}{\mathrm{~A}}{/tex} It is a vector quantity. The amount of charge crossing the area A in time {tex}\Delta t{/tex} is I {tex}\Delta t{/tex} , where I is the magnitude of the current. Hence, {tex}\mathrm{I} \Delta \mathrm{t}=\operatorname{ne~A}\left|\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{d}}\right| \Delta \mathrm{t}{/tex} {tex} {I} \Delta {t} =\frac{e^2 A}{m} \tau {n} \Delta {t}|{E}| {/tex} {tex}{I} =|{j}| {A} {/tex} {tex}|{j}| =\frac{n e^2}{m} \tau|{E}| {/tex} {tex}\vec{J} =\alpha \vec{E}{/tex}
solution: Current density is the amount of charge flowing per second per unit area normal to the flow. Alternatively: {tex}j=\frac{I}{\mathrm{~A}}{/tex} It is a vector quantity. The amount of charge crossing the area A in time {tex}\Delta t{/tex} is I {tex}\Delta t{/tex} , where I is the magnitude of the current. Hence, {tex}\mathrm{I} \Delta \mathrm{t}=\operatorname{ne~A}\left|\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{d}}\right| \Delta \mathrm{t}{/tex} {tex} {I} \Delta {t} =\frac{e^2 A}{m} \tau {n} \Delta {t}|{E}| {/tex} {tex}{I} =|{j}| {A} {/tex} {tex}|{j}| =\frac{n e^2}{m} \tau|{E}| {/tex} {tex}\vec{J} =\alpha \vec{E}{/tex}
solution_html:
Current density is the amount of charge flowing per second per unit area normal to the flow.
Alternatively:
{tex}j=\frac{I}{\mathrm{~A}}{/tex}
It is a vector quantity.

The amount of charge crossing the area A in time {tex}\Delta t{/tex} is I {tex}\Delta t{/tex}, where I is the magnitude of the current. Hence,
{tex}\mathrm{I} \Delta \mathrm{t}=\operatorname{ne~A}\left|\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{d}}\right| \Delta \mathrm{t}{/tex}
{tex} {I} \Delta {t} =\frac{e^2 A}{m} \tau {n} \Delta {t}|{E}| {/tex}
{tex}{I} =|{j}| {A} {/tex}
{tex}|{j}| =\frac{n e^2}{m} \tau|{E}| {/tex}
{tex}\vec{J} =\alpha \vec{E}{/tex}

question_html: Find the temperature at which the resistance of a wire made of silver will be twice its resistance at 20oC. Take 20oC as the reference temperature and temperature coefficient of resistance of silver at 20oC = 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 K-1.
question_text: Find the temperature at which the resistance of a wire made of silver will be twice its resistance at 20 o C. Take 20 o C as the reference temperature and temperature coefficient of resistance of silver at 20 o C = 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 K -1 .
explanation: R = R 0 [1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} (T - T 0 )] R = 2R 0 [Given] 2R 0 = R 0 [1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} (T - T 0 )] On solving T = T 0 + 250 T = 270 o C or 543 K
solution: R = R 0 [1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} (T - T 0 )] R = 2R 0 [Given] 2R 0 = R 0 [1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} (T - T 0 )] On solving T = T 0 + 250 T = 270 o C or 543 K
solution_html:
R = R0 [1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} (T - T0)]
R = 2R0 [Given]
2R0 = R0 [1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex}(T - T0)]
On solving
T = T0 + 250
T = 270oC or 543 K

subject: Physics
question_html:
Define resistivity of a conductor. How does the resistivity of a conductor depend upon the following:

  1. Number density of free electrons in the conductor (n)
  2. Their relaxation time {tex}(\tau){/tex}
question_text: Define resistivity of a conductor. How does the resistivity of a conductor depend upon the following: Number density of free electrons in the conductor (n) Their relaxation time {tex}(\tau){/tex}
explanation: Resistivity {tex}(\rho){/tex} of a conductor is a material property that quantifies how strongly the material opposes the flow of electric current. It is defined as the resistance per unit length and cross-sectional area. The resistivity depends on two key factors: Number Density of Free Electrons ( {tex}\boldsymbol{n}{/tex} ): Higher electron density generally leads to lower resistivity, as more charge carriers facilitate current flow. Relaxation Time {tex}(\tau){/tex} : This is the average time between collisions of charge carriers. Longer relaxation time results in lower resistivity, as electrons can travel further before scattering, enhancing conductivity. The relationship is given by {tex}\rho=\frac{1}{n q \mu}{/tex} , where {tex}q{/tex} is the charge of an electron and {tex}\mu{/tex} is the mobility.
solution: Resistivity {tex}(\rho){/tex} of a conductor is a material property that quantifies how strongly the material opposes the flow of electric current. It is defined as the resistance per unit length and cross-sectional area. The resistivity depends on two key factors: Number Density of Free Electrons ( {tex}\boldsymbol{n}{/tex} ): Higher electron density generally leads to lower resistivity, as more charge carriers facilitate current flow. Relaxation Time {tex}(\tau){/tex} : This is the average time between collisions of charge carriers. Longer relaxation time results in lower resistivity, as electrons can travel further before scattering, enhancing conductivity. The relationship is given by {tex}\rho=\frac{1}{n q \mu}{/tex} , where {tex}q{/tex} is the charge of an electron and {tex}\mu{/tex} is the mobility.
solution_html:
Resistivity {tex}(\rho){/tex} of a conductor is a material property that quantifies how strongly the material opposes the flow of electric current. It is defined as the resistance per unit length and cross-sectional area.
The resistivity depends on two key factors:

  1. Number Density of Free Electrons ({tex}\boldsymbol{n}{/tex}): Higher electron density generally leads to lower resistivity, as more charge carriers facilitate current flow.
  2. Relaxation Time {tex}(\tau){/tex} : This is the average time between collisions of charge carriers. Longer relaxation time results in lower resistivity, as electrons can travel further before scattering, enhancing conductivity.

The relationship is given by {tex}\rho=\frac{1}{n q \mu}{/tex}, where {tex}q{/tex} is the charge of an electron and {tex}\mu{/tex} is the mobility.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. The electron drift speed is only a few mm/s for currents in the range of a few amperes for a given conductor. How then is current established almost the instant a circuit is closed? Explain.
  2. 'V=IR is a statement of Ohm's Law' is not true. Explain.
question_text: The electron drift speed is only a few mm/s for currents in the range of a few amperes for a given conductor. How then is current established almost the instant a circuit is closed? Explain. 'V=IR is a statement of Ohm's Law' is not true. Explain.
explanation: Electric field is established throughout the circuit, almost instantly. It causes a local electron drift at every point, thus establishment of current does not have to wait for electrons from one end of the conductor to travel to other end. Ohm‟s law asserts that the plot of I versus V is linear i.e. R is independent of V, while equation V = IR defines resistance and it may be applied to all conducting devices whether they obey Ohm‟s law or not.
solution: Electric field is established throughout the circuit, almost instantly. It causes a local electron drift at every point, thus establishment of current does not have to wait for electrons from one end of the conductor to travel to other end. Ohm‟s law asserts that the plot of I versus V is linear i.e. R is independent of V, while equation V = IR defines resistance and it may be applied to all conducting devices whether they obey Ohm‟s law or not.
solution_html:
1. Electric field is established throughout the circuit, almost instantly. It causes a local electron drift at every point, thus establishment of current does not have to wait for electrons from one end of the conductor to travel to other end.
  2. Ohm‟s law asserts that the plot of I versus V is linear i.e. R is independent of V, while equation V = IR defines resistance and it may be applied to all conducting devices whether they obey Ohm‟s law or not.

question_text: Two batteries of emfs 6 V and 3 V and internal resistances {tex}0.8 \Omega{/tex} and {tex}0 \cdot 2 \Omega{/tex} respectively are connected in series to an external resistance {tex}R{/tex} , as shown in figure. Find the value of R so that the potential difference across the 6 V battery be zero.
explanation: Finding the value of R {tex}i=\frac{9}{R+1}{/tex} As potential difference across 6V is zero; {tex}6-\mathrm{ir}=0 \Rightarrow 6-\left(\frac{9}{R+1}\right)(0.8)=0{/tex} On solving; {tex}\mathrm{R}=0.2 \Omega{/tex}
solution: Finding the value of R {tex}i=\frac{9}{R+1}{/tex} As potential difference across 6V is zero; {tex}6-\mathrm{ir}=0 \Rightarrow 6-\left(\frac{9}{R+1}\right)(0.8)=0{/tex} On solving; {tex}\mathrm{R}=0.2 \Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
Finding the value of R
{tex}i=\frac{9}{R+1}{/tex}
As potential difference across 6V is zero;
{tex}6-\mathrm{ir}=0 \Rightarrow 6-\left(\frac{9}{R+1}\right)(0.8)=0{/tex}
On solving;
{tex}\mathrm{R}=0.2 \Omega{/tex}

question_html: A uniform wire of length L and area of cross-section A has resistance R. The wire is uniformly stretched so that its length increases by 25%. Calculate the percentage increase in the resistance of the wire.
question_text: A uniform wire of length L and area of cross-section A has resistance R. The wire is uniformly stretched so that its length increases by 25%. Calculate the percentage increase in the resistance of the wire.
explanation: As the wire is uniformly stretched; {tex} \Rightarrow A l=A^{\prime} l^{\prime} \Rightarrow A^{\prime}=\frac{l A}{1.25 l} {/tex} {tex} A^{\prime}=\frac{4}{5} A {/tex} {tex} R^{\prime}=\frac{\rho l^{\prime}}{A^{\prime}}=\frac{\rho(1.25 l)}{\frac{4}{5} A} {/tex} {tex} R^{\prime}=\frac{25}{16} R {/tex} % increase {tex} =\left(\frac{R^{\prime}-R}{R}\right) \times 100 {/tex} {tex} =\left(\frac{25}{16}-1\right) \times 100=56.25 \% {/tex}
solution: As the wire is uniformly stretched; {tex} \Rightarrow A l=A^{\prime} l^{\prime} \Rightarrow A^{\prime}=\frac{l A}{1.25 l} {/tex} {tex} A^{\prime}=\frac{4}{5} A {/tex} {tex} R^{\prime}=\frac{\rho l^{\prime}}{A^{\prime}}=\frac{\rho(1.25 l)}{\frac{4}{5} A} {/tex} {tex} R^{\prime}=\frac{25}{16} R {/tex} % increase {tex} =\left(\frac{R^{\prime}-R}{R}\right) \times 100 {/tex} {tex} =\left(\frac{25}{16}-1\right) \times 100=56.25 \% {/tex}
solution_html:
As the wire is uniformly stretched;
{tex} \Rightarrow A l=A^{\prime} l^{\prime} \Rightarrow A^{\prime}=\frac{l A}{1.25 l} {/tex}
{tex} A^{\prime}=\frac{4}{5} A {/tex}
{tex} R^{\prime}=\frac{\rho l^{\prime}}{A^{\prime}}=\frac{\rho(1.25 l)}{\frac{4}{5} A} {/tex}
{tex} R^{\prime}=\frac{25}{16} R {/tex}
% increase {tex} =\left(\frac{R^{\prime}-R}{R}\right) \times 100 {/tex}
{tex} =\left(\frac{25}{16}-1\right) \times 100=56.25 \% {/tex}

question_html: Electrons drift with speed {tex}v_d{/tex} in a conductor with potential difference V across its ends. If V is reduced to {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}}{2}\right){/tex}, their drift speed will become:
question_text: Electrons drift with speed {tex}v_d{/tex} in a conductor with potential difference V across its ends. If V is reduced to {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}}{2}\right){/tex} , their drift speed will become:

options.explanation: If the drift speed of electrons is vdv_dvd with a potential difference VVV, then reducing VVV to half results in the drift speed becoming {tex}\frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{d}}}{2}{/tex} . Thus, the drift speed decreases proportionally to the reduction in potential difference.

subject: Physics
question_html: A student is asked to connect four cells, each of emf E and internal resistance r, in series. But she/he connects one cell wrongly in series with the other cells. The equivalent emf and the equivalent internal resistance of the combination will be
question_text: A student is asked to connect four cells, each of emf E and internal resistance r, in series. But she/he connects one cell wrongly in series with the other cells. The equivalent emf and the equivalent internal resistance of the combination will be

options.explanation: When four cells, each with an emf {tex}E{/tex} and internal resistance {tex}r{/tex} , are connected in series but one cell is connected in the reverse direction, the situation can be analyzed as follows: 1. Equivalent EMF: Three cells contribute positively: {tex}E+E+E=3 E{/tex} One cell contributes negatively: {tex}-E{/tex} Thus, the total equivalent emf will be: {tex} E_{\text {total }}=3 E-E=2 E{/tex} 2. Equivalent Internal Resistance: Since all cells are in series, the total internal resistance is simply the sum of the internal resistances: {tex} r_{\text {total }}=r+r+r+r=4 r{/tex} Therefore, the equivalent emf is {tex}2 E{/tex} and the equivalent internal resistance is {tex}4 r{/tex} .

explanation: When four cells, each with an emf {tex}E{/tex} and internal resistance {tex}r{/tex} , are connected in series but one cell is connected in the reverse direction, the situation can be analyzed as follows: 1. Equivalent EMF: Three cells contribute positively: {tex}E+E+E=3 E{/tex} One cell contributes negatively: {tex}-E{/tex} Thus, the total equivalent emf will be: {tex} E_{\text {total }}=3 E-E=2 E{/tex} 2. Equivalent Internal Resistance: Since all cells are in series, the total internal resistance is simply the sum of the internal resistances: {tex} r_{\text {total }}=r+r+r+r=4 r{/tex} Therefore, the equivalent emf is {tex}2 E{/tex} and the equivalent internal resistance is {tex}4 r{/tex} .

subject: Physics
question_html: What is a Wheatstone bridge? Obtain the necessary conditions under which the Wheatstone bridge is balanced.
question_text: What is a Wheatstone bridge? Obtain the necessary conditions under which the Wheatstone bridge is balanced.
explanation: For loop ADBA: {tex} -{I}_1 {R}_1+{I}_2 {R}_2+{I}_{{g}} {G}=0{/tex} ...(1) For loop CBDC: {tex} {I}_4 {R}_4-{I}_3 {R}_3-{I}_{{g}} {G}=0{/tex} ...(2) For balanced wheatstone bridge, {tex}\operatorname{Ig}=0{/tex} And by applying Kirchoff's junction rule to junction D and B, {tex} {I}_1={I}_3 \& {I}_2={I}_4{/tex} From eqn (1) and (2) {tex} \frac{I_1}{I_2}= \frac{R_2}{R_1} \text { and } \frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_4}{R_3} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{R_2}{R_1}=\frac{R_4}{R_3} {/tex}
solution: For loop ADBA: {tex} -{I}_1 {R}_1+{I}_2 {R}_2+{I}_{{g}} {G}=0{/tex} ...(1) For loop CBDC: {tex} {I}_4 {R}_4-{I}_3 {R}_3-{I}_{{g}} {G}=0{/tex} ...(2) For balanced wheatstone bridge, {tex}\operatorname{Ig}=0{/tex} And by applying Kirchoff's junction rule to junction D and B, {tex} {I}_1={I}_3 \& {I}_2={I}_4{/tex} From eqn (1) and (2) {tex} \frac{I_1}{I_2}= \frac{R_2}{R_1} \text { and } \frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_4}{R_3} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{R_2}{R_1}=\frac{R_4}{R_3} {/tex}
solution_html:
For loop ADBA:
{tex} -{I}_1 {R}_1+{I}_2 {R}_2+{I}_{{g}} {G}=0{/tex} ...(1)
For loop CBDC:
{tex} {I}_4 {R}_4-{I}_3 {R}_3-{I}_{{g}} {G}=0{/tex} ...(2)
For balanced wheatstone bridge, {tex}\operatorname{Ig}=0{/tex}
And by applying Kirchoff's junction rule to junction D and B,
{tex} {I}_1={I}_3 \& {I}_2={I}_4{/tex}
From eqn (1) and (2)
{tex} \frac{I_1}{I_2}= \frac{R_2}{R_1} \text { and } \frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_4}{R_3} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{R_2}{R_1}=\frac{R_4}{R_3} {/tex}

question_html: In a uniform straight wire, conduction electrons move along +x direction. Let {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{j}{/tex} be the electric field and current density in the wire, respectively. Then:

{tex} \vec{E}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{j}{/tex} both are along {tex}-x{/tex} direction.

subject: Physics
question_html: The figure shows a circuit with three ideal batteries. Find the magnitude and direction of currents in the branches AG, BF and CD.
question_text: The figure shows a circuit with three ideal batteries. Find the magnitude and direction of currents in the branches AG, BF and CD.
explanation: By Kirchoff's Laws (at point B) {tex} {I}_1+{I}_2={I}_3{/tex} ...(1) In the closed loop AGFBA {tex} 3+2 {I}_3-6+4 {I}_2+2 {I}_3=0 {/tex} {tex} {I}_2+{I}_3=\frac{3}{4} {/tex} ...(2) From (i) {tex} 2 {I}_1+{I}_2=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} ...(3) In closed loop BFDCB {tex} -4 {I}_2+6+2 {I}_1-6+2 {I}_1=0 {/tex} {tex} {I}_2-{I}_1=0 {/tex} {tex} {I}_2={I}_1 {/tex} ...(4) Putting in (3) {tex} I_1=\frac{1}{4} A{/tex} From (4) {tex} I_2=\frac{1}{4} {~A}{/tex} From (2) {tex} I_3=\frac{1}{2} {~A}{/tex}
solution: By Kirchoff's Laws (at point B) {tex} {I}_1+{I}_2={I}_3{/tex} ...(1) In the closed loop AGFBA {tex} 3+2 {I}_3-6+4 {I}_2+2 {I}_3=0 {/tex} {tex} {I}_2+{I}_3=\frac{3}{4} {/tex} ...(2) From (i) {tex} 2 {I}_1+{I}_2=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} ...(3) In closed loop BFDCB {tex} -4 {I}_2+6+2 {I}_1-6+2 {I}_1=0 {/tex} {tex} {I}_2-{I}_1=0 {/tex} {tex} {I}_2={I}_1 {/tex} ...(4) Putting in (3) {tex} I_1=\frac{1}{4} A{/tex} From (4) {tex} I_2=\frac{1}{4} {~A}{/tex} From (2) {tex} I_3=\frac{1}{2} {~A}{/tex}
solution_html:
By Kirchoff's Laws (at point B)
{tex} {I}_1+{I}_2={I}_3{/tex} ...(1)
In the closed loop AGFBA
{tex} 3+2 {I}_3-6+4 {I}_2+2 {I}_3=0 {/tex}
{tex} {I}_2+{I}_3=\frac{3}{4} {/tex} ...(2)
From (i)
{tex} 2 {I}_1+{I}_2=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} ...(3)
In closed loop BFDCB
{tex} -4 {I}_2+6+2 {I}_1-6+2 {I}_1=0 {/tex}
{tex} {I}_2-{I}_1=0 {/tex}
{tex} {I}_2={I}_1 {/tex} ...(4)
Putting in (3)
{tex} I_1=\frac{1}{4} A{/tex}
From (4)
{tex} I_2=\frac{1}{4} {~A}{/tex}
From (2) {tex} I_3=\frac{1}{2} {~A}{/tex}

question_text: An electric field {tex}E{/tex} is maintained in a wire of length ' {tex}l{/tex} ' and area of cross-section ' {tex}a{/tex} '. Derive the relation between the current density ' {tex}\sigma{/tex} ' in the wire and the electric field E.

solution: {tex} V =I R {/tex} {tex} E l =\frac{I \rho l}{A} {/tex} {tex}\left(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{E} l, R=\frac{\rho l}{A}\right){/tex} {tex} E =\frac{I}{A} \rho {/tex} {tex} E =\sigma \rho {/tex}

{tex} V =I R {/tex}
