subject: Physics
question_html:
A battery of unknown emf E and internal resistance r is connected in a circuit as shown in the figure. When the key {tex}({K}){/tex} is open, the voltmeter reads {tex}10 \cdot 0 {~V}{/tex} and ammeter reads 0 A. In the closed circuit, the voltmeter reads 6.0 V and ammeter reads 2.0 A. Calculate:

  1. emf of the battery,
  2. internal resistance of the battery (r), and
  3. external resistance (R).
question_text: A battery of unknown emf E and internal resistance r is connected in a circuit as shown in the figure. When the key {tex}({K}){/tex} is open, the voltmeter reads {tex}10 \cdot 0 {~V}{/tex} and ammeter reads 0 A. In the closed circuit, the voltmeter reads 6.0 V and ammeter reads 2.0 A. Calculate: emf of the battery, internal resistance of the battery (r), and external resistance (R).
explanation: {tex}\mathrm{V}=E=10 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} (When key K is open and I = 0 A) {tex} {V} =E-{Ir} {/tex} (When key K is closed and I = 2 A) {tex} 6 =10-2 {r} {/tex} {tex} {r} =2 \Omega {/tex} {tex} {E} ={I}({r}+{R}) {/tex} {tex} 10 =2(2+{R}) {/tex} {tex} {R} =3 \Omega {/tex}
solution: {tex}\mathrm{V}=E=10 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} (When key K is open and I = 0 A) {tex} {V} =E-{Ir} {/tex} (When key K is closed and I = 2 A) {tex} 6 =10-2 {r} {/tex} {tex} {r} =2 \Omega {/tex} {tex} {E} ={I}({r}+{R}) {/tex} {tex} 10 =2(2+{R}) {/tex} {tex} {R} =3 \Omega {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\mathrm{V}=E=10 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} (When key K is open and I = 0 A)
  2. {tex} {V} =E-{Ir} {/tex} (When key K is closed and I = 2 A)
    {tex} 6 =10-2 {r} {/tex}
    {tex} {r} =2 \Omega {/tex}
  3. {tex} {E} ={I}({r}+{R}) {/tex}
    {tex} 10 =2(2+{R}) {/tex}
    {tex} {R} =3 \Omega {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Determine the current in branches AB, AC and BC of the network shown in figure.
question_text: Determine the current in branches AB, AC and BC of the network shown in figure.
explanation: For closed loop ADCA, {tex} 10-4\left({I}_1-I_2\right)+2\left({I}_2+{I}_3-{I}_1\right)-{I}_1=0 {/tex} {tex} 7 I_1-6 I_2-2 I_3=10 {/tex} ...(i) For closed loop ABCA, {tex} 10-4 I_2-2\left(I_2+{I}_3\right)-{I}_1=0 {/tex} {tex} I_1+6 I_2+2 I_3=10{/tex} ...(ii) For closed loop BCDED, {tex} 5-2\left({I}_2+{I}_3\right)-2\left({I}_2+{I}_3-{I}_1\right)=0 {/tex} {tex} 2 {I}_1-4 {I}_2-4 I_3=-5{/tex} ...(iii) Current in branch {tex}{AB}=I_2=\frac{5}{8} {~A}{/tex} Current in branch {tex}{AC}=I_1=2.5 {~A}{/tex} Current in branch {tex}{BC}=I_2+I_3=2.5 {~A}{/tex}
solution: For closed loop ADCA, {tex} 10-4\left({I}_1-I_2\right)+2\left({I}_2+{I}_3-{I}_1\right)-{I}_1=0 {/tex} {tex} 7 I_1-6 I_2-2 I_3=10 {/tex} ...(i) For closed loop ABCA, {tex} 10-4 I_2-2\left(I_2+{I}_3\right)-{I}_1=0 {/tex} {tex} I_1+6 I_2+2 I_3=10{/tex} ...(ii) For closed loop BCDED, {tex} 5-2\left({I}_2+{I}_3\right)-2\left({I}_2+{I}_3-{I}_1\right)=0 {/tex} {tex} 2 {I}_1-4 {I}_2-4 I_3=-5{/tex} ...(iii) Current in branch {tex}{AB}=I_2=\frac{5}{8} {~A}{/tex} Current in branch {tex}{AC}=I_1=2.5 {~A}{/tex} Current in branch {tex}{BC}=I_2+I_3=2.5 {~A}{/tex}
solution_html:
For closed loop ADCA,
{tex} 10-4\left({I}_1-I_2\right)+2\left({I}_2+{I}_3-{I}_1\right)-{I}_1=0 {/tex}
{tex} 7 I_1-6 I_2-2 I_3=10 {/tex} ...(i)
For closed loop ABCA,
{tex} 10-4 I_2-2\left(I_2+{I}_3\right)-{I}_1=0 {/tex}
{tex} I_1+6 I_2+2 I_3=10{/tex} ...(ii)
For closed loop BCDED,
{tex} 5-2\left({I}_2+{I}_3\right)-2\left({I}_2+{I}_3-{I}_1\right)=0 {/tex}
{tex} 2 {I}_1-4 {I}_2-4 I_3=-5{/tex} ...(iii)
Current in branch {tex}{AB}=I_2=\frac{5}{8} {~A}{/tex}
Current in branch {tex}{AC}=I_1=2.5 {~A}{/tex}
Current in branch {tex}{BC}=I_2+I_3=2.5 {~A}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Draw the circuit diagram of a Wheatstone bridge. Obtain the condition when no current flows through the galvanometer in it.
question_text: Draw the circuit diagram of a Wheatstone bridge. Obtain the condition when no current flows through the galvanometer in it.
explanation: By applying Kirchoff‟s loop rule to closed loops ADBA and CBDC {tex} -{I}_1 {R}_1+0+{I}_2 {R}_2 =0 {/tex} ...(i) [I g = 0] {tex} {I}_2 {R}_4+0-0-{I}_1 {R}_3 =0 {/tex} ...(ii) From eq (i) {tex} \frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}{/tex} From eq (ii) {tex} \frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_4}{R_3}{/tex} Hence, {tex} \frac{R_2}{R_1}=\frac{R_4}{R_3}{/tex}
solution: By applying Kirchoff‟s loop rule to closed loops ADBA and CBDC {tex} -{I}_1 {R}_1+0+{I}_2 {R}_2 =0 {/tex} ...(i) [I g = 0] {tex} {I}_2 {R}_4+0-0-{I}_1 {R}_3 =0 {/tex} ...(ii) From eq (i) {tex} \frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}{/tex} From eq (ii) {tex} \frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_4}{R_3}{/tex} Hence, {tex} \frac{R_2}{R_1}=\frac{R_4}{R_3}{/tex}
solution_html:
By applying Kirchoff‟s loop rule to closed loops ADBA and CBDC
{tex} -{I}_1 {R}_1+0+{I}_2 {R}_2 =0 {/tex} ...(i) [Ig = 0]
{tex} {I}_2 {R}_4+0-0-{I}_1 {R}_3 =0 {/tex} ...(ii)
From eq (i)
{tex} \frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}{/tex}
From eq (ii)
{tex} \frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_4}{R_3}{/tex}
Hence,
{tex} \frac{R_2}{R_1}=\frac{R_4}{R_3}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: What is meant by relaxation time of free electrons in a conductor? Show that the resistance of a conductor can be expressed by {tex}\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{m} l}{\mathrm{ne}^2 \tau \mathrm{~A}},{/tex} where symbols have their usual meanings.
question_text: What is meant by relaxation time of free electrons in a conductor? Show that the resistance of a conductor can be expressed by {tex}\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{m} l}{\mathrm{ne}^2 \tau \mathrm{~A}},{/tex} where symbols have their usual meanings.
explanation: Average time between two successive collisions of electron in presence of electric field Drift velocity of an electron {tex}v_d=\frac{e E}{m} \tau{/tex} ...(i) Current flowing through a conductor of length l and area of cross section A {tex}I=n e A v_d{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}I=\frac{n e^2 A E \tau}{m}=\frac{n e^2 A \tau V}{m l}{/tex} {tex}R=\frac{V}{I}=\frac{m l}{n e^2 \tau A}{/tex}
solution: Average time between two successive collisions of electron in presence of electric field Drift velocity of an electron {tex}v_d=\frac{e E}{m} \tau{/tex} ...(i) Current flowing through a conductor of length l and area of cross section A {tex}I=n e A v_d{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}I=\frac{n e^2 A E \tau}{m}=\frac{n e^2 A \tau V}{m l}{/tex} {tex}R=\frac{V}{I}=\frac{m l}{n e^2 \tau A}{/tex}
solution_html:
Average time between two successive collisions of electron in presence of electric field Drift velocity of an electron
{tex}v_d=\frac{e E}{m} \tau{/tex} ...(i)
Current flowing through a conductor of length l and area of cross section A
{tex}I=n e A v_d{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}I=\frac{n e^2 A E \tau}{m}=\frac{n e^2 A \tau V}{m l}{/tex}
{tex}R=\frac{V}{I}=\frac{m l}{n e^2 \tau A}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
When the terminals of a cell are connected to a conductor of resistance R, an electric current flows through the circuit. The electrolyte of the cell also offers some resistance in the path of the current, like the conductor. This resistance offered by the electrolyte is called internal resistance of the cell (r). It depends upon the nature of the electrolyte, the area of the electrodes immersed in the electrolyte and the temperature. Due to internal resistance, a part of the energy supplied by the cell is wasted in the form of heat.
When no current is drawn from the cell, the potential difference between the two electrodes in known as emf of the cell ({tex}\varepsilon{/tex}). With a current drawn from the cell, the potential difference between the two electrodes is termed as terminal potential difference (V).

Due to internal resistance, a part of the energy supplied by the cell is wasted in the form of heat. When no current is drawn from the cell, the potential difference between the two electrodes in known as emf of the cell ( {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} ). With a current drawn from the cell, the potential difference between the two electrodes is termed as terminal potential difference (V). Choose the incorrect statement:

options.text: The potential difference (V) between the two terminals of a cell in a closed circuit is always less than its emf {tex}(\varepsilon){/tex} , during discharge of the cell.

options.text: The internal resistance of a cell decreases with the decrease in temperature of the electrolyte.

options.explanation: The internal resistance of a cell decreases with the decrease in temperature of the electrolyte.

options.text: When current is drawn from the cell then {tex}{V}=\varepsilon-{Ir}{/tex}
options.html: When current is drawn from the cell then {tex}{V}=\varepsilon-{Ir}{/tex}

options.text: The graph between potential difference between the two terminals of the cell (V) and the current (I) through it is a straight line with a negative slope.
options.html: The graph between potential difference between the two terminals of the cell (V) and the current (I) through it is a straight line with a negative slope.

correct_option_text: The internal resistance of a cell decreases with the decrease in temperature of the electrolyte.
explanation: The internal resistance of a cell decreases with the decrease in temperature of the electrolyte.

question_html: Find the currents flowing through the branches AB and BC in the network shown.
question_text: Find the currents flowing through the branches AB and BC in the network shown.
explanation: Circuit diagram with distribution of current Using Kirchhoff's voltage rule In closed loop ABMNA, {tex}-5 {I}_{1}+10\left({I}-{I}_{1}\right)-5=0{/tex} ...(1) In closed loop ACDNA {tex}-5 {I}-20 {I}+10-5-5 {I}_{1}=0{/tex} ...(2) Solving eq (1) and (2) {tex}I_{1}=-\frac{3}{17} A \text { and } I=\frac{4}{17}{/tex} Magnitude of current in arm {tex}{AB}=\frac{3}{17} {~A}{/tex} Magnitude of current in arm {tex}{BC}=\frac{4}{17} {~A}{/tex}
solution: Circuit diagram with distribution of current Using Kirchhoff's voltage rule In closed loop ABMNA, {tex}-5 {I}_{1}+10\left({I}-{I}_{1}\right)-5=0{/tex} ...(1) In closed loop ACDNA {tex}-5 {I}-20 {I}+10-5-5 {I}_{1}=0{/tex} ...(2) Solving eq (1) and (2) {tex}I_{1}=-\frac{3}{17} A \text { and } I=\frac{4}{17}{/tex} Magnitude of current in arm {tex}{AB}=\frac{3}{17} {~A}{/tex} Magnitude of current in arm {tex}{BC}=\frac{4}{17} {~A}{/tex}
solution_html:
Circuit diagram with distribution of current

Using Kirchhoff's voltage rule
In closed loop ABMNA,
{tex}-5 {I}_{1}+10\left({I}-{I}_{1}\right)-5=0{/tex} ...(1)
In closed loop ACDNA
{tex}-5 {I}-20 {I}+10-5-5 {I}_{1}=0{/tex} ...(2)
Solving eq (1) and (2)
{tex}I_{1}=-\frac{3}{17} A \text { and } I=\frac{4}{17}{/tex}
Magnitude of current in arm {tex}{AB}=\frac{3}{17} {~A}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Two wires A and B of different metals have their lengths in ratio {tex}1: 2{/tex} and their radii in ratio 2:1 respectively. I-V graphs for them is shown in the figure. Find the ratio of their

  1. Resistances {tex}\left(R_{A} / R_{B}\right){/tex} and
  2. Resistivities {tex}\left(\sigma_{{A}} / \sigma_{{B}}\right){/tex}
question_text: Two wires A and B of different metals have their lengths in ratio {tex}1: 2{/tex} and their radii in ratio 2:1 respectively. I-V graphs for them is shown in the figure. Find the ratio of their Resistances {tex}\left(R_{A} / R_{B}\right){/tex} and Resistivities {tex}\left(\sigma_{{A}} / \sigma_{{B}}\right){/tex}
explanation: Slope of I-V graph {tex}=\left(\frac{\Delta {I}}{\Delta {V}}\right)=\frac{{I}}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{R_{A}}{R_{B}} =\frac{\text { Slope of B }}{\text { Slope of A }}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\tan 45^{\circ}}{\tan 30^{\circ}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{R_{A}}{R_{B}} =\sqrt{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sigma_{A}}{\sigma_{B}} =\frac{R_{A} \frac{A_{A}}{1_{A}}}{R_{B} \frac{A_{B}}{1_{B}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{R_{A}}{R_{B}} \cdot \frac{A_{A}}{A_{B}} \cdot \frac{1_{B}}{1_{A}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{3} \times \frac{4}{1} \times \frac{2}{1}{/tex} {tex}=8 \sqrt{3}{/tex}
solution: Slope of I-V graph {tex}=\left(\frac{\Delta {I}}{\Delta {V}}\right)=\frac{{I}}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{R_{A}}{R_{B}} =\frac{\text { Slope of B }}{\text { Slope of A }}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\tan 45^{\circ}}{\tan 30^{\circ}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{R_{A}}{R_{B}} =\sqrt{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sigma_{A}}{\sigma_{B}} =\frac{R_{A} \frac{A_{A}}{1_{A}}}{R_{B} \frac{A_{B}}{1_{B}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{R_{A}}{R_{B}} \cdot \frac{A_{A}}{A_{B}} \cdot \frac{1_{B}}{1_{A}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{3} \times \frac{4}{1} \times \frac{2}{1}{/tex} {tex}=8 \sqrt{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Slope of I-V graph {tex}=\left(\frac{\Delta {I}}{\Delta {V}}\right)=\frac{{I}}{{R}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{R_{A}}{R_{B}} =\frac{\text { Slope of B }}{\text { Slope of A }}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\tan 45^{\circ}}{\tan 30^{\circ}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{R_{A}}{R_{B}} =\sqrt{3}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{\sigma_{A}}{\sigma_{B}} =\frac{R_{A} \frac{A_{A}}{1_{A}}}{R_{B} \frac{A_{B}}{1_{B}}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{R_{A}}{R_{B}} \cdot \frac{A_{A}}{A_{B}} \cdot \frac{1_{B}}{1_{A}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\sqrt{3} \times \frac{4}{1} \times \frac{2}{1}{/tex}
    {tex}=8 \sqrt{3}{/tex}

question_html: The radius of a conducting wire AB uniformly decreases from its one end A to another end B. It is connected across a battery. How will (i) electric field, (ii) current density, and (iii) mobility of electrons change from end A to end B? Justify your answer in each case.
question_text: The radius of a conducting wire AB uniformly decreases from its one end A to another end B. It is connected across a battery. How will (i) electric field, (ii) current density, and (iii) mobility of electrons change from end A to end B? Justify your answer in each case.
explanation: With the decrease in area of cross-section. {tex}{E}=\frac{{I}}{{A}} \rho{/tex} , electric field increases {tex}{j}=\frac{{I}}{{A}}{/tex} , current density increases {tex}\mu_{{e}}=\frac{{e} \tau}{{m}}{/tex} , mobility remains same
solution: With the decrease in area of cross-section. {tex}{E}=\frac{{I}}{{A}} \rho{/tex} , electric field increases {tex}{j}=\frac{{I}}{{A}}{/tex} , current density increases {tex}\mu_{{e}}=\frac{{e} \tau}{{m}}{/tex} , mobility remains same
solution_html:
With the decrease in area of cross-section.

  1. {tex}{E}=\frac{{I}}{{A}} \rho{/tex}, electric field increases
  2. {tex}{j}=\frac{{I}}{{A}}{/tex}, current density increases
  3. {tex}\mu_{{e}}=\frac{{e} \tau}{{m}}{/tex}, mobility remains same

question_html: Find the temperature at which the resistance of a conductor increases by {tex}25 \%{/tex} of its value at {tex}27^{\circ} {C}{/tex}. The temperature coefficient of resistance of the conductor is {tex}2.0 \times 10^{-4}{ }^{\circ} {C}^{-1}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the temperature at which the resistance of a conductor increases by {tex}25 \%{/tex} of its value at {tex}27^{\circ} {C}{/tex} . The temperature coefficient of resistance of the conductor is {tex}2.0 \times 10^{-4}{ }^{\circ} {C}^{-1}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}{R}_{2}={R}_{1}+25 \% \text { of } {R}_{1}=1.25 {R}_{1}{/tex} Temperature coefficient of resistance {tex}\alpha=\frac{{R}_{2}-{R}_{1}}{{R}_{1} \cdot \Delta {~T}}{/tex} {tex}{T}_{2}-27=\frac{1.25 {R}_{1}-{R}_{1}}{{R}_{1} \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} {tex}{T}_{2}=1277^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
solution: {tex}{R}_{2}={R}_{1}+25 \% \text { of } {R}_{1}=1.25 {R}_{1}{/tex} Temperature coefficient of resistance {tex}\alpha=\frac{{R}_{2}-{R}_{1}}{{R}_{1} \cdot \Delta {~T}}{/tex} {tex}{T}_{2}-27=\frac{1.25 {R}_{1}-{R}_{1}}{{R}_{1} \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} {tex}{T}_{2}=1277^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}{R}_{2}={R}_{1}+25 \% \text { of } {R}_{1}=1.25 {R}_{1}{/tex}
Temperature coefficient of resistance
{tex}\alpha=\frac{{R}_{2}-{R}_{1}}{{R}_{1} \cdot \Delta {~T}}{/tex}
{tex}{T}_{2}-27=\frac{1.25 {R}_{1}-{R}_{1}}{{R}_{1} \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex}
{tex}{T}_{2}=1277^{\circ} {C}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Obtain a relation between the current flowing in a conductor and drift velocity of electrons in it. Hence, obtain Ohm's law.
question_text: Obtain a relation between the current flowing in a conductor and drift velocity of electrons in it. Hence, obtain Ohm's law.
explanation: Consider a conductor of length l and area of cross section A and potential difference V is applied across the ends of wire Volume of the wire = Al No. of e - in this volume = nAl Charge on the conductor = (nAl) e The amount of charge crossing the area A in time {tex}\Delta{/tex} t is I {tex}\Delta{/tex} t; where I is the magnitude of the current. Hence, I {tex}\Delta{/tex} t = +neA|V d | {tex}\Delta{/tex} t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = neAv d Since |v d | = {tex}\frac{e E}{m} \tau{/tex} {tex}I=n e A\left(\frac{e \tau}{m}\right) \frac{V}{l}=\left(\frac{n e^2 \tau}{m}\right) A\left(\frac{V}{l}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{V}{I}=\left(\frac{m}{n e^2 \tau}\right) \frac{I}{A}{/tex} Hence {tex}\frac{V}{I}{/tex} is constant for a given conductor at constant temperature. {tex}\Rightarrow V \alpha I{/tex}
solution: Consider a conductor of length l and area of cross section A and potential difference V is applied across the ends of wire Volume of the wire = Al No. of e - in this volume = nAl Charge on the conductor = (nAl) e The amount of charge crossing the area A in time {tex}\Delta{/tex} t is I {tex}\Delta{/tex} t; where I is the magnitude of the current. Hence, I {tex}\Delta{/tex} t = +neA|V d | {tex}\Delta{/tex} t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = neAv d Since |v d | = {tex}\frac{e E}{m} \tau{/tex} {tex}I=n e A\left(\frac{e \tau}{m}\right) \frac{V}{l}=\left(\frac{n e^2 \tau}{m}\right) A\left(\frac{V}{l}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{V}{I}=\left(\frac{m}{n e^2 \tau}\right) \frac{I}{A}{/tex} Hence {tex}\frac{V}{I}{/tex} is constant for a given conductor at constant temperature. {tex}\Rightarrow V \alpha I{/tex}
solution_html:
Consider a conductor of length l and area of cross section A and potential difference V is applied across the ends of wire

Volume of the wire = Al
No. of e- in this volume = nAl
Charge on the conductor = (nAl)e
The amount of charge crossing the area A in time {tex}\Delta{/tex}t is I{tex}\Delta{/tex}t; where I is the magnitude of the current. Hence,
I{tex}\Delta{/tex}t = +neA|Vd| {tex}\Delta{/tex}t
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = neAvd
Since |vd| = {tex}\frac{e E}{m} \tau{/tex}
{tex}I=n e A\left(\frac{e \tau}{m}\right) \frac{V}{l}=\left(\frac{n e^2 \tau}{m}\right) A\left(\frac{V}{l}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{V}{I}=\left(\frac{m}{n e^2 \tau}\right) \frac{I}{A}{/tex}
Hence {tex}\frac{V}{I}{/tex} is constant for a given conductor at constant temperature.
{tex}\Rightarrow V \alpha I{/tex}

question_html: Write two differences between the emf and terminal potential difference of a cell. What is the most important precaution that one should take while drawing current from a cell?
question_text: Write two differences between the emf and terminal potential difference of a cell. What is the most important precaution that one should take while drawing current from a cell?
explanation: Two differences The potential difference across the electrodes in open circuit is e.m.f. {tex}(\varepsilon){/tex} and in closed circuit is terminal potential difference (V). V depends on r and {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} is independent of r. Precaution Some external resistance should be connected to cell in series. Short circuiting should be avoided.
solution: Two differences The potential difference across the electrodes in open circuit is e.m.f. {tex}(\varepsilon){/tex} and in closed circuit is terminal potential difference (V). V depends on r and {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} is independent of r. Precaution Some external resistance should be connected to cell in series. Short circuiting should be avoided.
solution_html:
Two differences

  1. The potential difference across the electrodes in open circuit is e.m.f. {tex}(\varepsilon){/tex} and in closed circuit is terminal potential difference (V).
  2. V depends on r and {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} is independent of r.

Precaution

  1. Some external resistance should be connected to cell in series.
  2. Short circuiting should be avoided.

question_html: Two identical cells, each of emf E and internal resistance r, are connected with a load resistance R, first in series and then in parallel. Obtain the condition under which the current through R is same in both cases.
question_text: Two identical cells, each of emf E and internal resistance r, are connected with a load resistance R, first in series and then in parallel. Obtain the condition under which the current through R is same in both cases.
explanation: In series, {tex}\varepsilon_{\mathrm{eq}}=\varepsilon+\varepsilon=2 \varepsilon{/tex} {tex}I_s=\frac{2 \varepsilon}{2 r+R}{/tex} In parallel, {tex}\varepsilon_{\mathrm{eq}}=\varepsilon{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{\mathrm{r}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{I}_p=\frac{\varepsilon}{\frac{r}{2}+\mathrm{R}}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} I P = I S {tex}\frac{2 \varepsilon}{2 \mathrm{r}+\mathrm{R}}=\frac{\varepsilon}{\frac{\mathrm{r}}{2}+\mathrm{R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{2 r+R}=\frac{1}{\frac{r}{2}+R}{/tex} {tex}2\frac{r}{2}{/tex} + 2R = 2r + R r = R
solution: In series, {tex}\varepsilon_{\mathrm{eq}}=\varepsilon+\varepsilon=2 \varepsilon{/tex} {tex}I_s=\frac{2 \varepsilon}{2 r+R}{/tex} In parallel, {tex}\varepsilon_{\mathrm{eq}}=\varepsilon{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{\mathrm{r}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{I}_p=\frac{\varepsilon}{\frac{r}{2}+\mathrm{R}}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} I P = I S {tex}\frac{2 \varepsilon}{2 \mathrm{r}+\mathrm{R}}=\frac{\varepsilon}{\frac{\mathrm{r}}{2}+\mathrm{R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{2 r+R}=\frac{1}{\frac{r}{2}+R}{/tex} {tex}2\frac{r}{2}{/tex} + 2R = 2r + R r = R
solution_html:
In series,
{tex}\varepsilon_{\mathrm{eq}}=\varepsilon+\varepsilon=2 \varepsilon{/tex}
{tex}I_s=\frac{2 \varepsilon}{2 r+R}{/tex}
In parallel,
{tex}\varepsilon_{\mathrm{eq}}=\varepsilon{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{\mathrm{r}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{I}_p=\frac{\varepsilon}{\frac{r}{2}+\mathrm{R}}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} IP = IS
{tex}\frac{2 \varepsilon}{2 \mathrm{r}+\mathrm{R}}=\frac{\varepsilon}{\frac{\mathrm{r}}{2}+\mathrm{R}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{2}{2 r+R}=\frac{1}{\frac{r}{2}+R}{/tex}
{tex}2\frac{r}{2}{/tex} + 2R = 2r + R
r = R

question_html: Two cells of emf E1 and E2 and internal resistances r1 and r2 are connected in parallel, with their terminals of the same polarity connected together. Obtain an expression for the equivalent emf of the combination.
question_text: Two cells of emf E 1 and E 2 and internal resistances r 1 and r 2 are connected in parallel, with their terminals of the same polarity connected together. Obtain an expression for the equivalent emf of the combination.
explanation: Consider two cells of emfs E1 and E2 and internal resistances r1 and r2 as shown in the figure V {tex}\equiv{/tex} V(B 1 ) - V(B 2 ) = {tex}\varepsilon_1-I_1 r_1{/tex} V {tex}\equiv{/tex} V(B 1 ) - V(B 2 ) = {tex}\varepsilon_1-I_2 r_2{/tex} I = I 1 + I 2 {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon_1-V}{r_1}+\frac{\varepsilon_2-V}{r_2}{/tex} {tex}I=\left(\frac{\varepsilon_1}{r_1}+\frac{\varepsilon_2}{r_2}\right)-V\left(\frac{1}{r_1}+\frac{1}{r_2}\right){/tex} {tex}V=\frac{\varepsilon_1 r_2+\varepsilon_2 r_1}{r_1+r_2}-I \frac{r_1 \cdot r_2}{r_1+r_2}{/tex} {tex}\because V=\varepsilon_{e q}-I r_{e q}{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon_{e q}=\frac{\varepsilon_1 r_2+\varepsilon_2 r_1}{r_1+r_2}{/tex}
solution: Consider two cells of emfs E1 and E2 and internal resistances r1 and r2 as shown in the figure V {tex}\equiv{/tex} V(B 1 ) - V(B 2 ) = {tex}\varepsilon_1-I_1 r_1{/tex} V {tex}\equiv{/tex} V(B 1 ) - V(B 2 ) = {tex}\varepsilon_1-I_2 r_2{/tex} I = I 1 + I 2 {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon_1-V}{r_1}+\frac{\varepsilon_2-V}{r_2}{/tex} {tex}I=\left(\frac{\varepsilon_1}{r_1}+\frac{\varepsilon_2}{r_2}\right)-V\left(\frac{1}{r_1}+\frac{1}{r_2}\right){/tex} {tex}V=\frac{\varepsilon_1 r_2+\varepsilon_2 r_1}{r_1+r_2}-I \frac{r_1 \cdot r_2}{r_1+r_2}{/tex} {tex}\because V=\varepsilon_{e q}-I r_{e q}{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon_{e q}=\frac{\varepsilon_1 r_2+\varepsilon_2 r_1}{r_1+r_2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Consider two cells of emfs E1 and E2 and internal resistances r1 and r2 as shown in the figure


V {tex}\equiv{/tex} V(B1) - V(B2) = {tex}\varepsilon_1-I_1 r_1{/tex}
V {tex}\equiv{/tex} V(B1) - V(B2) = {tex}\varepsilon_1-I_2 r_2{/tex}
I = I1 + I2
{tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon_1-V}{r_1}+\frac{\varepsilon_2-V}{r_2}{/tex}
{tex}I=\left(\frac{\varepsilon_1}{r_1}+\frac{\varepsilon_2}{r_2}\right)-V\left(\frac{1}{r_1}+\frac{1}{r_2}\right){/tex}
{tex}V=\frac{\varepsilon_1 r_2+\varepsilon_2 r_1}{r_1+r_2}-I \frac{r_1 \cdot r_2}{r_1+r_2}{/tex}
{tex}\because V=\varepsilon_{e q}-I r_{e q}{/tex}
{tex}\varepsilon_{e q}=\frac{\varepsilon_1 r_2+\varepsilon_2 r_1}{r_1+r_2}{/tex}

question_html: When is more power delivered to a light bulb - just after it is turned on and the glow of the filament is increasing or after the glow becomes steady? Why?
question_text: When is more power delivered to a light bulb - just after it is turned on and the glow of the filament is increasing or after the glow becomes steady? Why?
explanation: Power delivered just after it is turned on, will be more because resistance of the bulb is low. After some time temperature of the bulb increases and resistance also increases and therefore power {tex}\left(\frac{V^2}{R}\right){/tex} becomes low.
solution: Power delivered just after it is turned on, will be more because resistance of the bulb is low. After some time temperature of the bulb increases and resistance also increases and therefore power {tex}\left(\frac{V^2}{R}\right){/tex} becomes low.

subject: Physics
question_html: A battery is connected first across the series combination and then across the parallel combination, of three resistances R, 2R and 3R. In which of the three resistances will power dissipated be maximum in the two cases? Justify your answer.
question_text: A battery is connected first across the series combination and then across the parallel combination, of three resistances R, 2R and 3R. In which of the three resistances will power dissipated be maximum in the two cases? Justify your answer.
explanation: For series Power dissipated will be maximum for 3R. Because current is same and power is proportional to resistance. For parallel Power dissipated will be maximum for R. Because voltage is same and power is inversely proportional to resistance.
solution: For series Power dissipated will be maximum for 3R. Because current is same and power is proportional to resistance. For parallel Power dissipated will be maximum for R. Because voltage is same and power is inversely proportional to resistance.
solution_html:
For series
Power dissipated will be maximum for 3R. Because current is same and power is proportional to resistance.
For parallel
Power dissipated will be maximum for R. Because voltage is same and power is inversely proportional to resistance.

question_html:
1. State Kirchhoff's rules. Use them to obtain the condition of balance for a Wheatstone Bridge.
  2. Use Kirchhoff's rule to determine the currents flowing through the branches MN, TO and SP in the circuit shown in the figure.
question_text: State Kirchhoff's rules. Use them to obtain the condition of balance for a Wheatstone Bridge. Use Kirchhoff's rule to determine the currents flowing through the branches MN, TO and SP in the circuit shown in the figure.
explanation: Kirchhoff’s junction rule - at any junction, the sum of the current entering the junction is equal to the sum of currents leaving the junction. Kirchhoff's second rule: The algebraic sum of changes in potential around any closed loop involving resistors and cells in the loop is zero. In balanced fridge Ig = 0, Hence I 1 = I 3 and I 2 = I 4 Using Kirchhoff’s loop rule for closed loops ADBA and CBDC - I 1 R 1 + 0 + I 1 R 1 = 0 (Ig=0) ...(i) In the second loop I 3 = I 1 , I 4 = I 2 I 2 R 4 + 0 - I 1 R 3 = 0 ...(ii) From equation (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_4}{R_3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{R_2}{R_1}=\frac{R_4}{R_3}{/tex} This is the condition for balanced Wheatstone bridge n loop MNOTM 2I + 4 I 1 = 8 ...(i) Loop OPSTO -I + 5 I 1 = -4 ...(ii) On solving Current in MN, I = 4A Current in TO, I 1 = 0A Current in SP, I - I 1 = 4A Hence, the currents flowing through the branches MN, TO and SP are 4 A,0 A and 4 A
solution: Kirchhoff’s junction rule - at any junction, the sum of the current entering the junction is equal to the sum of currents leaving the junction. Kirchhoff's second rule: The algebraic sum of changes in potential around any closed loop involving resistors and cells in the loop is zero. In balanced fridge Ig = 0, Hence I 1 = I 3 and I 2 = I 4 Using Kirchhoff’s loop rule for closed loops ADBA and CBDC - I 1 R 1 + 0 + I 1 R 1 = 0 (Ig=0) ...(i) In the second loop I 3 = I 1 , I 4 = I 2 I 2 R 4 + 0 - I 1 R 3 = 0 ...(ii) From equation (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_4}{R_3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{R_2}{R_1}=\frac{R_4}{R_3}{/tex} This is the condition for balanced Wheatstone bridge n loop MNOTM 2I + 4 I 1 = 8 ...(i) Loop OPSTO -I + 5 I 1 = -4 ...(ii) On solving Current in MN, I = 4A Current in TO, I 1 = 0A Current in SP, I - I 1 = 4A Hence, the currents flowing through the branches MN, TO and SP are 4 A,0 A and 4 A
solution_html:
1. Kirchhoff’s junction rule - at any junction, the sum of the current entering the junction is equal to the sum of currents leaving the junction.
    Kirchhoff's second rule: The algebraic sum of changes in potential around any closed loop involving resistors and cells in the loop is zero.

    In balanced fridge Ig = 0,
    Hence I1 = I3 and I2 = I4
    Using Kirchhoff’s loop rule for closed loops ADBA and CBDC
    - I1R1 + 0 + I1R1 = 0 (Ig=0) ...(i)
    In the second loop I3 = I1, I4 = I2
    I2R4 + 0 - I1R3 = 0 ...(ii)
    From equation (i) and (ii)
    {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_4}{R_3}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{R_2}{R_1}=\frac{R_4}{R_3}{/tex}
    This is the condition for balanced Wheatstone bridge
  2. n loop MNOTM
    2I + 4 I1 = 8 ...(i)
    Loop OPSTO
    -I + 5 I1 = -4 ...(ii)
    On solving
    Current in MN, I = 4A
    Current in TO, I1 = 0A
    Current in SP, I - I1 = 4A 
    Hence, the currents flowing through the branches MN, TO and SP are 4 A,0 A and 4 A

question_html:

What is the current through arm bg?
question_text: The following figure shows a circuit diagram. We can find the currents through and potential differences across different resistors using Kirchhoff's rules. What is the current through arm bg?
explanation: According to Kirchhoff’s loop rule for closed loop abgha -6 + 10 I 2 + I 1 = 0 I 1 + 10 I 2 = 6 ...(i) for closed loop acfha -6 + 10 (I 1 - I 2 ) + I 1 = 0 11 I 1 - 10 I 2 = 6 ...(ii) Adding (i) and (ii) 12 I 1 = 12 I 1 = 1 A I 2 = 0.5 A = current through arm bg
solution: According to Kirchhoff’s loop rule for closed loop abgha -6 + 10 I 2 + I 1 = 0 I 1 + 10 I 2 = 6 ...(i) for closed loop acfha -6 + 10 (I 1 - I 2 ) + I 1 = 0 11 I 1 - 10 I 2 = 6 ...(ii) Adding (i) and (ii) 12 I 1 = 12 I 1 = 1 A I 2 = 0.5 A = current through arm bg
solution_html:
According to Kirchhoff’s loop rule for closed loop abgha
-6 + 10 I2 + I1 = 0
I1 + 10 I2 = 6 ...(i)
for closed loop acfha
-6 + 10 (I1 - I2) + I1 = 0
11 I1 - 10 I2 = 6 ...(ii)
Adding (i) and (ii)
12 I1 = 12
I1 = 1 A
I2 = 0.5 A
= current through arm bg

subject: Physics
question_html: The masses of two cylindrical wires of copper are in the ratio of 1 : 3 and their lengths are in the ratio of 5 : 3. The ratio of their resistances will be:
question_text: The masses of two cylindrical wires of copper are in the ratio of 1 : 3 and their lengths are in the ratio of 5 : 3. The ratio of their resistances will be:

options.explanation: The resistance of the first wire is: R 1 = {tex}\rho\frac {(5\text L)}{\text A}{/tex} The resistance of the second wire is: R 2 = {tex}\rho\frac {(3\text L)}{3\text A}{/tex} = {tex}\rho \frac {\text L}{\text A}{/tex} Dividing R 1 by R 2 , we get: {tex}\frac {R_1}{R_2}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\rho\frac {\frac {(5\text L)}{\text A}}{\rho \frac {\text L}{\text A}}\right]{/tex} = 5 Therefore, the ratio of their resistances is 5 : 1, which simplifies to 5 : 1 or 1 : 5.

explanation: The resistance of the first wire is: R 1 = {tex}\rho\frac {(5\text L)}{\text A}{/tex} The resistance of the second wire is: R 2 = {tex}\rho\frac {(3\text L)}{3\text A}{/tex} = {tex}\rho \frac {\text L}{\text A}{/tex} Dividing R 1 by R 2 , we get: {tex}\frac {R_1}{R_2}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\rho\frac {\frac {(5\text L)}{\text A}}{\rho \frac {\text L}{\text A}}\right]{/tex} = 5 Therefore, the ratio of their resistances is 5 : 1, which simplifies to 5 : 1 or 1 : 5.

question_text: Define electrical conductivity of a wire. Give its SI unit. High current is to be drawn safely from (1) a low-voltage battery, and (2) a high-voltage battery. What can you say about the internal resistance of the two batteries? Calculate the total energy supplied by the batteries to the circuit shown in the figure, in one minute.
explanation: Definition: Electrical conductivity is defined as the measure of a material‟s ability to carry a current through it. S.I. Unit: (ohm) -1 -m -1 or S-m -1 Low voltage Battery- Internal resistance should be low. High voltage Battery- Internal resistance should be high. Applying Kirchhoff‟s loop rule 10 - I {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - 4 - 2 I - 6 I - 3I = 0 12 I = 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 0.5 A Heat energy H = I 2 Rt H = 0.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 180 J
solution: Definition: Electrical conductivity is defined as the measure of a material‟s ability to carry a current through it. S.I. Unit: (ohm) -1 -m -1 or S-m -1 Low voltage Battery- Internal resistance should be low. High voltage Battery- Internal resistance should be high. Applying Kirchhoff‟s loop rule 10 - I {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - 4 - 2 I - 6 I - 3I = 0 12 I = 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 0.5 A Heat energy H = I 2 Rt H = 0.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 180 J
solution_html:

    S.I. Unit: (ohm)-1 -m-1 or S-m-1

    10 - I {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - 4 - 2 I - 6 I - 3I = 0
    12 I = 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 0.5 A

question_html:
A potential difference V is applied across a conductor of length {tex}l{/tex} and cross-sectional area A. Briefly explain how the current density j in the conductor will be affected if

  1. the potential difference V is doubled.
  2. the conductor were gradually stretched to reduce its cross-sectional area to {tex}\frac{A}{2}{/tex} and then the same potential difference V is applied across it.
question_text: A potential difference V is applied across a conductor of length {tex}l{/tex} and cross-sectional area A. Briefly explain how the current density j in the conductor will be affected if the potential difference V is doubled. the conductor were gradually stretched to reduce its cross-sectional area to {tex}\frac{A}{2}{/tex} and then the same potential difference V is applied across it.
explanation: If potential difference 'V' is applied across a conductor of length 'l' and area of cross-section 'A' having the number of free electrons per unit volume n. Then, the charge in the conductor, {tex} {q}={neAl}{/tex} and {tex}{I}=\frac{q}{t}{/tex} {tex} {I}=n {ne} {v}_{{d}} \text { and } {v}_{{d}}=\frac{e E}{m} \times \tau{/tex} where {tex}\tau{/tex} is the relaxation time and 'm' is the mass of the electron. Therefore, the current per unit area i.e., current density, {tex} {J}=\frac{I}{A} {/tex} {tex} {~J}=n e v_{{d}} {/tex} {tex} {~J}=n e \times \frac{e E}{m} \times \tau {/tex} {tex} {J}=\frac{n e^2 \tau}{m} \times\left(\frac{V}{l}\right) {/tex} Hence, with an increase in potential different V, J increases. J does not change when the area decreases because, according to relation (i), J is not reliant on A.
solution: If potential difference 'V' is applied across a conductor of length 'l' and area of cross-section 'A' having the number of free electrons per unit volume n. Then, the charge in the conductor, {tex} {q}={neAl}{/tex} and {tex}{I}=\frac{q}{t}{/tex} {tex} {I}=n {ne} {v}_{{d}} \text { and } {v}_{{d}}=\frac{e E}{m} \times \tau{/tex} where {tex}\tau{/tex} is the relaxation time and 'm' is the mass of the electron. Therefore, the current per unit area i.e., current density, {tex} {J}=\frac{I}{A} {/tex} {tex} {~J}=n e v_{{d}} {/tex} {tex} {~J}=n e \times \frac{e E}{m} \times \tau {/tex} {tex} {J}=\frac{n e^2 \tau}{m} \times\left(\frac{V}{l}\right) {/tex} Hence, with an increase in potential different V, J increases. J does not change when the area decreases because, according to relation (i), J is not reliant on A.
solution_html:
1. If potential difference 'V' is applied across a conductor of length 'l' and area of cross-section 'A' having the number of free electrons per unit volume n.
    Then, the charge in the conductor,
    {tex} {q}={neAl}{/tex}
    and {tex}{I}=\frac{q}{t}{/tex}
    {tex} {I}=n {ne} {v}_{{d}} \text { and } {v}_{{d}}=\frac{e E}{m} \times \tau{/tex}
    where {tex}\tau{/tex} is the relaxation time and 'm' is the mass of the electron.
    Therefore, the current per unit area i.e., current density,
    {tex} {J}=\frac{I}{A} {/tex}
    {tex} {~J}=n e v_{{d}} {/tex}
    {tex} {~J}=n e \times \frac{e E}{m} \times \tau {/tex}
    {tex} {J}=\frac{n e^2 \tau}{m} \times\left(\frac{V}{l}\right) {/tex}
    Hence, with an increase in potential different V, J increases.
  2. J does not change when the area decreases because, according to relation (i), J is not reliant on A.

  2. Define 1 ohm.
  3. What is the resistance of a conductor through which a current of 0.5 A flows when a potential difference of 2V is applied across its ends?
question_text: State Ohm's Law. Represent it mathematically. Define 1 ohm. What is the resistance of a conductor through which a current of 0.5 A flows when a potential difference of 2V is applied across its ends?
explanation: Ohm's law state that keeping the physical conditions (temperature, pressure and material) constant, potential difference across the conductor is directly proportional to the current flowing through the two ends of the conductor. {tex}\vee \alpha I{/tex} {tex}\frac{V}{I}{/tex} = constant = R (where } R is called as resistance) Resistance offered by a conductor is said to be 1ohm when a potential difference of 1 V is set up between the ends of the conductor and 1 A current flows through the ends of the conductor. I = 0.5 A V = 2 V R = ? {tex}R = \frac{V}{I}=\frac{2}{0.5}=4 \Omega{/tex}
solution: Ohm's law state that keeping the physical conditions (temperature, pressure and material) constant, potential difference across the conductor is directly proportional to the current flowing through the two ends of the conductor. {tex}\vee \alpha I{/tex} {tex}\frac{V}{I}{/tex} = constant = R (where } R is called as resistance) Resistance offered by a conductor is said to be 1ohm when a potential difference of 1 V is set up between the ends of the conductor and 1 A current flows through the ends of the conductor. I = 0.5 A V = 2 V R = ? {tex}R = \frac{V}{I}=\frac{2}{0.5}=4 \Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Ohm's law state that keeping the physical conditions (temperature, pressure and material) constant, potential difference across the conductor is directly proportional to the current flowing through the two ends of the conductor.
    {tex}\vee \alpha I{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{V}{I}{/tex} = constant = R (where } R is called as resistance)
  2. Resistance offered by a conductor is said to be 1ohm when a potential difference of 1 V is set up between the ends of the conductor and 1 A current flows through the ends of the conductor.
  3. I = 0.5 A
    V = 2 V
    R = ?
    {tex}R = \frac{V}{I}=\frac{2}{0.5}=4 \Omega{/tex}

question_html: Name the device which converts electrical energy into light energy. Write three advantages of the device.
question_text: Name the device which converts electrical energy into light energy. Write three advantages of the device.
explanation: LED-light emitting diode converts electrical energy into light energy when it is forward-biased. Advantages: They do not emit UV emissions or heat. They are long-lasting. They consume almost 90% less energy than incandescent bulbs.
solution: LED-light emitting diode converts electrical energy into light energy when it is forward-biased. Advantages: They do not emit UV emissions or heat. They are long-lasting. They consume almost 90% less energy than incandescent bulbs.
solution_html:
LED-light emitting diode converts electrical energy into light energy when it is  forward-biased.
Advantages:

  1. They do not emit UV emissions or heat.
  2. They are long-lasting.
  3. They consume almost 90% less energy than incandescent bulbs.

If V 1 be the p.d across the bulb, V 1 2 = PR B = 500 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 10 4 V 1 = 100V Therefore the p.d across R = 200 - 100 = 100 V. Therefore R = R B = 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex}

question_html: Define the term mobility of charge carriers in a current-carrying conductor. Obtain the relation for mobility in terms of relaxation time.
question_text: Define the term mobility of charge carriers in a current-carrying conductor. Obtain the relation for mobility in terms of relaxation time.
explanation: Mobility is formally defined as the value of the drift velocity per unit of electric field strength; thus, the faster the particle moves at a given electric field strength, the larger the mobility . Mobility is generally denoted by {tex}\mu{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \quad \mu = \frac{v _ { d }}{ E}{/tex} ...(i) Drift velocity in term of relaxation time, {tex} { v } _ { d } = \frac { - e { E } } { m } \tau{/tex} In magnitude, {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { e E } { m } \tau{/tex} or {tex}\frac { v _ { d } } { E } = \frac { e \tau } { m }{/tex} {tex}\mu = \frac { e \tau } { m }{/tex} [From Eq. (i)] it gives the relation between mobility and relaxation time The faster the particle moves at a given electric field strength, the larger the mobility. The mobility of a particular type of particle in a given solid may vary with temperature.
solution: Mobility is formally defined as the value of the drift velocity per unit of electric field strength; thus, the faster the particle moves at a given electric field strength, the larger the mobility . Mobility is generally denoted by {tex}\mu{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \quad \mu = \frac{v _ { d }}{ E}{/tex} ...(i) Drift velocity in term of relaxation time, {tex} { v } _ { d } = \frac { - e { E } } { m } \tau{/tex} In magnitude, {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { e E } { m } \tau{/tex} or {tex}\frac { v _ { d } } { E } = \frac { e \tau } { m }{/tex} {tex}\mu = \frac { e \tau } { m }{/tex} [From Eq. (i)] it gives the relation between mobility and relaxation time The faster the particle moves at a given electric field strength, the larger the mobility. The mobility of a particular type of particle in a given solid may vary with temperature.
solution_html:
Mobility is formally defined as the value of the drift velocity per unit of electric field strength; thus, the faster the particle moves at a given electric field strength, the larger the mobility. Mobility is generally denoted by {tex}\mu{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore \quad \mu = \frac{v _ { d }}{ E}{/tex}  ...(i)
Drift velocity in term of relaxation time,
{tex} { v } _ { d } = \frac { - e { E } } { m } \tau{/tex}
In magnitude, {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { e E } { m } \tau{/tex} or {tex}\frac { v _ { d } } { E } = \frac { e \tau } { m }{/tex}
{tex}\mu = \frac { e \tau } { m }{/tex} [From Eq. (i)]  it gives the relation between mobility and relaxation time
The faster the particle moves at a given electric field strength, the larger the mobility. The mobility of a particular type of particle in a given solid may vary with temperature.

question_html:
1. Two cells of emf E1 and E2 have their internal resistances r1 and r2, respectively. Deduce an expression for the equivalent emf and internal resistance of their parallel combination when connected across an external resistance R. Assume that the two cells are supporting each other.
  2. In case the two cells are identical, each of emf E = 5 V and internal resistance r = 2{tex}\Omega{/tex}, calculate the voltage across the external resistance R = 10{tex}\Omega{/tex}.
question_text: Two cells of emf E 1 and E 2 have their internal resistances r 1 and r 2 , respectively. Deduce an expression for the equivalent emf and internal resistance of their parallel combination when connected across an external resistance R. Assume that the two cells are supporting each other. In case the two cells are identical, each of emf E = 5 V and internal resistance r = 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , calculate the voltage across the external resistance R = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} .
explanation: From the circuit, we can write V 1 = E 1 - I 1 r 1 ...(i) V 2 = E 2 - I 2 r 2 ...(ii) Since E 1 and E 2 are parallel V 1 = V 2 = V I = I 1 + I 2 {tex}I=\frac{E_{1}-V}{r_{1}}+\frac{E_{2}-V}{r_{2}}{/tex} {tex}I=\left(\frac{E_{1}}{r_{1}}+\frac{E_{2}}{r_{2}}\right)-V\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}I=\frac{E_{1} r_{2}+E_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1} r_{2}}-V\left(\frac{r_{1}+r_{2}}{r_{1} r_{2}}\right){/tex} or {tex}V=\left(\frac{E_{1} r_{2}+E_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1} r_{2}}\right) \times\left(\frac{r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}\right)-I\left(\frac{r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}\right){/tex} or {tex}V=\left(\frac{E_{1} r_{2}+E_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}\right)-I\left(\frac{r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}\right){/tex} Comparing above with {tex}V=E_{e q}-I_{e q} \times R_{c q}{/tex} We get {tex}E_{e q}=\frac{E_{1} r_{2}+E_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex} Given E 1 = E 2 = 5V r 1 = r 2 = 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and Resistance R=10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} E eq = {tex}\frac{5 \times 2+5 \times 2}{2+2}=\frac{20}{4}{/tex} = 5 V r eq = {tex}\frac{r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}=\frac{4}{4}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{E_{e q}}{R+r_{e q}}=\frac{5}{10+1}=\frac{5}{11} \mathrm{A}{/tex} Voltage across the external resistance , R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = IR V = {tex}\frac{5}{11} \times 10=\frac{50}{11} \mathrm{V}{/tex}
solution: From the circuit, we can write V 1 = E 1 - I 1 r 1 ...(i) V 2 = E 2 - I 2 r 2 ...(ii) Since E 1 and E 2 are parallel V 1 = V 2 = V I = I 1 + I 2 {tex}I=\frac{E_{1}-V}{r_{1}}+\frac{E_{2}-V}{r_{2}}{/tex} {tex}I=\left(\frac{E_{1}}{r_{1}}+\frac{E_{2}}{r_{2}}\right)-V\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}I=\frac{E_{1} r_{2}+E_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1} r_{2}}-V\left(\frac{r_{1}+r_{2}}{r_{1} r_{2}}\right){/tex} or {tex}V=\left(\frac{E_{1} r_{2}+E_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1} r_{2}}\right) \times\left(\frac{r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}\right)-I\left(\frac{r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}\right){/tex} or {tex}V=\left(\frac{E_{1} r_{2}+E_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}\right)-I\left(\frac{r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}\right){/tex} Comparing above with {tex}V=E_{e q}-I_{e q} \times R_{c q}{/tex} We get {tex}E_{e q}=\frac{E_{1} r_{2}+E_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex} Given E 1 = E 2 = 5V r 1 = r 2 = 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and Resistance R=10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} E eq = {tex}\frac{5 \times 2+5 \times 2}{2+2}=\frac{20}{4}{/tex} = 5 V r eq = {tex}\frac{r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}=\frac{4}{4}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{E_{e q}}{R+r_{e q}}=\frac{5}{10+1}=\frac{5}{11} \mathrm{A}{/tex} Voltage across the external resistance , R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = IR V = {tex}\frac{5}{11} \times 10=\frac{50}{11} \mathrm{V}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. From the circuit, we can write
    V1 = E1 - I1r1 ...(i)
    V2 = E2 - I2r2 ...(ii) 
    Since E1 and E2 are parallel
    V1 = V2 = V
    I = I1 + I2 
    {tex}I=\frac{E_{1}-V}{r_{1}}+\frac{E_{2}-V}{r_{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}I=\left(\frac{E_{1}}{r_{1}}+\frac{E_{2}}{r_{2}}\right)-V\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right){/tex}
    {tex}I=\frac{E_{1} r_{2}+E_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1} r_{2}}-V\left(\frac{r_{1}+r_{2}}{r_{1} r_{2}}\right){/tex}
    or {tex}V=\left(\frac{E_{1} r_{2}+E_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1} r_{2}}\right) \times\left(\frac{r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}\right)-I\left(\frac{r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}\right){/tex}
    or {tex}V=\left(\frac{E_{1} r_{2}+E_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}\right)-I\left(\frac{r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}\right){/tex}
    Comparing above with
    {tex}V=E_{e q}-I_{e q} \times R_{c q}{/tex}
    We get {tex}E_{e q}=\frac{E_{1} r_{2}+E_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex} 
  2. Given E1 = E2 = 5V
    r1 = r2 = 2{tex}\Omega{/tex}  and Resistance R=10{tex}\Omega{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Eeq = {tex}\frac{5 \times 2+5 \times 2}{2+2}=\frac{20}{4}{/tex} = 5 V
    req = {tex}\frac{r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}=\frac{4}{4}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} 
    I = {tex}\frac{E_{e q}}{R+r_{e q}}=\frac{5}{10+1}=\frac{5}{11} \mathrm{A}{/tex} 
    Voltage across the external resistance , R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = IR
    V = {tex}\frac{5}{11} \times 10=\frac{50}{11} \mathrm{V}{/tex}

question_html: An ammeter of resistance 0·8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} can measure a current up to 1.0 A. Find the value of shunt resistance required to convert this ammeter to measure a current up to 5.0 A.
question_text: An ammeter of resistance 0·8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} can measure a current up to 1.0 A. Find the value of shunt resistance required to convert this ammeter to measure a current up to 5.0 A.
explanation: To measure current upto 5A, the shunt S should have a value, such that for 5A input current through system, 4A should pass through shunt S and 1 A should pass through given ammeter. We know that Shunt (S) is given by {tex}S={R_A i_{A}\over i-i_{A}}{/tex} {tex}R_A {/tex} is Internal resistance of the ammeter. {tex}i_g = 4A , i = 5A, R_A = 0.80\Omega{/tex} i-i A = 5A-A=4A {tex}1 \times R _ { A } = 4 S \Rightarrow 1 \times 0.8 = 4 S{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = 0.2 \Omega{/tex}
solution: To measure current upto 5A, the shunt S should have a value, such that for 5A input current through system, 4A should pass through shunt S and 1 A should pass through given ammeter. We know that Shunt (S) is given by {tex}S={R_A i_{A}\over i-i_{A}}{/tex} {tex}R_A {/tex} is Internal resistance of the ammeter. {tex}i_g = 4A , i = 5A, R_A = 0.80\Omega{/tex} i-i A = 5A-A=4A {tex}1 \times R _ { A } = 4 S \Rightarrow 1 \times 0.8 = 4 S{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = 0.2 \Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
To measure current upto 5A, the shunt S should have a value, such that for 5A input current through system, 4A should pass through shunt S and 1 A should pass through given ammeter.
We know that Shunt (S) is given by
{tex}S={R_A i_{A}\over i-i_{A}}{/tex}
{tex}R_A {/tex} is Internal resistance of the ammeter. 
{tex}i_g = 4A , i = 5A, R_A = 0.80\Omega{/tex}
i-iA= 5A-A=4A
{tex}1 \times R _ { A } = 4 S \Rightarrow 1 \times 0.8 = 4 S{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = 0.2 \Omega{/tex}

question_html: Define the term drift velocity of electrons in a current-carrying conductor. Obtain the relationship between the current density and the drift velocity of electrons.
question_text: Define the term drift velocity of electrons in a current-carrying conductor. Obtain the relationship between the current density and the drift velocity of electrons.
explanation: The velocity gained by the accelerating electrons in uniform electric field inside the conductor is drift velocity. The average velocity, acquired by free electrons along the length of a metallic conductor, due to existing electric field is called drift velocity. Let n be the number density of free electrons in a conductor of length {tex}l{/tex} and area of cross-section A. Total charge in the conductor, Q = Ne = (nA {tex}l{/tex} )e Time taken, t is given by, t = {tex}\frac{\ell}{v_{d}}{/tex} Therefore, the current flowing across the conductor is given by, l = {tex}\frac{Q}{t}=\frac{(n A \ell) e}{\left(\ell / v_{d}\right)}{/tex} That is, I = neAv d , v d = {tex}I\over neA {/tex} and {tex}J=I/A{/tex} thusJ=ne v d ,it gives the relation between drift velocity and current density.
solution: The velocity gained by the accelerating electrons in uniform electric field inside the conductor is drift velocity. The average velocity, acquired by free electrons along the length of a metallic conductor, due to existing electric field is called drift velocity. Let n be the number density of free electrons in a conductor of length {tex}l{/tex} and area of cross-section A. Total charge in the conductor, Q = Ne = (nA {tex}l{/tex} )e Time taken, t is given by, t = {tex}\frac{\ell}{v_{d}}{/tex} Therefore, the current flowing across the conductor is given by, l = {tex}\frac{Q}{t}=\frac{(n A \ell) e}{\left(\ell / v_{d}\right)}{/tex} That is, I = neAv d , v d = {tex}I\over neA {/tex} and {tex}J=I/A{/tex} thusJ=ne v d ,it gives the relation between drift velocity and current density.
solution_html:
The velocity gained by the accelerating electrons in uniform electric field inside the conductor is drift velocity. The average velocity, acquired by free electrons along the length of a metallic conductor, due to existing electric field is called drift velocity.

Let n be the number density of free electrons in a conductor of length {tex}l{/tex} and area of cross-section A.
Total charge in the conductor, Q = Ne = (nA{tex}l{/tex})e
Time taken, t is given by,
t = {tex}\frac{\ell}{v_{d}}{/tex} 
Therefore, the current flowing across the conductor is given by,
l = {tex}\frac{Q}{t}=\frac{(n A \ell) e}{\left(\ell / v_{d}\right)}{/tex} 
That is, I = neAvd,

vd={tex}I\over neA {/tex}   and {tex}J=I/A{/tex} 

thusJ=ne vd ,it gives the relation between drift velocity and current density.

question_text: The electrical resistance of a conductor

options.text: varies directly proportional to its area of cross-section
options.html: varies directly proportional to its area of cross-section

options.text: decreases with increase in its temperature
options.html: decreases with increase in its temperature

options.text: decreases with increase in its conductivity
options.html: decreases with increase in its conductivity
options.explanation: Resistance is defined as the property of a conductor to resist the flow of charges through it. The resistance of the conductor is numerically given as the ratio of potential difference across its length to the current flowing through it. It is denoted by "R" and its SI unit is ohm (Ω)

options.text: is independent of its shape but depends only on its volume

  2. A conductor of uniform cross-sectional area is connected across a dc source of variable voltage. Draw a graph showing variation of drift velocity of electrons (vd) as a function of current density (J) in it.
question_text: Differentiate between the random velocity and the drift velocity of electrons in an electrical conductor. Give their order of magnitudes. A conductor of uniform cross-sectional area is connected across a dc source of variable voltage. Draw a graph showing variation of drift velocity of electrons (v d ) as a function of current density (J) in it.
explanation: Random velocity: The velocity acquired by the free electrons in the absence of electric field. Average random velocity = 0 Drift velocity: The average velocity acquired by the free electrons in presence of the electric field. Average drift velocity is non zero Drift speed is nearly 10 -5 times smaller than random velocity
solution: Random velocity: The velocity acquired by the free electrons in the absence of electric field. Average random velocity = 0 Drift velocity: The average velocity acquired by the free electrons in presence of the electric field. Average drift velocity is non zero Drift speed is nearly 10 -5 times smaller than random velocity
solution_html:
1. Random velocity: The velocity acquired by the free electrons in the absence of
    electric field. Average random velocity = 0
    Drift velocity: The average velocity acquired by the free electrons in presence of the electric field. Average drift velocity is non zero
    Drift speed is nearly 10-5 times smaller than random velocity

question_html:
1. Differentiate between electrical resistance and resistivity of a conductor.
  2. Two metallic rods, each of length L, area of cross A1 and A2, having resistivities {tex}\rho_1{/tex} and {tex}\rho_2{/tex} are connected in parallel across a d.c. battery. Obtain the expression for the effective resistivity of this combination.
question_text: Differentiate between electrical resistance and resistivity of a conductor. Two metallic rods, each of length L, area of cross A 1 and A 2, having resistivities {tex}\rho_1{/tex} and {tex}\rho_2{/tex} are connected in parallel across a d.c. battery. Obtain the expression for the effective resistivity of this combination.
explanation: Electrical resistance Resistivity It is the property of material due to which it opposes the flow of electricity through the conductor. The resistivity is defined as the resistance of a material of 1 metre length and 1 square metre area of cross section. Unit : Ohm Unit : Ohm-metre Symbol : R Symbol : {tex}\rho{/tex} Depends on length, cross-section area of conductor and temperature. Depends on temperature and material of the conductor. For resistor R 1 , Resistivity = {tex}\rho_1{/tex} Length = L Area = A 1 {tex}\therefore R_1=\rho_1 \times \frac{L}{A_1}{/tex} For resistor R 2 , Resistivity = {tex}\rho_2{/tex} Length = L Area = A 2 {tex}\therefore R_2=\rho_2 \times \frac{L}{A_2}{/tex} For the equivalent resistor, Resistivity = {tex}\rho{/tex} Length = L Area = A = A 1 + A 2 {tex}\therefore R=\rho \times \frac{L}{A}{/tex} Since, the resistors are connected in parallel, Equivalent resistance {tex}=R=\frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2}{/tex} or, {tex}\rho \times \frac{L}{A}=\frac{\frac{\rho_1 L}{A_1} \times \frac{\rho_2 L}{A_2}}{\frac{\rho_1 L}{A_1}+\frac{\rho_2 L}{A_2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\rho{/tex} = Effective resistivity {tex}=\frac{\rho_1 \rho_2\left(A_1+A_2\right)}{\rho_1 A_2+\rho_2 A_1}{/tex}
solution: Electrical resistance Resistivity It is the property of material due to which it opposes the flow of electricity through the conductor. The resistivity is defined as the resistance of a material of 1 metre length and 1 square metre area of cross section. Unit : Ohm Unit : Ohm-metre Symbol : R Symbol : {tex}\rho{/tex} Depends on length, cross-section area of conductor and temperature. Depends on temperature and material of the conductor. For resistor R 1 , Resistivity = {tex}\rho_1{/tex} Length = L Area = A 1 {tex}\therefore R_1=\rho_1 \times \frac{L}{A_1}{/tex} For resistor R 2 , Resistivity = {tex}\rho_2{/tex} Length = L Area = A 2 {tex}\therefore R_2=\rho_2 \times \frac{L}{A_2}{/tex} For the equivalent resistor, Resistivity = {tex}\rho{/tex} Length = L Area = A = A 1 + A 2 {tex}\therefore R=\rho \times \frac{L}{A}{/tex} Since, the resistors are connected in parallel, Equivalent resistance {tex}=R=\frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2}{/tex} or, {tex}\rho \times \frac{L}{A}=\frac{\frac{\rho_1 L}{A_1} \times \frac{\rho_2 L}{A_2}}{\frac{\rho_1 L}{A_1}+\frac{\rho_2 L}{A_2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\rho{/tex} = Effective resistivity {tex}=\frac{\rho_1 \rho_2\left(A_1+A_2\right)}{\rho_1 A_2+\rho_2 A_1}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Electrical resistance Resistivity
    It is the property of material due to which it opposes the flow of electricity through the conductor. The resistivity is defined as the resistance of a material of 1 metre length and 1 square metre area of cross section.
    Unit : Ohm Unit : Ohm-metre
    Symbol : R Symbol : {tex}\rho{/tex}
    Depends on length, cross-section area of conductor and temperature. Depends on temperature and material of the conductor.
  2. For resistor R1,
    Resistivity = {tex}\rho_1{/tex}
    Length = L
    Area = A1
    {tex}\therefore R_1=\rho_1 \times \frac{L}{A_1}{/tex}
    For resistor R2,
    Resistivity = {tex}\rho_2{/tex}
    Length = L
    Area = A2
    {tex}\therefore R_2=\rho_2 \times \frac{L}{A_2}{/tex}
    For the equivalent resistor,
    Resistivity = {tex}\rho{/tex}
    Length = L
    Area = A = A1 + A2
    {tex}\therefore R=\rho \times \frac{L}{A}{/tex}
    Since, the resistors are connected in parallel,
    Equivalent resistance {tex}=R=\frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2}{/tex}
    or, {tex}\rho \times \frac{L}{A}=\frac{\frac{\rho_1 L}{A_1} \times \frac{\rho_2 L}{A_2}}{\frac{\rho_1 L}{A_1}+\frac{\rho_2 L}{A_2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\rho{/tex} = Effective resistivity {tex}=\frac{\rho_1 \rho_2\left(A_1+A_2\right)}{\rho_1 A_2+\rho_2 A_1}{/tex}

1. Define internal resistance of a cell.
  2. A cell of emf E and internal resistance r is connected across a variable resistor R. Plot the shape of graphs showing variation of terminal voltage V with (i) Rand (ii) circuit current I.
question_text: Define internal resistance of a cell. A cell of emf E and internal resistance r is connected across a variable resistor R. Plot the shape of graphs showing variation of terminal voltage V with (i) Rand (ii) circuit current I.
explanation: Internal resistance is the resistance which is present within the cell that resists the current flow inside the cell when connected to an external circuit with some external resistance. Thus, it causes a voltage drop when current flows through it. It is the resistance provided by the electrolyte and electrodes which is present in a cell. So, internal resistance is offered by the electrodes and electrolyte which oppose the current flow inside the cell. V vs. R graph V vs. I graph
solution: Internal resistance is the resistance which is present within the cell that resists the current flow inside the cell when connected to an external circuit with some external resistance. Thus, it causes a voltage drop when current flows through it. It is the resistance provided by the electrolyte and electrodes which is present in a cell. So, internal resistance is offered by the electrodes and electrolyte which oppose the current flow inside the cell. V vs. R graph V vs. I graph
solution_html:
1. Internal resistance is the resistance which is present within the cell that resists the current flow inside the cell when connected to an external circuit with some external resistance. Thus, it causes a voltage drop when current flows through it.

    It is the resistance provided by the electrolyte and electrodes which is present in a cell. So, internal resistance is offered by the electrodes and electrolyte which oppose the current flow inside the cell.

question_html: A battery of emf 12 V and internal resistance 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is connected to an external resistance R. If the current in the resistance is 0.5 A, calculate the value of (a) R, and (b) the terminal voltage of the battery.
question_text: A battery of emf 12 V and internal resistance 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is connected to an external resistance R. If the current in the resistance is 0.5 A, calculate the value of (a) R, and (b) the terminal voltage of the battery.
explanation: I = {tex}\frac{E}{R+r}{/tex} 0.5 = {tex}\frac{12}{R+4}{/tex} R = 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex} V = E - Ir or V = IR V = 12 - 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 10 volt Hence, the terminal voltage of the battery will be 10 volt.
solution: I = {tex}\frac{E}{R+r}{/tex} 0.5 = {tex}\frac{12}{R+4}{/tex} R = 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex} V = E - Ir or V = IR V = 12 - 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 10 volt Hence, the terminal voltage of the battery will be 10 volt.
solution_html:
I = {tex}\frac{E}{R+r}{/tex}
0.5 = {tex}\frac{12}{R+4}{/tex}
R = 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
V = E - Ir or V = IR
V = 12 - 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 10 volt
Hence, the terminal voltage of the battery will be 10 volt.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Write the relationship between mobility and drift velocity in a current carrying conductor.
  2. Two aluminium wires have their lengths in the ratio 2 : 3 and radii in the ratio 1 : 3. These are connected in parallel across a battery of emf E and of negligible internal resistance. Find the ratio of drift velocities of the electrons in the two wires.
question_text: Write the relationship between mobility and drift velocity in a current carrying conductor. Two aluminium wires have their lengths in the ratio 2 : 3 and radii in the ratio 1 : 3. These are connected in parallel across a battery of emf E and of negligible internal resistance. Find the ratio of drift velocities of the electrons in the two wires.
explanation: {tex}\mu=\frac{V_d}{E}{/tex} V d = {tex}\frac{e \tau E}{m}=\frac{e \tau V}{m l}{/tex} {tex}\frac{V_{d 1}}{V_{d 2}}=\frac{l_2}{l_1}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} Hence, the ratio of drift velocities of the electrons in the two wires is 3:2
solution: {tex}\mu=\frac{V_d}{E}{/tex} V d = {tex}\frac{e \tau E}{m}=\frac{e \tau V}{m l}{/tex} {tex}\frac{V_{d 1}}{V_{d 2}}=\frac{l_2}{l_1}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} Hence, the ratio of drift velocities of the electrons in the two wires is 3:2
solution_html:
1. {tex}\mu=\frac{V_d}{E}{/tex}
  2. Vd = {tex}\frac{e \tau E}{m}=\frac{e \tau V}{m l}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{V_{d 1}}{V_{d 2}}=\frac{l_2}{l_1}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
    Hence, the ratio of drift velocities of the electrons in the two wires is 3:2

subject: Physics
question_html: Using Kirchhoff ’s rules, calculate the current (Ig) that flows through the galvanometer of resistance 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex} in the circuit diagram shown in the figure.
question_text: Using Kirchhoff ’s rules, calculate the current (I g ) that flows through the galvanometer of resistance 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex} in the circuit diagram shown in the figure.
explanation: Considering the mesh BADB, we have 100 I 1 + 15 I g - 60 I 2 = 0 20I 1 + 3I g -12 I 2 = 0 ... (i) Considering the mesh BCDB, we have 10(I 1 - I g ) -15I g - 5(I 2 + I g ) = 0 10I 1 - 30I g - 5I 2 = 0 2I 1 - 6I g - I 2 = 0 ...(ii) Considering the mesh ADCEA, we have 60I 2 + 5(I 2 + I g ) = 10 65I 2 + 5I g = 0 13I 2 + I g = 2 ...(iii) Multiplying eq2 by 10 20I 1 - 60I g - 10I 2 = 0 ... eq 4 From (iv) and (i), we have 63I g - 2I 2 = 10 I 2 = 31.5 I g ...(v) Substituting the value of I 2 in (iii), we get 13(31.5I g ) + I g = 2 410.5I g = 2 I g = 4.87 mA Hence, the current (I g ) that flows through the galvanometer of resistance 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is 4.87 mA.
solution: Considering the mesh BADB, we have 100 I 1 + 15 I g - 60 I 2 = 0 20I 1 + 3I g -12 I 2 = 0 ... (i) Considering the mesh BCDB, we have 10(I 1 - I g ) -15I g - 5(I 2 + I g ) = 0 10I 1 - 30I g - 5I 2 = 0 2I 1 - 6I g - I 2 = 0 ...(ii) Considering the mesh ADCEA, we have 60I 2 + 5(I 2 + I g ) = 10 65I 2 + 5I g = 0 13I 2 + I g = 2 ...(iii) Multiplying eq2 by 10 20I 1 - 60I g - 10I 2 = 0 ... eq 4 From (iv) and (i), we have 63I g - 2I 2 = 10 I 2 = 31.5 I g ...(v) Substituting the value of I 2 in (iii), we get 13(31.5I g ) + I g = 2 410.5I g = 2 I g = 4.87 mA Hence, the current (I g ) that flows through the galvanometer of resistance 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is 4.87 mA.
solution_html:
Considering the mesh BADB, we have
100 I1 + 15 Ig - 60 I2 = 0
20I1 + 3Ig -12 I2 = 0 ... (i)
Considering the mesh BCDB, we have
10(I1 - Ig) -15Ig - 5(I2 + Ig) = 0
10I1- 30Ig - 5I2 = 0
2I1 - 6Ig - I2 = 0 ...(ii)
Considering the mesh ADCEA, we have
60I2 + 5(I2 + Ig) = 10
65I2 + 5Ig = 0
13I2 + Ig = 2 ...(iii)
Multiplying eq2 by 10
20I1 - 60Ig - 10I2 = 0 ... eq 4
From (iv) and (i), we have
63Ig - 2I2 = 10
I2 = 31.5 Ig ...(v)
Substituting the value of I2 in (iii), we get
13(31.5Ig) + Ig = 2
410.5Ig = 2
Ig = 4.87 mA
Hence, the current (Ig) that flows through the galvanometer of resistance 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is 4.87 mA.

subject: Physics
question_html: A uniform wire is cut into three parts with their lengths in the ratio 2 : 3 : 6. The ends of each of these three part are connected across an ideal battery of 10 V. If a current of 5 A is drawn from the battery, find the initial resistance of the wire.
question_text: A uniform wire is cut into three parts with their lengths in the ratio 2 : 3 : 6. The ends of each of these three part are connected across an ideal battery of 10 V. If a current of 5 A is drawn from the battery, find the initial resistance of the wire.
explanation: I net = I 1 + I 2 +I 3 = {tex}\frac{E}{R_1}+\frac{E}{R_2}+\frac{E}{R_3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{10}{2 x}+\frac{10}{3 x}+\frac{10}{6 x}{/tex} {tex}5=\frac{60}{6 x}{/tex} ; x = 2 Therefore: R = 4 + 6 + 12 = 22 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Hence, initial resistance of the wire is 22 {tex}\Omega{/tex} .
solution: I net = I 1 + I 2 +I 3 = {tex}\frac{E}{R_1}+\frac{E}{R_2}+\frac{E}{R_3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{10}{2 x}+\frac{10}{3 x}+\frac{10}{6 x}{/tex} {tex}5=\frac{60}{6 x}{/tex} ; x = 2 Therefore: R = 4 + 6 + 12 = 22 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Hence, initial resistance of the wire is 22 {tex}\Omega{/tex} .
solution_html:
Inet = I1 + I2 +I3
= {tex}\frac{E}{R_1}+\frac{E}{R_2}+\frac{E}{R_3}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{10}{2 x}+\frac{10}{3 x}+\frac{10}{6 x}{/tex}
{tex}5=\frac{60}{6 x}{/tex} ; x = 2
Therefore:
R = 4 + 6 + 12 = 22 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
Hence, initial resistance of the wire is 22 {tex}\Omega{/tex}.

question_html: Using Kirchhoff's rules, calculate the current through the 40{tex}\Omega{/tex} and 20{tex}\Omega{/tex} resistors in the following circuit:
question_text: Using Kirchhoff's rules, calculate the current through the 40 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistors in the following circuit:
explanation: In Loop ABCDA using the Kirchoff's 2nd law, 80 - 20 I 2 + 40 I 1 = 0 4 = I 2 - 2I 1 .....(1) In Loop DCFED, using the Kirchoff's 2nd law, -40 I 1 - 10 (I 1 + I 2 ) + 40 = 0 -50 I 1 - 10 I 2 + 40 = 0 5I 1 + I 2 = 4 .......(2) Solving (1) and (2) we get, I 1 = 0 A and I 2 = 4 A Thus, the current through the 40 ohm resistor is 0 A and 20 ohm resistor is 4 A.
solution: In Loop ABCDA using the Kirchoff's 2nd law, 80 - 20 I 2 + 40 I 1 = 0 4 = I 2 - 2I 1 .....(1) In Loop DCFED, using the Kirchoff's 2nd law, -40 I 1 - 10 (I 1 + I 2 ) + 40 = 0 -50 I 1 - 10 I 2 + 40 = 0 5I 1 + I 2 = 4 .......(2) Solving (1) and (2) we get, I 1 = 0 A and I 2 = 4 A Thus, the current through the 40 ohm resistor is 0 A and 20 ohm resistor is 4 A.
solution_html:
In Loop ABCDA using the Kirchoff's 2nd law,
80 - 20 I2 + 40 I1 = 0                
4 = I2 - 2I1  .....(1)
In Loop DCFED, using the Kirchoff's 2nd law,
-40 I1 - 10 (I1 + I2) + 40 = 0
-50 I1 - 10 I2 + 40 = 0
5I1 + I2 = 4    .......(2)
Solving (1) and (2) we get,
I1 = 0 A and I2 = 4 A
Thus, the current through the 40 ohm resistor is 0 A and 20 ohm resistor is 4 A.

subject: Physics
question_html: How does the mobility of electrons in a conductor change, if the potential difference applied across the conductor is doubled, keeping the length and temperature of the conductor constant?
question_text: How does the mobility of electrons in a conductor change, if the potential difference applied across the conductor is doubled, keeping the length and temperature of the conductor constant?
explanation: If the drift velocity of the electrons is v d , then, the mobility is {tex}\mu=\frac{V_d}E{/tex} ......(1) As drift velocity is given by {tex}V_d=\frac{eE}m\times\tau{/tex} .....(2) From (1) and (2), {tex}\mu=\frac{e\tau}m{/tex} So, mobility depends on relaxation time which further depends on temperature of conductor. So mobility is independent of potential difference applied and will remain unchanged on doubling the potential difference.
solution: If the drift velocity of the electrons is v d , then, the mobility is {tex}\mu=\frac{V_d}E{/tex} ......(1) As drift velocity is given by {tex}V_d=\frac{eE}m\times\tau{/tex} .....(2) From (1) and (2), {tex}\mu=\frac{e\tau}m{/tex} So, mobility depends on relaxation time which further depends on temperature of conductor. So mobility is independent of potential difference applied and will remain unchanged on doubling the potential difference.
solution_html:
If the drift velocity of the electrons is vd, then, the mobility is
{tex}\mu=\frac{V_d}E{/tex}......(1)
As drift velocity is given by
{tex}V_d=\frac{eE}m\times\tau{/tex} .....(2)
From (1) and (2),
{tex}\mu=\frac{e\tau}m{/tex}
So, mobility depends on relaxation time which further depends on temperature of conductor. So mobility is independent of potential difference applied and will remain unchanged on doubling the potential difference.

question_html: Calculate the potential difference across the 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor in the given electrical circuit, using Kirchhoff's rules.
question_text: Calculate the potential difference across the 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor in the given electrical circuit, using Kirchhoff's rules.
explanation: I = I 1 + I 2 ...(i) In loop ABCDA, -8 + 2I 1 - 1 {tex}\times{/tex} I 2 + 6 = 0 ...(ii) In loop DEFCD, -4I - 1 {tex}\times{/tex} I 2 + 6 = 0 4I + I 2 = 6 4(I 1 + I 2 ) + I 2 = 6 4I 1 + 5I 2 = 6 ...(iii) From equations (i), (ii) and (iii), we get {tex}I_1=\frac{8}{7} \mathrm{~A}, I_2=\frac{2}{7} \mathrm{~A}, I=\frac{10}{7} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} Potential difference across resistor 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is: {tex}V=\frac{10}{7} \times 4=\frac{40}{7} \text { volt } {/tex}
solution: I = I 1 + I 2 ...(i) In loop ABCDA, -8 + 2I 1 - 1 {tex}\times{/tex} I 2 + 6 = 0 ...(ii) In loop DEFCD, -4I - 1 {tex}\times{/tex} I 2 + 6 = 0 4I + I 2 = 6 4(I 1 + I 2 ) + I 2 = 6 4I 1 + 5I 2 = 6 ...(iii) From equations (i), (ii) and (iii), we get {tex}I_1=\frac{8}{7} \mathrm{~A}, I_2=\frac{2}{7} \mathrm{~A}, I=\frac{10}{7} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} Potential difference across resistor 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is: {tex}V=\frac{10}{7} \times 4=\frac{40}{7} \text { volt } {/tex}
solution_html:
I = I1 + I2 ...(i)
In loop ABCDA,
-8 + 2I1 - 1 {tex}\times{/tex} I2 + 6 = 0 ...(ii)
In loop DEFCD,
-4I - 1 {tex}\times{/tex} I2 + 6 = 0
4I + I2 = 6
4(I1 + I2) + I2 = 6
4I1 + 5I2 = 6 ...(iii)
From equations (i), (ii) and (iii), we get
{tex}I_1=\frac{8}{7} \mathrm{~A}, I_2=\frac{2}{7} \mathrm{~A}, I=\frac{10}{7} \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
Potential difference across resistor 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is:
{tex}V=\frac{10}{7} \times 4=\frac{40}{7} \text { volt } {/tex}

question_html: Twelve wires each having a resistance of 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} are connected to form a cubical network. A battery of 10 V and negligible internal resistance is connected across the diagonally opposite corners of this network. Determine its equivalent resistance and the current along each edge of the cube.
question_text: Twelve wires each having a resistance of 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} are connected to form a cubical network. A battery of 10 V and negligible internal resistance is connected across the diagonally opposite corners of this network. Determine its equivalent resistance and the current along each edge of the cube.
explanation: Applying loop rule to ABCC 'EFA 3I + 3 {tex}\frac{I}{2}{/tex} + 3I - 10 = 0 {tex}\frac{15}{2}I{/tex} = 10 I = {tex}\frac{2 \times 10}{15}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{20}{15}{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} A R tot = {tex}\frac{V}{3 I}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{10 \times 15}{3 \times 20}{/tex} = 2.5 {tex} \Omega{/tex} Current = I AB (= I AA' = I AD = I D'C' = I B'C' = I CC = {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} A = I DD' (= I A'B' = I A'D' = I DC = I BC = I BB' ) = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} A
solution: Applying loop rule to ABCC 'EFA 3I + 3 {tex}\frac{I}{2}{/tex} + 3I - 10 = 0 {tex}\frac{15}{2}I{/tex} = 10 I = {tex}\frac{2 \times 10}{15}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{20}{15}{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} A R tot = {tex}\frac{V}{3 I}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{10 \times 15}{3 \times 20}{/tex} = 2.5 {tex} \Omega{/tex} Current = I AB (= I AA' = I AD = I D'C' = I B'C' = I CC = {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} A = I DD' (= I A'B' = I A'D' = I DC = I BC = I BB' ) = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} A
solution_html:
Applying loop rule to ABCC 'EFA
3I + 3{tex}\frac{I}{2}{/tex} + 3I - 10 = 0
{tex}\frac{15}{2}I{/tex} = 10
I = {tex}\frac{2 \times 10}{15}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{20}{15}{/tex}A = {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex}A
Rtot = {tex}\frac{V}{3 I}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{10 \times 15}{3 \times 20}{/tex} = 2.5 {tex} \Omega{/tex}
Current = IAB(= IAA' = IAD = ID'C' = IB'C' = ICC = {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex}A
= IDD' (= IA'B' = IA'D' = IDC = IBC = IBB') = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}A

question_html: Why is the terminal voltage of a cell generally less than the emf of the cell?
question_text: Why is the terminal voltage of a cell generally less than the emf of the cell?
explanation: EMF = Terminal p.d. + P.D. across internal resistance {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = V + Ir A real cell has always some internal resistance, a part of the emf is consumed in doing work against the internal resistance in a closed circuit. So {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} > V or V < {tex}\varepsilon{/tex}
solution: EMF = Terminal p.d. + P.D. across internal resistance {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = V + Ir A real cell has always some internal resistance, a part of the emf is consumed in doing work against the internal resistance in a closed circuit. So {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} > V or V < {tex}\varepsilon{/tex}
solution_html:
EMF = Terminal p.d. + P.D. across internal resistance
{tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = V + Ir
A real cell has always some internal resistance, a part of the emf is consumed in doing work against the internal resistance in a closed circuit.
So {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} > V or V < {tex}\varepsilon{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Use Kirchhoff’s rules to obtain the balance condition in Wheatstone bridge.
  2. Give one practical application that is based on this principle.
question_text: Use Kirchhoff’s rules to obtain the balance condition in Wheatstone bridge. Give one practical application that is based on this principle.
explanation: Applying kirchoff’s loop rule to ADBA and CBDC -I 1 R 1 + 0 + I 2 R 2 = 0 I 2 R 4 + 0 - I 1 R 3 = 0 Since, I g = 0, I 3 = I 1 , I 4 = I 2 {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_4}{R_3} \quad{/tex} and {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{R_4}{R_3}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}{/tex} (Balance Condition) A practical device using the principle of wheatstone bridge is meter bridge.
solution: Applying kirchoff’s loop rule to ADBA and CBDC -I 1 R 1 + 0 + I 2 R 2 = 0 I 2 R 4 + 0 - I 1 R 3 = 0 Since, I g = 0, I 3 = I 1 , I 4 = I 2 {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_4}{R_3} \quad{/tex} and {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{R_4}{R_3}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}{/tex} (Balance Condition) A practical device using the principle of wheatstone bridge is meter bridge.
solution_html:
1. Applying kirchoff’s loop rule to ADBA and CBDC
    -I1R1 + 0 + I2R2 = 0
    I2R4 + 0 - I1R3 = 0
    Since, Ig = 0, I3 = I1, I4 = I2
    {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_4}{R_3} \quad{/tex} and {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \frac{R_4}{R_3}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}{/tex} (Balance Condition)
  2. A practical device using the principle of wheatstone bridge is meter bridge.
