question_html: The plot of the variation of potential difference across a combination of three identical cells in series versus current is shown below. What is the emf and internal resistance of each cell?
question_text: The plot of the variation of potential difference across a combination of three identical cells in series versus current is shown below. What is the emf and internal resistance of each cell?
explanation: Let E be the emf of each cell. then according to the graph So, 3E = 6 Volt , E = 2 Volt Internal resistance of three cells each of resistance {tex}r{/tex} can be calculated as {tex}V = I \times 3 r{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 r = \frac { V } { I } = \frac { 6 } { 1 } \Rightarrow r = 2 \Omega{/tex} so internal resistance of the circuit is 2 ohm.
solution: Let E be the emf of each cell. then according to the graph So, 3E = 6 Volt , E = 2 Volt Internal resistance of three cells each of resistance {tex}r{/tex} can be calculated as {tex}V = I \times 3 r{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 r = \frac { V } { I } = \frac { 6 } { 1 } \Rightarrow r = 2 \Omega{/tex} so internal resistance of the circuit is 2 ohm.
solution_html:
Let E be the emf of each cell. then according to the graph
So, 3E = 6 Volt , E = 2 Volt
Internal resistance of three cells each of resistance {tex}r{/tex} can be calculated as
{tex}V = I \times 3 r{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 r = \frac { V } { I } = \frac { 6 } { 1 } \Rightarrow r = 2 \Omega{/tex}
so internal resistance of the circuit is 2 ohm.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Derive an expression for drift velocity of free electrons.
  2. How does drift velocity of electrons in a metallic conductor vary with increase in temperature? Explain.
question_text: Derive an expression for drift velocity of free electrons. How does drift velocity of electrons in a metallic conductor vary with increase in temperature? Explain.
explanation: When a conductor is subjected to an electric field E, each electron experiences a force: F = - eE, and free electron acquires an acceleration, a = F/m = -eE/m ... (i) where, m = mass of electron, e = electronic charge and E = electric field. Free electron starts accelerating and gains velocity and collide with atoms and molecules of the conductor. The average time difference between two consecutive collisions is known as relaxation time of electron and can be calculated as - {tex}\overline { \tau } = \frac { \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } } { n }{/tex} ...(ii) where, {tex}\tau_{1}, \; \tau_{2}.... \; \tau_{n}{/tex} are the average time difference(relaxation time) between 1st, 2nd, ...nth collisions. {tex}\therefore{/tex} v 1 , v 2 , ... v n , are velocities gained by electron in 1st, 2nd, ..., nth collisions with initial thermal velocities u 1 , u 2 , ..., u n , respectively. {tex}\therefore \quad { v } _ { 1 } = { u } _ { 1 } + { a } \tau _ { 1 }{/tex} Similarly, {tex}\mathop {{v_2}}\limits_ \vdots = \mathop {{u_2}}\limits_ \vdots + \mathop {a{\tau _2}}\limits_ \vdots {/tex} {tex}{v_n} = {u_n} + a{\tau _2}{/tex} The drift speed v d may be defined as {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { v _ { 1 } + v _ { 2 } + \ldots + v _ { n } } { n }{/tex} {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { \left( u _ { 1 } + u _ { 2 } + \ldots + u _ { n } \right) + a \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex} {tex} { v } _ { d } = \frac { \left( { u } _ { 1 } + { u } _ { 2 } + \ldots + { u } _ { n } \right) } { n } + \frac { { a } \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex} {tex}v _ { d } = 0 + { a } \tau{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} Average thermal velocity in n collisions = 0] {tex}{ v } _ { d } = - ( e { E } / m ) \tau{/tex} [from Eq. (i)] This is the required expression of drift speed of free electrons which shows it is directly proportional to relaxation time. As the temperature of a conductor is increased, the thermal agitation increases and the collisions become more frequent. The average time {tex}\tau{/tex} between the successive collisions decreases and hence the drift speed decreases. Thus, the conductivity decreases and the resistivity of the conductor increases.
solution: When a conductor is subjected to an electric field E, each electron experiences a force: F = - eE, and free electron acquires an acceleration, a = F/m = -eE/m ... (i) where, m = mass of electron, e = electronic charge and E = electric field. Free electron starts accelerating and gains velocity and collide with atoms and molecules of the conductor. The average time difference between two consecutive collisions is known as relaxation time of electron and can be calculated as - {tex}\overline { \tau } = \frac { \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } } { n }{/tex} ...(ii) where, {tex}\tau_{1}, \; \tau_{2}.... \; \tau_{n}{/tex} are the average time difference(relaxation time) between 1st, 2nd, ...nth collisions. {tex}\therefore{/tex} v 1 , v 2 , ... v n , are velocities gained by electron in 1st, 2nd, ..., nth collisions with initial thermal velocities u 1 , u 2 , ..., u n , respectively. {tex}\therefore \quad { v } _ { 1 } = { u } _ { 1 } + { a } \tau _ { 1 }{/tex} Similarly, {tex}\mathop {{v_2}}\limits_ \vdots = \mathop {{u_2}}\limits_ \vdots + \mathop {a{\tau _2}}\limits_ \vdots {/tex} {tex}{v_n} = {u_n} + a{\tau _2}{/tex} The drift speed v d may be defined as {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { v _ { 1 } + v _ { 2 } + \ldots + v _ { n } } { n }{/tex} {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { \left( u _ { 1 } + u _ { 2 } + \ldots + u _ { n } \right) + a \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex} {tex} { v } _ { d } = \frac { \left( { u } _ { 1 } + { u } _ { 2 } + \ldots + { u } _ { n } \right) } { n } + \frac { { a } \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex} {tex}v _ { d } = 0 + { a } \tau{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} Average thermal velocity in n collisions = 0] {tex}{ v } _ { d } = - ( e { E } / m ) \tau{/tex} [from Eq. (i)] This is the required expression of drift speed of free electrons which shows it is directly proportional to relaxation time. As the temperature of a conductor is increased, the thermal agitation increases and the collisions become more frequent. The average time {tex}\tau{/tex} between the successive collisions decreases and hence the drift speed decreases. Thus, the conductivity decreases and the resistivity of the conductor increases.
solution_html:
1. When a conductor is subjected to an electric field E, each electron experiences a force:
    F = - eE, and free electron acquires an
    acceleration, a = F/m = -eE/m ... (i)
    where, m = mass of electron, e = electronic charge and E = electric field.
    Free electron starts accelerating and gains velocity and collide with atoms and molecules of the conductor. The average time difference between two consecutive collisions is known as relaxation time of electron and can be calculated as -
    {tex}\overline { \tau } = \frac { \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } } { n }{/tex} ...(ii)
    where, {tex}\tau_{1}, \; \tau_{2}.... \; \tau_{n}{/tex} are the average time difference(relaxation time) between 1st, 2nd, ...nth collisions.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} v1, v2, ... vn, are velocities gained by electron in 1st, 2nd, ..., nth collisions with initial thermal velocities u1, u2, ..., un, respectively.
    {tex}\therefore \quad { v } _ { 1 } = { u } _ { 1 } + { a } \tau _ { 1 }{/tex}
    Similarly,
    {tex}\mathop {{v_2}}\limits_ \vdots = \mathop {{u_2}}\limits_ \vdots + \mathop {a{\tau _2}}\limits_ \vdots {/tex}
    {tex}{v_n} = {u_n} + a{\tau _2}{/tex}
    The drift speed vd may be defined as
    {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { v _ { 1 } + v _ { 2 } + \ldots + v _ { n } } { n }{/tex}
    {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { \left( u _ { 1 } + u _ { 2 } + \ldots + u _ { n } \right) + a \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex}
    {tex} { v } _ { d } = \frac { \left( { u } _ { 1 } + { u } _ { 2 } + \ldots + { u } _ { n } \right) } { n } + \frac { { a } \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex}
    {tex}v _ { d } = 0 + { a } \tau{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Average thermal velocity in n collisions = 0]
    {tex}{ v } _ { d } = - ( e { E } / m ) \tau{/tex} [from Eq. (i)]
    This is the required expression of drift speed of free electrons which shows it is directly proportional to relaxation time.

  2. As the temperature of a conductor is increased, the thermal agitation increases and the collisions become more frequent. The average time {tex}\tau{/tex} between the successive collisions decreases and hence the drift speed decreases. Thus, the conductivity decreases and the resistivity of the conductor increases.

subject: Physics
question_html: Two wires one of copper and other manganin have the same resistance and equal length. Which wire is thicker and why?
question_text: Two wires one of copper and other manganin have the same resistance and equal length. Which wire is thicker and why?
explanation: We know that resistance of a wire is given as, {tex}\mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho \mathrm{L} } {\mathrm{A}}{/tex} let the resistivity and area of copper wire is {tex}\rho_{1}{/tex} and A 1 and let the resistivity and area of manganin wire is {tex}\rho_{2}{/tex} and A 2 since resistance and length of both the wires are equal, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac {\rho_{1} }{ A_{1}} = \frac{\rho_{2} }{ A_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{A_{1} }{ A_{2}} = \frac{\rho_{1} }{ \rho_{2}}{/tex} since {tex}\rho_{1}<\rho_{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} A 1 < A 2 Hence manganin wire will be thicker. Another way, The area is directly proportional to resistivity, if length and resistance are constant. Resistivity is greater for manganin than for copper, so manganin wire is thicker than that of copper wire.
solution: We know that resistance of a wire is given as, {tex}\mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho \mathrm{L} } {\mathrm{A}}{/tex} let the resistivity and area of copper wire is {tex}\rho_{1}{/tex} and A 1 and let the resistivity and area of manganin wire is {tex}\rho_{2}{/tex} and A 2 since resistance and length of both the wires are equal, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac {\rho_{1} }{ A_{1}} = \frac{\rho_{2} }{ A_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{A_{1} }{ A_{2}} = \frac{\rho_{1} }{ \rho_{2}}{/tex} since {tex}\rho_{1}<\rho_{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} A 1 < A 2 Hence manganin wire will be thicker. Another way, The area is directly proportional to resistivity, if length and resistance are constant. Resistivity is greater for manganin than for copper, so manganin wire is thicker than that of copper wire.
solution_html:
We know that resistance of a wire is given as, {tex}\mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho \mathrm{L} } {\mathrm{A}}{/tex}
let the resistivity and area of copper wire is {tex}\rho_{1}{/tex} and A1 and let the resistivity and area of manganin wire is {tex}\rho_{2}{/tex} and A2 
since resistance and length of both the wires are equal,
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac {\rho_{1} }{ A_{1}} = \frac{\rho_{2} }{ A_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{A_{1} }{ A_{2}} = \frac{\rho_{1} }{ \rho_{2}}{/tex}
since {tex}\rho_{1}<\rho_{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} A1 < A2
Hence manganin wire will be thicker.
Another way,
The area is directly proportional to resistivity, if length and resistance are constant. Resistivity is greater for manganin than for copper, so manganin wire is thicker than that of copper wire.

question_html: Distinguish between emf {tex}(\varepsilon){/tex} and terminal voltage (V) of a cell having internal resistance r. Draw a plot showing the variation of terminal voltage (V) versus the current (I) drawn from the cell. Using this plot, show how does one can determine the internal resistance of the cell?
question_text: Distinguish between emf {tex}(\varepsilon){/tex} and terminal voltage (V) of a cell having internal resistance r. Draw a plot showing the variation of terminal voltage (V) versus the current (I) drawn from the cell. Using this plot, show how does one can determine the internal resistance of the cell?
explanation: Difference between emf {tex}(\varepsilon ){/tex} and terminal voltage (V) emf Terminal voltage It is the potential difference between two terminals of the cells when no current is flowing through it. It is the potential difference between two terminals when current passes through it. It is the cause. It is the effect. Following plot is showing variation of terminal voltage versus the current. Note: - Negative slope gives internal resistance, where {tex}\mathrm V=\varepsilon-\mathrm{Ir} {/tex} Or {tex}\mathrm r=\frac{\varepsilon-\mathrm{V}}{I}{/tex}
solution: Difference between emf {tex}(\varepsilon ){/tex} and terminal voltage (V) emf Terminal voltage It is the potential difference between two terminals of the cells when no current is flowing through it. It is the potential difference between two terminals when current passes through it. It is the cause. It is the effect. Following plot is showing variation of terminal voltage versus the current. Note: - Negative slope gives internal resistance, where {tex}\mathrm V=\varepsilon-\mathrm{Ir} {/tex} Or {tex}\mathrm r=\frac{\varepsilon-\mathrm{V}}{I}{/tex}
solution_html:
Difference between emf {tex}(\varepsilon ){/tex} and terminal voltage (V)

emf Terminal voltage
It is the potential difference between two terminals of the cells when no current is flowing through it. It is the potential difference between two terminals when current passes through it.
It is the cause. It is the effect.

question_html: Two cells of emfs 1.5 V and 2.0 V having internal resistances 0.2{tex}\Omega{/tex} and 0.3{tex}\Omega{/tex} respectively are connected in parallel. Calculate the emf and internal resistance of the equivalent cell.
question_text: Two cells of emfs 1.5 V and 2.0 V having internal resistances 0.2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 0.3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} respectively are connected in parallel. Calculate the emf and internal resistance of the equivalent cell.
explanation: According to question Equivalent emf {tex}= \frac {E _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } + E _ { 2 } r _ { 1 }}{ r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 }}{/tex} i.e. {tex}= \frac { ( 1.5 \times 0.3 ) + ( 2 \times 0.2 ) } { 0.2 + 0.3 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 0.45 + 0.4 } { 0.5 } = \frac { 0.85 } { 0.5 } = 1.7 \mathrm { V }{/tex} Equivalent internal resistance {tex}= \frac { r _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } } = \frac { 0.2 \times 0.3 } { 0.2 + 0.3 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 0.06 } { 0.5 } = \frac { 6 } { 50 } = 0.12 \Omega{/tex}
solution: According to question Equivalent emf {tex}= \frac {E _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } + E _ { 2 } r _ { 1 }}{ r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 }}{/tex} i.e. {tex}= \frac { ( 1.5 \times 0.3 ) + ( 2 \times 0.2 ) } { 0.2 + 0.3 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 0.45 + 0.4 } { 0.5 } = \frac { 0.85 } { 0.5 } = 1.7 \mathrm { V }{/tex} Equivalent internal resistance {tex}= \frac { r _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } } = \frac { 0.2 \times 0.3 } { 0.2 + 0.3 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 0.06 } { 0.5 } = \frac { 6 } { 50 } = 0.12 \Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
According to question

Equivalent emf {tex}= \frac {E _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } + E _ { 2 } r _ { 1 }}{ r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 }}{/tex}
i.e. {tex}= \frac { ( 1.5 \times 0.3 ) + ( 2 \times 0.2 ) } { 0.2 + 0.3 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 0.45 + 0.4 } { 0.5 } = \frac { 0.85 } { 0.5 } = 1.7 \mathrm { V }{/tex}
Equivalent internal resistance
{tex}= \frac { r _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } } = \frac { 0.2 \times 0.3 } { 0.2 + 0.3 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 0.06 } { 0.5 } = \frac { 6 } { 50 } = 0.12 \Omega{/tex}

question_html: Why connecting wires are made of copper?
question_text: Why connecting wires are made of copper?
explanation: Because of its very low resistivity (or very high conductivity), the resistance of the connecting wires made of copper do not add to the resistance of the circuit. Thus, current in the circuit is not affected practically.

question_html:
1. Define the term of drift velocity.
  2. On the basis of electron drift, derive an expression for resistivity of a conductor in terms of number density of free electrons and relaxation time. On what factors does resistivity of a conductor depend?
  3. Why alloys like Constantan and Manganin are used for making standard resistors?
question_text: Define the term of drift velocity. On the basis of electron drift, derive an expression for resistivity of a conductor in terms of number density of free electrons and relaxation time. On what factors does resistivity of a conductor depend? Why alloys like Constantan and Manganin are used for making standard resistors?
explanation: Drift Velocity : It the average velocity with which electrons move along the conductor under influence of electric field. Or Average velocity acquired by the electrons in the conductor in the presence of external electric field. {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { I } { n e A }{/tex} Specific resistance or resistivity of the material of a conductor is defined as the resistance of a unit length with unit area of cross-section of the material of the conductor. The unit of resistivity is ohm-meter or {tex}\Omega{/tex} -m. we know that {tex}R = \rho ( l / A ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \rho = R A / l{/tex} From Ohm's law, we get V = IR {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E {tex}l{/tex} = neAv d R {tex}\Rightarrow \quad R = E l / n e A v _ { d } \text { and } v _ { d } = e E \tau / m{/tex} So, {tex}R = \frac { E l \times m } { n e ^ { 2 } A E \tau } = \frac { m l } { n e ^ { 2 } A \tau }{/tex} Substituting the value of {tex}R = \frac { m l } { n e ^ { 2 } A \tau }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we have {tex}\rho = \left( m l / n e ^ { 2 } A \tau \right) \cdot A / l{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Resistivity of the material is given by, {tex}\rho = m / n e ^ { 2 } \tau{/tex} where {tex}\tau = {/tex} average relaxation time. From the above formula, it is clear that resistivity of a conductor depends upon the following factors: {tex}\rho \propto \frac { 1 } { n }{/tex} , i.e., the resistivity of material is inversely proportional to the number density of free electrons . As the free electron density depends upon the nature of material, so resistivity of a conductor depends on the nature of the material. {tex}\rho \propto \mathbf { 1 } / \tau{/tex} , i.e. the resistivity of a material is inversely proportional to the average relaxation time {tex}\tau{/tex} of free electrons in the conductor. As the value of {tex}\tau{/tex} depends on the temperature as temperature increases, {tex}\tau{/tex} decreases, hence {tex}\rho{/tex} increases. Alloys like Constantan and Manganin are used for making standard resistors because they have high value of resistivity temperature coefficient of resistance is less
solution: Drift Velocity : It the average velocity with which electrons move along the conductor under influence of electric field. Or Average velocity acquired by the electrons in the conductor in the presence of external electric field. {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { I } { n e A }{/tex} Specific resistance or resistivity of the material of a conductor is defined as the resistance of a unit length with unit area of cross-section of the material of the conductor. The unit of resistivity is ohm-meter or {tex}\Omega{/tex} -m. we know that {tex}R = \rho ( l / A ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \rho = R A / l{/tex} From Ohm's law, we get V = IR {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E {tex}l{/tex} = neAv d R {tex}\Rightarrow \quad R = E l / n e A v _ { d } \text { and } v _ { d } = e E \tau / m{/tex} So, {tex}R = \frac { E l \times m } { n e ^ { 2 } A E \tau } = \frac { m l } { n e ^ { 2 } A \tau }{/tex} Substituting the value of {tex}R = \frac { m l } { n e ^ { 2 } A \tau }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we have {tex}\rho = \left( m l / n e ^ { 2 } A \tau \right) \cdot A / l{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Resistivity of the material is given by, {tex}\rho = m / n e ^ { 2 } \tau{/tex} where {tex}\tau = {/tex} average relaxation time. From the above formula, it is clear that resistivity of a conductor depends upon the following factors: {tex}\rho \propto \frac { 1 } { n }{/tex} , i.e., the resistivity of material is inversely proportional to the number density of free electrons . As the free electron density depends upon the nature of material, so resistivity of a conductor depends on the nature of the material. {tex}\rho \propto \mathbf { 1 } / \tau{/tex} , i.e. the resistivity of a material is inversely proportional to the average relaxation time {tex}\tau{/tex} of free electrons in the conductor. As the value of {tex}\tau{/tex} depends on the temperature as temperature increases, {tex}\tau{/tex} decreases, hence {tex}\rho{/tex} increases. Alloys like Constantan and Manganin are used for making standard resistors because they have high value of resistivity temperature coefficient of resistance is less
solution_html:
1. Drift Velocity : It the average velocity with which electrons move along the conductor under influence of electric field. Or Average velocity acquired by the electrons in the conductor in the presence of external electric field.{tex}v _ { d } = \frac { I } { n e A }{/tex}
  2. Specific resistance or resistivity of the material of a conductor is defined as the resistance of a unit length with unit area of cross-section of the material of the conductor.

    The unit of resistivity is ohm-meter or {tex}\Omega{/tex}-m.
    we know that
    {tex}R = \rho ( l / A ){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \rho = R A / l{/tex}
    From Ohm's law, we get
    V = IR
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E{tex}l{/tex}= neAvdR
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad R = E l / n e A v _ { d } \text { and } v _ { d } = e E \tau / m{/tex}
    So, {tex}R = \frac { E l \times m } { n e ^ { 2 } A E \tau } = \frac { m l } { n e ^ { 2 } A \tau }{/tex}
    Substituting the value of {tex}R = \frac { m l } { n e ^ { 2 } A \tau }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we have
     {tex}\rho = \left( m l / n e ^ { 2 } A \tau \right) \cdot A / l{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Resistivity of the material is given by, {tex}\rho = m / n e ^ { 2 } \tau{/tex} 
    where {tex}\tau = {/tex} average relaxation time.
    From the above formula, it is clear that resistivity of a conductor depends upon the following factors:

    1. {tex}\rho \propto \frac { 1 } { n }{/tex}, i.e., the resistivity of material is inversely proportional to the number density of free electrons . As the free electron density depends upon the nature of material, so resistivity of a conductor depends on the nature of the material.
    2. {tex}\rho \propto \mathbf { 1 } / \tau{/tex}, i.e. the resistivity of a material is inversely proportional to the average relaxation time {tex}\tau{/tex} of free electrons in the conductor. As the value of {tex}\tau{/tex} depends on the temperature as temperature increases, {tex}\tau{/tex} decreases, hence {tex}\rho{/tex} increases.

  3. Alloys like Constantan and Manganin are used for making standard resistors because

    1. they have high value of resistivity
    2. temperature coefficient of resistance is less

question_html:
A battery of emf 12 V and internal resistance {tex}2\Omega{/tex} is connected to a {tex}4\Omega{/tex} resistor as shown in the figure.

  1. Show that a voltmeter when placed across the cell and across the resistor, in turn, gives the same reading.
  2. To record the voltage and the current in the circuit, why is voltmeter placed in parallel and ammeter in series in the circuit?
question_text: A battery of emf 12 V and internal resistance {tex}2\Omega{/tex} is connected to a {tex}4\Omega{/tex} resistor as shown in the figure. Show that a voltmeter when placed across the cell and across the resistor, in turn, gives the same reading. To record the voltage and the current in the circuit, why is voltmeter placed in parallel and ammeter in series in the circuit?
explanation: According to question, {tex}R = 2 + 4 = 6 \Omega{/tex} Net current in circuit {tex}= \frac { 12 } { 6 } = 2 A{/tex} Voltage across the battery, {tex}V _ { b } = 12 - 2 \times 2 = 8 \mathrm { V }{/tex} Voltage across the resistance, {tex}V _ { r } = I R = 2 \times 4 = 8 \mathrm { V }{/tex} In order to measure the device's voltage by a voltmeter, it must be connected in parallel to that device. This is necessary because device in parallel experiences the same potential difference. An ammeter is connected in series with the circuit because the purpose of the ammeter is to measure the current through the circuit Since, the ammeter is a low impedance device. Connecting in parallel with the circuit would cause a short-circuit, damaging the ammeter of the circuit.
solution: According to question, {tex}R = 2 + 4 = 6 \Omega{/tex} Net current in circuit {tex}= \frac { 12 } { 6 } = 2 A{/tex} Voltage across the battery, {tex}V _ { b } = 12 - 2 \times 2 = 8 \mathrm { V }{/tex} Voltage across the resistance, {tex}V _ { r } = I R = 2 \times 4 = 8 \mathrm { V }{/tex} In order to measure the device's voltage by a voltmeter, it must be connected in parallel to that device. This is necessary because device in parallel experiences the same potential difference. An ammeter is connected in series with the circuit because the purpose of the ammeter is to measure the current through the circuit Since, the ammeter is a low impedance device. Connecting in parallel with the circuit would cause a short-circuit, damaging the ammeter of the circuit.
solution_html:
According to question,


{tex}R = 2 + 4 = 6 \Omega{/tex}

  1. Net current in circuit {tex}= \frac { 12 } { 6 } = 2 A{/tex}
    Voltage across the battery,
    {tex}V _ { b } = 12 - 2 \times 2 = 8 \mathrm { V }{/tex}
    Voltage across the resistance,
    {tex}V _ { r } = I R = 2 \times 4 = 8 \mathrm { V }{/tex}
  2. In order to measure the device's voltage by a voltmeter, it must be connected in parallel to that device. This is necessary because device in parallel experiences the same potential difference. An ammeter is connected in series with the circuit because the purpose of the ammeter is to measure the current through the circuit Since, the ammeter is a low impedance device. Connecting in parallel with the circuit would cause a short-circuit, damaging the ammeter of the circuit.

subject: Physics
question_html: Define the term relaxation time in a conductor.
question_text: Define the term relaxation time in a conductor.
explanation: Relaxation Time (t): The average time elapsing between two successive collisions of an electron inside a conductor under the application of an external electric field is called the relaxation time. It is measured in second. Relaxation time = mean free path of electron/drift velocity.
solution: Relaxation Time (t): The average time elapsing between two successive collisions of an electron inside a conductor under the application of an external electric field is called the relaxation time. It is measured in second. Relaxation time = mean free path of electron/drift velocity.
solution_html:
Relaxation Time (t): The average time elapsing between two successive collisions of an electron inside a conductor under the application of an external electric field is called the relaxation time. It is measured in second.

Relaxation time = mean free path of electron/drift velocity.

question_html:
The temperature coefficient of resistivity, for two materials A and B, are 0.0031 / °C and 0.0068 / °C respectively.
Two resistors R1 and R2 made from materials A and B, respectively, have resistances of 200 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 100 {tex}\Omega{/tex} at 0°C. Show on a diagram, the colour code, of a carbon resistor, that would have a resistance equal to the series combination of R1 and R2 at a temperature of 100°C.
(Neglect the ring corresponding to the tolerance of the carbon resistor).
question_text: The temperature coefficient of resistivity, for two materials A and B, are 0.0031 / °C and 0.0068 / °C respectively. Two resistors R 1 and R 2 made from materials A and B, respectively, have resistances of 200 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 100 {tex}\Omega{/tex} at 0°C. Show on a diagram, the colour code, of a carbon resistor, that would have a resistance equal to the series combination of R 1 and R 2 at a temperature of 100°C. (Neglect the ring corresponding to the tolerance of the carbon resistor).
explanation: R 1 = R 0 (1 + {tex}\alpha \Delta t{/tex} )( in general form) R 1 = 200 ohm, R 2 = 100 ohm. For Resistance R 1 {tex}R_{1}^{\prime}{/tex} = R 1 (1 + {tex}\alpha \Delta t{/tex} ) = 200 (1 + 0.0031 {tex}\times{/tex} 100) = 262 {tex}\Omega{/tex} For Resistance R 2 {tex}R_{2}^{\prime}{/tex} = 100 (1 + 0.0068 {tex}\times{/tex} 100) = 168 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Hence, Total Resistance in series combination of R 1 and R 2 at 100 o C : R = {tex}R_{1}^{\prime}+R_{2}^{\prime}{/tex} = 262 + 168 = 430 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution: R 1 = R 0 (1 + {tex}\alpha \Delta t{/tex} )( in general form) R 1 = 200 ohm, R 2 = 100 ohm. For Resistance R 1 {tex}R_{1}^{\prime}{/tex} = R 1 (1 + {tex}\alpha \Delta t{/tex} ) = 200 (1 + 0.0031 {tex}\times{/tex} 100) = 262 {tex}\Omega{/tex} For Resistance R 2 {tex}R_{2}^{\prime}{/tex} = 100 (1 + 0.0068 {tex}\times{/tex} 100) = 168 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Hence, Total Resistance in series combination of R 1 and R 2 at 100 o C : R = {tex}R_{1}^{\prime}+R_{2}^{\prime}{/tex} = 262 + 168 = 430 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
R1 = R0 (1 + {tex}\alpha \Delta t{/tex})( in general form)
R1 = 200 ohm, R2 = 100 ohm.
For Resistance R1
{tex}R_{1}^{\prime}{/tex} = R1 (1 + {tex}\alpha \Delta t{/tex})
= 200 (1 + 0.0031 {tex}\times{/tex} 100)
= 262 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
For Resistance R2
{tex}R_{2}^{\prime}{/tex} = 100 (1 + 0.0068 {tex}\times{/tex} 100)
= 168 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
Hence, Total Resistance in series combination of R1 and R2 at 100oC :
R = {tex}R_{1}^{\prime}+R_{2}^{\prime}{/tex} = 262 + 168
= 430 {tex}\Omega{/tex}

question_html:
1. Use Kirchhoff's rules, calculate the current in the arm AC of the given circuit.
  2. On what principle does the metre bridgework? Why are the metal strips used in the bridge?
question_text: Use Kirchhoff's rules, calculate the current in the arm AC of the given circuit. On what principle does the metre bridgework? Why are the metal strips used in the bridge?
explanation: Applying kirchoff's junction rule at A node, I 3 = I 1 + I 2 ...(i) Applying kirchoff's second rule in loop EFCA, -30I 1 + 40 - 40I 3 = 0 3I 1 + 4I 3 = 4 ...(ii) In loop EFDB, -30I 1 + 20I 2 - 80 = 0 -3I 1 + 2I 2 = 8 ...(iii) from equation (i) put the value of I 3 in equation (ii), 3I 1 + 4 (I 1 + I 2 ) = 4 7I 1 + 4I 2 = 4 ...(iv) ( by multiplying the equation 3rd by 2 and subtract 3rd and 4th equation ) we get, 13I 1 = -12 I 1 = {tex}-\frac{12}{13}{/tex} A Put I 1 in equation (iv), 7 {tex}\times-\frac{12}{13}{/tex} + 4I 2 = 4 4I 2 = 4 + {tex}\frac{84}{13}{/tex} I 2 = {tex}\frac{34}{13}{/tex} A from equation (i), I 3 = {tex}-\frac{12}{13}+\frac{34}{13}{/tex} Thus the current through the arm AC is given by,I 3 = {tex}\frac{22}{13}{/tex} A Metre bridge works on the principle of the Wheat stone bridge. A meter bridge is used in finding the unknown resistance of a conductor as that of in wheatstone bridge.The thick metal strips are used to minimise the resistance of connection wires because the connection resistance have not been accounted in the formula. (resistance is inversely proportional to cross-section area, so thick wire has low resistance, moreover, the resistivity of copper is small compared to manganin.
solution: Applying kirchoff's junction rule at A node, I 3 = I 1 + I 2 ...(i) Applying kirchoff's second rule in loop EFCA, -30I 1 + 40 - 40I 3 = 0 3I 1 + 4I 3 = 4 ...(ii) In loop EFDB, -30I 1 + 20I 2 - 80 = 0 -3I 1 + 2I 2 = 8 ...(iii) from equation (i) put the value of I 3 in equation (ii), 3I 1 + 4 (I 1 + I 2 ) = 4 7I 1 + 4I 2 = 4 ...(iv) ( by multiplying the equation 3rd by 2 and subtract 3rd and 4th equation ) we get, 13I 1 = -12 I 1 = {tex}-\frac{12}{13}{/tex} A Put I 1 in equation (iv), 7 {tex}\times-\frac{12}{13}{/tex} + 4I 2 = 4 4I 2 = 4 + {tex}\frac{84}{13}{/tex} I 2 = {tex}\frac{34}{13}{/tex} A from equation (i), I 3 = {tex}-\frac{12}{13}+\frac{34}{13}{/tex} Thus the current through the arm AC is given by,I 3 = {tex}\frac{22}{13}{/tex} A Metre bridge works on the principle of the Wheat stone bridge. A meter bridge is used in finding the unknown resistance of a conductor as that of in wheatstone bridge.The thick metal strips are used to minimise the resistance of connection wires because the connection resistance have not been accounted in the formula. (resistance is inversely proportional to cross-section area, so thick wire has low resistance, moreover, the resistivity of copper is small compared to manganin.
solution_html:
1. Applying kirchoff's junction rule at A node,
    I3 = I1 + I2 ...(i)
    Applying kirchoff's second rule in loop EFCA,
    -30I1 + 40 - 40I3 = 0
    3I1 + 4I3 = 4 ...(ii)
    In loop EFDB,
    -30I1 + 20I2 - 80 = 0
    -3I1 + 2I2 = 8 ...(iii)
    from equation (i) put the value of I3 in equation (ii),
    3I1+ 4 (I1 + I2) = 4
    7I1 + 4I2 = 4 ...(iv) ( by multiplying the equation 3rd by 2 and subtract 3rd and 4th equation ) we get,
    13I1 = -12
    I1 = {tex}-\frac{12}{13}{/tex} A
    Put I1 in equation (iv),
    7 {tex}\times-\frac{12}{13}{/tex} + 4I2 = 4
    4I2 = 4 + {tex}\frac{84}{13}{/tex}
    I2 = {tex}\frac{34}{13}{/tex} A
    from equation (i),
    I3 = {tex}-\frac{12}{13}+\frac{34}{13}{/tex}
    Thus the current through the arm AC is given by,I3 = {tex}\frac{22}{13}{/tex} A
  2.  Metre bridge works on the principle of the Wheat stone bridge. A meter bridge is used in finding the unknown resistance of a conductor as that of in wheatstone bridge.The thick metal strips are used to minimise the resistance of connection wires because the connection resistance have not been accounted in the formula. (resistance is inversely proportional to cross-section area, so thick wire has low resistance, moreover, the resistivity of copper is small compared to manganin.

question_html: Two identical cells of emf 1.5 V each joined in parallel provide supply to an external circuit consisting of two resistances of 17 {tex}\Omega{/tex} each joined in parallel. A very high resistance voltmeter reads the terminal voltage of cells to be 1.4 V. Calculate the internal resistance of each cell.
question_text: Two identical cells of emf 1.5 V each joined in parallel provide supply to an external circuit consisting of two resistances of 17 {tex}\Omega{/tex} each joined in parallel. A very high resistance voltmeter reads the terminal voltage of cells to be 1.4 V. Calculate the internal resistance of each cell.
explanation: Here {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 1.5 V, V = 1.4 V The resistance of external circuit = Total resistance of two resistances of 17 {tex}\Omega{/tex} connected in parallel or R = {tex}\frac{R_{1} R_{2}}{R_{1} \ + \ R_{2}}=\frac{17 \ \times \ 17}{17 \ + \ 17} \Omega{/tex} = 8.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Let {tex}r^\prime{/tex} be the total internal resistance of the two cells. Then {tex}r^{\prime}=R\left[\frac{\varepsilon-V}{V}\right]=8.5\left[\frac{1.5-1.4}{1.4}\right] \Omega=0.6 \Omega{/tex} As the two cells of internal resistance r {tex}\Omega{/tex} each have been connected in parallel, therefore. {tex}\frac{1}{r^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{r}+\frac{1}{r}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{0.6}=\frac{2}{r}{/tex} or r = 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 1.2 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution: Here {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 1.5 V, V = 1.4 V The resistance of external circuit = Total resistance of two resistances of 17 {tex}\Omega{/tex} connected in parallel or R = {tex}\frac{R_{1} R_{2}}{R_{1} \ + \ R_{2}}=\frac{17 \ \times \ 17}{17 \ + \ 17} \Omega{/tex} = 8.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Let {tex}r^\prime{/tex} be the total internal resistance of the two cells. Then {tex}r^{\prime}=R\left[\frac{\varepsilon-V}{V}\right]=8.5\left[\frac{1.5-1.4}{1.4}\right] \Omega=0.6 \Omega{/tex} As the two cells of internal resistance r {tex}\Omega{/tex} each have been connected in parallel, therefore. {tex}\frac{1}{r^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{r}+\frac{1}{r}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{0.6}=\frac{2}{r}{/tex} or r = 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 1.2 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
Here {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 1.5 V, V = 1.4 V
The resistance of external circuit = Total resistance of two resistances of 17 {tex}\Omega{/tex} connected in parallel
or R = {tex}\frac{R_{1} R_{2}}{R_{1} \ + \ R_{2}}=\frac{17 \ \times \ 17}{17 \ + \ 17} \Omega{/tex} = 8.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex}

Let {tex}r^\prime{/tex} be the total internal resistance of the two cells.
Then
{tex}r^{\prime}=R\left[\frac{\varepsilon-V}{V}\right]=8.5\left[\frac{1.5-1.4}{1.4}\right] \Omega=0.6 \Omega{/tex}
As the two cells of internal resistance r {tex}\Omega{/tex} each have been connected in parallel, therefore.
{tex}\frac{1}{r^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{r}+\frac{1}{r}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{0.6}=\frac{2}{r}{/tex}
or r = 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 1.2 {tex}\Omega{/tex}

  2. Describe briefly the device, based on the above question. Draw a circuit diagram for this device and discuss, in brief, how it is used for finding an unknown resistance.
question_text: Obtain the condition under which the current flowing, in the current detecting device, used in the circuit shown in the figure, becomes zero. Describe briefly the device, based on the above question. Draw a circuit diagram for this device and discuss, in brief, how it is used for finding an unknown resistance.
explanation: The given circuit can be redrawn as shown : It is, therefore, a Wheatstone Bridge. Therefore, we have to find the bridge balance condition Using Kirchhoff's laws, we get (when i g = 0) i 1 = i 3 and i 2 = i 4 For the loop ABDA, we have -i 1 P + i 2 X = 0 or i 1 P = i 2 X For the loop BCDB, we have, -i 3 Q + i 4 R = 0 or i 3 Q = i 4 R Dividing we get, {tex}\frac{i_{1} P}{i_{3} Q}=\frac{i_{2} X}{i_{4} R}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{X}{R}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} i 1 = i 3 and i 2 = i 4 ) A simple device, based on the above condition, is the metre bridge. It has a (uniform cross-section) wire of length 1 m stretched out between two thick metallic clamps. It has two gaps for connection a resistance box and the unknown resistance. The circuit diagram, for the metre bridge, is shown here. We move the jockey, on the wire of the meter bridge, till we find a point at which the deflection in G, is zero, we can have(value of R is known to us) {tex}\frac{R}{l_{1}}=\frac{X}{l_{2}}{/tex} or X = {tex}R\left(\frac{l_{2}}{l_{1}}\right){/tex} Knowing R, and finding l 1 and l 2 = (100 - l 1 ), we can easily calculate X.(Unknown resistance)
solution: The given circuit can be redrawn as shown : It is, therefore, a Wheatstone Bridge. Therefore, we have to find the bridge balance condition Using Kirchhoff's laws, we get (when i g = 0) i 1 = i 3 and i 2 = i 4 For the loop ABDA, we have -i 1 P + i 2 X = 0 or i 1 P = i 2 X For the loop BCDB, we have, -i 3 Q + i 4 R = 0 or i 3 Q = i 4 R Dividing we get, {tex}\frac{i_{1} P}{i_{3} Q}=\frac{i_{2} X}{i_{4} R}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{X}{R}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} i 1 = i 3 and i 2 = i 4 ) A simple device, based on the above condition, is the metre bridge. It has a (uniform cross-section) wire of length 1 m stretched out between two thick metallic clamps. It has two gaps for connection a resistance box and the unknown resistance. The circuit diagram, for the metre bridge, is shown here. We move the jockey, on the wire of the meter bridge, till we find a point at which the deflection in G, is zero, we can have(value of R is known to us) {tex}\frac{R}{l_{1}}=\frac{X}{l_{2}}{/tex} or X = {tex}R\left(\frac{l_{2}}{l_{1}}\right){/tex} Knowing R, and finding l 1 and l 2 = (100 - l 1 ), we can easily calculate X.(Unknown resistance)
solution_html:
1. The given circuit can be redrawn as shown :

    It is, therefore, a Wheatstone Bridge. Therefore, we have to find the bridge balance condition
    Using Kirchhoff's laws, we get (when ig = 0)
    i1 = i3
    and i2 = i4
    For the loop ABDA, we have
    -i1P + i2X = 0 or i1P = i2X
    For the loop BCDB, we have,
    -i3Q + i4R = 0 or i3Q = i4R
    Dividing we get,
    {tex}\frac{i_{1} P}{i_{3} Q}=\frac{i_{2} X}{i_{4} R}{/tex}
    or {tex}\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{X}{R}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} i1 = i3 and i2 = i4)
  2.  A simple device, based on the above condition, is the metre bridge. It has a (uniform cross-section) wire of length 1 m stretched out between two thick metallic clamps. It has two gaps for connection a resistance box and the unknown resistance.
    The circuit diagram, for the metre bridge, is shown here.

    We move the jockey, on the wire of the meter bridge, till we find a point at which the deflection in G, is zero, we can have(value of R is known to us)
    {tex}\frac{R}{l_{1}}=\frac{X}{l_{2}}{/tex}
    or X = {tex}R\left(\frac{l_{2}}{l_{1}}\right){/tex}
    Knowing R, and finding l1 and l2 = (100 - l1), we can easily calculate X.(Unknown resistance)

subject: Physics
question_html:
The reading of the (ideal) ammeter, in the circuit shown here, is equal too.

  1. I when key K1 is closed but key K2 is open.
  2. {tex}\frac{I}{2}{/tex} when both keys K1 and K2 are closed.
    Find the expression of the resistance of X in terms of the resistances of R and S.
question_text: The reading of the (ideal) ammeter, in the circuit shown here, is equal too. I when key K 1 is closed but key K 2 is open. {tex}\frac{I}{2}{/tex} when both keys K 1 and K 2 are closed. Find the expression of the resistance of X in terms of the resistances of R and S.
explanation: Current I when K 2 is open {tex}I=\frac{E}{R+X}{/tex} Current I'when K 2 is closed {tex}I'=\frac{E}{R+\left(\frac{S X}{S+X}\right)}=\frac{E(S+X)}{R(S+X)+S X}{/tex} ( so , S and X are parallel ) Current flowing through X, ( {tex}I'=I/2{/tex} ) {tex}\frac{I}{2}=\frac{I^{\prime} S}{S+X}=\frac{E S}{R(S+X)+S X}{/tex} (by putting the value of I ,we get) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{E}{2(R+X)}=\frac{E S}{R(S+X)+S X}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(R + X)S = R(S + X) + SX 2RS + 2XS = RS + RX + SX RS = RX - SX {tex}X=\frac{R S}{(R-S)}{/tex}
solution: Current I when K 2 is open {tex}I=\frac{E}{R+X}{/tex} Current I'when K 2 is closed {tex}I'=\frac{E}{R+\left(\frac{S X}{S+X}\right)}=\frac{E(S+X)}{R(S+X)+S X}{/tex} ( so , S and X are parallel ) Current flowing through X, ( {tex}I'=I/2{/tex} ) {tex}\frac{I}{2}=\frac{I^{\prime} S}{S+X}=\frac{E S}{R(S+X)+S X}{/tex} (by putting the value of I ,we get) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{E}{2(R+X)}=\frac{E S}{R(S+X)+S X}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(R + X)S = R(S + X) + SX 2RS + 2XS = RS + RX + SX RS = RX - SX {tex}X=\frac{R S}{(R-S)}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Current I when K2 is open
    {tex}I=\frac{E}{R+X}{/tex}
  2. Current I'when K2 is closed
    {tex}I'=\frac{E}{R+\left(\frac{S X}{S+X}\right)}=\frac{E(S+X)}{R(S+X)+S X}{/tex} ( so , S and X are parallel )
    Current flowing through X, ({tex}I'=I/2{/tex})
    {tex}\frac{I}{2}=\frac{I^{\prime} S}{S+X}=\frac{E S}{R(S+X)+S X}{/tex} (by putting the value of I ,we get)
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{E}{2(R+X)}=\frac{E S}{R(S+X)+S X}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(R + X)S = R(S + X) + SX
    2RS + 2XS = RS + RX + SX
    RS = RX - SX
    {tex}X=\frac{R S}{(R-S)}{/tex}

question_text: When 5 V potential difference is applied across a wire of length 0.1 m, the drift speed of electrons is 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m/s. If the electron density fa the wire is 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 28 m -3 , calculate the resistivity of the material of the wire.
explanation: R = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A}{/tex} and I = enAv d {tex}\therefore{/tex} V = RI = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A}{/tex} . enAv d or {tex}\rho=\frac{V}{e n v_{d} l}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{1.6 \ \times \ 10^{-19} \ \times \ 8 \ \times \ 10^{28} \ \times \ 2.5 \ \times \ 10^{-4} \ \times \ 0.1}{/tex} = 1.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} m
solution: R = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A}{/tex} and I = enAv d {tex}\therefore{/tex} V = RI = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A}{/tex} . enAv d or {tex}\rho=\frac{V}{e n v_{d} l}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{1.6 \ \times \ 10^{-19} \ \times \ 8 \ \times \ 10^{28} \ \times \ 2.5 \ \times \ 10^{-4} \ \times \ 0.1}{/tex} = 1.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} m

R = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A}{/tex} and I = enAvd
{tex}\therefore{/tex} V = RI = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A}{/tex}. enAvd
or {tex}\rho=\frac{V}{e n v_{d} l}{/tex}

question_html: A battery of emf 12 V and internal resistance 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is connected to a 4{tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor as shown in the figure. Show that a voltmeter when placed across the cell and across the resistor, in turn, gives the same reading.
question_text: A battery of emf 12 V and internal resistance 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is connected to a 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor as shown in the figure. Show that a voltmeter when placed across the cell and across the resistor, in turn, gives the same reading.
explanation: R eq = R + r = 4 + 2 = 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} {tex}\therefore I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R_{e q}}=\frac{12 \mathrm{~V}}{6 \Omega}{/tex} = 2A Terminal p.d. across the cell, V = E - Ir = 12 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 8 V P.D. across 4Q resistance = 4 {tex}\Omega \ \times{/tex} 2 A = 8 V Hence, the voltmeter gives the same reading in the two cases.
solution: R eq = R + r = 4 + 2 = 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} {tex}\therefore I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R_{e q}}=\frac{12 \mathrm{~V}}{6 \Omega}{/tex} = 2A Terminal p.d. across the cell, V = E - Ir = 12 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 8 V P.D. across 4Q resistance = 4 {tex}\Omega \ \times{/tex} 2 A = 8 V Hence, the voltmeter gives the same reading in the two cases.
solution_html:
Req = R + r = 4 + 2 = 6{tex}\Omega{/tex}
{tex}\therefore I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R_{e q}}=\frac{12 \mathrm{~V}}{6 \Omega}{/tex} = 2A
Terminal p.d. across the cell,
V = E - Ir = 12 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 8 V
P.D. across 4Q resistance = 4{tex}\Omega \ \times{/tex} 2 A = 8 V
Hence, the voltmeter gives the same reading in the two cases.

subject: Physics
question_html: Derive an expression for drift velocity of free electrons.
question_text: Derive an expression for drift velocity of free electrons.
explanation: Expression for the drift velocity of electrons: When a potential difference is applied across a conductor, an electric field is produced and free electrons are acted upon by an electric force (=-eE) As a result, electrons accelerate and keep colliding with each other and acquire a constant average velocity (v d ). Therefore, F e = -E e {tex}\Rightarrow F_{e}=\frac{-e V}{1}{/tex} We know, a = {tex}\frac{-\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{-\mathrm{eV}}{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} Also, v = u + at Here, u = 0; Relaxation time, t = {tex}\tau{/tex} Therefore, v d = - a {tex}\tau{/tex} v d = {tex}\frac{-\mathrm{eV}}{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{r}{/tex}
solution: Expression for the drift velocity of electrons: When a potential difference is applied across a conductor, an electric field is produced and free electrons are acted upon by an electric force (=-eE) As a result, electrons accelerate and keep colliding with each other and acquire a constant average velocity (v d ). Therefore, F e = -E e {tex}\Rightarrow F_{e}=\frac{-e V}{1}{/tex} We know, a = {tex}\frac{-\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{-\mathrm{eV}}{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} Also, v = u + at Here, u = 0; Relaxation time, t = {tex}\tau{/tex} Therefore, v d = - a {tex}\tau{/tex} v d = {tex}\frac{-\mathrm{eV}}{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{r}{/tex}
solution_html:
Expression for the drift velocity of electrons:
When a potential difference is applied across a conductor, an electric field is produced and free electrons are acted upon by an electric force (=-eE)
As a result, electrons accelerate and keep colliding with each other and acquire a constant average velocity (vd).
Therefore,
Fe = -Ee
{tex}\Rightarrow F_{e}=\frac{-e V}{1}{/tex}
We know,
a = {tex}\frac{-\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{-\mathrm{eV}}{\mathrm{m}}{/tex}
Also,
v = u  + at
Here, u = 0;
Relaxation time, t = {tex}\tau{/tex}
Therefore,
vd = - a{tex}\tau{/tex}
vd = {tex}\frac{-\mathrm{eV}}{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{r}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A student connects a cell, of emf {tex}\varepsilon_{2}{/tex} and internal resistance r2 with a cell of emf {tex}\varepsilon_{1}{/tex} and internal resistance r1, such that their combination has a net internal resistance less than r1. This combination is then connected across a resistance R.
Draw a diagram of the 'set-up' and obtain an expression for the current flowing through the resistance.
question_text: A student connects a cell, of emf {tex}\varepsilon_{2}{/tex} and internal resistance r 2 with a cell of emf {tex}\varepsilon_{1}{/tex} and internal resistance r 1 , such that their combination has a net internal resistance less than r 1 . This combination is then connected across a resistance R. Draw a diagram of the 'set-up' and obtain an expression for the current flowing through the resistance.
explanation: As the effective internal resistance of two cells is less than the internal resistance r 1 of one cells so the cell are connected in parallel between the two point R 1 and R 2 . The circuit is the potential difference between point R 1 and R 2 . The potential difference between the terminal of the first cell is {tex}V=V_{B_{1}}-V_{B_{2}}=\varepsilon_{1}-I_{1} r_{1}{/tex} or {tex}I_{1}=\frac{\varepsilon_{1}-V}{r_{1}}{/tex} Potential difference between the terminal of second cell is {tex}V=V_{B_{1}}-V_{B_{2}}=\epsilon_{2}-I_{2} r_{2}{/tex} or {tex}I_{2}=\frac{\varepsilon_{2}-V}{r_{2}}{/tex} Hence current in external resistance {tex}I=I_{1}+I_{2}=\frac{\varepsilon_{1}-V}{r_{1}}+\frac{\varepsilon_{2}-V}{r_{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{\varepsilon_{1}}{r_{1}}+\frac{\varepsilon_{2}}{r_{2}}\right)-V\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} or {tex}I=\left(\frac{\varepsilon_{1}}{r_{1}}+\frac{\varepsilon_{2}}{r_{2}}\right)-I R\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} V = IR] or {tex}I\left[1+R\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right)\right]=\left(\frac{\epsilon_{1}}{r_{1}}+\frac{\varepsilon_{2}}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left[\frac{r_{1} r_{2}+R\left(r_{2} r_{1}\right)}{r_{1} r_{2}}\right]=\frac{\varepsilon_{1} r_{2}+\varepsilon_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1} r_{2}}{/tex} or {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon_{1} r_{2}+\varepsilon_{2} r_{1}}{\left(r_{1} \times r_{2}\right)+R\left(r_{2}+r_{1}\right)}{/tex}
solution: As the effective internal resistance of two cells is less than the internal resistance r 1 of one cells so the cell are connected in parallel between the two point R 1 and R 2 . The circuit is the potential difference between point R 1 and R 2 . The potential difference between the terminal of the first cell is {tex}V=V_{B_{1}}-V_{B_{2}}=\varepsilon_{1}-I_{1} r_{1}{/tex} or {tex}I_{1}=\frac{\varepsilon_{1}-V}{r_{1}}{/tex} Potential difference between the terminal of second cell is {tex}V=V_{B_{1}}-V_{B_{2}}=\epsilon_{2}-I_{2} r_{2}{/tex} or {tex}I_{2}=\frac{\varepsilon_{2}-V}{r_{2}}{/tex} Hence current in external resistance {tex}I=I_{1}+I_{2}=\frac{\varepsilon_{1}-V}{r_{1}}+\frac{\varepsilon_{2}-V}{r_{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{\varepsilon_{1}}{r_{1}}+\frac{\varepsilon_{2}}{r_{2}}\right)-V\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} or {tex}I=\left(\frac{\varepsilon_{1}}{r_{1}}+\frac{\varepsilon_{2}}{r_{2}}\right)-I R\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} V = IR] or {tex}I\left[1+R\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right)\right]=\left(\frac{\epsilon_{1}}{r_{1}}+\frac{\varepsilon_{2}}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left[\frac{r_{1} r_{2}+R\left(r_{2} r_{1}\right)}{r_{1} r_{2}}\right]=\frac{\varepsilon_{1} r_{2}+\varepsilon_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1} r_{2}}{/tex} or {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon_{1} r_{2}+\varepsilon_{2} r_{1}}{\left(r_{1} \times r_{2}\right)+R\left(r_{2}+r_{1}\right)}{/tex}
solution_html:
As the effective internal resistance of two cells is less than the internal resistance r1 of one cells so the cell are connected in parallel between the two point R1 and R2. The circuit is the potential difference between point R1 and R2. The potential difference between the terminal of the first cell is

{tex}V=V_{B_{1}}-V_{B_{2}}=\varepsilon_{1}-I_{1} r_{1}{/tex} or {tex}I_{1}=\frac{\varepsilon_{1}-V}{r_{1}}{/tex}
Potential difference between the terminal of second cell is
{tex}V=V_{B_{1}}-V_{B_{2}}=\epsilon_{2}-I_{2} r_{2}{/tex} or {tex}I_{2}=\frac{\varepsilon_{2}-V}{r_{2}}{/tex}
Hence current in external resistance
{tex}I=I_{1}+I_{2}=\frac{\varepsilon_{1}-V}{r_{1}}+\frac{\varepsilon_{2}-V}{r_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{\varepsilon_{1}}{r_{1}}+\frac{\varepsilon_{2}}{r_{2}}\right)-V\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right){/tex}
or {tex}I=\left(\frac{\varepsilon_{1}}{r_{1}}+\frac{\varepsilon_{2}}{r_{2}}\right)-I R\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} V = IR]
or {tex}I\left[1+R\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right)\right]=\left(\frac{\epsilon_{1}}{r_{1}}+\frac{\varepsilon_{2}}{r_{2}}\right){/tex}
or {tex}\left[\frac{r_{1} r_{2}+R\left(r_{2} r_{1}\right)}{r_{1} r_{2}}\right]=\frac{\varepsilon_{1} r_{2}+\varepsilon_{2} r_{1}}{r_{1} r_{2}}{/tex}
or {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon_{1} r_{2}+\varepsilon_{2} r_{1}}{\left(r_{1} \times r_{2}\right)+R\left(r_{2}+r_{1}\right)}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Derive an expression for drift velocity of free electrons in a conductor in terms of relaxation time.
question_text: Derive an expression for drift velocity of free electrons in a conductor in terms of relaxation time.
explanation: The drift velocity is the average velocity that a particle, such as an electron, attains in a material due to an electric field. It can also be referred to as axial drift velocity. When a conductor is subjected to an electric field E, each electron experiences a force F = - eE, and free electron acquires an acceleration, a= F/m = - eE/m ... (i) where, m = mass of electron, e = electronic charge and E = electric field. Free electron starts accelerating and gains velocity and collide with atoms and molecules of the conductor. The average time difference between two consecutive collisions is known as relaxation time of electron and {tex}\overline { \tau } = \frac { \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } } { n }{/tex} ...(ii) where, {tex}\tau_{1}, \; \tau_{2}.... \; \tau_{n}{/tex} are the average time difference between 1st, 2nd, ...nth collisions. {tex}\therefore{/tex} v 1 , v 2 , ... v n , are velocities gained by electron in 1st, 2nd, ..., nth collisions with initial thermal velocities u 1 , u 2 , ..., u n , respectively. {tex}\therefore \quad { v } _ { 1 } = { u } _ { 1 } + { a } \tau _ { 1 }{/tex} Similarly, {tex}\mathop {{v_2}}\limits_ \vdots = \mathop {{u_2}}\limits_ \vdots + \mathop {a{\tau _2}}\limits_ \vdots {/tex} {tex}{v_n} = {u_n} + a{\tau _2}{/tex} The drift speed v d may be defined as {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { v _ { 1 } + v _ { 2 } + \ldots + v _ { n } } { n }{/tex} {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { \left( u _ { 1 } + u _ { 2 } + \ldots + u _ { n } \right) + a \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex} {tex} { v } _ { d } = \frac { \left( { u } _ { 1 } + { u } _ { 2 } + \ldots + { u } _ { n } \right) } { n } + \frac { { a } \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex} {tex}v _ { d } = 0 + { a } \tau{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} Average thermal velocity in n collisions = 0] {tex}{ v } _ { d } = - ( e { E } / m ) \tau{/tex} [from Eq. (i)] This is the required expression of drift speed of free electrons in a conductor in a presence of voltage source.
solution: The drift velocity is the average velocity that a particle, such as an electron, attains in a material due to an electric field. It can also be referred to as axial drift velocity. When a conductor is subjected to an electric field E, each electron experiences a force F = - eE, and free electron acquires an acceleration, a= F/m = - eE/m ... (i) where, m = mass of electron, e = electronic charge and E = electric field. Free electron starts accelerating and gains velocity and collide with atoms and molecules of the conductor. The average time difference between two consecutive collisions is known as relaxation time of electron and {tex}\overline { \tau } = \frac { \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } } { n }{/tex} ...(ii) where, {tex}\tau_{1}, \; \tau_{2}.... \; \tau_{n}{/tex} are the average time difference between 1st, 2nd, ...nth collisions. {tex}\therefore{/tex} v 1 , v 2 , ... v n , are velocities gained by electron in 1st, 2nd, ..., nth collisions with initial thermal velocities u 1 , u 2 , ..., u n , respectively. {tex}\therefore \quad { v } _ { 1 } = { u } _ { 1 } + { a } \tau _ { 1 }{/tex} Similarly, {tex}\mathop {{v_2}}\limits_ \vdots = \mathop {{u_2}}\limits_ \vdots + \mathop {a{\tau _2}}\limits_ \vdots {/tex} {tex}{v_n} = {u_n} + a{\tau _2}{/tex} The drift speed v d may be defined as {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { v _ { 1 } + v _ { 2 } + \ldots + v _ { n } } { n }{/tex} {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { \left( u _ { 1 } + u _ { 2 } + \ldots + u _ { n } \right) + a \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex} {tex} { v } _ { d } = \frac { \left( { u } _ { 1 } + { u } _ { 2 } + \ldots + { u } _ { n } \right) } { n } + \frac { { a } \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex} {tex}v _ { d } = 0 + { a } \tau{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} Average thermal velocity in n collisions = 0] {tex}{ v } _ { d } = - ( e { E } / m ) \tau{/tex} [from Eq. (i)] This is the required expression of drift speed of free electrons in a conductor in a presence of voltage source.
solution_html:
The drift velocity is the average velocity that a particle, such as an electron, attains in a material due to an electric field. It can also be referred to as axial drift velocity.

When a conductor is subjected to an electric field E, each electron experiences a force
F = - eE, and free electron acquires an
acceleration, a= F/m = - eE/m ... (i)
where, m = mass of electron, e = electronic charge and E = electric field.
Free electron starts accelerating and gains velocity and collide with atoms and molecules of the conductor. The average time difference between two consecutive collisions is known as relaxation time of electron and
{tex}\overline { \tau } = \frac { \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } } { n }{/tex} ...(ii)
where, {tex}\tau_{1}, \; \tau_{2}.... \; \tau_{n}{/tex} are the average time difference between 1st, 2nd, ...nth collisions.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} v1, v2, ... vn, are velocities gained by electron in 1st, 2nd, ..., nth collisions with initial thermal velocities u1, u2, ..., un, respectively.
{tex}\therefore \quad { v } _ { 1 } = { u } _ { 1 } + { a } \tau _ { 1 }{/tex}
Similarly,
{tex}\mathop {{v_2}}\limits_ \vdots = \mathop {{u_2}}\limits_ \vdots + \mathop {a{\tau _2}}\limits_ \vdots {/tex}
{tex}{v_n} = {u_n} + a{\tau _2}{/tex}
The drift speed vd may be defined as
{tex}v _ { d } = \frac { v _ { 1 } + v _ { 2 } + \ldots + v _ { n } } { n }{/tex}
{tex}v _ { d } = \frac { \left( u _ { 1 } + u _ { 2 } + \ldots + u _ { n } \right) + a \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex}
{tex} { v } _ { d } = \frac { \left( { u } _ { 1 } + { u } _ { 2 } + \ldots + { u } _ { n } \right) } { n } + \frac { { a } \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex}
{tex}v _ { d } = 0 + { a } \tau{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Average thermal velocity in n collisions = 0]
{tex}{ v } _ { d } = - ( e { E } / m ) \tau{/tex} [from Eq. (i)]
This is the required expression of drift speed of free electrons in a conductor in a presence of voltage source.

question_html: Explain the term drift velocity of electrons in a conductor. Hence obtain the expression for the current through a conductor in terms of drift velocity.
question_text: Explain the term drift velocity of electrons in a conductor. Hence obtain the expression for the current through a conductor in terms of drift velocity .
explanation: Drift velocity: It is the average velocity acquired by the free electrons superimposed over the random motion in the direction opposite to electric field and along the length of the metallic conductor. Let n = number of free electrons per unit volume, v d = Drift velocity of electrons Total number of free electrons passing through a cross section in unit time {tex}\frac Nt{/tex} = Anv d So, total charge passing through a cross section in unit time. i.e., current, I = {tex}\frac Qt = \frac Nt{/tex} = Anev d
solution: Drift velocity: It is the average velocity acquired by the free electrons superimposed over the random motion in the direction opposite to electric field and along the length of the metallic conductor. Let n = number of free electrons per unit volume, v d = Drift velocity of electrons Total number of free electrons passing through a cross section in unit time {tex}\frac Nt{/tex} = Anv d So, total charge passing through a cross section in unit time. i.e., current, I = {tex}\frac Qt = \frac Nt{/tex} = Anev d
solution_html:
Drift velocity: It is the average velocity acquired by the free electrons superimposed over the random motion in the direction opposite to electric field and along the length of the metallic conductor.
Let n = number of free electrons per unit volume, vd = Drift velocity of electrons Total number of free electrons passing through a cross section in unit time {tex}\frac Nt{/tex} = Anvd
So, total charge passing through a cross section in unit time.
i.e., current, I = {tex}\frac Qt = \frac Nt{/tex} = Anevd

question_html: Define the term mobility of charge carriers in a conductor. Write its SI unit.
question_text: Define the term mobility of charge carriers in a conductor. Write its SI unit.
explanation: Electron mobility is how quickly an electron can move through a conductor. It is determined by the drift velocity and the strength of the electric field as shown in this equation: {tex}\mu = \frac { \text { Drift velocity } \left( V _ { d } \right) } { \text { Electric field } ( E ) } {/tex} Its SI unit is m 2 /V-s
solution: Electron mobility is how quickly an electron can move through a conductor. It is determined by the drift velocity and the strength of the electric field as shown in this equation: {tex}\mu = \frac { \text { Drift velocity } \left( V _ { d } \right) } { \text { Electric field } ( E ) } {/tex} Its SI unit is m 2 /V-s
solution_html:
Electron mobility is how quickly an electron can move through a conductor. It is determined by the drift velocity and the strength of the electric field as shown in this equation:
{tex}\mu = \frac { \text { Drift velocity } \left( V _ { d } \right) } { \text { Electric field } ( E ) } {/tex}
Its SI unit is m2/V-s

question_html: Plot a graph showing the variation of resistivity of a conductor with temperature.
question_text: Plot a graph showing the variation of resistivity of a conductor with temperature.
explanation: The resistivity of a metallic conductor is given by {tex}\rho = \rho _ { 0 } \left[ 1 + \alpha \left( T - T _ { 0 } \right) \right]{/tex} where, {tex}\rho _ { 0 }{/tex} = resistivity at reference temperature T 0 = reference temperature and {tex}\alpha{/tex} = coefficient of resistivity having unit /Kelvin. From the above relation, we can say that the graph between resistivity of a conductor with temperature is straight line. But, at temperatures much lower than 273 K ( i.e. 0°C), the graph deviates considerably from a straight line as shown in the figure.
solution: The resistivity of a metallic conductor is given by {tex}\rho = \rho _ { 0 } \left[ 1 + \alpha \left( T - T _ { 0 } \right) \right]{/tex} where, {tex}\rho _ { 0 }{/tex} = resistivity at reference temperature T 0 = reference temperature and {tex}\alpha{/tex} = coefficient of resistivity having unit /Kelvin. From the above relation, we can say that the graph between resistivity of a conductor with temperature is straight line. But, at temperatures much lower than 273 K ( i.e. 0°C), the graph deviates considerably from a straight line as shown in the figure.

The resistivity of a metallic conductor is given by
{tex}\rho = \rho _ { 0 } \left[ 1 + \alpha \left( T - T _ { 0 } \right) \right]{/tex}
where, {tex}\rho _ { 0 }{/tex} = resistivity at reference temperature
T0 = reference temperature and
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = coefficient of resistivity having unit /Kelvin.
From the above relation, we can say that the graph between resistivity of a conductor with temperature is straight line. But, at temperatures much lower than 273 K ( i.e. 0°C), the graph deviates considerably from a straight line as shown in the figure.

question_html: Using the concept of drift velocity of charge carriers in a conductor, deduce the relationship between current density and resistivity of the conductor.
question_text: Using the concept of drift velocity of charge carriers in a conductor, deduce the relationship between current density and resistivity of the conductor.
explanation: The average velocity attained by some particle such as an electron due to the influence of an electric field is termed as the drift velocity. The movement or the motion of the particles is assumed to be along a plane and hence the motion can also be referred to as the axial drift velocity. As we know that, I = neAv d Also, current density J is given by J = {tex}\frac{I}{A}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad | { J } | = \frac { n e ^ { 2 } } { m } \tau { E } \quad \left[ \because v _ { d } = \frac { e \tau E } { m } \right]{/tex} or {tex}J = ( \frac{1}{\rho} ) E \left[ \because \rho = m / n e ^ { 2 } \tau \right]{/tex}
solution: The average velocity attained by some particle such as an electron due to the influence of an electric field is termed as the drift velocity. The movement or the motion of the particles is assumed to be along a plane and hence the motion can also be referred to as the axial drift velocity. As we know that, I = neAv d Also, current density J is given by J = {tex}\frac{I}{A}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad | { J } | = \frac { n e ^ { 2 } } { m } \tau { E } \quad \left[ \because v _ { d } = \frac { e \tau E } { m } \right]{/tex} or {tex}J = ( \frac{1}{\rho} ) E \left[ \because \rho = m / n e ^ { 2 } \tau \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
The average velocity attained by some particle such as an electron due to the influence of an electric field is termed as the drift velocity. The movement or the motion of the particles is assumed to be along a plane and hence the motion can also be referred to as the axial drift velocity.
As we know that, I = neAvd
Also, current density J is given by
J = {tex}\frac{I}{A}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad | { J } | = \frac { n e ^ { 2 } } { m } \tau { E } \quad \left[ \because v _ { d } = \frac { e \tau E } { m } \right]{/tex}
or {tex}J = ( \frac{1}{\rho} ) E \left[ \because \rho = m / n e ^ { 2 } \tau \right]{/tex}

question_html: I-V graph for a metallic wire at two different temperatures T1 and T2 is as shown in the figure below. Which of the two temperature is lower and why?
question_text: I-V graph for a metallic wire at two different temperatures T 1 and T 2 is as shown in the figure below. Which of the two temperature is lower and why?
explanation: To solve this problem we will use ohm's law which states that V = I {tex}\times{/tex} R {tex}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{R}}} = \frac{{\text{I}}}{{\text{V}}}{/tex} So, slope of the graph is inversely proportional to resistance. Consider the figure, Since slope of 1 > slope of 2, {tex}\frac{{{{\text{I}}_{\text{1}}}}}{{{{\text{V}}_{\text{1}}}}}> \frac{{{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}}}{{{{\text{V}}_{\text{2}}}}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{{\text{V}}_{\text{2}}}}}{{{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}}}>\frac{{{{\text{V}}_{\text{1}}}}}{{{{\text{I}}_{\text{1}}}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \; R _ { 2 } > R _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\because \frac{ V}{ I} = R{/tex} Also, we know that resistance is directly proportional to the temperature as R 2 > R 1 Therefore, T 2 > T 1 .
solution: To solve this problem we will use ohm's law which states that V = I {tex}\times{/tex} R {tex}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{R}}} = \frac{{\text{I}}}{{\text{V}}}{/tex} So, slope of the graph is inversely proportional to resistance. Consider the figure, Since slope of 1 > slope of 2, {tex}\frac{{{{\text{I}}_{\text{1}}}}}{{{{\text{V}}_{\text{1}}}}}> \frac{{{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}}}{{{{\text{V}}_{\text{2}}}}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{{\text{V}}_{\text{2}}}}}{{{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}}}>\frac{{{{\text{V}}_{\text{1}}}}}{{{{\text{I}}_{\text{1}}}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \; R _ { 2 } > R _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\because \frac{ V}{ I} = R{/tex} Also, we know that resistance is directly proportional to the temperature as R 2 > R 1 Therefore, T 2 > T 1 .
solution_html:
To solve this problem we will use ohm's law which states that V = I{tex}\times{/tex}R
{tex}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{R}}} = \frac{{\text{I}}}{{\text{V}}}{/tex}
So, slope of the graph is inversely proportional to resistance. Consider the figure,

Since slope of 1 > slope of 2,
{tex}\frac{{{{\text{I}}_{\text{1}}}}}{{{{\text{V}}_{\text{1}}}}}> \frac{{{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}}}{{{{\text{V}}_{\text{2}}}}}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{{\text{V}}_{\text{2}}}}}{{{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}}}>\frac{{{{\text{V}}_{\text{1}}}}}{{{{\text{I}}_{\text{1}}}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \; R _ { 2 } > R _ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\because \frac{ V}{ I} = R{/tex}
Also, we know that resistance is directly proportional to the temperature as R2 > R1
Therefore, T2 > T1.

question_html:
Graph showing the variation of current versus voltage for a material GaAs is shown in the figure. Identify the region

  1. of negative resistance.
  2. where Ohm's law is obeyed
question_text: Graph showing the variation of current versus voltage for a material GaAs is shown in the figure. Identify the region of negative resistance. where Ohm's law is obeyed
explanation: As Resistance is the voltage divided by current, so region having negative slope will have negative resistance. So, DE is the region of negative resistance because the slope of curve in this part is negative. BC is the region where Ohm's law is obeyed because in this part, the current varies linearly with the voltage.
solution: As Resistance is the voltage divided by current, so region having negative slope will have negative resistance. So, DE is the region of negative resistance because the slope of curve in this part is negative. BC is the region where Ohm's law is obeyed because in this part, the current varies linearly with the voltage.

1. As Resistance is the voltage divided by current, so region having negative slope will have negative resistance. So, DE is the region of negative resistance because the slope of curve in this part is negative.
  2. BC is the region where Ohm's law is obeyed because in this part, the current varies linearly with the voltage.
