question_html: Use Kirchhoff's rules to obtain conditions for the balance conditions in a Wheatstone bridge.
question_text: Use Kirchhoff's rules to obtain conditions for the balance conditions in a Wheatstone bridge.
explanation: Applying Kirchhoff's loop law to close loop ABDA, we get I 1 R - I g G - I 2 P = 0 ...(i) Here, G is the resistance of the galvanometer. Applying Kirchhoff's loop law in the closed loop BDCB,we get {tex}I _ { g } G + \left( I _ { 1 } + I _ { g } \right) S - \left( I _ { 2 } - I _ { g } \right) Q = 0{/tex} .. (ii) When the Wheatstone bridge is balanced, no current flows through the galvanometer, i.e. I g = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} From Eq. (i), we get I 1 R - I 2 P = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 R = I 2 P {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { I _ { 1 } } { I _ { 2 } } = \frac { P } { R }{/tex} .. (iii) Similarly,from Eq. (ii), we get I 1 S - I 2 Q = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 S = I 2 Q = 0..... (iv) From Eqs. (iii) and (iv), we get {tex}\frac { P } { R } = \frac { Q } { S } \Rightarrow \frac { P } { Q } = \frac { R } { S }{/tex} This is the required balance condition in a Wheatstone bridge arrangement and also helps us to determine unknown resistance.
solution: Applying Kirchhoff's loop law to close loop ABDA, we get I 1 R - I g G - I 2 P = 0 ...(i) Here, G is the resistance of the galvanometer. Applying Kirchhoff's loop law in the closed loop BDCB,we get {tex}I _ { g } G + \left( I _ { 1 } + I _ { g } \right) S - \left( I _ { 2 } - I _ { g } \right) Q = 0{/tex} .. (ii) When the Wheatstone bridge is balanced, no current flows through the galvanometer, i.e. I g = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} From Eq. (i), we get I 1 R - I 2 P = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 R = I 2 P {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { I _ { 1 } } { I _ { 2 } } = \frac { P } { R }{/tex} .. (iii) Similarly,from Eq. (ii), we get I 1 S - I 2 Q = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 S = I 2 Q = 0..... (iv) From Eqs. (iii) and (iv), we get {tex}\frac { P } { R } = \frac { Q } { S } \Rightarrow \frac { P } { Q } = \frac { R } { S }{/tex} This is the required balance condition in a Wheatstone bridge arrangement and also helps us to determine unknown resistance.
solution_html:
Applying Kirchhoff's loop law to close loop ABDA, we get
I1R - IgG - I2P = 0 ...(i)

Here, G is the resistance of the galvanometer.
Applying Kirchhoff's loop law in the closed loop BDCB,we get
{tex}I _ { g } G + \left( I _ { 1 } + I _ { g } \right) S - \left( I _ { 2 } - I _ { g } \right) Q = 0{/tex} .. (ii)
When the Wheatstone bridge is balanced, no current flows through the galvanometer,
i.e. Ig = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex}From Eq. (i), we get
I1R - I2P = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I1R = I2P 
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { I _ { 1 } } { I _ { 2 } } = \frac { P } { R }{/tex} .. (iii)
Similarly,from Eq. (ii), we get
I1S - I2Q = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I1S = I2Q = 0..... (iv)
 From Eqs. (iii) and (iv), we get
{tex}\frac { P } { R } = \frac { Q } { S } \Rightarrow \frac { P } { Q } = \frac { R } { S }{/tex}
This is the required balance condition in a Wheatstone bridge arrangement and also helps us to determine unknown resistance.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A cell of emf E and internal resistance r is connected across a variable load resistor R. Draw the plots of the terminal voltage V versus (i) resistance R and (ii) current I.
It is found that when R = 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex}, the current is 1A and when R is increased to {tex}9\Omega{/tex}, the current reduces to 0.5 A. Find the values of the emf E and internal resistance r.
question_text: A cell of emf E and internal resistance r is connected across a variable load resistor R. Draw the plots of the terminal voltage V versus (i) resistance R and (ii) current I. It is found that when R = 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , the current is 1A and when R is increased to {tex}9\Omega{/tex} , the current reduces to 0.5 A. Find the values of the emf E and internal resistance r.
explanation: Internal resistance usually means the electrical resistance inside batteries and power supplies that can limit the potential difference that can be supplied to an external load {tex}\because \quad V = \left( \frac { E } { R + r } \right) R = \frac { E } { 1 + r / R }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} with the increase of R, V increases Graph between terminal voltage(V) and Current (I) When {tex}R = 4 \Omega{/tex} and I = 1A. We know that, terminal voltage, V = E - Ir. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = IR = 4 = E - Ir {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E - r = 4 ....(i) When {tex}R = 9 \Omega{/tex} and I = 0.5A, then V = IR = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 9 = E - 0.5r {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E - 0 5r = 4.5 ...(ii) On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex}r = 1 \Omega{/tex} and E = 5V So from the above calculation, it was found that the internal resistance of the cell is 1 ohm and emf is 5 volt.
solution: Internal resistance usually means the electrical resistance inside batteries and power supplies that can limit the potential difference that can be supplied to an external load {tex}\because \quad V = \left( \frac { E } { R + r } \right) R = \frac { E } { 1 + r / R }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} with the increase of R, V increases Graph between terminal voltage(V) and Current (I) When {tex}R = 4 \Omega{/tex} and I = 1A. We know that, terminal voltage, V = E - Ir. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = IR = 4 = E - Ir {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E - r = 4 ....(i) When {tex}R = 9 \Omega{/tex} and I = 0.5A, then V = IR = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 9 = E - 0.5r {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E - 0 5r = 4.5 ...(ii) On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex}r = 1 \Omega{/tex} and E = 5V So from the above calculation, it was found that the internal resistance of the cell is 1 ohm and emf is 5 volt.
solution_html:
Internal resistance usually means the electrical resistance inside batteries and power supplies that can limit the potential difference that can be supplied to an external load
{tex}\because \quad V = \left( \frac { E } { R + r } \right) R = \frac { E } { 1 + r / R }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} with the increase of R, V increases
Graph between terminal voltage(V) and Current (I)

When {tex}R = 4 \Omega{/tex} and I = 1A.
We know that, terminal voltage, V = E - Ir.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = IR = 4 = E - Ir
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E - r = 4 ....(i)
When {tex}R = 9 \Omega{/tex} and I = 0.5A, then
V = IR = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 9 = E - 0.5r
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E - 0 5r = 4.5 ...(ii)
On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}r = 1 \Omega{/tex} and E = 5V
So from the above calculation, it was found that the internal resistance of the cell is 1 ohm and emf is 5 volt.

subject: Physics
question_html: Find the relation between drift velocity and relaxation time of charge carriers in a conductor. A conductor of length L is connected to a DC source of emf {tex}E'{/tex}. If the length of the conductor is tripled by stretching it, keeping  {tex}E'{/tex}constant, explain how its drift velocity would be affected.
question_text: Find the relation between drift velocity and relaxation time of charge carriers in a conductor. A conductor of length L is connected to a DC source of emf {tex}E'{/tex} . If the length of the conductor is tripled by stretching it, keeping {tex}E'{/tex} constant, explain how its drift velocity would be affected.
explanation: We know that {tex}V_d={a\tau\over2}{/tex} {tex}\frac {qE}{2 m_e} × \tau{/tex} Where E = electric field, a = average acceleration, q = charge of the carrier and m e = effective mass of charge The conductor connected to DC source of emf {tex}E'{/tex} . Suppose, initial length of the conductor, l i = l New length, l f = 3l We know that, Drift velocity, {tex}v _ { d } \propto E{/tex} Thus, {tex}\frac { \left( v _ { d } \right) _ { f } } { \left( v _ { d } \right) _i } = {E_f\over E_i}{/tex} we know that, {tex}E={E'\over L}{/tex} where E= electric field, E' = emf of the battery and L = length of the conductor. {tex}{/tex} Now it is given that a conductor of length L is connected to a D.C source of emf ‘ E ' {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}E_i={E'\over L}{/tex} Also when length of the conductor is tripled by stretching it, keeping ‘ E ' constant, we can write {tex}E_f={E'\over3 L}{/tex} {tex}\frac { \left( v _ { d } \right) _ { f } } { \left( v _ { d } \right) _i } = {E_f\over E_i}={{E'\over3 L}\over{E'\over L}}{/tex} {tex}{\left( v _ { d } \right) _ { f } \over\left( v _ { d } \right) _ { i }} = {1 \over 3}{/tex} Thus, {tex}{\left( v _ { d } \right) _ { f } } ={ {\left( v _ { d } \right) _ { i }} \over 3}{/tex} Thus, drift velocity decreases by three times.
solution: We know that {tex}V_d={a\tau\over2}{/tex} {tex}\frac {qE}{2 m_e} × \tau{/tex} Where E = electric field, a = average acceleration, q = charge of the carrier and m e = effective mass of charge The conductor connected to DC source of emf {tex}E'{/tex} . Suppose, initial length of the conductor, l i = l New length, l f = 3l We know that, Drift velocity, {tex}v _ { d } \propto E{/tex} Thus, {tex}\frac { \left( v _ { d } \right) _ { f } } { \left( v _ { d } \right) _i } = {E_f\over E_i}{/tex} we know that, {tex}E={E'\over L}{/tex} where E= electric field, E' = emf of the battery and L = length of the conductor. {tex}{/tex} Now it is given that a conductor of length L is connected to a D.C source of emf ‘ E ' {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}E_i={E'\over L}{/tex} Also when length of the conductor is tripled by stretching it, keeping ‘ E ' constant, we can write {tex}E_f={E'\over3 L}{/tex} {tex}\frac { \left( v _ { d } \right) _ { f } } { \left( v _ { d } \right) _i } = {E_f\over E_i}={{E'\over3 L}\over{E'\over L}}{/tex} {tex}{\left( v _ { d } \right) _ { f } \over\left( v _ { d } \right) _ { i }} = {1 \over 3}{/tex} Thus, {tex}{\left( v _ { d } \right) _ { f } } ={ {\left( v _ { d } \right) _ { i }} \over 3}{/tex} Thus, drift velocity decreases by three times.
solution_html:
We know that 
{tex}V_d={a\tau\over2}{/tex} {tex}\frac {qE}{2 m_e} × \tau{/tex}
Where E =  electric field, a  = average acceleration, q = charge of the carrier and me = effective mass of charge 
The conductor connected to DC source of emf {tex}E'{/tex}.
Suppose, initial length of the conductor, li = l
New length, lf = 3l
We know that,
Drift velocity, {tex}v _ { d } \propto E{/tex} 
Thus,
{tex}\frac { \left( v _ { d } \right) _ { f } } { \left( v _ { d } \right) _i } = {E_f\over E_i}{/tex}
we know that,
{tex}E={E'\over L}{/tex}
where E= electric field, E' = emf of the battery and L = length of the conductor.
{tex}{/tex}Now it is given that a conductor of length L is connected to a D.C source of emf ‘ E '{tex}\therefore{/tex}
{tex}E_i={E'\over L}{/tex}
Also when length of the conductor is tripled by stretching it, keeping ‘ E ' constant, we can write
{tex}E_f={E'\over3 L}{/tex}
{tex}\frac { \left( v _ { d } \right) _ { f } } { \left( v _ { d } \right) _i } = {E_f\over E_i}={{E'\over3 L}\over{E'\over L}}{/tex}
{tex}{\left( v _ { d } \right) _ { f } \over\left( v _ { d } \right) _ { i }} = {1 \over 3}{/tex}
Thus, {tex}{\left( v _ { d } \right) _ { f } } ={ {\left( v _ { d } \right) _ { i }} \over 3}{/tex}
Thus, drift velocity decreases by three times.

question_html: In the two electric circuits shown in the figures, determine the readings of ideal ammeter (A) and the ideal voltmeter (V).
question_text: In the two electric circuits shown in the figures, determine the readings of ideal ammeter (A) and the ideal voltmeter (V).
explanation: In circuit (a) Total emf = 15V Total Resistance = 2 ohm Current i = {tex}\left(\frac{15}{2}\right) A=7.5 A{/tex} Potential difference between the terminals of 6V battery, V = E - iR = 6 - (7.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1) = -1.5 volt In CIrcuit (b) Effective emf = 9 - 6 = 3V Current i = {tex}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) A=1.5 A{/tex} Potential Difference across 6V cell, V = E + iR = 6 + 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 7.5 volt
solution: In circuit (a) Total emf = 15V Total Resistance = 2 ohm Current i = {tex}\left(\frac{15}{2}\right) A=7.5 A{/tex} Potential difference between the terminals of 6V battery, V = E - iR = 6 - (7.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1) = -1.5 volt In CIrcuit (b) Effective emf = 9 - 6 = 3V Current i = {tex}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) A=1.5 A{/tex} Potential Difference across 6V cell, V = E + iR = 6 + 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 7.5 volt
solution_html:
In circuit (a)
Total emf = 15V
Total Resistance = 2 ohm
Current i = {tex}\left(\frac{15}{2}\right) A=7.5 A{/tex}
Potential difference between the terminals of 6V battery,
V = E - iR
= 6 - (7.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1) = -1.5 volt

In CIrcuit (b)
Effective emf = 9 - 6 = 3V
Current i = {tex}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) A=1.5 A{/tex}
Potential Difference across 6V cell,
V = E + iR
 = 6 + 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1
= 7.5 volt

question_html: Use Kirchhoff's rules to determine the potential difference between the points A and D. When no current flows in the arm BE of the electric network shown in the figure below:
question_text: Use Kirchhoff's rules to determine the potential difference between the points A and D. When no current flows in the arm BE of the electric network shown in the figure below:
explanation: As no current flows in arm BE so the potential difference across R 1 ​ will be zero. Now applying Kirchhoff's law for loop AFEBA, 6 + 1 + 3 = 2i + 3i or i = 2A The potential difference across A and D along AFD, V A - 2i + 1 - 3i = V D ​ or V A - V D = 5i - 1 = 5(2) - 1 = 9V
solution: As no current flows in arm BE so the potential difference across R 1 ​ will be zero. Now applying Kirchhoff's law for loop AFEBA, 6 + 1 + 3 = 2i + 3i or i = 2A The potential difference across A and D along AFD, V A - 2i + 1 - 3i = V D ​ or V A - V D = 5i - 1 = 5(2) - 1 = 9V
solution_html:
As no current flows in arm BE so the potential difference across R1​ will be zero.
Now applying Kirchhoff's law for loop AFEBA, 6 + 1 + 3 = 2i + 3i or i = 2A
The potential difference across A and D along AFD, VA - 2i + 1 - 3i = VD​
or VA - VD = 5i - 1 = 5(2) -  1 = 9V

question_html:
1. Deduce the relation between current I flowing through a conductor and drift velocity vd of the electrons.
  2. Figure shows a plot of current I flowing through the cross-section of a wire versus the time t. Use the plot to find the charge flowing in 10 through the wire.
question_text: Deduce the relation between current I flowing through a conductor and drift velocity v d of the electrons. Figure shows a plot of current I flowing through the cross-section of a wire versus the time t. Use the plot to find the charge flowing in 10 through the wire.
explanation: The average velocity with which an electron moves through a conductor when potential difference is applied across it, is called drift velocity. let n = number density of electrons in the conductor. Then total charge per unit volume is Q = ne Total number of free electrons in the conductor N = n {tex}\times{/tex} volume of conductor PQ or N = nAl , l is length of the conductor and A is it's cross-sectional Area Time t in which an electron moves from P to Q, all N free electrons pass through cross-section Q. t = l/v d where, v d is the drift velocity of electrons in the conductor. So, electric current flowing through conductor is given by {tex}I = \frac { q } { t } = \frac { N e } { t } = \frac { n A l e } { l / v _ { d } } \Rightarrow I = n e A v _ { d }{/tex} This gives the relation between electric current and drift velocity. Area under I-t curve on t-axis is charge flowing through the conductor {tex}Q = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 5 \times 5 + ( 5 \times 5 )= 37.5 \mathrm { C }{/tex}
solution: The average velocity with which an electron moves through a conductor when potential difference is applied across it, is called drift velocity. let n = number density of electrons in the conductor. Then total charge per unit volume is Q = ne Total number of free electrons in the conductor N = n {tex}\times{/tex} volume of conductor PQ or N = nAl , l is length of the conductor and A is it's cross-sectional Area Time t in which an electron moves from P to Q, all N free electrons pass through cross-section Q. t = l/v d where, v d is the drift velocity of electrons in the conductor. So, electric current flowing through conductor is given by {tex}I = \frac { q } { t } = \frac { N e } { t } = \frac { n A l e } { l / v _ { d } } \Rightarrow I = n e A v _ { d }{/tex} This gives the relation between electric current and drift velocity. Area under I-t curve on t-axis is charge flowing through the conductor {tex}Q = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 5 \times 5 + ( 5 \times 5 )= 37.5 \mathrm { C }{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The average velocity with which an electron moves through a conductor when potential difference is applied across it, is called drift velocity. let n = number density of electrons in the conductor. Then total charge per unit volume is Q = ne 
    Total number of free electrons in the conductor 
    N = n {tex}\times{/tex} volume of conductor PQ
    or N = nAl , l is length of the conductor and A is it's cross-sectional Area

    Time t in which an electron moves from P to Q, all N free electrons pass through cross-section Q.
    t = l/vd
    where, vd is the drift velocity of electrons in the conductor. So, electric current flowing through conductor is given by
    {tex}I = \frac { q } { t } = \frac { N e } { t } = \frac { n A l e } { l / v _ { d } } \Rightarrow I = n e A v _ { d }{/tex}
    This gives the relation between electric current and drift velocity.
     

  2. Area under I-t curve on t-axis is charge flowing through the conductor
    {tex}Q = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 5 \times 5 + ( 5 \times 5 )= 37.5 \mathrm { C }{/tex}

question_html:
A cell of emf 'E' and internal resistance 'r' is connected across a variable load resistor R. Draw the plots of the terminal voltage V versus (i) R and (ii) the current I.
It is found that when R = 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex}, the current is 1 A when R is increased to 9 {tex}\Omega{/tex}, the current reduces to 0.5 A. Find the values of the emf E and internal resistance r.
question_text: A cell of emf 'E' and internal resistance 'r' is connected across a variable load resistor R. Draw the plots of the terminal voltage V versus (i) R and (ii) the current I. It is found that when R = 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , the current is 1 A when R is increased to 9 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , the current reduces to 0.5 A. Find the values of the emf E and internal resistance r.
explanation: {tex}I=\frac{E}{R+r}{/tex} (V=E-Ir and V=IR) {tex}I=\frac{E}{4+r}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E = 4 + r ...(i) 0.5 = {tex}\frac{E}{9+r}{/tex} Also E = 4.5 + 0.5r ...(ii) From equation (i) and (ii), 4 + r = 4.5 + 0.5r {tex}\therefore{/tex} r = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} (internal resistance) Using this value of r, we get, E = 5V
solution: {tex}I=\frac{E}{R+r}{/tex} (V=E-Ir and V=IR) {tex}I=\frac{E}{4+r}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E = 4 + r ...(i) 0.5 = {tex}\frac{E}{9+r}{/tex} Also E = 4.5 + 0.5r ...(ii) From equation (i) and (ii), 4 + r = 4.5 + 0.5r {tex}\therefore{/tex} r = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} (internal resistance) Using this value of r, we get, E = 5V
solution_html:
2. {tex}I=\frac{E}{R+r}{/tex}(V=E-Ir and V=IR)
    {tex}I=\frac{E}{4+r}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E = 4 + r ...(i)
    0.5 = {tex}\frac{E}{9+r}{/tex}
    Also E = 4.5 + 0.5r ...(ii)
    From equation (i) and (ii),
    4 + r = 4.5 + 0.5r
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} r = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} (internal resistance)
    Using this value of r, we get,
    E = 5V

question_html: Two cells of E.M.F. 10 V and 2 V and internal resistances 10 Q and 5 Q respectively, are connected in parallel as shown. Find the effective voltage across R.
question_text: Two cells of E.M.F. 10 V and 2 V and internal resistances 10 Q and 5 Q respectively, are connected in parallel as shown. Find the effective voltage across R.
explanation: {tex}E{{eq}}=\left(\frac{E_{1}}{r_{1}}+\frac{E_{2}}{r_{2}}\right) \div \left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} (polarity of the emf are opposite) {tex}E_{e q}{/tex} = {tex}(\frac{10}{10} - \frac{2}{5}) \div (\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{5}){/tex} {tex}E_{e q}{/tex} = 2 V thus equivalent voltage=2V
solution: {tex}E{{eq}}=\left(\frac{E_{1}}{r_{1}}+\frac{E_{2}}{r_{2}}\right) \div \left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} (polarity of the emf are opposite) {tex}E_{e q}{/tex} = {tex}(\frac{10}{10} - \frac{2}{5}) \div (\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{5}){/tex} {tex}E_{e q}{/tex} = 2 V thus equivalent voltage=2V
solution_html:
{tex}E{{eq}}=\left(\frac{E_{1}}{r_{1}}+\frac{E_{2}}{r_{2}}\right) \div \left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} (polarity of the emf are opposite)
{tex}E_{e q}{/tex} = {tex}(\frac{10}{10} - \frac{2}{5}) \div (\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{5}){/tex}
{tex}E_{e q}{/tex} = 2 V  thus equivalent voltage=2V

question_html: V-I graph for a metallic wire at two different temperatures T1 and T2 is as shown in the Figure. Which of the two temperatures T1 and T2 is higher and why?
question_text: V-I graph for a metallic wire at two different temperatures T 1 and T 2 is as shown in the Figure. Which of the two temperatures T 1 and T 2 is higher and why?
explanation: If the same potential difference V is applied, current equal to I 1 and I 2 will flow through the wire, when its temperature is kept at T 1 and T 2 respectively. Since R = {tex}\frac{V}{I}{/tex} , it follows that resistance of the wire is more at temperature T 2 . Further, as in case of metals, the resistance increases with rise of temperature, T 2 > T 1 .
solution: If the same potential difference V is applied, current equal to I 1 and I 2 will flow through the wire, when its temperature is kept at T 1 and T 2 respectively. Since R = {tex}\frac{V}{I}{/tex} , it follows that resistance of the wire is more at temperature T 2 . Further, as in case of metals, the resistance increases with rise of temperature, T 2 > T 1 .

Since R ={tex}\frac{V}{I}{/tex}, it follows that resistance of the wire is more at temperature T2 . Further, as in case of metals, the resistance increases with rise of temperature, T2 > T1.

question_html: Across a metallic conductor of non-uniform cross-section, a constant potential difference is applied. The quantity which remains constant along the conductor is:
question_text: Across a metallic conductor of non-uniform cross-section, a constant potential difference is applied. The quantity which remains constant along the conductor is:

options.explanation: A conductor of non-uniform cross-section is a series combination of many conductors of different cross-sections. So I through them remains constant. {tex}v_{d}=\frac{I}{e n A} \Rightarrow v_{d} \propto \frac{1}{A}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{I}{\sigma A} \Rightarrow E \propto \frac{1}{A}{/tex} {tex}j=\frac{I}{A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad j \propto \frac{1}{A}{/tex} Thus v d , E, and j all change with A.

explanation: A conductor of non-uniform cross-section is a series combination of many conductors of different cross-sections. So I through them remains constant. {tex}v_{d}=\frac{I}{e n A} \Rightarrow v_{d} \propto \frac{1}{A}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{I}{\sigma A} \Rightarrow E \propto \frac{1}{A}{/tex} {tex}j=\frac{I}{A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad j \propto \frac{1}{A}{/tex} Thus v d , E, and j all change with A.

question_html: In the circuit diagram given in Figure, the cells E1 and E2 have emfs 4 V and 8 V and internal resistances 0.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 1.0 {tex}\Omega{/tex} respectively. Calculate the current in each resistance.
question_text: In the circuit diagram given in Figure, the cells E 1 and E 2 have emfs 4 V and 8 V and internal resistances 0.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 1.0 {tex}\Omega{/tex} respectively. Calculate the current in each resistance.
explanation: Effective emf of the circuit = {tex}\varepsilon_{2}-\varepsilon_{1}{/tex} = 8 - 4 = 4 V Total resistance of the circuit = 1 + 0.5 + {tex}\Omega+\frac{3 \ \times \ 6}{3 \ + \ 6}=8 \Omega{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current in the circuit, I = {tex}\frac{4}{8}{/tex} = 0.5 A Current through 4.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance = I = 0.5 A P.D. across the parallel combination of 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistances is V = {tex}R^{\prime} I=\frac{3 \ \times \ 6}{3 \ + \ 6} \times 0.5{/tex} = 1 V Current through 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance = {tex}\frac{1 \mathrm{~V}}{3 \Omega}=\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} Current through 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance = {tex}\frac{1 \mathrm{~V}}{6 \Omega}=\frac{1}{6} \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
solution: Effective emf of the circuit = {tex}\varepsilon_{2}-\varepsilon_{1}{/tex} = 8 - 4 = 4 V Total resistance of the circuit = 1 + 0.5 + {tex}\Omega+\frac{3 \ \times \ 6}{3 \ + \ 6}=8 \Omega{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current in the circuit, I = {tex}\frac{4}{8}{/tex} = 0.5 A Current through 4.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance = I = 0.5 A P.D. across the parallel combination of 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistances is V = {tex}R^{\prime} I=\frac{3 \ \times \ 6}{3 \ + \ 6} \times 0.5{/tex} = 1 V Current through 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance = {tex}\frac{1 \mathrm{~V}}{3 \Omega}=\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} Current through 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance = {tex}\frac{1 \mathrm{~V}}{6 \Omega}=\frac{1}{6} \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
solution_html:
Effective emf of the circuit = {tex}\varepsilon_{2}-\varepsilon_{1}{/tex} = 8 - 4 = 4 V
Total resistance of the circuit
= 1 + 0.5 + {tex}\Omega+\frac{3 \ \times \ 6}{3 \ + \ 6}=8 \Omega{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Current in the circuit, I = {tex}\frac{4}{8}{/tex} = 0.5 A
Current through 4.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance = I = 0.5 A
P.D. across the parallel combination of 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistances is
V = {tex}R^{\prime} I=\frac{3 \ \times \ 6}{3 \ + \ 6} \times 0.5{/tex} = 1 V
Current through 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance = {tex}\frac{1 \mathrm{~V}}{3 \Omega}=\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
Current through 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance = {tex}\frac{1 \mathrm{~V}}{6 \Omega}=\frac{1}{6} \mathrm{~A}{/tex}

question_text: A conductor of length l is connected to a d.c. source of emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} . If the length of the conductor is tripled by stretching it, keeping {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} constant, explain how its drift velocity would be affected.
explanation: E = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{l}{/tex} and v d = {tex}\frac{e E \tau}{m}{/tex} For given emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} , the field E becomes {tex}\frac 13{/tex} rd when the length is tripled. {tex}\therefore{/tex} New drift velocity, {tex}v_{d}^{\prime}=\frac{v_{d}}{3}{/tex}

E = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{l}{/tex} and vd = {tex}\frac{e E \tau}{m}{/tex}
For given emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex}, the field E becomes {tex}\frac 13{/tex}rd when the length is tripled.

question_html: Use Kirchhoff's rules to obtain conditions for the balance condition in a Wheatstone bridge.
question_text: Use Kirchhoff's rules to obtain conditions for the balance condition in a Wheatstone bridge.
explanation: Applying Kirchhoff's loop rule to closed loop ADBA, -I 1 R 1 + I g R G + I 2 R 2 = 0 (I g = 0) ...(i) For loop CRDC, -(I 2 - l g )R 4 + I g R G + (I 1 + I g )R 3 = 0 ...(ii) From equation (i), {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}{/tex} From equation (ii), {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_4}{R_3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{R_1}{R_2}=\frac{R_3}{R_4}{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{S}{/tex}
solution: Applying Kirchhoff's loop rule to closed loop ADBA, -I 1 R 1 + I g R G + I 2 R 2 = 0 (I g = 0) ...(i) For loop CRDC, -(I 2 - l g )R 4 + I g R G + (I 1 + I g )R 3 = 0 ...(ii) From equation (i), {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}{/tex} From equation (ii), {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_4}{R_3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{R_1}{R_2}=\frac{R_3}{R_4}{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{S}{/tex}
solution_html:
Applying Kirchhoff's loop rule to closed loop ADBA,
-I1R1 + IgRG + I2R2 = 0 (Ig = 0) ...(i)

For loop CRDC,
-(I2 - lg)R4 + IgRG + (I1 + Ig)R3 = 0 ...(ii)
From equation (i),
{tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}{/tex}
From equation (ii),
{tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_4}{R_3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{R_1}{R_2}=\frac{R_3}{R_4}{/tex}
or, {tex}\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{S}{/tex}

question_html:
Plot a graph showing the variation of current density (j) versus the electric field (E) for two conductors of different materials. What information from this plot regarding the properties of the conducting material, can be obtained which can be used to select suitable materials for use in making

  1. standard resistance and
  2. connecting wires in electric circuits?

Electron drift speed is estimated to be of the order of mm s-1. Yet a large current of the order of few amperes can be set-up in the wire. Explain briefly.
question_text: Plot a graph showing the variation of current density (j) versus the electric field (E) for two conductors of different materials. question_html: Define the term drift velocity of charge carriers in a conductor and write its relationship with the current flowing through it.
question_text: Define the term drift velocity of charge carriers in a conductor and write its relationship with the current flowing through it.
explanation: The drift velocity is the average velocity that a particle, such as an electron, attains in a material due to an electric field. It can also be referred to as axial drift velocity. Its relationship is expressed as {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { I } { n e A }{/tex} where 'I' is the current flowing through the conductor, n is the concentration of free electrons, e is the electronic charge and A is the cross-sectional area.

The drift velocity is the average velocity that a particle, such as an electron, attains in a material due to an electric field. It can also be referred to as axial drift velocity.
Its relationship is expressed as
{tex}v _ { d } = \frac { I } { n e A }{/tex}
where 'I' is the current flowing through the conductor, n is the concentration of free electrons, e is the electronic charge and A is the cross-sectional area.

question_html: Derive an expression for the current density of a conductor in terms of the drift speed of electrons.
question_text: Derive an expression for the current density of a conductor in terms of the drift speed of electrons.
explanation: Let potential difference V is applied across a conductor of length I and hence an electric field E produced inside the conductor. {tex}\therefore \quad E = \frac { V } { l }{/tex} ....(i) Let n = number density of free electrons A = cross-sectional area of conductor e = electrons charge. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of free electrons present in length l of conductor = nAI {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total charge contained in length l which can contribute in current, q = (nAl)e ... (ii) The time taken by free electron to cross the length l of conductor is t = l/v d where, v d = drift speed of electron. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current through the conductor [from Eqs. (i) and (ii)] I = q/t {tex}I = \frac { ( n A l ) e } { t } = \frac { ( n A l ) e } { \left( l/v _ { d } \right) } = n e A v _ { d }{/tex} Current density J = {tex}\frac{I}{A}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{neAv_d}{A}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad J = ne v _ { d } , \text { i.e. } J \propto v _ { d }{/tex} Thus, current density of conductor is proportional to drift speed of electrons.
solution: Let potential difference V is applied across a conductor of length I and hence an electric field E produced inside the conductor. {tex}\therefore \quad E = \frac { V } { l }{/tex} ....(i) Let n = number density of free electrons A = cross-sectional area of conductor e = electrons charge. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of free electrons present in length l of conductor = nAI {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total charge contained in length l which can contribute in current, q = (nAl)e ... (ii) The time taken by free electron to cross the length l of conductor is t = l/v d where, v d = drift speed of electron. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current through the conductor [from Eqs. (i) and (ii)] I = q/t {tex}I = \frac { ( n A l ) e } { t } = \frac { ( n A l ) e } { \left( l/v _ { d } \right) } = n e A v _ { d }{/tex} Current density J = {tex}\frac{I}{A}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{neAv_d}{A}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad J = ne v _ { d } , \text { i.e. } J \propto v _ { d }{/tex} Thus, current density of conductor is proportional to drift speed of electrons.
solution_html:
Let potential difference V is applied across a conductor of length I and hence an electric field E produced inside the conductor.
{tex}\therefore \quad E = \frac { V } { l }{/tex} ....(i)

Let n = number density of free electrons
A = cross-sectional area of conductor
e = electrons charge.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of free electrons present in length l of conductor = nAI
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total charge contained in length l which can contribute in current,
q = (nAl)e ... (ii)
The time taken by free electron to cross the length l of conductor is
t = l/vd
where, vd = drift speed of electron.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Current through the conductor [from Eqs. (i) and (ii)]
I = q/t
{tex}I = \frac { ( n A l ) e } { t } = \frac { ( n A l ) e } { \left( l/v _ { d } \right) } = n e A v _ { d }{/tex}
Current density J = {tex}\frac{I}{A}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{neAv_d}{A}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad J = ne v _ { d } , \text { i.e. } J \propto v _ { d }{/tex}
Thus, current density of conductor is proportional to drift speed of electrons.

question_html: How does the random motion of free electrons in a conductor get affected when a potential difference is applied across its ends?
question_text: How does the random motion of free electrons in a conductor get affected when a potential difference is applied across its ends?
explanation: Conductors contain free electrons. In the absence of any external electric field, the free electrons are in random motion just like the molecules of gas in a container and the net current through wire is zero. If the ends of the wire are connected to a battery, an electric field (E) will setup at every point within the wire. Due to the electric effect of the battery, the electrons will experience a force in the direction opposite to E and move against the direction of applied Electric field along with their zig zag motion as shown in figure.

question_html: A cell of emf E and internal resistance r is connected across a variable resistor R. Plot a graph showing variation of terminal voltage V of the cell versus the current I. Using the plot, show the emf of the cell and its internal resistance can be determined.
question_text: A cell of emf E and internal resistance r is connected across a variable resistor R. Plot a graph showing variation of terminal voltage V of the cell versus the current I. Using the plot, show the emf of the cell and its internal resistance can be determined.
explanation: Internal resistance usually means the electrical resistance inside batteries and power supplies that can limit the potential difference that can be supplied to an external load. We know that, V = E - Ir The plot between V and I is a straight line of positive intercept and negative slope as shown in figure below. The value of potential difference corresponding to zero current gives emf of the cell. Maximum current is drawn when terminal voltage is zero, so V = E - Ir {tex} \Rightarrow 0 = E - {I_{\max }}r \Rightarrow r = \frac{E}{{{I_{\max }}}}{/tex} Internal resistances within power supplies are normally constant and independent of use unless the power supply gets hot as a result of short circuits or low resistance loads. In that case, the internal resistance is likely to increase slightly.

In that case, the internal resistance is likely to increase slightly.

question_html: In the electric network shown in the figure, use Kirchhoff's rules to calculate the power consumed by the resistance R = {tex} 4\Omega{/tex}.
question_text: In the electric network shown in the figure, use Kirchhoff's rules to calculate the power consumed by the resistance R = {tex} 4\Omega{/tex} .
explanation: In closed mesh ABCDA, 2I 1 + 4(I 1 + I 2 ) = 12 6I 1 + 4I 2 = 12 3I 1 + 2I 2 = 6 ...(i) In closed mesh BCFEB, (I 1 + I 2 )4 = 6 2I 1 + 2I 2 = 3 ...(ii) On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get, I 1 = 3A {tex}\therefore{/tex} Putting the value of I 1 in Eq. (i), we get 3 {tex}\times {/tex} 3 + 2I 2 = 6 I 2 = -1.5 A Net current in arm BC = I 1 + I 2 = 3 - 1.5 = +1.5A {tex}\therefore{/tex} Power consumed by 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance {tex}= I ^ { 2 } R = ( 1.5 ) ^ { 2 } \times 4 = 2.25 \times 4 = 9 \mathrm { W }{/tex} So, power consumed in the circuit is 9 watt.
solution: In closed mesh ABCDA, 2I 1 + 4(I 1 + I 2 ) = 12 6I 1 + 4I 2 = 12 3I 1 + 2I 2 = 6 ...(i) In closed mesh BCFEB, (I 1 + I 2 )4 = 6 2I 1 + 2I 2 = 3 ...(ii) On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get, I 1 = 3A {tex}\therefore{/tex} Putting the value of I 1 in Eq. (i), we get 3 {tex}\times {/tex} 3 + 2I 2 = 6 I 2 = -1.5 A Net current in arm BC = I 1 + I 2 = 3 - 1.5 = +1.5A {tex}\therefore{/tex} Power consumed by 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance {tex}= I ^ { 2 } R = ( 1.5 ) ^ { 2 } \times 4 = 2.25 \times 4 = 9 \mathrm { W }{/tex} So, power consumed in the circuit is 9 watt.
solution_html:
In closed mesh ABCDA,
2I1 + 4(I1 + I2) = 12
6I1 + 4I2 = 12
3I1 + 2I2 = 6 ...(i)
In closed mesh BCFEB, 
(I1 + I2)4 = 6
2I1 + 2I2 = 3 ...(ii)
On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get, I1 = 3A
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Putting the value of I1 in Eq. (i), we get
3 {tex}\times {/tex} 3 + 2I2 = 6
I2 = -1.5 A
Net current in arm BC
= I1 + I2 = 3 - 1.5 = +1.5A
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Power consumed by 4{tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance
{tex}= I ^ { 2 } R = ( 1.5 ) ^ { 2 } \times 4 = 2.25 \times 4 = 9 \mathrm { W }{/tex}
So, power consumed in the circuit is 9 watt.

subject: Physics
question_html: Draw a plot showing the variation of resistivity of a (i) conductor and (ii) semiconductor, with the increase in temperature. How does one explain this behaviour in terms of number density of charge carriers and the relaxation time?
question_text: Draw a plot showing the variation of resistivity of a (i) conductor and (ii) semiconductor, with the increase in temperature. How does one explain this behaviour in terms of number density of charge carriers and the relaxation time?
explanation: For conductor For semiconductor Relaxation time is the time between two successive collisions of an electron with atoms/ions of the conductor. The relation between resistivity and relaxation time is given by: {tex}\rho = \frac { m } { n e ^ { 2 } \tau }{/tex} In conductors, average relaxation time decreases with increase in temperature due to which resistivity increases with increase in temperature. In semiconductors, the increase in number density (with increase in temperature) is more than the decrease in relaxation time, the net result is therefore a decrease in resistivity with increase in temperature.
solution: For conductor For semiconductor Relaxation time is the time between two successive collisions of an electron with atoms/ions of the conductor. The relation between resistivity and relaxation time is given by: {tex}\rho = \frac { m } { n e ^ { 2 } \tau }{/tex} In conductors, average relaxation time decreases with increase in temperature due to which resistivity increases with increase in temperature. In semiconductors, the increase in number density (with increase in temperature) is more than the decrease in relaxation time, the net result is therefore a decrease in resistivity with increase in temperature.

1. For conductor
  2. For semiconductor

Relaxation time is the time between two successive collisions of an electron with atoms/ions of the conductor.
The relation between resistivity and relaxation time is given by:
{tex}\rho = \frac { m } { n e ^ { 2 } \tau }{/tex}
In conductors, average relaxation time decreases with increase in temperature due to which resistivity increases with increase in temperature.
In semiconductors, the increase in number density (with increase in temperature) is more than the decrease in relaxation time, the net result is therefore a decrease in resistivity with increase in temperature.

Drift velocity, vd = ?
We know that, I = neAvd
where
I = current
n = number of free electrons per unit volume or electron density
A = cross-sectional area of the conductor
e = charge of an electron
{tex}\Rightarrow v _ { d } = \frac { I } { n e A } {/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1.8 } { 9 \times 10 ^ { 28 } \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } \times 2.5 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } }{/tex}
= 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 m/s

question_html: Write the expression for the drift velocity of charge carriers in a conductor of length l across which a potential difference V is applied.
question_text: Write the expression for the drift velocity of charge carriers in a conductor of length l across which a potential difference V is applied.
explanation: Since drift velocity, {tex}v_{d}=\frac{-e E}{m} \tau{/tex} ...(i) But, {tex}E=\frac{V}{l}{/tex} ...(ii) Now, from equation (i) and (ii), Drift velocity, {tex}v_{d}=\frac{-e V}{m l} \tau{/tex} Drift velocity is directly proportional to relaxation time.
solution: Since drift velocity, {tex}v_{d}=\frac{-e E}{m} \tau{/tex} ...(i) But, {tex}E=\frac{V}{l}{/tex} ...(ii) Now, from equation (i) and (ii), Drift velocity, {tex}v_{d}=\frac{-e V}{m l} \tau{/tex} Drift velocity is directly proportional to relaxation time.
solution_html:
Since drift velocity, {tex}v_{d}=\frac{-e E}{m} \tau{/tex} ...(i)
But, {tex}E=\frac{V}{l}{/tex} ...(ii)
Now, from equation (i) and (ii),

Drift velocity, {tex}v_{d}=\frac{-e V}{m l} \tau{/tex}

Drift velocity is directly proportional to relaxation time.

question_html: Define ionic mobility. Write its relationship with relaxation time. Give its SI unit. How does one understand the temperature dependence of resistivity of a semiconductor.
question_text: Define ionic mobility. Write its relationship with relaxation time. Give its SI unit. How does one understand the temperature dependence of resistivity of a semiconductor.
explanation: Mobility of an ion is defined as the drift velocity per unit electric field i.e. {tex}\mu = \frac {vd}E = \frac {e\tau}m{/tex} Its unit is m 2 /Vs. When temperature increases covalent bonds of neighbouring atoms break and charge carrier become free to cause conductive so resistivity of semiconductor decreases with rise of temperature.
solution: Mobility of an ion is defined as the drift velocity per unit electric field i.e. {tex}\mu = \frac {vd}E = \frac {e\tau}m{/tex} Its unit is m 2 /Vs. When temperature increases covalent bonds of neighbouring atoms break and charge carrier become free to cause conductive so resistivity of semiconductor decreases with rise of temperature.

Mobility of an ion is defined as the drift velocity per unit electric field i.e.
{tex}\mu = \frac {vd}E = \frac {e\tau}m{/tex}
Its unit is m2 /Vs.
When temperature increases covalent bonds of neighbouring atoms break and charge carrier become free to cause conductive so resistivity of semiconductor decreases with rise of temperature.

subject: Physics
question_html: Define mobility of a charge carrier. Write the relation expressing mobility in terms of relaxation time. Give its SI unit.
question_text: Define mobility of a charge carrier. Write the relation expressing mobility in terms of relaxation time. Give its SI unit.
explanation: Mobility is formally defined as the value of the drift velocity per unit of electric field strength; thus, the faster the particle moves at a given electric field strength, the larger the mobility . Mobility is generally denoted by {tex}\mu{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \mu = \frac{v _ { d }}{E}{/tex} ...(i) The SI unit of mobility is m 2 V -1 s -1. Drift velocity in term of relaxation time, {tex} { v } _ { d } = \frac { - e { E } } { m } \tau{/tex} In magnitude, {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { e E } { m } \tau{/tex} or {tex}\frac { v _ { d } } { E } = \frac { e \tau } { m }{/tex} {tex}\mu = \frac { e \tau } { m }{/tex} [From Eq. (i)] The faster the particle moves at a given electric field strength, the larger the mobility. The mobility of a particular type of particle in a given solid may vary with temperature.
solution: Mobility is formally defined as the value of the drift velocity per unit of electric field strength; thus, the faster the particle moves at a given electric field strength, the larger the mobility . Mobility is generally denoted by {tex}\mu{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \mu = \frac{v _ { d }}{E}{/tex} ...(i) The SI unit of mobility is m 2 V -1 s -1. Drift velocity in term of relaxation time, {tex} { v } _ { d } = \frac { - e { E } } { m } \tau{/tex} In magnitude, {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { e E } { m } \tau{/tex} or {tex}\frac { v _ { d } } { E } = \frac { e \tau } { m }{/tex} {tex}\mu = \frac { e \tau } { m }{/tex} [From Eq. (i)] The faster the particle moves at a given electric field strength, the larger the mobility. The mobility of a particular type of particle in a given solid may vary with temperature.
solution_html:
Mobility is formally defined as the value of the drift velocity per unit of electric field strength; thus, the faster the particle moves at a given electric field strength, the larger the mobility. Mobility is generally denoted by {tex}\mu{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore \mu = \frac{v _ { d }}{E}{/tex}  ...(i)
The SI unit of mobility is m2 V-1 s-1.
Drift velocity in term of relaxation time,
{tex} { v } _ { d } = \frac { - e { E } } { m } \tau{/tex}
In magnitude, {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { e E } { m } \tau{/tex} or {tex}\frac { v _ { d } } { E } = \frac { e \tau } { m }{/tex}
{tex}\mu = \frac { e \tau } { m }{/tex} [From Eq. (i)]
The faster the particle moves at a given electric field strength, the larger the mobility. The mobility of a particular type of particle in a given solid may vary with temperature.

subject: Physics
question_html: Write a relation between current and drift velocity of electrons in a conductor. Use this relation to explain how the resistance of a conductor changes with the rise in temperature?
question_text: Write a relation between current and drift velocity of electrons in a conductor. Use this relation to explain how the resistance of a conductor changes with the rise in temperature?
explanation: Relation between current and drift velocity of electrons in a conductor is given by {tex}I = A n e v _ { d } \Rightarrow V / R = A n e v _ { d }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad v _ { d } \propto \frac { 1 } { R }{/tex} where, I = current, A = area of conductor, n = number density of electrons and v d = drift velocity. With the increase in temperature of a metallic conductor, resistance increases and hence drift velocity decreases.
solution: Relation between current and drift velocity of electrons in a conductor is given by {tex}I = A n e v _ { d } \Rightarrow V / R = A n e v _ { d }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad v _ { d } \propto \frac { 1 } { R }{/tex} where, I = current, A = area of conductor, n = number density of electrons and v d = drift velocity. With the increase in temperature of a metallic conductor, resistance increases and hence drift velocity decreases.
solution_html:
Relation between current and drift velocity of electrons in a conductor is given by
{tex}I = A n e v _ { d } \Rightarrow V / R = A n e v _ { d }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad v _ { d } \propto \frac { 1 } { R }{/tex}
where, I = current, A = area of conductor, n = number density of electrons and vd = drift velocity.
With the increase in temperature of a metallic conductor, resistance increases and hence drift velocity decreases.

V = E - Ir

E = EMF of cell
r = internal resistance of cell

question_html: Two materials Si and Cu, are cooled from 300 K to 60 K. What will be the effect on their resistivity?
question_text: Two materials Si and Cu, are cooled from 300 K to 60 K. What will be the effect on their resistivity?
explanation: The specific resistance is given by {tex}\rho=\rho_0(1+\alpha T){/tex} where {tex}\alpha= {/tex} temperature coefficient of resistivity {tex}\alpha{/tex} is positive for metals (like copper etc.) and it is negative for semiconductors (like silicon etc.) Thus, In copper, the resistivity decreases with decrease in temperature i.e. on cooling. In silicon, the resistivity increases with decrease in temperature i.e. on cooling.
solution: The specific resistance is given by {tex}\rho=\rho_0(1+\alpha T){/tex} where {tex}\alpha= {/tex} temperature coefficient of resistivity {tex}\alpha{/tex} is positive for metals (like copper etc.) and it is negative for semiconductors (like silicon etc.) Thus, In copper, the resistivity decreases with decrease in temperature i.e. on cooling. In silicon, the resistivity increases with decrease in temperature i.e. on cooling.
solution_html:
The specific resistance is given by 
{tex}\rho=\rho_0(1+\alpha T){/tex}
where {tex}\alpha= {/tex} temperature coefficient of resistivity 

{tex}\alpha{/tex} is positive for metals (like copper etc.) and it is negative for semiconductors (like silicon etc.)
Thus,
In copper, the resistivity decreases with decrease in temperature i.e. on cooling.
In silicon, the resistivity increases with decrease in temperature i.e. on cooling.

question_html: Use Kirchhoff's rules to determine the value of the current I1 flowing in the circuit shown in the figure.
question_text: Use Kirchhoff's rules to determine the value of the current I 1 flowing in the circuit shown in the figure.
explanation: Applying Kirchhoff's junction rule at V , we get I 3 = I 1 + I 2 Applying Kirchhoff's loop rule to the loop ahdcba, we get 30 I 1 + 20 I 3 = 20 or 30 I 1 + 20(I 1 + I 2 ) = 20 or 50 I 1 + 20 I 2 = 20 5 I 1 + 2 I 2 = 2 ...(i) Again, from the loop agfedcba, we ; 20 I 2 + 20 I 3 = 80 + 20 or 20 I 2 + 20(I 1 + I 2 ) = 100 or 20 I 1 + 40 I 2 = 100 or I 1 + 2 I 2 = 5 ...(ii) Subtracting (ii) from (i), we get 4 I 1 = -3 or I 1 = -0.75 A The negative sign shows that the actual direction of current I 1 is opposite to that shown in the given circuit diagram.
solution: Applying Kirchhoff's junction rule at V , we get I 3 = I 1 + I 2 Applying Kirchhoff's loop rule to the loop ahdcba, we get 30 I 1 + 20 I 3 = 20 or 30 I 1 + 20(I 1 + I 2 ) = 20 or 50 I 1 + 20 I 2 = 20 5 I 1 + 2 I 2 = 2 ...(i) Again, from the loop agfedcba, we ; 20 I 2 + 20 I 3 = 80 + 20 or 20 I 2 + 20(I 1 + I 2 ) = 100 or 20 I 1 + 40 I 2 = 100 or I 1 + 2 I 2 = 5 ...(ii) Subtracting (ii) from (i), we get 4 I 1 = -3 or I 1 = -0.75 A The negative sign shows that the actual direction of current I 1 is opposite to that shown in the given circuit diagram.
solution_html:
Applying Kirchhoff's junction rule at V , we get
I3 = I1 + I2
Applying Kirchhoff's loop rule to the loop ahdcba, we get
30 I1 + 20 I3 = 20
or 30 I1 + 20(I1 + I2) = 20
or 50 I1 + 20 I2 = 20
5 I1 + 2 I2 = 2 ...(i)
Again, from the loop agfedcba, we ;
20 I2 + 20 I3 = 80 + 20
or 20 I2 + 20(I1 + I2) = 100
or 20 I1 + 40 I2 = 100
or I1 + 2 I2 = 5 ...(ii)
Subtracting (ii) from (i), we get
4 I1 = -3 or I1 = -0.75 A
The negative sign shows that the actual direction of current I1 is opposite to that shown in the given circuit diagram.

subject: Physics
question_html:
An ammeter of resistance 0.80{tex}\Omega{/tex} can measure current up to 1.0 A.

  1. What must be the value of shunt resistance to enable the ammeter to measure current up to 5.0 A?
  2. What is the combined resistance of the ammeter and the shunt?
question_text: An ammeter of resistance 0.80 {tex}\Omega{/tex} can measure current up to 1.0 A. What must be the value of shunt resistance to enable the ammeter to measure current up to 5.0 A? What is the combined resistance of the ammeter and the shunt?
explanation: To measure current upto 5A, the shunt S should have a value, such that for 5A input current through system, 4A should pass through shunt S and 1 A should pass through given ammeter. We know that Shunt (S) is given by {tex}S={R_A ig\over i-ig}{/tex} {tex}R_A {/tex} is Internal resistance of the ammeter. {tex}i_g = 4A , I = 5A, R_A = 0.80\Omega{/tex} {tex}1 \times R _ { A } = 4 S \Rightarrow 1 \times 0.8 = 4 S{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = 0.2 \Omega{/tex} Thus, the shunt resistance should be {tex}0.2\Omega{/tex} in order to measure current 5A through the circuit. Combined resistance of the ammeter and the shunt, as they are in parallel combination. {tex}R = \frac { 0.8S } { 0.5 + S } = \frac { 0.8 \times 0.2 } { 0.8 + 0.2 } = \frac { 0.16 } { 1 } = 0.16\Omega{/tex}
solution: To measure current upto 5A, the shunt S should have a value, such that for 5A input current through system, 4A should pass through shunt S and 1 A should pass through given ammeter. We know that Shunt (S) is given by {tex}S={R_A ig\over i-ig}{/tex} {tex}R_A {/tex} is Internal resistance of the ammeter. {tex}i_g = 4A , I = 5A, R_A = 0.80\Omega{/tex} {tex}1 \times R _ { A } = 4 S \Rightarrow 1 \times 0.8 = 4 S{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = 0.2 \Omega{/tex} Thus, the shunt resistance should be {tex}0.2\Omega{/tex} in order to measure current 5A through the circuit. Combined resistance of the ammeter and the shunt, as they are in parallel combination. {tex}R = \frac { 0.8S } { 0.5 + S } = \frac { 0.8 \times 0.2 } { 0.8 + 0.2 } = \frac { 0.16 } { 1 } = 0.16\Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
1. To measure current upto 5A, the shunt S should have a value, such that for 5A input current through system, 4A should pass through shunt S and 1 A should pass through given ammeter.
    We know that Shunt (S) is given by
    {tex}S={R_A ig\over i-ig}{/tex}
    {tex}R_A {/tex}     is Internal resistance of the ammeter. 
    {tex}i_g = 4A , I = 5A, R_A = 0.80\Omega{/tex}
    {tex}1 \times R _ { A } = 4 S \Rightarrow 1 \times 0.8 = 4 S{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = 0.2 \Omega{/tex}
    Thus, the shunt resistance should be {tex}0.2\Omega{/tex} in order to measure current 5A through the circuit.
  2. Combined resistance of the ammeter and the shunt, as they are in parallel combination.
    {tex}R = \frac { 0.8S } { 0.5 + S } = \frac { 0.8 \times 0.2 } { 0.8 + 0.2 } = \frac { 0.16 } { 1 } = 0.16\Omega{/tex}

question_html:
Define the current sensitivity of a galvanometer. Write its SI unit.
Figure shows two circuits each having a galvanometer and a battery of 3 V.
When the galvanometer in each arrangement do not show any deflection, obtain the ratio R1/R2.
question_text: Define the current sensitivity of a galvanometer. Write its SI unit. Figure shows two circuits each having a galvanometer and a battery of 3 V. When the galvanometer in each arrangement do not show any deflection, obtain the ratio R 1 /R 2 .
explanation: Current sensitivity of a galvanometer is defined as the deflection produced in the galvanometer when a unit current flows through it. The SI unit of current sensitivity is rad. A -1 . Current sensitivity is expressed as {tex}\frac {\theta }{I} = \frac {NAB}{K}{/tex} where N, A, B and K are number of turns, cross-sectional area, magnetic field intensity and galvanometer's constant respectively. For balanced Wheatstone bridge, there will be no deflection in the galvanometer. {tex}\therefore \frac { 4 } { R _ { 1 } } = \frac { 6 } { 9 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad R _ { 1 } = \frac { 4 \times 9 } { 6 } = 6 \Omega{/tex} For the equivalent circuit. when the Wheatstone bridge is balanced, there will be no deflection in the galvanometer. {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { 12 } { 8 } = \frac { 6 } { R _ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad R _ { 2 } = \frac { 6 \times 8 } { 12 } = 4 \Omega{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { R _ { 1 } } { R _ { 2 } } = \frac { 6 } { 4 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex}
solution: Current sensitivity of a galvanometer is defined as the deflection produced in the galvanometer when a unit current flows through it. The SI unit of current sensitivity is rad. A -1 . Current sensitivity is expressed as {tex}\frac {\theta }{I} = \frac {NAB}{K}{/tex} where N, A, B and K are number of turns, cross-sectional area, magnetic field intensity and galvanometer's constant respectively. For balanced Wheatstone bridge, there will be no deflection in the galvanometer. {tex}\therefore \frac { 4 } { R _ { 1 } } = \frac { 6 } { 9 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad R _ { 1 } = \frac { 4 \times 9 } { 6 } = 6 \Omega{/tex} For the equivalent circuit. when the Wheatstone bridge is balanced, there will be no deflection in the galvanometer. {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { 12 } { 8 } = \frac { 6 } { R _ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad R _ { 2 } = \frac { 6 \times 8 } { 12 } = 4 \Omega{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { R _ { 1 } } { R _ { 2 } } = \frac { 6 } { 4 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Current sensitivity of a galvanometer is defined as the deflection produced in the galvanometer when a unit current flows through it. The SI unit of current sensitivity is rad. A-1. Current sensitivity is expressed as {tex}\frac {\theta }{I} = \frac {NAB}{K}{/tex} where N, A, B and K are number of turns,  cross-sectional area, magnetic field intensity and galvanometer's constant respectively. 

For balanced Wheatstone bridge, there will be no deflection in the galvanometer.
{tex}\therefore \frac { 4 } { R _ { 1 } } = \frac { 6 } { 9 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad R _ { 1 } = \frac { 4 \times 9 } { 6 } = 6 \Omega{/tex}

For the equivalent circuit. when the Wheatstone bridge is  balanced, there will be no deflection in the galvanometer.
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { 12 } { 8 } = \frac { 6 } { R _ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad R _ { 2 } = \frac { 6 \times 8 } { 12 } = 4 \Omega{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { R _ { 1 } } { R _ { 2 } } = \frac { 6 } { 4 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex}

question_html: Draw a graph showing the variation of resistivity with temperature for nichrome. Which property of nichrome is used to make standard resistance coils?
question_text: Draw a graph showing the variation of resistivity with temperature for nichrome. Which property of nichrome is used to make standard resistance coils?
explanation: The resistivity of nichrome as a function of absolute temperature: At low temperature (or zero), the resistivity increases as a higher power of temperature. Then, it linearly increases with an increase in temperature. Nichrome has high resistivity and small temperature coefficient of resistivity due to which it is used for making coils.
solution: The resistivity of nichrome as a function of absolute temperature: At low temperature (or zero), the resistivity increases as a higher power of temperature. Then, it linearly increases with an increase in temperature. Nichrome has high resistivity and small temperature coefficient of resistivity due to which it is used for making coils.
solution_html:
The resistivity of nichrome as a function of absolute temperature: At low temperature (or zero), the resistivity increases as a higher power of temperature. Then, it linearly increases with an increase in temperature.

Nichrome has high resistivity and small temperature coefficient of resistivity due to which it is used for making coils.

question_html:
A battery of emf E and internal resistance r gives a current of 0.5 A with an external resistor of {tex}12\Omega{/tex} and a current of 0.25 A with an external resistor of {tex}25\Omega {/tex}. Then calculate -

  1. Internal resistance
  2. emf of the battery.
question_text: A battery of emf E and internal resistance r gives a current of 0.5 A with an external resistor of {tex}12\Omega{/tex} and a current of 0.25 A with an external resistor of {tex}25\Omega {/tex} . Then calculate - Internal resistance emf of the battery.
explanation: As we know, {tex}I = \frac{E}{{R + r}}{/tex} Now, {tex}0.5 = \frac{E}{{12 + r}}{/tex} and {tex}0.25 = \frac{E}{{25 + r}}{/tex} Dividing, {tex}\frac{{0.5}}{{0.25}} = \frac{{25 + r}}{{12 + r}}{/tex} or, {tex}2 = \frac{{25 + r}}{{12 + r}}{/tex} or {tex}r = 1\Omega{/tex} {tex}0.5 = \frac{E}{{12 + 1}} \Rightarrow{/tex} E = 6.5 V
solution: As we know, {tex}I = \frac{E}{{R + r}}{/tex} Now, {tex}0.5 = \frac{E}{{12 + r}}{/tex} and {tex}0.25 = \frac{E}{{25 + r}}{/tex} Dividing, {tex}\frac{{0.5}}{{0.25}} = \frac{{25 + r}}{{12 + r}}{/tex} or, {tex}2 = \frac{{25 + r}}{{12 + r}}{/tex} or {tex}r = 1\Omega{/tex} {tex}0.5 = \frac{E}{{12 + 1}} \Rightarrow{/tex} E = 6.5 V
solution_html:
1. As we know,
    {tex}I = \frac{E}{{R + r}}{/tex}
    Now, {tex}0.5 = \frac{E}{{12 + r}}{/tex} and {tex}0.25 = \frac{E}{{25 + r}}{/tex}
    Dividing, {tex}\frac{{0.5}}{{0.25}} = \frac{{25 + r}}{{12 + r}}{/tex}
    or, {tex}2 = \frac{{25 + r}}{{12 + r}}{/tex} or {tex}r = 1\Omega{/tex}
  2. {tex}0.5 = \frac{E}{{12 + 1}} \Rightarrow{/tex} E = 6.5 V

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State Kirchhoff's rules for an electric network. Using Kirchhoff's rules, obtain the balance condition in terms of the resistances of four arms of Wheatstone bridge.
  2. In the meter bridge experimental set up, shown in the figure, the null point D is obtained at a distance of 40 cm from end A of the meter bridge wire. If a resistance of {tex}10\Omega{/tex} is connected in series with R1, null point is obtained at AD = 60 cm. Calculate the values of R1 and R2.
question_text: State Kirchhoff's rules for an electric network. Using Kirchhoff's rules, obtain the balance condition in terms of the resistances of four arms of Wheatstone bridge. In the meter bridge experimental set up, shown in the figure, the null point D is obtained at a distance of 40 cm from end A of the meter bridge wire. If a resistance of {tex}10\Omega{/tex} is connected in series with R 1 , null point is obtained at AD = 60 cm. Calculate the values of R 1 and R 2.
explanation: Kirchhoff's I rule: It states that, at any junction, the sum of the currents entering the junction is equal to the sum of the currents leaving the junction. Kirchhoff's II rule: It states that, the algebraic sum of the charges in potential around any closed loop involving resistors and cells in the loop is zero. Conditions balance of a Wheatstone bridge: P, Q, R and S are four resistance forming a closed bridge, called Wheatstone bridge. A battery is connected across A and C, while a galvanometer is connected B and D. Current is absent in the galvanometer's balance point. Derivation of Formula: Let the current given by battery in the balanced position be I. This current on reaching point A is divided into two parts I 1 and I 2 . At the balanced point, current is zero. Applying Kirchhoff's I law at point A, I - I 1 - I 2 = 0 or I = I 1 + I 2 ...... (i) Applying Kirchhoff's II law to closed mesh ABDA, -I 1 P + I 2 R = 0 or I 1 P = I 2 R ..... (ii) Applying Kirchhoff's II law to mesh BCDB, -I 1 Q + I 2 S = 0 or I 1 Q = I 2 S ...... (iii) Dividing equation (ii) by (iii), we get {tex} \frac{\mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{Q}}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{S}} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{P}{Q} = \frac{R}{S}{/tex} ; which is the required condition of balance for Wheatstone bridge. For null point at D, balance length l 1 = 40cm So, {tex}\frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}=\frac{A D}{D C}=\frac{40}{(100-40)}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} ..... (i) If resistance 10 {tex}\Omega {/tex} is connected in series or R 1 , balance point shifts towards {tex}'\lambda '{/tex} i.e., AD = 60cm {tex}\frac{R_{1}+10}{R_{2}}=\frac{A D^{\prime}}{D^{\prime} C}=\frac{60}{100-60}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} ..... (ii) {tex}\frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}+\frac{10}{R_{2}}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} From equations (i) and (ii), we have {tex}\frac{2}{3}+\frac{10}{R_{2}}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{10}{R_{2}}=\frac{3}{2}-\frac{2}{3}=\frac{9-4}{6}=\frac{5}{6}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow R_{2}=\frac{10 \times 6}{5}=12 \ \mathrm{ohm}{/tex} From equation (i), we have {tex}\frac{R_{1}}{12}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow R_{1}=\frac{12 \times 2}{3}=8 \ \text { ohm }{/tex}
solution: Kirchhoff's I rule: It states that, at any junction, the sum of the currents entering the junction is equal to the sum of the currents leaving the junction. Kirchhoff's II rule: It states that, the algebraic sum of the charges in potential around any closed loop involving resistors and cells in the loop is zero. Conditions balance of a Wheatstone bridge: P, Q, R and S are four resistance forming a closed bridge, called Wheatstone bridge. A battery is connected across A and C, while a galvanometer is connected B and D. Current is absent in the galvanometer's balance point. Derivation of Formula: Let the current given by battery in the balanced position be I. This current on reaching point A is divided into two parts I 1 and I 2 . At the balanced point, current is zero. Applying Kirchhoff's I law at point A, I - I 1 - I 2 = 0 or I = I 1 + I 2 ...... (i) Applying Kirchhoff's II law to closed mesh ABDA, -I 1 P + I 2 R = 0 or I 1 P = I 2 R ..... (ii) Applying Kirchhoff's II law to mesh BCDB, -I 1 Q + I 2 S = 0 or I 1 Q = I 2 S ...... (iii) Dividing equation (ii) by (iii), we get {tex} \frac{\mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{Q}}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{S}} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{P}{Q} = \frac{R}{S}{/tex} ; which is the required condition of balance for Wheatstone bridge. For null point at D, balance length l 1 = 40cm So, {tex}\frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}=\frac{A D}{D C}=\frac{40}{(100-40)}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} ..... (i) If resistance 10 {tex}\Omega {/tex} is connected in series or R 1 , balance point shifts towards {tex}'\lambda '{/tex} i.e., AD = 60cm {tex}\frac{R_{1}+10}{R_{2}}=\frac{A D^{\prime}}{D^{\prime} C}=\frac{60}{100-60}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} ..... (ii) {tex}\frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}+\frac{10}{R_{2}}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} From equations (i) and (ii), we have {tex}\frac{2}{3}+\frac{10}{R_{2}}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{10}{R_{2}}=\frac{3}{2}-\frac{2}{3}=\frac{9-4}{6}=\frac{5}{6}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow R_{2}=\frac{10 \times 6}{5}=12 \ \mathrm{ohm}{/tex} From equation (i), we have {tex}\frac{R_{1}}{12}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow R_{1}=\frac{12 \times 2}{3}=8 \ \text { ohm }{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Kirchhoff's I rule:  It states that, at any junction, the sum of the currents entering the junction is equal to the sum of the currents leaving the junction.
    Kirchhoff's II rule: It states that, the algebraic sum of the charges in potential around any closed loop involving resistors and cells in the loop is zero.
    Conditions balance of a Wheatstone bridge:
    P, Q, R and S are four resistance forming a closed bridge, called Wheatstone bridge.

    A battery is connected across A and C, while a galvanometer is connected B and D. Current is absent in the galvanometer's balance point.
    Derivation of Formula: Let the current given by battery in the balanced position be I. This current on reaching point A is divided into two parts I1 and I2. At the balanced point, current is zero.
    Applying Kirchhoff's I law at point A,
    I - I1 - I2 = 0 or
    I = I1 + I2 ...... (i)
    Applying Kirchhoff's II law to closed mesh ABDA,
    -I1P + I2R = 0 or
    I1P = I2R ..... (ii)
    Applying Kirchhoff's II law to mesh BCDB,
    -I1Q + I2S = 0 or
    I1Q = I2S ...... (iii)
    Dividing equation (ii) by (iii), we get
    {tex} \frac{\mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{Q}}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{S}} {/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{P}{Q} = \frac{R}{S}{/tex}; which is the required condition of balance for Wheatstone bridge.

  2. For null point at D, balance length l1 = 40cm
    So, {tex}\frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}=\frac{A D}{D C}=\frac{40}{(100-40)}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} ..... (i)
    If resistance 10{tex}\Omega {/tex} is connected in series or R1, balance point shifts towards {tex}'\lambda '{/tex} i.e., AD = 60cm
    {tex}\frac{R_{1}+10}{R_{2}}=\frac{A D^{\prime}}{D^{\prime} C}=\frac{60}{100-60}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} ..... (ii)
    {tex}\frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}+\frac{10}{R_{2}}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
    From equations (i) and (ii), we have
    {tex}\frac{2}{3}+\frac{10}{R_{2}}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{10}{R_{2}}=\frac{3}{2}-\frac{2}{3}=\frac{9-4}{6}=\frac{5}{6}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow R_{2}=\frac{10 \times 6}{5}=12 \ \mathrm{ohm}{/tex}
    From equation (i), we have
    {tex}\frac{R_{1}}{12}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow R_{1}=\frac{12 \times 2}{3}=8 \ \text { ohm }{/tex}

question_text: Thermo emf of a thermocouple varies with the temperature {tex}\theta{/tex} of the hot junction as {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = a {tex}\theta{/tex} + b {tex}\theta{/tex} 2 volt where the ratio ( {tex}\frac ab{/tex} ) is 700°C. question_text: A battery of emf E and internal resistance r ohm when connected across an external resistance of 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , produces a current of 1 A. When connected across a resistance of 11 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , it produces a current of 0.5 A. Determine the emf and the internal resistance of the cell.
explanation: E = I(R + r) .... (i) E = (5 + r) .... (ii) Using eqns. (i) and (ii) 5 + r = 0.5(11 + r) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5 + r = 5.5 + 0.5r {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.5r = 0.5 {tex}r=1 \Omega{/tex} E = 6V
solution: E = I(R + r) .... (i) E = (5 + r) .... (ii) Using eqns. (i) and (ii) 5 + r = 0.5(11 + r) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5 + r = 5.5 + 0.5r {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.5r = 0.5 {tex}r=1 \Omega{/tex} E = 6V
solution_html:
E = I(R + r) .... (i)
E = (5 + r) .... (ii)


Using eqns. (i) and (ii)
5 + r = 0.5(11 + r)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5 + r = 5.5 + 0.5r
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.5r = 0.5
{tex}r=1 \Omega{/tex}
E = 6V

question_html: When electrons drift in a metal from lower to higher potential, does it mean that all the free electrons of the metal are moving in the same direction?
question_text: When electrons drift in a metal from lower to higher potential, does it mean that all the free electrons of the metal are moving in the same direction?
explanation: No, the drift speed of electrons is superposed over the random velocities of the electrons and they move in zig- zag path.
solution: No, the drift speed of electrons is superposed over the random velocities of the electrons and they move in zig- zag path.
solution_html: No, the drift speed of electrons is superposed over the random velocities of the electrons and they move in zig- zag path.

question_html: Two wires of equal length, one of copper and the other of manganin have the same resistance. Which wire is thicker?
question_text: Two wires of equal length, one of copper and the other of manganin have the same resistance. Which wire is thicker?
explanation: According to the question, both the wires are of equal length, so l Mn = l cu Given that resistances of both the wires are equal, so R Mn = R Cu {tex}\therefore \frac { \rho _ { M n } } { A _ { M n } } = \frac { \rho _ { C u } } { A _ { C u } }{/tex} We know that, copper is better conductor than manganin, therefore, copper will have less resistivity i.e. {tex}\rho _ { \mathrm { Cu } } < \rho _ { \mathrm { Mn } }{/tex} So, {tex}A _ { \mathrm { Mn } } > A _ { \mathrm { Cu } } \quad [\because \rho \propto A ]{/tex} so , wire of manganin will be thicker than that of copper.
solution: According to the question, both the wires are of equal length, so l Mn = l cu Given that resistances of both the wires are equal, so R Mn = R Cu {tex}\therefore \frac { \rho _ { M n } } { A _ { M n } } = \frac { \rho _ { C u } } { A _ { C u } }{/tex} We know that, copper is better conductor than manganin, therefore, copper will have less resistivity i.e. {tex}\rho _ { \mathrm { Cu } } < \rho _ { \mathrm { Mn } }{/tex} So, {tex}A _ { \mathrm { Mn } } > A _ { \mathrm { Cu } } \quad [\because \rho \propto A ]{/tex} so , wire of manganin will be thicker than that of copper.
solution_html:
According to the question, both the wires are of equal length,
so lMn= lcu
Given that resistances of both the wires are equal, so
RMn = RCu
{tex}\therefore \frac { \rho _ { M n } } { A _ { M n } } = \frac { \rho _ { C u } } { A _ { C u } }{/tex}
We know that, copper is better conductor than manganin, therefore, copper will have less resistivity
i.e. {tex}\rho _ { \mathrm { Cu } } < \rho _ { \mathrm { Mn } }{/tex}
So, {tex}A _ { \mathrm { Mn } } > A _ { \mathrm { Cu } } \quad [\because \rho \propto A ]{/tex}
so , wire of manganin will be thicker than that of copper.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A conductor of length I is connected to a DC source of potential V. If the length of the conductor is tripled by gradually stretching it, keeping V constant, how will

  1. drift speed of electrons and
  2. resistance of the conductor be affected? Justify your answer
question_text: A conductor of length I is connected to a DC source of potential V. If the length of the conductor is tripled by gradually stretching it, keeping V constant, how will drift speed of electrons and resistance of the conductor be affected? Justify your answer
explanation: When a wire is stretched, then there is no change in the matter of the wire, hence its volume remains constant Here, the potential V = constant, l' = 3 I Drift speed of electrons {tex}= \frac { V } { n e l \rho }{/tex} where, n is number of electrons, e is charge on electron, I is the length of the conductor and p is the resistivity of conductor. {tex}\therefore \quad v \propto \frac { 1 } { l }{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} other factors are constant] So, when length is tripled, drift velocity gets one-third. Resistance of the conductor is given as {tex}R = \rho ( l / A ){/tex} where ρ = Resistivity, l = length of the wire, A = Area of cross section of wire Here, wire is stretched to triple its length, that means the mass of the wire remains same in both the conditions. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass before stretching = Mass after stretching (Volume {tex}\times{/tex} Density) before stretching = (Volume {tex}\times{/tex} Density) after stretching (Area of cross-section {tex}\times{/tex} Length) before stretching = (Area of cross-section {tex}\times{/tex} Length) after stretching ( {tex}\because{/tex} Density is same in both cases) {tex}\therefore{/tex} A 1 l 1 = A 2 l 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A 1 l = A 2 (3 l) [ {tex}\because{/tex} length is tripled after stretching] A 2 = A 1 /3 i.e. When length is tripled area of cross-section is reduced to A/3. Hence, {tex}R' = \rho \frac { l ^ { \prime } } { A ^ { \prime } } = \rho \frac { 3 l } { A / 3 } = 9 \rho \frac { l } { A } = 9 R{/tex} Thus, above calculation shows that new resistance will be 9 times of its initial value.
solution: When a wire is stretched, then there is no change in the matter of the wire, hence its volume remains constant Here, the potential V = constant, l' = 3 I Drift speed of electrons {tex}= \frac { V } { n e l \rho }{/tex} where, n is number of electrons, e is charge on electron, I is the length of the conductor and p is the resistivity of conductor. {tex}\therefore \quad v \propto \frac { 1 } { l }{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} other factors are constant] So, when length is tripled, drift velocity gets one-third. Resistance of the conductor is given as {tex}R = \rho ( l / A ){/tex} where ρ = Resistivity, l = length of the wire, A = Area of cross section of wire Here, wire is stretched to triple its length, that means the mass of the wire remains same in both the conditions. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass before stretching = Mass after stretching (Volume {tex}\times{/tex} Density) before stretching = (Volume {tex}\times{/tex} Density) after stretching (Area of cross-section {tex}\times{/tex} Length) before stretching = (Area of cross-section {tex}\times{/tex} Length) after stretching ( {tex}\because{/tex} Density is same in both cases) {tex}\therefore{/tex} A 1 l 1 = A 2 l 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A 1 l = A 2 (3 l) [ {tex}\because{/tex} length is tripled after stretching] A 2 = A 1 /3 i.e. When length is tripled area of cross-section is reduced to A/3. Hence, {tex}R' = \rho \frac { l ^ { \prime } } { A ^ { \prime } } = \rho \frac { 3 l } { A / 3 } = 9 \rho \frac { l } { A } = 9 R{/tex} Thus, above calculation shows that new resistance will be 9 times of its initial value.
solution_html:
When a wire is stretched, then there is no change in the matter of the wire, hence its volume remains constant
Here, the potential V = constant, l' = 3 I

  1. Drift speed of electrons {tex}= \frac { V } { n e l \rho }{/tex}
    where, n is number of electrons, e is charge on electron, I is the length of the conductor and p is the resistivity of conductor.
    {tex}\therefore \quad v \propto \frac { 1 } { l }{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} other factors are constant]
    So, when length is tripled, drift velocity gets one-third.
  2. Resistance of the conductor is given as
    {tex}R = \rho ( l / A ){/tex} where ρ = Resistivity, l = length of the wire, A = Area of cross section of wire
    Here, wire is stretched to triple its length, that means the mass of the wire remains same in both the conditions.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass before stretching = Mass after stretching
    (Volume {tex}\times{/tex} Density) before stretching = (Volume {tex}\times{/tex} Density) after stretching
    (Area of cross-section {tex}\times{/tex} Length) before stretching = (Area of cross-section {tex}\times{/tex} Length) after stretching ({tex}\because{/tex} Density is same in both cases)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} A1l1 = A2l2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A1 l = A2 (3 l) [{tex}\because{/tex} length is tripled after stretching]
    A2 = A1/3
    i.e. When length is tripled area of cross-section is reduced to A/3.
    Hence, {tex}R' = \rho \frac { l ^ { \prime } } { A ^ { \prime } } = \rho \frac { 3 l } { A / 3 } = 9 \rho \frac { l } { A } = 9 R{/tex}
    Thus, above calculation shows that new resistance will be 9 times of its initial value.
