question_html:
1. Derive the relation between current density J and potential difference V across a current carrying conductor of length l, area of cross-section A and the number density n of free electrons.
  2. Estimate the average drift speed of conduction electrons in a copper wire of cross-sectional area 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m2 carrying a current of 1.5 A. [Assume that the number density of conduction electrons is 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 1028 m-3]
question_text: Derive the relation between current density J and potential difference V across a current carrying conductor of length l, area of cross-section A and the number density n of free electrons. Estimate the average drift speed of conduction electrons in a copper wire of cross-sectional area 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m 2 carrying a current of 1.5 A. [Assume that the number density of conduction electrons is 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 28 m -3 ]
explanation: Let us consider a conductor of length ' l ' , cross-sectional Area ' A ' and having ' n ' number of electrons per unit volume drifting with drift velocity {tex}v_d{/tex} . {tex}I = neAv_d ...(i){/tex} When these electrons are moving, they collide with another electrons and in between the two successive collision the time taken is called relaxation time {tex}(\tau).{/tex} Electric field inside the wire is given by E = V/l ...(ii) where,E is electric field, l is the length of the conductor and V is potential. If relaxation time is {tex}\tau{/tex} , the drift speed {tex}(v_d){/tex} is given by, {tex}v _ { d } = e \tau E / m{/tex} where, m = mass of electron, {tex}\tau{/tex} = relaxation charge, e = electronic charge and E = electric field. Putting the value of V d in Eq. (i). we get {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \frac { n e ^ { 2 } \tau } { m } A E{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}I = m e ^ { 2 } \tau A V / m l{/tex} [From Eqs. (ii)] {tex}\Rightarrow J = I / A = n e ^ { 2 } \tau V / m l{/tex} Given, {tex}I = 1.5 A , n = 9 \times 10 ^ { 28 } \mathrm { m } ^ { - 3 }{/tex} {tex}A = 1.0 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\because \quad v _ { d } ={ I \over { neA }}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v _ { d } = \frac { 15 } { 9 \times 10 ^ { 28 } \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } \times 1.0 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v _ { d } = 1.04 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m } / \mathrm { s }{/tex}
solution: Let us consider a conductor of length ' l ' , cross-sectional Area ' A ' and having ' n ' number of electrons per unit volume drifting with drift velocity {tex}v_d{/tex} . {tex}I = neAv_d ...(i){/tex} When these electrons are moving, they collide with another electrons and in between the two successive collision the time taken is called relaxation time {tex}(\tau).{/tex} Electric field inside the wire is given by E = V/l ...(ii) where,E is electric field, l is the length of the conductor and V is potential. If relaxation time is {tex}\tau{/tex} , the drift speed {tex}(v_d){/tex} is given by, {tex}v _ { d } = e \tau E / m{/tex} where, m = mass of electron, {tex}\tau{/tex} = relaxation charge, e = electronic charge and E = electric field. Putting the value of V d in Eq. (i). we get {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \frac { n e ^ { 2 } \tau } { m } A E{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}I = m e ^ { 2 } \tau A V / m l{/tex} [From Eqs. (ii)] {tex}\Rightarrow J = I / A = n e ^ { 2 } \tau V / m l{/tex} Given, {tex}I = 1.5 A , n = 9 \times 10 ^ { 28 } \mathrm { m } ^ { - 3 }{/tex} {tex}A = 1.0 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\because \quad v _ { d } ={ I \over { neA }}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v _ { d } = \frac { 15 } { 9 \times 10 ^ { 28 } \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } \times 1.0 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v _ { d } = 1.04 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m } / \mathrm { s }{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Let us consider a conductor of length ' l ' , cross-sectional Area ' A ' and having ' n ' number of electrons per unit volume drifting with drift velocity {tex}v_d{/tex}.
    {tex}I = neAv_d ...(i){/tex}
    When these electrons are moving, they collide with another electrons and in between the two successive collision the time taken is called relaxation time {tex}(\tau).{/tex}
    Electric field inside the wire is given by
    E = V/l ...(ii)
    where,E is electric field, l is the length of the conductor and V is potential.
    If relaxation time is {tex}\tau{/tex}, the drift speed {tex}(v_d){/tex} is given by,
    {tex}v _ { d } = e \tau E / m{/tex}
    where, m = mass of electron, {tex}\tau{/tex} = relaxation charge, e = electronic charge and E = electric field.
    Putting the value of Vd in Eq. (i). we get
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \frac { n e ^ { 2 } \tau } { m } A E{/tex} ...(iii)
    {tex}I = m e ^ { 2 } \tau A V / m l{/tex} [From Eqs. (ii)]
    {tex}\Rightarrow J = I / A = n e ^ { 2 } \tau V / m l{/tex}
  2. Given, {tex}I = 1.5 A , n = 9 \times 10 ^ { 28 } \mathrm { m } ^ { - 3 }{/tex}
    {tex}A = 1.0 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    {tex}\because \quad v _ { d } ={ I \over { neA }}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow v _ { d } = \frac { 15 } { 9 \times 10 ^ { 28 } \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } \times 1.0 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow v _ { d } = 1.04 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m } / \mathrm { s }{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A battery of emf 10 V and internal resistance {tex}3\Omega{/tex} is connected to a resistor. If the current in the circuit is 0.5 A, find

  1. the resistance of the resistor.
  2. the terminal voltage of the battery.
question_text: A battery of emf 10 V and internal resistance {tex}3\Omega{/tex} is connected to a resistor. If the current in the circuit is 0.5 A, find the resistance of the resistor. the terminal voltage of the battery.
explanation: Given, E = 10V, {tex}r = 3 \Omega{/tex} , I = 0.5A Total resistance of circuit {tex}R + r = \frac { E } { I } = \frac { 10 } { 0.5 } = 20 \Omega{/tex} External resistance, R = 20 - r = 20 - 3 = {tex} 17 \Omega{/tex} Terminal voltage, V = IR = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 17 = 8.5 V
solution: Given, E = 10V, {tex}r = 3 \Omega{/tex} , I = 0.5A Total resistance of circuit {tex}R + r = \frac { E } { I } = \frac { 10 } { 0.5 } = 20 \Omega{/tex} External resistance, R = 20 - r = 20 - 3 = {tex} 17 \Omega{/tex} Terminal voltage, V = IR = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 17 = 8.5 V
solution_html:
Given, E = 10V, {tex}r = 3 \Omega{/tex}, I = 0.5A
Total resistance of circuit
{tex}R + r = \frac { E } { I } = \frac { 10 } { 0.5 } = 20 \Omega{/tex}

  1. External resistance, R = 20 - r = 20 - 3 = {tex} 17 \Omega{/tex}
  2. Terminal voltage, V = IR = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 17 = 8.5 V

question_html: Define relaxation time of the free electrons drifting in a conductor. How it is related to the drift velocity of free electrons? Use this relation to deduce the expression for the electrical resistivity of the material.
question_text: Define relaxation time of the free electrons drifting in a conductor. How it is related to the drift velocity of free electrons? Use this relation to deduce the expression for the electrical resistivity of the material.
explanation: Relaxation time ( {tex}\tau{/tex} ): The average time interval between two successive collisions for the free electrons drifting within a conductor (due to the action of the applied electric field) is called the relaxation time. {tex}v _ { d } = {- e E \tau \over m}{/tex} …(i) where E = electric field due to applied potential difference, {tex}\tau{/tex} = relaxation time, m = mass of electron and e = electronic charge Consider a conductor of length ‘l’, area of cross-section A and current density n. Current flowing through the conductor is given by, {tex}I = -neAv_d{/tex} …(ii) from (i) and (ii) {tex}I = - n e A \left( - \frac { e E \tau } { m } \right){/tex} …(iii) Electric field applied across the ends is given by, {tex}E={V\over l}{/tex} …(iv) So current flowing through the conductor becomes from (iii) and (iv), {tex}I = {n e ^ { 2 } A \tau{ V \over l } \over m} {[ \because E = {V \over l }}]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { V } { I } = \frac { m l } { n e ^ { 2 } A \tau } {/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad R = \rho \frac { l } { A } \left[ \because \frac { V } { I } = R \text { and } \rho = \frac { m } { n e ^ { 2 } \tau } \right]{/tex} This is the required expression for the electrical resistivity of the material.
solution: Relaxation time ( {tex}\tau{/tex} ): The average time interval between two successive collisions for the free electrons drifting within a conductor (due to the action of the applied electric field) is called the relaxation time. {tex}v _ { d } = {- e E \tau \over m}{/tex} …(i) where E = electric field due to applied potential difference, {tex}\tau{/tex} = relaxation time, m = mass of electron and e = electronic charge Consider a conductor of length ‘l’, area of cross-section A and current density n. Current flowing through the conductor is given by, {tex}I = -neAv_d{/tex} …(ii) from (i) and (ii) {tex}I = - n e A \left( - \frac { e E \tau } { m } \right){/tex} …(iii) Electric field applied across the ends is given by, {tex}E={V\over l}{/tex} …(iv) So current flowing through the conductor becomes from (iii) and (iv), {tex}I = {n e ^ { 2 } A \tau{ V \over l } \over m} {[ \because E = {V \over l }}]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { V } { I } = \frac { m l } { n e ^ { 2 } A \tau } {/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad R = \rho \frac { l } { A } \left[ \because \frac { V } { I } = R \text { and } \rho = \frac { m } { n e ^ { 2 } \tau } \right]{/tex} This is the required expression for the electrical resistivity of the material.
solution_html:
Relaxation time ({tex}\tau{/tex}): The average time interval between two successive collisions for the free electrons drifting within a conductor (due to the action of the applied electric field) is called the relaxation time.

{tex}v _ { d } = {- e E \tau \over m}{/tex}…(i)
where E = electric field due to applied potential difference,
{tex}\tau{/tex} = relaxation time,
m = mass of electron and
e = electronic charge
Consider a conductor of length ‘l’, area of cross-section A and current density n.
Current flowing through the conductor is given by,
{tex}I = -neAv_d{/tex}  …(ii)
from (i) and (ii)
{tex}I = - n e A \left( - \frac { e E \tau } { m } \right){/tex}…(iii)
Electric field applied across the ends is given by,
{tex}E={V\over l}{/tex}…(iv)
So current flowing through the conductor becomes from (iii) and (iv),
{tex}I = {n e ^ { 2 } A \tau{ V \over l } \over m} {[ \because E = {V \over l }}]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { V } { I } = \frac { m l } { n e ^ { 2 } A \tau } {/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad R = \rho \frac { l } { A } \left[ \because \frac { V } { I } = R \text { and } \rho = \frac { m } { n e ^ { 2 } \tau } \right]{/tex}
This is the required expression for the electrical resistivity of the material.

question_html: Using Kirchhoff's rules, determine the value of unknown resistance R in the circuit, so that no current flows through {tex}4\Omega{/tex} resistance. Also, find the potential difference between points A and D.
question_text: Using Kirchhoff's rules, determine the value of unknown resistance R in the circuit, so that no current flows through {tex}4\Omega{/tex} resistance. Also, find the potential difference between points A and D.
explanation: Applying Kirchhoff's second law in mesh AFEBA, -1 {tex}\times{/tex} I - 1 {tex}\times{/tex} I - 6 + 9 = 0 -2I + 3 = 0 {tex}I = \frac { 3 } { 2 } A{/tex} ....(i) Applying Kirchhoff's second law in mesh AFDCA, - 1 {tex}\times{/tex} I -1 {tex}\times{/tex} I {tex}\times{/tex} -I {tex}\times{/tex} R - 3 + 9 = 0 -2I - IR + 6 = 0 2I + IR = 6 ...(ii) From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\left( 2 \times \frac { 3 } { 2 } \right) + \frac { 3 } { 2 } R = 6{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad R = 2 \Omega{/tex} For potential difference across A and D along AFD, {tex}V _ { A } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \times 1 - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \times 1 = V _ { D }{/tex} V A - V D = 3V
solution: Applying Kirchhoff's second law in mesh AFEBA, -1 {tex}\times{/tex} I - 1 {tex}\times{/tex} I - 6 + 9 = 0 -2I + 3 = 0 {tex}I = \frac { 3 } { 2 } A{/tex} ....(i) Applying Kirchhoff's second law in mesh AFDCA, - 1 {tex}\times{/tex} I -1 {tex}\times{/tex} I {tex}\times{/tex} -I {tex}\times{/tex} R - 3 + 9 = 0 -2I - IR + 6 = 0 2I + IR = 6 ...(ii) From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\left( 2 \times \frac { 3 } { 2 } \right) + \frac { 3 } { 2 } R = 6{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad R = 2 \Omega{/tex} For potential difference across A and D along AFD, {tex}V _ { A } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \times 1 - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \times 1 = V _ { D }{/tex} V A - V D = 3V
solution_html:
Applying Kirchhoff's second law in mesh AFEBA,
-1 {tex}\times{/tex} I - 1 {tex}\times{/tex} I - 6 + 9 = 0
-2I + 3 = 0
{tex}I = \frac { 3 } { 2 } A{/tex} ....(i)

Applying Kirchhoff's second law in mesh AFDCA,
- 1 {tex}\times{/tex} I -1 {tex}\times{/tex} I {tex}\times{/tex} -I {tex}\times{/tex} R - 3 + 9 = 0
-2I - IR + 6 = 0
2I + IR = 6 ...(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\left( 2 \times \frac { 3 } { 2 } \right) + \frac { 3 } { 2 } R = 6{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad R = 2 \Omega{/tex}
For potential difference across A and D along AFD,
{tex}V _ { A } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \times 1 - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \times 1 = V _ { D }{/tex}
VA - VD = 3V

question_html: The network PQRS, shown in the circuit diagram, has the batteries of 4 V and 5 V and negligible internal resistance. A milliammeter of {tex} 20\Omega{/tex} resistance is connected between P and R. Calculate the reading in the milliammeter.
question_text: The network PQRS, shown in the circuit diagram, has the batteries of 4 V and 5 V and negligible internal resistance. A milliammeter of {tex} 20\Omega{/tex} resistance is connected between P and R. Calculate the reading in the milliammeter.
explanation: The given diagram is shown below Applying Kirchhoff's second law i.e. KVL to the loop PRSP, {tex}- I _ { 3 } \times 20 - I _ { 2 } \times 200 + 5 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4I 3 + 40I 2 = 1 ...(i) For loop PRQP, -20I 3 - 60I 1 + 4 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5I 3 + 15I 1 = 1 ....(ii) Applying Kirchhoff's first law i.e. KCL we get, I 3 = I 1 + I 2 ....(iii) From Eqs. (i) and (iii), we have 4I 1 + 44I 2 = 1....(iv) Also from eqs. (ii) and (iii) , we have 20I 1 + 5I 2 = 1....(v) On solving eqs (iii), (iv) and (v) we get {tex}I _ { 3 } = \frac { 11 } { 172 } A = \frac { 11000 } { 172 } \mathrm { mA }{/tex} {tex}I _ { 2 } = \frac { 4000 } { 215 } \mathrm { mA } , I _ { 1 } = \frac { 39000 } { 860 } \mathrm { mA }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The reading in the millimeter will be {tex}\frac { 11000 } { 172 } \mathrm { mA }{/tex}
solution: The given diagram is shown below Applying Kirchhoff's second law i.e. KVL to the loop PRSP, {tex}- I _ { 3 } \times 20 - I _ { 2 } \times 200 + 5 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4I 3 + 40I 2 = 1 ...(i) For loop PRQP, -20I 3 - 60I 1 + 4 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5I 3 + 15I 1 = 1 ....(ii) Applying Kirchhoff's first law i.e. KCL we get, I 3 = I 1 + I 2 ....(iii) From Eqs. (i) and (iii), we have 4I 1 + 44I 2 = 1....(iv) Also from eqs. (ii) and (iii) , we have 20I 1 + 5I 2 = 1....(v) On solving eqs (iii), (iv) and (v) we get {tex}I _ { 3 } = \frac { 11 } { 172 } A = \frac { 11000 } { 172 } \mathrm { mA }{/tex} {tex}I _ { 2 } = \frac { 4000 } { 215 } \mathrm { mA } , I _ { 1 } = \frac { 39000 } { 860 } \mathrm { mA }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The reading in the millimeter will be {tex}\frac { 11000 } { 172 } \mathrm { mA }{/tex}
solution_html:
The given diagram is shown below


Applying Kirchhoff's second law i.e. KVL to the loop PRSP,
{tex}- I _ { 3 } \times 20 - I _ { 2 } \times 200 + 5 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4I3 + 40I2 = 1 ...(i)
For loop PRQP,
-20I3 - 60I1 + 4 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5I3 + 15I1 = 1 ....(ii)
Applying Kirchhoff's first law i.e. KCL we get,
I3 = I1 + I2 ....(iii)
From Eqs. (i) and (iii), we have
4I1 + 44I2 = 1....(iv) 

Also from eqs. (ii) and (iii) , we have

20I1 + 5I2 = 1....(v) 
On solving eqs (iii), (iv) and (v) we get
{tex}I _ { 3 } = \frac { 11 } { 172 } A = \frac { 11000 } { 172 } \mathrm { mA }{/tex}
{tex}I _ { 2 } = \frac { 4000 } { 215 } \mathrm { mA } , I _ { 1 } = \frac { 39000 } { 860 } \mathrm { mA }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The reading in the millimeter will be {tex}\frac { 11000 } { 172 } \mathrm { mA }{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Calculate the value of the resistance R in the circuit shown in the figure, so that the current in the circuit is 0.2A. What would be the potential difference between points A and B?
question_text: Calculate the value of the resistance R in the circuit shown in the figure, so that the current in the circuit is 0.2A. What would be the potential difference between points A and B?
explanation: For BCD, the equivalent resistance R 1 = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} + 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Across BA, equivalent resistance R 2 , {tex}\frac { 1 } { R _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 10 } + \frac { 1 } { 30 } + \frac { 1 } { 15 }{/tex} = {tex} \frac { 3 \ + \ 1 \ + \ 2 } { 30 } = \frac { 6 } { 30 } = \frac { 1 } { 5 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad R _ { 2 } = 5 \Omega{/tex} On applying Kirchoff's law, we have 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2 + R {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2 + 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2 = 8 - 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R = 5 ohm Potential difference between points A and B, V BA = I {tex}\times{/tex} R 2 = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 V BA = 1 V {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V AB = -1V
solution: For BCD, the equivalent resistance R 1 = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} + 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Across BA, equivalent resistance R 2 , {tex}\frac { 1 } { R _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 10 } + \frac { 1 } { 30 } + \frac { 1 } { 15 }{/tex} = {tex} \frac { 3 \ + \ 1 \ + \ 2 } { 30 } = \frac { 6 } { 30 } = \frac { 1 } { 5 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad R _ { 2 } = 5 \Omega{/tex} On applying Kirchoff's law, we have 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2 + R {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2 + 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2 = 8 - 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R = 5 ohm Potential difference between points A and B, V BA = I {tex}\times{/tex} R 2 = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 V BA = 1 V {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V AB = -1V
solution_html:
For BCD, the equivalent resistance
R1 = 5{tex}\Omega{/tex} + 5{tex}\Omega{/tex} = 10{tex}\Omega{/tex}
Across BA, equivalent resistance R2,
{tex}\frac { 1 } { R _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 10 } + \frac { 1 } { 30 } + \frac { 1 } { 15 }{/tex}
= {tex} \frac { 3 \ + \ 1 \ + \ 2 } { 30 } = \frac { 6 } { 30 } = \frac { 1 } { 5 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad R _ { 2 } = 5 \Omega{/tex}
On applying Kirchoff's law, we have
5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2 + R {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2 + 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2 = 8 - 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R = 5 ohm 
Potential difference between points A and B,
VBA = I {tex}\times{/tex} R2 = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5
VBA = 1 V {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} VAB = -1V

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Use Kirchhoffs rules to obtain the balance condition in a Wheatstone bridge.
  2. Calculate the value of R in the balance condition of the Wheatstone bridge, if the carbon resistor connected across the arm CD has the colour sequence red, red and orange, as shown in the figure.
  3. If now the resistance of the arms BC and CD are interchanged, to obtain the balance condition, another carbon resistor is connected in place or R. What would now be sequence of colour bands of the carbon resistor?
question_text: Use Kirchhoffs rules to obtain the balance condition in a Wheatstone bridge. Calculate the value of R in the balance condition of the Wheatstone bridge, if the carbon resistor connected across the arm CD has the colour sequence red, red and orange, as shown in the figure. If now the resistance of the arms BC and CD are interchanged, to obtain the balance condition, another carbon resistor is connected in place or R. What would now be sequence of colour bands of the carbon resistor?
explanation: A Wheatstone bridge arrangement is shown as below: Using Kirchoff's second law to the loop ABDA we get {tex}\mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{P}-\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{R}=0 : \mathrm{G}{/tex} is the galvanometer resistance. Applying Kirchoff's law to loop BCDB, we get {tex}\left(l_{1}-l_{g}\right) Q-\left(l_{2}+l_{g}\right) S-G l_{g}=0{/tex} When the bridge is balanced l g = 0 Then, the equations can be written as, {tex}l_{1} \mathrm{P}-\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{R}=0 {/tex} or {tex}\text { l }_{1} \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{R}{/tex} ... (1) {tex}\mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{Q}-\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{S}=0{/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{l}_{1} \mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{S}{/tex} ..... (2) on dividing equation (1) by (2), we get {tex}\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{S}{/tex} , which is the balanced condition of a Wheatstone bridge. Let a carbon resistor S is given to the bridge arm CD. Then, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 R}{R}=\frac{2 R}{S}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{R}{S}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R = S = 22 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} After interchanging the resistances, the balanced bridge would be {tex}\frac{2 R}{X}=\frac{22 \times 10^{3}}{2 \times 22 \times 10^{3}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Here X is the resistance of arm AD {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = 4R = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 22 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 = 88 k {tex}\Omega{/tex} Hence colour code is Grey orange.
solution: A Wheatstone bridge arrangement is shown as below: Using Kirchoff's second law to the loop ABDA we get {tex}\mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{P}-\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{R}=0 : \mathrm{G}{/tex} is the galvanometer resistance. Applying Kirchoff's law to loop BCDB, we get {tex}\left(l_{1}-l_{g}\right) Q-\left(l_{2}+l_{g}\right) S-G l_{g}=0{/tex} When the bridge is balanced l g = 0 Then, the equations can be written as, {tex}l_{1} \mathrm{P}-\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{R}=0 {/tex} or {tex}\text { l }_{1} \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{R}{/tex} ... (1) {tex}\mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{Q}-\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{S}=0{/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{l}_{1} \mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{S}{/tex} ..... (2) on dividing equation (1) by (2), we get {tex}\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{S}{/tex} , which is the balanced condition of a Wheatstone bridge. Let a carbon resistor S is given to the bridge arm CD. Then, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 R}{R}=\frac{2 R}{S}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{R}{S}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R = S = 22 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} After interchanging the resistances, the balanced bridge would be {tex}\frac{2 R}{X}=\frac{22 \times 10^{3}}{2 \times 22 \times 10^{3}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Here X is the resistance of arm AD {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = 4R = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 22 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 = 88 k {tex}\Omega{/tex} Hence colour code is Grey orange.
solution_html:
1. A Wheatstone bridge arrangement is shown as below:

    Using Kirchoff's second law to the loop ABDA we get
    {tex}\mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{P}-\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{R}=0 : \mathrm{G}{/tex} is the galvanometer resistance.
    Applying Kirchoff's law to loop BCDB, we get
    {tex}\left(l_{1}-l_{g}\right) Q-\left(l_{2}+l_{g}\right) S-G l_{g}=0{/tex}
    When the bridge is balanced lg = 0
    Then, the equations can be written as,
    {tex}l_{1} \mathrm{P}-\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{R}=0 {/tex} or {tex}\text { l }_{1} \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{R}{/tex} ... (1)
    {tex}\mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{Q}-\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{S}=0{/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{l}_{1} \mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{S}{/tex} ..... (2)
    on dividing equation (1) by (2), we get
    {tex}\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{S}{/tex}, which is the balanced condition of a Wheatstone bridge.
  2. Let a carbon resistor S is given to the bridge arm CD. Then,
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 R}{R}=\frac{2 R}{S}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{R}{S}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R = S = 22 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
  3. After interchanging the resistances, the balanced bridge would be
    {tex}\frac{2 R}{X}=\frac{22 \times 10^{3}}{2 \times 22 \times 10^{3}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    Here X is the resistance of arm AD 
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = 4R = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 22 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 = 88 k{tex}\Omega{/tex}
    Hence colour code is Grey orange.

subject: Physics
question_html: Calculate the value of the resistance R in the circuit shown in the figure so that the current in the circuit is 0.2 A. What would be the potential difference between points B and E?
question_text: Calculate the value of the resistance R in the circuit shown in the figure so that the current in the circuit is 0.2 A. What would be the potential difference between points B and E?
explanation: The equivalent resistance for arm BCD is R 1 = 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex} effective resistance between B and E is given by {tex}\frac{1}{R_{2}}=\frac{1}{15}+\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{30}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{R_{2 }}=\frac{2+3+1}{30}{/tex} R 2 = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} the potential between B and E is given by V BE = IR 2 = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 1.0 V Now applying KVL for left side, 8−3 = V BE + 0.2R + 15(0.2) or 5 = 1 + 0.2R + 3 or R = 5Ω
solution: The equivalent resistance for arm BCD is R 1 = 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex} effective resistance between B and E is given by {tex}\frac{1}{R_{2}}=\frac{1}{15}+\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{30}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{R_{2 }}=\frac{2+3+1}{30}{/tex} R 2 = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} the potential between B and E is given by V BE = IR 2 = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 1.0 V Now applying KVL for left side, 8−3 = V BE + 0.2R + 15(0.2) or 5 = 1 + 0.2R + 3 or R = 5Ω
solution_html:
The equivalent resistance for arm BCD is R1 = 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
effective resistance between  B and E is given by 
{tex}\frac{1}{R_{2}}=\frac{1}{15}+\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{30}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{R_{2 }}=\frac{2+3+1}{30}{/tex}
R2 = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
the potential between B and E is given by  VBE = IR2
= 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 1.0 V
Now applying KVL for left side, 8−3 = VBE + 0.2R + 15(0.2)
or 5 = 1 + 0.2R + 3 or R = 5Ω

question_html: Charge passing through a conductor of cross-section area A = 0.3 m2 is given by q = 3t2 + 5t + 2 in coulomb, where t is in second. What is the value of drift velocity at t = 2s? (Given, n = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1025/m3)
question_text: Charge passing through a conductor of cross-section area A = 0.3 m 2 is given by q = 3t 2 + 5t + 2 in coulomb, where t is in second. What is the value of drift velocity at t = 2s? (Given, n = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 25 /m 3 )

options.explanation: q = 3t 2 + 5t + 2, I = {tex}\frac{d q}{d t}{/tex} = 6t + 5 At t = 2s, I = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 5 = 17A v d = {tex}\frac{I}{e n A}=\frac{17}{1.6 \ \times \ 10^{-19} \ \times \ 2 \ \times \ 10^{25} \ \times \ 0.3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{17 \times 10^{-6}}{0.96}{/tex} = 1.77 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 m/s

explanation: q = 3t 2 + 5t + 2, I = {tex}\frac{d q}{d t}{/tex} = 6t + 5 At t = 2s, I = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 5 = 17A v d = {tex}\frac{I}{e n A}=\frac{17}{1.6 \ \times \ 10^{-19} \ \times \ 2 \ \times \ 10^{25} \ \times \ 0.3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{17 \times 10^{-6}}{0.96}{/tex} = 1.77 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 m/s

question_text: A cell of constant emf is first connected to a resistance R 1 and then connected to a resistance R 2 . If power delivered in both cases is same, then the internal resistance of the cell is:

options.explanation: P 1 = P 2 {tex}\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{R_{1} \ + \ r}\right)^{2} R_{1}=\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{R_{2} \ + \ r}\right)^{2} R_{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{R_{1}}{\left(R_{1} \ + \ r\right)^{2}}=\frac{R_{2}}{\left(R_{2} \ + \ r\right)^{2}}{/tex} On solving r = {tex}\sqrt{R_{1} R_{2}}{/tex}

explanation: P 1 = P 2 {tex}\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{R_{1} \ + \ r}\right)^{2} R_{1}=\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{R_{2} \ + \ r}\right)^{2} R_{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{R_{1}}{\left(R_{1} \ + \ r\right)^{2}}=\frac{R_{2}}{\left(R_{2} \ + \ r\right)^{2}}{/tex} On solving r = {tex}\sqrt{R_{1} R_{2}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A cell of emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} and internal resistance r is connected to two external resistances R1 and R2 (R2 > R1) and a perfect ammeter. The current in the circuit is measured in four different situations:

  1. Without any external resistance in the circuit
  2. With resistance R1 only
  3. With resistance R2 only
  4. With both R1 and R2 used in series combination, and
  5. With R1 and R2, used in parallel combination.

The currents measured in the four cases are 0.42 A, 0.6 A, 1.05 A, 1.4 A, and 4.2 A but not necessarily in that order. Identify the currents corresponding to the four cases mentioned above.
question_text: A cell of emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} and internal resistance r is connected to two external resistances R 1 and R 2 (R 2 > R 1 ) and a perfect ammeter. The current in the circuit is measured in four different situations: Without any external resistance in the circuit With resistance R 1 only With resistance R 2 only With both R 1 and R 2 used in series combination, and With R 1 and R 2 , used in parallel combination. The currents measured in the four cases are 0.42 A, 0.6 A, 1.05 A, 1.4 A, and 4.2 A but not necessarily in that order. Identify the currents corresponding to the four cases mentioned above.
explanation: Total resistances in the five cases are: r, r + R 1 , r + R 2 , r + R 1 + R 2 {tex}r+\frac{R_{1} R_{2}}{R_{1} \ + \ R_{2}}{/tex} As R 2 > R 1 these resistances in increasing order are r, {tex}r+\frac{R_{1} R_{2}}{R_{1} \ + \ R_{2}}{/tex} , r + R 1 , r + R 2 , r + R 1 + R 2 The currents in decreasing order are: 4.2 A, 1.4 A, 1.05 A, 0.6 A, 0.42 A {tex}\therefore \frac{\varepsilon}{r}{/tex} = 4.2 ...(i) {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{r \ + \ R_{1}}{/tex} = 1.05 A ...(ii) {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{r \ + \ R_{2}}{/tex} = 0.6 A ...(iii) {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{r \ + \ R_{1} \ + \ R_{2}}{/tex} = 0.42 A ...(iv) {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{r \ + \ \frac{R_{1} R_{2}}{R_{1} \ + \ R_{2}}}{/tex} = 1.4 A ...(v) On dividing (i) by (ii) {tex}\frac{r \ + \ R_{1}}{r}=\frac{4.2}{1.05}{/tex} or 1 + {tex}\frac{R_{1}}{r}{/tex} = 4 or R 1 = 3r On dividing (i) by (iii), {tex}\frac{r \ + \ R_{2}}{r}=\frac{4.2}{0.6}{/tex} or {tex}1+\frac{R_{2}}{r}{/tex} = 7 or R 2 = 6r From (i), {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 4.2 r Putting the above values in (iv), we get {tex}\frac{4.2 r}{r \ + \ 3 r \ + \ 6 r}{/tex} = 0.42 or r = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Hence {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 4.2 V, r = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , R 1 = 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and R 2 = 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution: Total resistances in the five cases are: r, r + R 1 , r + R 2 , r + R 1 + R 2 {tex}r+\frac{R_{1} R_{2}}{R_{1} \ + \ R_{2}}{/tex} As R 2 > R 1 these resistances in increasing order are r, {tex}r+\frac{R_{1} R_{2}}{R_{1} \ + \ R_{2}}{/tex} , r + R 1 , r + R 2 , r + R 1 + R 2 The currents in decreasing order are: 4.2 A, 1.4 A, 1.05 A, 0.6 A, 0.42 A {tex}\therefore \frac{\varepsilon}{r}{/tex} = 4.2 ...(i) {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{r \ + \ R_{1}}{/tex} = 1.05 A ...(ii) {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{r \ + \ R_{2}}{/tex} = 0.6 A ...(iii) {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{r \ + \ R_{1} \ + \ R_{2}}{/tex} = 0.42 A ...(iv) {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{r \ + \ \frac{R_{1} R_{2}}{R_{1} \ + \ R_{2}}}{/tex} = 1.4 A ...(v) On dividing (i) by (ii) {tex}\frac{r \ + \ R_{1}}{r}=\frac{4.2}{1.05}{/tex} or 1 + {tex}\frac{R_{1}}{r}{/tex} = 4 or R 1 = 3r On dividing (i) by (iii), {tex}\frac{r \ + \ R_{2}}{r}=\frac{4.2}{0.6}{/tex} or {tex}1+\frac{R_{2}}{r}{/tex} = 7 or R 2 = 6r From (i), {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 4.2 r Putting the above values in (iv), we get {tex}\frac{4.2 r}{r \ + \ 3 r \ + \ 6 r}{/tex} = 0.42 or r = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Hence {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 4.2 V, r = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , R 1 = 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and R 2 = 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
Total resistances in the five cases are:

  1. r,
  2. r + R1,
  3. r + R2,
  4. r + R1 + R2
  5. {tex}r+\frac{R_{1} R_{2}}{R_{1} \ + \ R_{2}}{/tex}

As R2 > R1 these resistances in increasing order are r, {tex}r+\frac{R_{1} R_{2}}{R_{1} \ + \ R_{2}}{/tex}, r + R1, r + R2, r + R1 + R2
The currents in decreasing order are:
4.2 A, 1.4 A, 1.05 A, 0.6 A, 0.42 A
{tex}\therefore \frac{\varepsilon}{r}{/tex} = 4.2 ...(i)
{tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{r \ + \ R_{1}}{/tex} = 1.05 A ...(ii)
{tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{r \ + \ R_{2}}{/tex} = 0.6 A ...(iii)
{tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{r \ + \ R_{1} \ + \ R_{2}}{/tex} = 0.42 A ...(iv)
{tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{r \ + \ \frac{R_{1} R_{2}}{R_{1} \ + \ R_{2}}}{/tex} = 1.4 A ...(v)
On dividing (i) by (ii)
{tex}\frac{r \ + \ R_{1}}{r}=\frac{4.2}{1.05}{/tex} or 1 + {tex}\frac{R_{1}}{r}{/tex} = 4 or R1 = 3r
On dividing (i) by (iii),
{tex}\frac{r \ + \ R_{2}}{r}=\frac{4.2}{0.6}{/tex}
or {tex}1+\frac{R_{2}}{r}{/tex} = 7
or R2 = 6r
From (i), {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 4.2 r
Putting the above values in (iv), we get
{tex}\frac{4.2 r}{r \ + \ 3 r \ + \ 6 r}{/tex} = 0.42
or r = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
Hence {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 4.2 V, r = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex}, R1 = 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and R2 = 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Define resistivity of a conductor. Write its SI unit.
question_text: Define resistivity of a conductor. Write its SI unit.
explanation: Resistivity is a measure of how strongly a material opposes the flow of electric current. A definition of resistivity is the electrical resistance per unit length and per unit of cross-sectional area. This is for a particular material at a specified temperature. The resistivity of a material of the conductor does not depend on the geometry of the conductor. SI unit of resistivity is ohm-metre ( {tex}\Omega{/tex} -m)
solution: Resistivity is a measure of how strongly a material opposes the flow of electric current. A definition of resistivity is the electrical resistance per unit length and per unit of cross-sectional area. This is for a particular material at a specified temperature. The resistivity of a material of the conductor does not depend on the geometry of the conductor. SI unit of resistivity is ohm-metre ( {tex}\Omega{/tex} -m)
solution_html:
Resistivity is a measure of how strongly a material opposes the flow of electric current.
A definition of resistivity is the electrical resistance per unit length and per unit of cross-sectional area. This is for a particular material at a specified temperature. The resistivity of a material of the conductor does not depend on the geometry of the conductor.
SI unit of resistivity is ohm-metre ({tex}\Omega{/tex}-m)

question_html: Plot a graph showing the temperature dependence of resistivity for a typical semiconductor. How is this behaviour explained?
question_text: Plot a graph showing the temperature dependence of resistivity for a typical semiconductor. How is this behaviour explained?
explanation: Variation of resistivity ( {tex}\rho {/tex} ) with temperature (T) is shown below: Explanation: In semiconductor the number density of free electrons ( {tex}\rho {/tex} ) increases with increase in temperature (T) and consequently the relaxation period decreases. But the effect of increase in {tex}\rho {/tex} has higher impact than decrease of T. So, resistivity decreases with increase in temperature.
solution: Variation of resistivity ( {tex}\rho {/tex} ) with temperature (T) is shown below: Explanation: In semiconductor the number density of free electrons ( {tex}\rho {/tex} ) increases with increase in temperature (T) and consequently the relaxation period decreases. But the effect of increase in {tex}\rho {/tex} has higher impact than decrease of T. So, resistivity decreases with increase in temperature.
solution_html:
Variation of resistivity ({tex}\rho {/tex}) with temperature (T) is shown below:

Explanation: In semiconductor the number density of free electrons ({tex}\rho {/tex}) increases with increase in temperature (T) and consequently the relaxation period decreases. But the effect of increase in {tex}\rho {/tex} has higher impact than decrease of T. So, resistivity decreases with increase in temperature.

subject: Physics
question_html:
Using Kirchhoff's rules in the given circuit, determine

  1. the current I2 in the arm EF.
  2. the voltage drop across the unknown resistor R and
question_text: Using Kirchhoff's rules in the given circuit, determine the current I 2 in the arm EF. the voltage drop across the unknown resistor R and
explanation: Applying Kirchhoff's second rule in the closed mesh ABFEA, V B - 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 3 = V A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V B - V A = - 2 V = V A - V B = + 2V Potential drop across R is 1 V as R, BF and upper row are in parallel. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Potential across AB = potential across BF 3 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = 4 - 2I 2 2I 2 = 2A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 2 = 1A Potential across R = potential across AB = potential across BF = 3 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = 2V
solution: Applying Kirchhoff's second rule in the closed mesh ABFEA, V B - 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 3 = V A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V B - V A = - 2 V = V A - V B = + 2V Potential drop across R is 1 V as R, BF and upper row are in parallel. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Potential across AB = potential across BF 3 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = 4 - 2I 2 2I 2 = 2A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 2 = 1A Potential across R = potential across AB = potential across BF = 3 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = 2V
solution_html:
1. Applying Kirchhoff's second rule in the closed mesh ABFEA,
    VB - 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 3 = VA {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} VB - VA = - 2
    V = VA - VB = + 2V
    Potential drop across R is 1 V as R, BF and upper row are in parallel.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Potential across AB = potential across BF
    3 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = 4 - 2I2
    2I2 = 2A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I2 = 1A
  2. Potential across R = potential across AB = potential across BF
    = 3 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = 2V

subject: Physics
question_html:
Potential difference across terminals of a cell were measured (in volts) against different currents ( in ampere) flowing through the cell. A graph was drawn which has a straight line ABC. Using the data given in the graph determine,

  1. the emf and
  2. maximum current obtained from the cell, and
  3. the internal resistance of the cell.
question_text: Potential difference across terminals of a cell were measured (in volts) against different currents ( in ampere) flowing through the cell. A graph was drawn which has a straight line ABC. Using the data given in the graph determine, the emf and maximum current obtained from the cell, and the internal resistance of the cell.
explanation: The value of potential difference corresponding to zero current gives the emf of cell. This value is 1.4 V. Maximum current is drawn from the cell when the terminal potential difference is zero. The current corresponding to zero value of the terminal potential difference is 0.28 A. This is the maximum value of the current. {tex}\Rightarrow r=\frac EI{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r=\frac{1.4}{0.28}{/tex} {tex}\\\Rightarrow r=5\;ohm{/tex}
solution: The value of potential difference corresponding to zero current gives the emf of cell. This value is 1.4 V. Maximum current is drawn from the cell when the terminal potential difference is zero. The current corresponding to zero value of the terminal potential difference is 0.28 A. This is the maximum value of the current. {tex}\Rightarrow r=\frac EI{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r=\frac{1.4}{0.28}{/tex} {tex}\\\Rightarrow r=5\;ohm{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The value of potential difference corresponding to zero current gives the emf of cell. This value is 1.4 V.
  2. Maximum current is drawn from the cell when the terminal potential difference is zero.
  3. The current corresponding to zero value of the terminal potential difference is 0.28 A. This is the maximum value of the current.
    {tex}\Rightarrow r=\frac EI{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow r=\frac{1.4}{0.28}{/tex}
    {tex}\\\Rightarrow r=5\;ohm{/tex}

question_html: A current of 2 A flows through a 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor when connected across a battery. The same battery supplies a current of 0.5 A when connected across a 9 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor. The internal resistance of the battery is:
question_text: A current of 2 A flows through a 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor when connected across a battery. The same battery supplies a current of 0.5 A when connected across a 9 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor. The internal resistance of the battery is:

A straight line plot showing the terminal potential difference (V) of a cell as a function of current (I) drawn from it is shown in the figure. Using this plot, determine

Current in circuit, i = {tex}\frac{E}{R+r}{/tex}

question_text: A straight line plot showing the terminal potential difference (V) of a cell as a function of current (I) drawn from it is shown in the figure. Using this plot, determine Current in circuit, i = {tex}\frac{E}{R+r}{/tex} the emf and internal resistance of the cell.
explanation: The value of potential difference corresponding to zero current givesthe emf of cell. This value is 1.4 V. Maximum current is drawn from the cell when the terminal potential difference is zero. The current corresponding to zero value of terminal potential difference is 0.28 A. This is maximum value of current. {tex}\therefore{/tex} r = {tex}\frac EI{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.4}{0.28} \Omega{/tex} ; r = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution: The value of potential difference corresponding to zero current givesthe emf of cell. This value is 1.4 V. Maximum current is drawn from the cell when the terminal potential difference is zero. The current corresponding to zero value of terminal potential difference is 0.28 A. This is maximum value of current. {tex}\therefore{/tex} r = {tex}\frac EI{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.4}{0.28} \Omega{/tex} ; r = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The value of potential difference corresponding to zero current givesthe emf of cell. This value is 1.4 V.
  2. Maximum current is drawn from the cell when the terminal potential difference is zero. The current corresponding to zero value of terminal potential difference is 0.28 A. This is maximum value of current.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} r = {tex}\frac EI{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.4}{0.28} \Omega{/tex}; r = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex}

question_html: In the circuit shown in Figure, R1 = 4{tex}\Omega{/tex}, R2 = R3 = 5{tex}\Omega{/tex}, R4 = 10{tex}\Omega{/tex} and {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 6 V. Work out the equivalent resistance of the circuit and the current in each resistor.
question_text: In the circuit shown in Figure, R 1 = 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , R 2 = R 3 = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , R 4 = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 6 V. Work out the equivalent resistance of the circuit and the current in each resistor.
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{R_{A B}}=\frac{1}{R_{2}}+\frac{1}{R_{3}}+\frac{1}{R_{4}}=\frac{1}{5}+\frac{1}{5}+\frac{1}{10}=\frac{5}{10}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} R AB = 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} R = R 1 + R AB = 4 + 2 = 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} I 1 = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{6 \mathrm{~V}}{6 \Omega}{/tex} = 1A V AB = I 1 R AB = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 2V {tex}\therefore{/tex} I 2 = I 3 = {tex}\frac 25{/tex} A = 0.4 A and I 4 = {tex}\frac{2}{10}{/tex} = 0.2 A
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{R_{A B}}=\frac{1}{R_{2}}+\frac{1}{R_{3}}+\frac{1}{R_{4}}=\frac{1}{5}+\frac{1}{5}+\frac{1}{10}=\frac{5}{10}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} R AB = 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} R = R 1 + R AB = 4 + 2 = 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} I 1 = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{6 \mathrm{~V}}{6 \Omega}{/tex} = 1A V AB = I 1 R AB = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 2V {tex}\therefore{/tex} I 2 = I 3 = {tex}\frac 25{/tex} A = 0.4 A and I 4 = {tex}\frac{2}{10}{/tex} = 0.2 A
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{1}{R_{A B}}=\frac{1}{R_{2}}+\frac{1}{R_{3}}+\frac{1}{R_{4}}=\frac{1}{5}+\frac{1}{5}+\frac{1}{10}=\frac{5}{10}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} RAB = 2{tex}\Omega{/tex}
R = R1 + RAB = 4 + 2 = 6{tex}\Omega{/tex}
I1 = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{6 \mathrm{~V}}{6 \Omega}{/tex} = 1A
VAB = I1 RAB = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 2V
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I2 = I3 = {tex}\frac 25{/tex}A = 0.4 A and I4 = {tex}\frac{2}{10}{/tex} = 0.2 A

subject: Physics
question_html:
Define the terms

  1. drift velocity,
  2. relaxation time.

A conductor of length L is connected to a dc source of emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex}. If this conductor is replaced by another conductor of same material and same area of cross-section but of length 3 L, how will the drift velocity change?
question_text: Define the terms drift velocity, relaxation time. A conductor of length L is connected to a dc source of emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} . If this conductor is replaced by another conductor of same material and same area of cross-section but of length 3 L, how will the drift velocity change?
explanation: Drift Velocity: When a potential difference is applied across a conductor, the free electrons drift towards the direction of positive potential. The small average velocity of free electrons along the direction of positive potential is called the drift velocity. Relaxation Time: The time of free travel of a free electron between two successive collisions of electron with lattice ions/atoms is called the relaxation time. Drift velocity, V d = {tex}\frac{e \tau}{m} \frac{\varepsilon}{L} \alpha \frac{1}{\mathrm{~L}}{/tex} When length L is made 3 L, drift velocity becomes one third.
solution: Drift Velocity: When a potential difference is applied across a conductor, the free electrons drift towards the direction of positive potential. The small average velocity of free electrons along the direction of positive potential is called the drift velocity. Relaxation Time: The time of free travel of a free electron between two successive collisions of electron with lattice ions/atoms is called the relaxation time. Drift velocity, V d = {tex}\frac{e \tau}{m} \frac{\varepsilon}{L} \alpha \frac{1}{\mathrm{~L}}{/tex} When length L is made 3 L, drift velocity becomes one third.
solution_html:
1. Drift Velocity: When a potential difference is applied across a conductor, the free electrons drift towards the direction of positive potential. The small average velocity of free electrons along the direction of positive potential is called the drift velocity.
  2. Relaxation Time: The time of free travel of a free electron between two successive collisions of electron with lattice ions/atoms is called the relaxation time.
    Drift velocity, Vd = {tex}\frac{e \tau}{m} \frac{\varepsilon}{L} \alpha \frac{1}{\mathrm{~L}}{/tex}
    When length L is made 3 L, drift velocity becomes one third.

question_html: Two conducting wires X and Y of same diameter but different materials are joined in series across a battery. If the number density of electrons in X is twice than that in Y, then find the ratio of drift velocity of electrons in the two wires.
question_text: Two conducting wires X and Y of same diameter but different materials are joined in series across a battery. If the number density of electrons in X is twice than that in Y, then find the ratio of drift velocity of electrons in the two wires.
explanation: The drift velocity is the average velocity that a particle, such as an electron, attains in a material due to an electric field. It can also be referred to as axial drift velocity . Given that number density in X = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} Number density in Y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n x = 2n y ...(i) As current is common for the entire circuit, i.e. I = n X A X e(v d ) x = n Y A Y e(v d ) Y Also, the diameters of the wires are same A X = A Y {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { \left( v _ { d } \right) _ { x } } { \left( v _ { d } \right) _ { Y } } = \frac { n _ { Y } } { n _ { X } } = \frac { n _ { Y } } { 2 n _ { Y } }{/tex} [From Eq. (i)] {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} So the required value is 1 : 2
solution: The drift velocity is the average velocity that a particle, such as an electron, attains in a material due to an electric field. It can also be referred to as axial drift velocity . Given that number density in X = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} Number density in Y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n x = 2n y ...(i) As current is common for the entire circuit, i.e. I = n X A X e(v d ) x = n Y A Y e(v d ) Y Also, the diameters of the wires are same A X = A Y {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { \left( v _ { d } \right) _ { x } } { \left( v _ { d } \right) _ { Y } } = \frac { n _ { Y } } { n _ { X } } = \frac { n _ { Y } } { 2 n _ { Y } }{/tex} [From Eq. (i)] {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} So the required value is 1 : 2
solution_html:
The drift velocity is the average velocity that a particle, such as an electron, attains in a material due to an electric field. It can also be referred to as axial drift velocity.
Given that number density in X = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} Number density in Y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} nx = 2ny ...(i)
As current is common for the entire circuit,
i.e. I = nXAX e(vd)x = nYAYe(vd)Y
Also, the diameters of the wires are same
AX = AY
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { \left( v _ { d } \right) _ { x } } { \left( v _ { d } \right) _ { Y } } = \frac { n _ { Y } } { n _ { X } } = \frac { n _ { Y } } { 2 n _ { Y } }{/tex} [From Eq. (i)]
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
So the required value is 1 : 2

question_html: Three cells of emf E, 2E and 5E having internal resistances r, 2r and 3r respectively are connected across a variable resistance R as shown in the figure. Find the expression for the current. Plot a graph for variation of current with R.
question_text: Three cells of emf E, 2E and 5E having internal resistances r, 2r and 3r respectively are connected across a variable resistance R as shown in the figure. Find the expression for the current. Plot a graph for variation of current with R.
explanation: According to the question , E 1 = E , E 2 = -2E and E 3 = 5E, r 1 = r, r 2 = 2r and r 3 = 3r Net EMF E net = E 1 + E 2 + E 3 E net = E - 2E + 5E = 4E {tex}E_{net}= E-2E+5E=4E{/tex} Equivalent resistance = r 1 + r 2 + r 3 + R = r + 2r + 3r + R = 6r + R {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current, {tex}I = \frac { 4 \varepsilon } { 6 r + R }{/tex} The graph for variation of current I with resistance R is shown above
solution: According to the question , E 1 = E , E 2 = -2E and E 3 = 5E, r 1 = r, r 2 = 2r and r 3 = 3r Net EMF E net = E 1 + E 2 + E 3 E net = E - 2E + 5E = 4E {tex}E_{net}= E-2E+5E=4E{/tex} Equivalent resistance = r 1 + r 2 + r 3 + R = r + 2r + 3r + R = 6r + R {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current, {tex}I = \frac { 4 \varepsilon } { 6 r + R }{/tex} The graph for variation of current I with resistance R is shown above
solution_html:
According to the question ,  E1 = E , E2 = -2E and E3 = 5E, r1 = r, r2 = 2r and r3 = 3r
Net EMF Enet = E1 + E2 + E3
Enet = E - 2E + 5E = 4E
{tex}E_{net}= E-2E+5E=4E{/tex}
Equivalent resistance = r1 + r2 + r3 + R = r + 2r + 3r + R = 6r + R
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Current, {tex}I = \frac { 4 \varepsilon } { 6 r + R }{/tex}

The graph for variation of current I with resistance R is shown above

question_html: Calculate the steady current through the {tex}2\Omega{/tex} resistor in the circuit shown in the figure.
question_text: Calculate the steady current through the {tex}2\Omega{/tex} resistor in the circuit shown in the figure.
explanation: In DC circuit, initially capacitor is in steady state i.e. it offers infinite resistance. Therefore, no current flows through capacitor and {tex}4\Omega{/tex} resistance, so resistance will produce no effect. {tex}\therefore{/tex} In circuit AB, the effective resistance between {tex}2\Omega \ and\ 3 \Omega{/tex} which are connected in parallel combination is given by {tex}R _ { A B } = \frac { 2 \times 3 } { 2 + 3 }{/tex} {tex}= 1.2 \Omega \left[ \because \frac { 1 } { R_{eq} } = \frac { 1 } { R _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { R _ { 2 } } \Rightarrow R = \frac { R _ { 1 } R _ { 2 } } { R _ { 1 } + R _ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} Total resistance of the circuit = 1.2+ 2.8 = {tex}4\Omega{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} these two are connected in series combination] Given , Voltage = 6 V, total resistance(R) {tex}=4\Omega{/tex} Net current drawn from the cell, {tex}I = \frac { V } { R ( \text { total resistance } ) } = \frac { 6 } { 4 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } = 1.5 \mathrm { A }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Potential difference between A and B {tex}V _ { A B } = I R _ { A B } = 1.5 \times 1.2 \Rightarrow V _ { A B } = 1.80 \mathrm { V }{/tex} Current through {tex}2\Omega{/tex} resistance, {tex}I ^ { \prime } = \frac{V _ {AB} }{ 2} \Omega = \frac{1.8}{ 2} = 0.9 \mathrm { A }{/tex}
solution: In DC circuit, initially capacitor is in steady state i.e. it offers infinite resistance. Therefore, no current flows through capacitor and {tex}4\Omega{/tex} resistance, so resistance will produce no effect. {tex}\therefore{/tex} In circuit AB, the effective resistance between {tex}2\Omega \ and\ 3 \Omega{/tex} which are connected in parallel combination is given by {tex}R _ { A B } = \frac { 2 \times 3 } { 2 + 3 }{/tex} {tex}= 1.2 \Omega \left[ \because \frac { 1 } { R_{eq} } = \frac { 1 } { R _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { R _ { 2 } } \Rightarrow R = \frac { R _ { 1 } R _ { 2 } } { R _ { 1 } + R _ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} Total resistance of the circuit = 1.2+ 2.8 = {tex}4\Omega{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} these two are connected in series combination] Given , Voltage = 6 V, total resistance(R) {tex}=4\Omega{/tex} Net current drawn from the cell, {tex}I = \frac { V } { R ( \text { total resistance } ) } = \frac { 6 } { 4 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } = 1.5 \mathrm { A }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Potential difference between A and B {tex}V _ { A B } = I R _ { A B } = 1.5 \times 1.2 \Rightarrow V _ { A B } = 1.80 \mathrm { V }{/tex} Current through {tex}2\Omega{/tex} resistance, {tex}I ^ { \prime } = \frac{V _ {AB} }{ 2} \Omega = \frac{1.8}{ 2} = 0.9 \mathrm { A }{/tex}
solution_html:
In DC circuit, initially capacitor is in steady state i.e. it offers infinite resistance. Therefore, no current flows through capacitor and {tex}4\Omega{/tex} resistance, so resistance will produce no effect.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} In circuit AB, the effective resistance between {tex}2\Omega \ and\ 3 \Omega{/tex} which are connected in parallel combination is given by

{tex}R _ { A B } = \frac { 2 \times 3 } { 2 + 3 }{/tex}
{tex}= 1.2 \Omega \left[ \because \frac { 1 } { R_{eq} } = \frac { 1 } { R _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { R _ { 2 } } \Rightarrow R = \frac { R _ { 1 } R _ { 2 } } { R _ { 1 } + R _ { 2 } } \right]{/tex}

Total resistance of the circuit = 1.2+ 2.8 = {tex}4\Omega{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} these two are connected in series combination]
Given ,
Voltage = 6 V, total resistance(R) {tex}=4\Omega{/tex}
Net current drawn from the cell,
{tex}I = \frac { V } { R ( \text { total resistance } ) } = \frac { 6 } { 4 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } = 1.5 \mathrm { A }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Potential difference between A and B
{tex}V _ { A B } = I R _ { A B } = 1.5 \times 1.2 \Rightarrow V _ { A B } = 1.80 \mathrm { V }{/tex}
Current through {tex}2\Omega{/tex} resistance,
{tex}I ^ { \prime } = \frac{V _ {AB} }{ 2} \Omega = \frac{1.8}{ 2} = 0.9 \mathrm { A }{/tex}

question_html: Two cells of emf 2E and E and internal resistances 2r and r respectively, are connected in parallel. Obtain the expressions for the equivalent emf and the internal resistance of the combination.
question_text: Two cells of emf 2E and E and internal resistances 2r and r respectively, are connected in parallel. Obtain the expressions for the equivalent emf and the internal resistance of the combination.
explanation: Given, emf of first cell = 2E emf of second cell = E Internal resistance of first cell = 2r Internal resistance of second cell = r Net current, I = I 1 + I 2 ...(i) E 1 = 2E For cell-I V = V A - V B = 2E - I 1 (2r) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { 1 } = \frac { 2 E - V } { 2 r }{/tex} ....(ii) For cell-II, V = V A - V B = E - I 2 r {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { 2 } = \frac { E - V } { r }{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\because{/tex} From Eqs. (ii) and (iii), substituting in Eq. (i), we get {tex}I = \frac { 2 E - V } { 2 r } + \frac { E - V } { r }{/tex} On rearranging the term, we get {tex}V = \frac { 4 E } { 3 } - I \left( \frac { 2 r } { 3 } \right){/tex} But for equivalent of combination, V = E eq - I(r eq ) On comparing, {tex}E _ { \mathrm { eq } } = \frac { 4 E } { 3 } , r _ { \mathrm { eq } } = \frac { 2 r } { 3 }{/tex}
solution: Given, emf of first cell = 2E emf of second cell = E Internal resistance of first cell = 2r Internal resistance of second cell = r Net current, I = I 1 + I 2 ...(i) E 1 = 2E For cell-I V = V A - V B = 2E - I 1 (2r) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { 1 } = \frac { 2 E - V } { 2 r }{/tex} ....(ii) For cell-II, V = V A - V B = E - I 2 r {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { 2 } = \frac { E - V } { r }{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\because{/tex} From Eqs. (ii) and (iii), substituting in Eq. (i), we get {tex}I = \frac { 2 E - V } { 2 r } + \frac { E - V } { r }{/tex} On rearranging the term, we get {tex}V = \frac { 4 E } { 3 } - I \left( \frac { 2 r } { 3 } \right){/tex} But for equivalent of combination, V = E eq - I(r eq ) On comparing, {tex}E _ { \mathrm { eq } } = \frac { 4 E } { 3 } , r _ { \mathrm { eq } } = \frac { 2 r } { 3 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, emf of first cell = 2E
emf of second cell = E
Internal resistance of first cell = 2r
Internal resistance of second cell = r
Net current, I = I1 + I2 ...(i)
E1 = 2E

For cell-I
V = VA - VB = 2E - I1(2r)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { 1 } = \frac { 2 E - V } { 2 r }{/tex} ....(ii)
For cell-II, V = VA - VB = E - I2r
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { 2 } = \frac { E - V } { r }{/tex} ...(iii)
{tex}\because{/tex} From Eqs. (ii) and (iii), substituting in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}I = \frac { 2 E - V } { 2 r } + \frac { E - V } { r }{/tex}
On rearranging the term, we get
{tex}V = \frac { 4 E } { 3 } - I \left( \frac { 2 r } { 3 } \right){/tex}
But for equivalent of combination,
V = Eeq - I(req)
On comparing,
{tex}E _ { \mathrm { eq } } = \frac { 4 E } { 3 } , r _ { \mathrm { eq } } = \frac { 2 r } { 3 }{/tex}

question_html:
Two cells of emf E1, E2 and internal resistances r1 and r2 respectively are connected in parallel as shown in the figure.

Deduce the expressions for

  1. the equivalent emf of the combination.
  2. the equivalent resistance of the combination.
question_text: Two cells of emf E 1 , E 2 and internal resistances r 1 and r 2 respectively are connected in parallel as shown in the figure. Deduce the expressions for the equivalent emf of the combination. the equivalent resistance of the combination.
explanation: Let us assume that I 1 = current through E 1 and I 2 = current through {tex}E_2{/tex} according to Kirchhoffs first rule, I = I 1 + I 2 Now, let V = potential difference across the parallel combination . Then {tex}V = E _ { 1 } - I _ { 1 } r _ { 1 } \text { or } I _ { 1 } = \frac { E _ { 1 } - V } { r _ { 1 } }{/tex} , and similarly {tex}I _ { 2 } = \frac { E _ { 2 } - V } { r _ { 2 } }{/tex} Thus, substituting for I 1 and I 2 , we have {tex}I = \frac { E _ { 1 } - V } { r _ { 1 } } + \frac { E _ { 2 } - V } { r _ { 2 } }{/tex} or {tex}I = \left( \frac { E _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } } + \frac { E _ { 2 } } { r _ { 2 } } \right) - V \left( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { r _ { 2 } } \right){/tex} {tex}V = \left( \frac { E _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } + E _ { 2 } r _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } } \right) - I \left( \frac { r _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } } \right){/tex} ...(i) Comparing the above equation with, V = E eq - Ir eq we get {tex}E_{eq} = \frac { E _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } + E _ { 2 } r _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } }{/tex} This is equivalent emf of the combination. {tex}r_{eq}= \frac { r _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } }{/tex} This is equivalent resistance of the combination
solution: Let us assume that I 1 = current through E 1 and I 2 = current through {tex}E_2{/tex} according to Kirchhoffs first rule, I = I 1 + I 2 Now, let V = potential difference across the parallel combination . Then {tex}V = E _ { 1 } - I _ { 1 } r _ { 1 } \text { or } I _ { 1 } = \frac { E _ { 1 } - V } { r _ { 1 } }{/tex} , and similarly {tex}I _ { 2 } = \frac { E _ { 2 } - V } { r _ { 2 } }{/tex} Thus, substituting for I 1 and I 2 , we have {tex}I = \frac { E _ { 1 } - V } { r _ { 1 } } + \frac { E _ { 2 } - V } { r _ { 2 } }{/tex} or {tex}I = \left( \frac { E _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } } + \frac { E _ { 2 } } { r _ { 2 } } \right) - V \left( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { r _ { 2 } } \right){/tex} {tex}V = \left( \frac { E _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } + E _ { 2 } r _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } } \right) - I \left( \frac { r _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } } \right){/tex} ...(i) Comparing the above equation with, V = E eq - Ir eq we get {tex}E_{eq} = \frac { E _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } + E _ { 2 } r _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } }{/tex} This is equivalent emf of the combination. {tex}r_{eq}= \frac { r _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } }{/tex} This is equivalent resistance of the combination
solution_html:
Let us assume that  I1 = current through E1 and I2 = current through {tex}E_2{/tex} 
according to Kirchhoffs first rule,  I = I1 + I2
Now, let V = potential difference across the parallel combination . Then 
{tex}V = E _ { 1 } - I _ { 1 } r _ { 1 } \text { or } I _ { 1 } = \frac { E _ { 1 } - V } { r _ { 1 } }{/tex}, and similarly 
{tex}I _ { 2 } = \frac { E _ { 2 } - V } { r _ { 2 } }{/tex}
Thus, substituting for I1 and I2, we have
{tex}I = \frac { E _ { 1 } - V } { r _ { 1 } } + \frac { E _ { 2 } - V } { r _ { 2 } }{/tex}
or {tex}I = \left( \frac { E _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } } + \frac { E _ { 2 } } { r _ { 2 } } \right) - V \left( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { r _ { 2 } } \right){/tex}
{tex}V = \left( \frac { E _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } + E _ { 2 } r _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } } \right) - I \left( \frac { r _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } } \right){/tex} ...(i)
Comparing the above equation with, V = Eeq - Ireq we get

  1. {tex}E_{eq} = \frac { E _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } + E _ { 2 } r _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } }{/tex}
    This is equivalent emf of the combination.
  2. {tex}r_{eq}= \frac { r _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } }{/tex}
    This is equivalent resistance of the combination

question_html: State Kirchhoff's rules. Use these rules to write the expressions for the currents I1, I2 and I3 in the circuit diagram shown in figure below.
question_text: State Kirchhoff's rules. Use these rules to write the expressions for the currents I 1 , I 2 and I 3 in the circuit diagram shown in figure below.
explanation: Kirchhoff's first rule: The algebraic sum of the electric currents at any junction of electric circuit is equal to zero, i.e. the sum of current entering into a junction is equal to the sum of current leaving the junction. {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \Sigma I = 0{/tex} Kirchhoffs second rule or loop rule In any closed mesh of electrical circuit, the algebraic sum of emfs of cells and the product of currents and resistances is always equal to zero. i.e. {tex}\Sigma E + \Sigma I R = 0{/tex} For given circuit, At, F, applying junction rule, I 3 = I 1 + I 2 ...(i) In mesh ABCFA, -2 - 4I 1 + 3I 2 + 1 = 0 4I 1 - 3I 2 = -1 ...(ii) In mesh FCDEF, -1 - 3I 2 - 2I 3 + 4 = 0 3I 2 + 2I 3 = 3 On solving, I 1 , I 2 and I 3 , we get {tex}I _ { 1 } = \frac { 2 } { 13 } A , \quad I _ { 2 } = \frac { 7 } { 13 } A{/tex} and {tex}I _ { 3 } = \frac { 9 } { 13 } A{/tex} Thus by using KCL & KVL, we can calculate the values of current flowing in each branch.
solution: Kirchhoff's first rule: The algebraic sum of the electric currents at any junction of electric circuit is equal to zero, i.e. the sum of current entering into a junction is equal to the sum of current leaving the junction. {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \Sigma I = 0{/tex} Kirchhoffs second rule or loop rule In any closed mesh of electrical circuit, the algebraic sum of emfs of cells and the product of currents and resistances is always equal to zero. i.e. {tex}\Sigma E + \Sigma I R = 0{/tex} For given circuit, At, F, applying junction rule, I 3 = I 1 + I 2 ...(i) In mesh ABCFA, -2 - 4I 1 + 3I 2 + 1 = 0 4I 1 - 3I 2 = -1 ...(ii) In mesh FCDEF, -1 - 3I 2 - 2I 3 + 4 = 0 3I 2 + 2I 3 = 3 On solving, I 1 , I 2 and I 3 , we get {tex}I _ { 1 } = \frac { 2 } { 13 } A , \quad I _ { 2 } = \frac { 7 } { 13 } A{/tex} and {tex}I _ { 3 } = \frac { 9 } { 13 } A{/tex} Thus by using KCL & KVL, we can calculate the values of current flowing in each branch.
solution_html:
Kirchhoff's first rule: The algebraic sum of the electric currents at any junction of electric circuit is equal to zero, i.e. the sum of current entering into a junction is equal to the sum of current leaving the junction.
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \Sigma I = 0{/tex}
Kirchhoffs second rule or loop rule In any closed mesh of electrical circuit, the algebraic sum of emfs of cells and the product of currents and resistances is always equal to zero.
i.e. {tex}\Sigma E + \Sigma I R = 0{/tex}

For given circuit,

At, F, applying junction rule,
I3 = I1 + I2 ...(i)
In mesh ABCFA,
-2 - 4I1 + 3I2 + 1 = 0
4I1 - 3I2 = -1 ...(ii)
In mesh FCDEF,
-1 - 3I2 - 2I3 + 4 = 0
3I2 + 2I3 = 3
On solving, I1, I2 and I3, we get
{tex}I _ { 1 } = \frac { 2 } { 13 } A , \quad I _ { 2 } = \frac { 7 } { 13 } A{/tex}
and {tex}I _ { 3 } = \frac { 9 } { 13 } A{/tex}
Thus by using KCL & KVL, we can calculate the values of current flowing in each branch.

question_html: Calculate the steady current through the {tex}2\Omega{/tex} resistor in the circuit shown in the figure below.
question_text: Calculate the steady current through the {tex}2\Omega{/tex} resistor in the circuit shown in the figure below.
explanation: No current flows through {tex}4\Omega{/tex} resistor as capacitor offers infinite resistance in DC circuits. Also, {tex}2\Omega{/tex} and {tex}3\Omega{/tex} are in parallel combination {tex}\therefore \quad R _ { A B } = \frac { 2 \times 3 } { 2 + 3 } = \frac { 6 } { 5 } = 1.2 \mathrm { A }{/tex} Applying Kirchhoff's second rule in outer loop AB and cell. Let I current flow through outer loop in clockwise direction. -1.2I - 2.8I + 6 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4I = 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}I = \frac { 3 } { 2 } A{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Potential difference across AB, {tex}V _ { A B } = I R _ { A B } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \times 1.2 = 1.8 \mathrm { V }{/tex} {tex}\therefore 3 \Omega \text { and } 2 \Omega{/tex} are in parallel combination. Potential difference across {tex}2\Omega{/tex} resistor is 1.8V. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current I through {tex}2\Omega{/tex} resistor is given by {tex}I ^ { \prime } = \frac { V } { R } = \frac { 1.8 } { 2 } = 0.9 \mathrm { A } \Rightarrow I ^ { \prime } = 0.9 \mathrm { A }{/tex}
solution: No current flows through {tex}4\Omega{/tex} resistor as capacitor offers infinite resistance in DC circuits. Also, {tex}2\Omega{/tex} and {tex}3\Omega{/tex} are in parallel combination {tex}\therefore \quad R _ { A B } = \frac { 2 \times 3 } { 2 + 3 } = \frac { 6 } { 5 } = 1.2 \mathrm { A }{/tex} Applying Kirchhoff's second rule in outer loop AB and cell. Let I current flow through outer loop in clockwise direction. -1.2I - 2.8I + 6 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4I = 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}I = \frac { 3 } { 2 } A{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Potential difference across AB, {tex}V _ { A B } = I R _ { A B } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \times 1.2 = 1.8 \mathrm { V }{/tex} {tex}\therefore 3 \Omega \text { and } 2 \Omega{/tex} are in parallel combination. Potential difference across {tex}2\Omega{/tex} resistor is 1.8V. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current I through {tex}2\Omega{/tex} resistor is given by {tex}I ^ { \prime } = \frac { V } { R } = \frac { 1.8 } { 2 } = 0.9 \mathrm { A } \Rightarrow I ^ { \prime } = 0.9 \mathrm { A }{/tex}
solution_html:
No current flows through {tex}4\Omega{/tex} resistor as capacitor offers infinite resistance in DC circuits.
Also, {tex}2\Omega{/tex} and {tex}3\Omega{/tex} are in parallel combination
{tex}\therefore \quad R _ { A B } = \frac { 2 \times 3 } { 2 + 3 } = \frac { 6 } { 5 } = 1.2 \mathrm { A }{/tex}
Applying Kirchhoff's second rule in outer loop AB and cell.
Let I current flow through outer loop in clockwise direction.
-1.2I - 2.8I + 6 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4I = 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}I = \frac { 3 } { 2 } A{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Potential difference across AB,
{tex}V _ { A B } = I R _ { A B } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \times 1.2 = 1.8 \mathrm { V }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore 3 \Omega \text { and } 2 \Omega{/tex} are in parallel combination.
Potential difference across {tex}2\Omega{/tex} resistor is 1.8V.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Current I through {tex}2\Omega{/tex} resistor is given by
{tex}I ^ { \prime } = \frac { V } { R } = \frac { 1.8 } { 2 } = 0.9 \mathrm { A } \Rightarrow I ^ { \prime } = 0.9 \mathrm { A }{/tex}

question_text: State Kirchhoff's rules. Apply Kirchhoff's rules to the loops ACBPA and ACBQA to write the expressions for the currents I 1 , I 2 and I 3 in the network shown in figure.
explanation: By Kirchhoff's junction rule, I 3 = I 1 + I 2 ... (i) For loop ACBQA, 0.5 I 1 - I 2 = 6 - 10 = -4 ... (ii) From loop ACBPA, 12I 3 + 0.5 I 1 = 6 ...(iii) On solving equations (i), (ii) and (iii), we get {tex}I_{1}=-\frac{84}{37} \mathrm{~A}, \quad I_{2}=\frac{106}{37} \mathrm{~A}, \quad I_{3}=-\frac{22}{37} \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
solution: By Kirchhoff's junction rule, I 3 = I 1 + I 2 ... (i) For loop ACBQA, 0.5 I 1 - I 2 = 6 - 10 = -4 ... (ii) From loop ACBPA, 12I 3 + 0.5 I 1 = 6 ...(iii) On solving equations (i), (ii) and (iii), we get {tex}I_{1}=-\frac{84}{37} \mathrm{~A}, \quad I_{2}=\frac{106}{37} \mathrm{~A}, \quad I_{3}=-\frac{22}{37} \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
solution_html:
By Kirchhoff's junction rule,

I3 = I1 + I2 ... (i)
For loop ACBQA,
0.5 I1 - I2 = 6 - 10 = -4 ... (ii)
From loop ACBPA,
12I3 + 0.5 I1 = 6 ...(iii)
On solving equations (i), (ii) and (iii), we get
{tex}I_{1}=-\frac{84}{37} \mathrm{~A}, \quad I_{2}=\frac{106}{37} \mathrm{~A}, \quad I_{3}=-\frac{22}{37} \mathrm{~A}{/tex}

question_html:
Write any two factors on which internal resistance of a cell depends.
The reading on a high resistance voltmeter, when a cell is connected across it, is 2.2 V. When the terminals of the cell are also connected to a resistance of {tex} 5\Omega{/tex} as shown in the circuit, the voltmeter reading drops to 1.8 V. Find the internal resistance of the cell.
question_text: Write any two factors on which internal resistance of a cell depends. The reading on a high resistance voltmeter, when a cell is connected across it, is 2.2 V. When the terminals of the cell are also connected to a resistance of {tex} 5\Omega{/tex} as shown in the circuit, the voltmeter reading drops to 1.8 V. Find the internal resistance of the cell.
explanation: The high resistance voltmeter means that no current will flow through it hence, there is no potential difference across it. So, the reading shown by the high resistance voltmeter can be taken as the emf of the cell. The internal resistance of a cell depends on the concentration of electrolyte and distance between the two electrodes The emf of cell (E) = 2.2 V The terminal voltage across cell when 5 {tex} \Omega{/tex} resistance (R) connected across it (V) = 1.8V Let internal resistance = r {tex}\because{/tex} Internal resistance, {tex}r = R \left( \frac { E } { V } - 1 \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore r = 5 \left( \frac { 2.2 } { 1.8 } - 1 \right) = 5 \times \frac { 0.4 } { 1.8 } = \frac { 2 } { 1.8 } = \frac { 10 } { 9 } \Omega{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r = \frac { 10 } { 9 } \Omega{/tex}
solution: The high resistance voltmeter means that no current will flow through it hence, there is no potential difference across it. So, the reading shown by the high resistance voltmeter can be taken as the emf of the cell. The internal resistance of a cell depends on the concentration of electrolyte and distance between the two electrodes The emf of cell (E) = 2.2 V The terminal voltage across cell when 5 {tex} \Omega{/tex} resistance (R) connected across it (V) = 1.8V Let internal resistance = r {tex}\because{/tex} Internal resistance, {tex}r = R \left( \frac { E } { V } - 1 \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore r = 5 \left( \frac { 2.2 } { 1.8 } - 1 \right) = 5 \times \frac { 0.4 } { 1.8 } = \frac { 2 } { 1.8 } = \frac { 10 } { 9 } \Omega{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r = \frac { 10 } { 9 } \Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
The high resistance voltmeter means that no current will flow through it hence, there is no potential difference across it. So, the reading shown by the high resistance voltmeter can be taken as the emf of the cell.
The internal resistance of a cell depends on

  1. the concentration of electrolyte and
  2. distance between the two electrodes

The emf of cell (E) = 2.2 V
The terminal voltage across cell when 5{tex} \Omega{/tex} resistance (R) connected across it (V) = 1.8V
Let internal resistance = r
{tex}\because{/tex} Internal resistance,
{tex}r = R \left( \frac { E } { V } - 1 \right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore r = 5 \left( \frac { 2.2 } { 1.8 } - 1 \right) = 5 \times \frac { 0.4 } { 1.8 } = \frac { 2 } { 1.8 } = \frac { 10 } { 9 } \Omega{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow r = \frac { 10 } { 9 } \Omega{/tex}

1. State Kirchhoffs rules.
  2. A battery of 10 V and negligible internal resistance is connected across the diagonally opposite corners of a cubical network consisting of 12 resistors each of 1{tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance.

    Use Kirchhoffs rules to determine
    1. the total current in the network.
    2. the equivalent resistance of the network
question_text: State Kirchhoffs rules. A battery of 10 V and negligible internal resistance is connected across the diagonally opposite corners of a cubical network consisting of 12 resistors each of 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance. Use Kirchhoffs rules to determine the total current in the network. the equivalent resistance of the network
explanation: Kirchhoffs Ist rule or Junction Rule: The algebraic sum of electric currents at any junction of electric circuit is equal to zero i.e., {tex}\sum I =0{/tex} Kirchhoff's IInd rule or Voltage Law: In any closed mesh of electrical circuit, the algebraic sum of emfs of cells and the product of currents and resistances is always equal to zero. i.e., {tex}\sum E+\sum I R=0{/tex} a) Let 6I current be drawn from the cell. Since the paths AA', AD and AB are symmetrical, current through them is same. As per Kirchhoff's junction rule, the current distribution is shown in the figure. Let the equivalent resistance across the combination be R. E = V A - V B = (6I)R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6IR = 10 [ {tex}\because{/tex} E = 10 V] ....(i) Applying Kirchoff's second rule in loop AA'B'C'A - 2I {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - I {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - 2I {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 10 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5I = 10 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 2A Total current in the network = 6I = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 12 A b) From Eq. (i), 6IR = 10 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} R = 10 R = {tex}\frac{10}{12}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{6 }{/tex} {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution: Kirchhoffs Ist rule or Junction Rule: The algebraic sum of electric currents at any junction of electric circuit is equal to zero i.e., {tex}\sum I =0{/tex} Kirchhoff's IInd rule or Voltage Law: In any closed mesh of electrical circuit, the algebraic sum of emfs of cells and the product of currents and resistances is always equal to zero. i.e., {tex}\sum E+\sum I R=0{/tex} a) Let 6I current be drawn from the cell. Since the paths AA', AD and AB are symmetrical, current through them is same. As per Kirchhoff's junction rule, the current distribution is shown in the figure. Let the equivalent resistance across the combination be R. E = V A - V B = (6I)R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6IR = 10 [ {tex}\because{/tex} E = 10 V] ....(i) Applying Kirchoff's second rule in loop AA'B'C'A - 2I {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - I {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - 2I {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 10 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5I = 10 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 2A Total current in the network = 6I = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 12 A b) From Eq. (i), 6IR = 10 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} R = 10 R = {tex}\frac{10}{12}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{6 }{/tex} {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Kirchhoffs Ist rule or Junction Rule: The algebraic sum of electric currents at any junction of electric circuit is equal to zero i.e., {tex}\sum I =0{/tex}
    Kirchhoff's IInd rule or Voltage Law: In any closed mesh of electrical circuit, the algebraic sum of emfs of cells and the product of currents and resistances is always equal to zero.
    i.e., {tex}\sum E+\sum I R=0{/tex}
  2. a) Let 6I current be drawn from the cell. Since the paths AA', AD and AB are symmetrical, current through them is same. As per Kirchhoff's junction rule, the current distribution is shown in the figure.

    Let the equivalent resistance across the combination be R.
    E = VA - VB = (6I)R
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6IR = 10 [{tex}\because{/tex} E = 10 V] ....(i)
    Applying Kirchoff's second rule in loop AA'B'C'A
    - 2I {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - I {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - 2I {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 10 = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5I = 10 
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 2A
    Total current in the network = 6I = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 12 A

    b) From Eq. (i), 6IR = 10 
    6 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} R = 10 
    R = {tex}\frac{10}{12}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{6 }{/tex}{tex}\Omega{/tex}

question_html: Two conducting wires X and Y of same diameter but of different materials are joined in series across a battery. If the number density of electrons in X is twice that in Y, find the ratio of drift velocity of electrons in the two metals.
question_text: Two conducting wires X and Y of same diameter but of different materials are joined in series across a battery. If the number density of electrons in X is twice that in Y, find the ratio of drift velocity of electrons in the two metals.
explanation: The drift velocity of electrons in a conductor, {tex}v_{d}=\frac{I}{n e \mathrm{A}}{/tex} Since the two conductors are joined in series across a battery, the same current will pass through them. Also, as the two wires are of same diameters, their areas of crosssection will also be the same. If n X and n Y are the numbers of free electrons per unit volume in the two conductors, then {tex}\frac{\left(v_{d}\right)_{X}}{\left(v_{d}\right)_{Y}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{I}}{n_{X} e \mathrm{A}} \times \frac{n_{\mathrm{Y}} e \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{n_{\mathrm{Y}}}{n_{\mathrm{X}}}=\frac{n_{\mathrm{Y}}}{2 n_{\mathrm{Y}}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution: The drift velocity of electrons in a conductor, {tex}v_{d}=\frac{I}{n e \mathrm{A}}{/tex} Since the two conductors are joined in series across a battery, the same current will pass through them. Also, as the two wires are of same diameters, their areas of crosssection will also be the same. If n X and n Y are the numbers of free electrons per unit volume in the two conductors, then {tex}\frac{\left(v_{d}\right)_{X}}{\left(v_{d}\right)_{Y}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{I}}{n_{X} e \mathrm{A}} \times \frac{n_{\mathrm{Y}} e \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{n_{\mathrm{Y}}}{n_{\mathrm{X}}}=\frac{n_{\mathrm{Y}}}{2 n_{\mathrm{Y}}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
The drift velocity of electrons in a conductor,
{tex}v_{d}=\frac{I}{n e \mathrm{A}}{/tex}
Since the two conductors are joined in series across a battery, the same current will pass through them. Also, as the two wires are of same diameters, their areas of crosssection will also be the same. If nX and nY are the numbers of free electrons per unit volume in the two conductors, then
{tex}\frac{\left(v_{d}\right)_{X}}{\left(v_{d}\right)_{Y}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{I}}{n_{X} e \mathrm{A}} \times \frac{n_{\mathrm{Y}} e \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{n_{\mathrm{Y}}}{n_{\mathrm{X}}}=\frac{n_{\mathrm{Y}}}{2 n_{\mathrm{Y}}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
