question_html: On what factors does the internal resistance of a cell depend?
question_text: On what factors does the internal resistance of a cell depend?
explanation: Internal resistance of a cell depends on the following factors : The nature of electrolyte The nature of electrodes The distance between electrodes The area of electrodes inside the electrolyte
solution: Internal resistance of a cell depends on the following factors : The nature of electrolyte The nature of electrodes The distance between electrodes The area of electrodes inside the electrolyte
solution_html:
Internal resistance of a cell depends on the following factors :

  1. The nature of electrolyte
  2. The nature of electrodes
  3. The distance between electrodes
  4. The area of electrodes inside the electrolyte

question_html: A cell, with finite internal resistance r, is connected across two external resistances R1 and R2 (R1 > R2), one by one. In which case would the terminal potential difference of the cell more? Justify your answer.
question_text: A cell, with finite internal resistance r, is connected across two external resistances R 1 and R 2 (R 1 > R 2 ), one by one. In which case would the terminal potential difference of the cell more? Justify your answer.
explanation: The terminal potential difference of the cell, {tex}\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{I} r=\mathrm{E}-\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}+r} r{/tex} For external resistance R 1 : {tex}\mathrm{V}_{1}=\mathrm{E}-\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}+r} r{/tex} For external resistance R 2 : {tex}\mathrm{V}_{2}=\mathrm{E}-\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}+r} r{/tex} Since R 1 > R 2 , it follows that V 1 > V 2
solution: The terminal potential difference of the cell, {tex}\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{I} r=\mathrm{E}-\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}+r} r{/tex} For external resistance R 1 : {tex}\mathrm{V}_{1}=\mathrm{E}-\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}+r} r{/tex} For external resistance R 2 : {tex}\mathrm{V}_{2}=\mathrm{E}-\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}+r} r{/tex} Since R 1 > R 2 , it follows that V 1 > V 2
solution_html:
The terminal potential difference of the cell,
{tex}\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{I} r=\mathrm{E}-\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}+r} r{/tex}
For external resistance R1:
{tex}\mathrm{V}_{1}=\mathrm{E}-\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}+r} r{/tex}
For external resistance R2:
{tex}\mathrm{V}_{2}=\mathrm{E}-\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}+r} r{/tex}
Since R1 > R2, it follows that V1 > V2

question_html: A cell of e.m.f. 2 V and internal resistance 0.1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is connected to a 3.9 {tex}\Omega{/tex} external resistance. What will be the potential difference across the terminals of the cell?
question_text: A cell of e.m.f. 2 V and internal resistance 0.1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is connected to a 3.9 {tex}\Omega{/tex} external resistance. What will be the potential difference across the terminals of the cell?
explanation: Here, E = 2 V ; r = 0.1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and R = 3.9 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . The terminal potential difference is given by V = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}+r} \mathrm{R}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \ \times \ 3 \cdot 9}{3 \cdot 9 \ + \ 0 \cdot 1}=1 \cdot 95 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
solution: Here, E = 2 V ; r = 0.1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and R = 3.9 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . The terminal potential difference is given by V = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}+r} \mathrm{R}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \ \times \ 3 \cdot 9}{3 \cdot 9 \ + \ 0 \cdot 1}=1 \cdot 95 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, E = 2 V ; r = 0.1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and R = 3.9{tex}\Omega{/tex}. The terminal potential difference is given by
V = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}+r} \mathrm{R}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{2 \ \times \ 3 \cdot 9}{3 \cdot 9 \ + \ 0 \cdot 1}=1 \cdot 95 \mathrm{V}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Calculate the steady state current in the {tex}2\Omega {/tex} resistor shown in the figure below. The internal resistance of the battery is negligible and the capacitance of the capacitor is {tex}0.2\mu F{/tex}.
question_text: Calculate the steady state current in the {tex}2\Omega {/tex} resistor shown in the figure below. The internal resistance of the battery is negligible and the capacitance of the capacitor is {tex}0.2\mu F{/tex} .
explanation: Equivalent resistance of {tex}2\Omega {/tex} and {tex}3\Omega {/tex} in parallel will be {tex}{R_P} = \frac{{2 \times 3}}{{2 + 3}} = \frac{6}{5} = 1.2\Omega {/tex} Since capacitor provides infinite resistance to direct current, hence no current flows in capacitor arm. Therefore, total resistance of current carrying arms, {tex}R = 1.2 + 2.8 = 4\Omega {/tex} Hence current from battery will be {tex}I = \frac{6}{4} = 1.5A{/tex} Potential difference across A and B = IR P {tex} = 1.5 \times 1.2 = 1.80V{/tex} Current through {tex}2\Omega {/tex} resistor {tex} = \frac{{potential\;difference}}{{resis\tan ce}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{1.8}}{2} = 0.9A{/tex}
solution: Equivalent resistance of {tex}2\Omega {/tex} and {tex}3\Omega {/tex} in parallel will be {tex}{R_P} = \frac{{2 \times 3}}{{2 + 3}} = \frac{6}{5} = 1.2\Omega {/tex} Since capacitor provides infinite resistance to direct current, hence no current flows in capacitor arm. Therefore, total resistance of current carrying arms, {tex}R = 1.2 + 2.8 = 4\Omega {/tex} Hence current from battery will be {tex}I = \frac{6}{4} = 1.5A{/tex} Potential difference across A and B = IR P {tex} = 1.5 \times 1.2 = 1.80V{/tex} Current through {tex}2\Omega {/tex} resistor {tex} = \frac{{potential\;difference}}{{resis\tan ce}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{1.8}}{2} = 0.9A{/tex}
solution_html:
Equivalent resistance of {tex}2\Omega {/tex} and {tex}3\Omega {/tex} in parallel will be
{tex}{R_P} = \frac{{2 \times 3}}{{2 + 3}} = \frac{6}{5} = 1.2\Omega {/tex}
Since capacitor provides infinite resistance to direct current, hence no current flows in capacitor arm.
Therefore, total resistance of current carrying arms,
{tex}R = 1.2 + 2.8 = 4\Omega {/tex}
Hence current from battery will be
{tex}I = \frac{6}{4} = 1.5A{/tex}
Potential difference across A and B = IRP
{tex} = 1.5 \times 1.2 = 1.80V{/tex}
Current through {tex}2\Omega {/tex} resistor
{tex} = \frac{{potential\;difference}}{{resis\tan ce}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{1.8}}{2} = 0.9A{/tex}

question_html: A potential difference V is applied across a conductor of length l. How is the drift velocity affected when V is doubled and l is halved?
question_text: A potential difference V is applied across a conductor of length l. How is the drift velocity affected when V is doubled and l is halved?
explanation: Drift velocity, v d = {tex}\frac{e E \tau}{m}=\frac{e V \tau}{m l} \Rightarrow v_{d}^{\prime}=\frac{e 2 V \tau}{m(\frac l 2)}=4 v_{d}{/tex} When V is doubled and l is halved, drift velocity becomes 4 times original v d .
solution: Drift velocity, v d = {tex}\frac{e E \tau}{m}=\frac{e V \tau}{m l} \Rightarrow v_{d}^{\prime}=\frac{e 2 V \tau}{m(\frac l 2)}=4 v_{d}{/tex} When V is doubled and l is halved, drift velocity becomes 4 times original v d .
solution_html:
Drift velocity,
vd = {tex}\frac{e E \tau}{m}=\frac{e V \tau}{m l} \Rightarrow v_{d}^{\prime}=\frac{e 2 V \tau}{m(\frac l 2)}=4 v_{d}{/tex}
When V is doubled and l is halved, drift velocity becomes 4 times original vd.

question_html:
Two cells of emf E1, E2 and internal resistances r1 and r2 respectively are connected in parallel as shown in the figure.

Deduce the expressions for

  1. the equivalent emf of the combination.
  2. the equivalent resistance of the combination and
  3. the potential difference between the points A and B
question_text: Two cells of emf E 1 , E 2 and internal resistances r 1 and r 2 respectively are connected in parallel as shown in the figure. Deduce the expressions for the equivalent emf of the combination. the equivalent resistance of the combination and the potential difference between the points A and B
explanation: Let I 1 and I 2 be the currents in two cells with emfs, E 1 and E 2 and internal resistances, r 1 and r 2 , So, I = I 1 + I 2 No, let v be the potential difference between the points, A and B. Since, the first cell is connected between the points A and B. V = Potential difference across first cell V = E 1 - I 1 r 1 or I 1 = {tex}\frac{E_1-V}{r_1}{/tex} Now, the second cell is also connected between the points, A and B. So, {tex}I_2=\frac{E_2-V}{r_2}{/tex} Thus, substituting for I 1 and I 2 , {tex}I=\frac{E_1-V}{r_1}+\frac{E_2-V}{r_2}{/tex} or {tex}I=\left(\frac{E_1}{r_1}+\frac{E_2}{r_2}\right)-V\left(\frac{1}{r_1}+\frac{1}{r_2}\right){/tex} {tex}V=\left(\frac{E_1 r_2+E_2 r_1}{r_1+r_2}\right)-I\left(\frac{r_1 r_2}{r_1+r_2}\right){/tex} ...(i) If E is effective emf and r, the effective internal resistance of the parallel combination of the two cells, then V = E - Ir ...(ii) Comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii). we get {tex}E=\frac{E_1 r_2+E_2 r_1}{r_1+r_2}{/tex} This is equivalent emf of the combination {tex}r=\frac{r_1 r_2}{r_1+r_2}{/tex} This is equivalent resistance of the combination. the potential difference between the points A and B is V = E - Ir.
solution: Let I 1 and I 2 be the currents in two cells with emfs, E 1 and E 2 and internal resistances, r 1 and r 2 , So, I = I 1 + I 2 No, let v be the potential difference between the points, A and B. Since, the first cell is connected between the points A and B. V = Potential difference across first cell V = E 1 - I 1 r 1 or I 1 = {tex}\frac{E_1-V}{r_1}{/tex} Now, the second cell is also connected between the points, A and B. So, {tex}I_2=\frac{E_2-V}{r_2}{/tex} Thus, substituting for I 1 and I 2 , {tex}I=\frac{E_1-V}{r_1}+\frac{E_2-V}{r_2}{/tex} or {tex}I=\left(\frac{E_1}{r_1}+\frac{E_2}{r_2}\right)-V\left(\frac{1}{r_1}+\frac{1}{r_2}\right){/tex} {tex}V=\left(\frac{E_1 r_2+E_2 r_1}{r_1+r_2}\right)-I\left(\frac{r_1 r_2}{r_1+r_2}\right){/tex} ...(i) If E is effective emf and r, the effective internal resistance of the parallel combination of the two cells, then V = E - Ir ...(ii) Comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii). we get {tex}E=\frac{E_1 r_2+E_2 r_1}{r_1+r_2}{/tex} This is equivalent emf of the combination {tex}r=\frac{r_1 r_2}{r_1+r_2}{/tex} This is equivalent resistance of the combination. the potential difference between the points A and B is V = E - Ir.
solution_html:
Let I1 and I2 be the currents in two cells with emfs, E1 and E2 and internal resistances, r1 and r2,

So, I = I1 + I2
No, let v be the potential difference between the points, A and B. Since, the first cell is connected between the points A and B.
V = Potential difference across first cell
V = E1 - I1 r1 or I1 = {tex}\frac{E_1-V}{r_1}{/tex}
Now, the second cell is also connected between the points, A and B. So,
{tex}I_2=\frac{E_2-V}{r_2}{/tex}
Thus, substituting for I1 and I2,
{tex}I=\frac{E_1-V}{r_1}+\frac{E_2-V}{r_2}{/tex}
or {tex}I=\left(\frac{E_1}{r_1}+\frac{E_2}{r_2}\right)-V\left(\frac{1}{r_1}+\frac{1}{r_2}\right){/tex}
{tex}V=\left(\frac{E_1 r_2+E_2 r_1}{r_1+r_2}\right)-I\left(\frac{r_1 r_2}{r_1+r_2}\right){/tex} ...(i)
If E is effective emf and r, the effective internal resistance of the parallel combination of the two cells, then
V = E - Ir ...(ii)
Comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii). we get

  1. {tex}E=\frac{E_1 r_2+E_2 r_1}{r_1+r_2}{/tex}
    This is equivalent emf of the combination
  2. {tex}r=\frac{r_1 r_2}{r_1+r_2}{/tex}
    This is equivalent resistance of the combination.
  3. the potential difference between the points A and B is
    V = E - Ir.

subject: Physics
question_html: A 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} non-insulated resistance wire is bent 180° in the middle and the two halves are twisted together. What will be its new resistance?
question_text: A 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} non-insulated resistance wire is bent 180° in the middle and the two halves are twisted together. What will be its new resistance?
explanation: The relation between the length, area of cross section and resistance of a conductor is given as R = {tex}\rho\frac{ l}{A}{/tex} . In this case, a piece of wire is bent through 180 degrees at its mid point and the two halves are twisted together. So, the length is halved {tex}(\frac{l}{2}){/tex} and the area of cross section is doubled (2A). The equation is rewritten as {tex}\mathrm{R}^{\prime}=\rho \frac{\frac{1}{2}}{2 \mathrm{~A}}=\frac{1}{4} \times \rho \frac{\mathrm{l}}{\mathrm{A}}{/tex} Solving both equations we get, R' = {tex}\frac {R}{4}{/tex} .
solution: The relation between the length, area of cross section and resistance of a conductor is given as R = {tex}\rho\frac{ l}{A}{/tex} . In this case, a piece of wire is bent through 180 degrees at its mid point and the two halves are twisted together. So, the length is halved {tex}(\frac{l}{2}){/tex} and the area of cross section is doubled (2A). The equation is rewritten as {tex}\mathrm{R}^{\prime}=\rho \frac{\frac{1}{2}}{2 \mathrm{~A}}=\frac{1}{4} \times \rho \frac{\mathrm{l}}{\mathrm{A}}{/tex} Solving both equations we get, R' = {tex}\frac {R}{4}{/tex} .
solution_html:
The relation between the length, area of cross section and resistance of a conductor is given as R = {tex}\rho\frac{ l}{A}{/tex}.
In this case, a piece of wire is bent through 180 degrees at its mid point and the two halves are twisted together. So, the length is halved {tex}(\frac{l}{2}){/tex} and the area of cross section is doubled (2A).
The equation is rewritten as
{tex}\mathrm{R}^{\prime}=\rho \frac{\frac{1}{2}}{2 \mathrm{~A}}=\frac{1}{4} \times \rho \frac{\mathrm{l}}{\mathrm{A}}{/tex}
Solving both equations we get,
R' = {tex}\frac {R}{4}{/tex}.

subject: Physics
question_html: Obtain the formula for the power loss in a conductor of resistance R, carrying a current I.
question_text: Obtain the formula for the power loss in a conductor of resistance R, carrying a current I.
explanation: Consider a conductor MN having resistance R. The potentials of the two terminals are suppose V M and V N . Such that, V M - V N = V (As, V M > V N ) At any time interval {tex}\Delta t{/tex} , current through the conductor will be {tex}I = \frac{\Delta q}{ \Delta t}{/tex} where, {tex}\Delta q{/tex} = charge drifted through the conductor. The electrical potential energies of the charge {tex}\Delta q{/tex} at M and N are {tex}\Delta U _ { M } = \Delta q V _ { M }{/tex} and {tex}\Delta U _ { N } = \Delta q V _ { N }{/tex} , respectively. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Change in potential energy, {tex}\Delta U = \Delta U _ { N } - \Delta U _ { M }{/tex} As, loss of potential energy = gain in kinetic energy {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \Delta K = - \Delta U = I V \Delta t{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Energy dissipated per unit time (called power loss) is {tex}P = \frac { I V \Delta t } { \Delta t } = V I = I ^ { 2 } R \quad ( \because V = I R ){/tex}
solution: Consider a conductor MN having resistance R. The potentials of the two terminals are suppose V M and V N . Such that, V M - V N = V (As, V M > V N ) At any time interval {tex}\Delta t{/tex} , current through the conductor will be {tex}I = \frac{\Delta q}{ \Delta t}{/tex} where, {tex}\Delta q{/tex} = charge drifted through the conductor. The electrical potential energies of the charge {tex}\Delta q{/tex} at M and N are {tex}\Delta U _ { M } = \Delta q V _ { M }{/tex} and {tex}\Delta U _ { N } = \Delta q V _ { N }{/tex} , respectively. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Change in potential energy, {tex}\Delta U = \Delta U _ { N } - \Delta U _ { M }{/tex} As, loss of potential energy = gain in kinetic energy {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \Delta K = - \Delta U = I V \Delta t{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Energy dissipated per unit time (called power loss) is {tex}P = \frac { I V \Delta t } { \Delta t } = V I = I ^ { 2 } R \quad ( \because V = I R ){/tex}
solution_html:
Consider a conductor MN having resistance R.

The potentials of the two terminals are suppose VM and VN.
Such that, VM - VN = V (As, VM > VN)
At any time interval {tex}\Delta t{/tex}, current through the conductor will be
{tex}I = \frac{\Delta q}{ \Delta t}{/tex}
where, {tex}\Delta q{/tex} = charge drifted through the conductor.
The electrical potential energies of the charge
{tex}\Delta q{/tex} at M and N are {tex}\Delta U _ { M } = \Delta q V _ { M }{/tex} and {tex}\Delta U _ { N } = \Delta q V _ { N }{/tex}, respectively.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Change in potential energy, {tex}\Delta U = \Delta U _ { N } - \Delta U _ { M }{/tex}
As, loss of potential energy = gain in kinetic energy
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \Delta K = - \Delta U = I V \Delta t{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Energy dissipated per unit time (called power loss) is
{tex}P = \frac { I V \Delta t } { \Delta t } = V I = I ^ { 2 } R \quad ( \because V = I R ){/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: For the circuit diagram of a Wheatstone bridge shown in the figure, use Kirchhoff's laws to obtain its balance condition.
question_text: For the circuit diagram of a Wheatstone bridge shown in the figure, use Kirchhoff's laws to obtain its balance condition.
explanation: In balanced Wheatstone bridge if no current flow through galvanometer, that means while applying Kirchhoff's law, we can neglect this path. No current flows through the galvanometer G when circuit is balanced. Applying Kirchhoff's second rule, we have In mesh ABDA, {tex}\therefore{/tex} -I 1 R 1 + (I - I 1 ) R 4 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 R 1 = (I - I 1 ) R 4 .... (i) In mesh BCDB, -I 1 R 2 + (I - I 1 ) R 3 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 R 2 = (I - I 1 ) R 3 ...(ii) On dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get {tex}\frac { I _ { 1 } R _ { 1 } } { I _ { 1 } R _ { 2 } } = \frac { \left( I - I _ { 1 } \right) R _ { 4 } } { \left( I - I _ { 1 } \right) R _ { 3 } }\Rightarrow\; \frac { R _ { 1 } } { R _ { 2 } } = \frac { R _ { 4 } } { R _ { 3 } }{/tex} This is necessary and required balanced condition of balanced Wheatstone bridge.
solution: In balanced Wheatstone bridge if no current flow through galvanometer, that means while applying Kirchhoff's law, we can neglect this path. No current flows through the galvanometer G when circuit is balanced. Applying Kirchhoff's second rule, we have In mesh ABDA, {tex}\therefore{/tex} -I 1 R 1 + (I - I 1 ) R 4 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 R 1 = (I - I 1 ) R 4 .... (i) In mesh BCDB, -I 1 R 2 + (I - I 1 ) R 3 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 R 2 = (I - I 1 ) R 3 ...(ii) On dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get {tex}\frac { I _ { 1 } R _ { 1 } } { I _ { 1 } R _ { 2 } } = \frac { \left( I - I _ { 1 } \right) R _ { 4 } } { \left( I - I _ { 1 } \right) R _ { 3 } }\Rightarrow\; \frac { R _ { 1 } } { R _ { 2 } } = \frac { R _ { 4 } } { R _ { 3 } }{/tex} This is necessary and required balanced condition of balanced Wheatstone bridge.
solution_html:
In balanced Wheatstone bridge if no current flow through galvanometer, that means while applying Kirchhoff's law, we can neglect this path. No current flows through the galvanometer G when circuit is balanced.

Applying Kirchhoff's second rule, we have

In mesh ABDA,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} -I1 R1 + (I - I1) R4 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I1 R1 = (I - I1) R4 ....(i)

In mesh BCDB,
-I1 R2 + (I - I1) R3 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I1 R2 = (I - I1) R3 ...(ii)
On dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}\frac { I _ { 1 } R _ { 1 } } { I _ { 1 } R _ { 2 } } = \frac { \left( I - I _ { 1 } \right) R _ { 4 } } { \left( I - I _ { 1 } \right) R _ { 3 } }\Rightarrow\; \frac { R _ { 1 } } { R _ { 2 } } = \frac { R _ { 4 } } { R _ { 3 } }{/tex}
This is necessary and required balanced condition of balanced Wheatstone bridge.

question_html: State Kirchhoff's rules. Use Kirchhoff's rules to show that no current flows in the given circuit.
question_text: State Kirchhoff's rules. Use Kirchhoff's rules to show that no current flows in the given circuit.
explanation: Kirchoff's laws First law: It states that the sum of all the currents meeting at a point called junction is always zero. Second law: It states that in a closed loop the sum of all the potential differences is always zero. In the diagram, let I current flows in the circuit in the clockwise direction then using Kirchoff's laws (taking clockwise direction positive) +2 + Ir 1 + Ir 2 - 2 = 0 I(r 1 + r 2 ) = 0 or I = 0 as r 1 and r 2 are non zero.
solution: Kirchoff's laws First law: It states that the sum of all the currents meeting at a point called junction is always zero. Second law: It states that in a closed loop the sum of all the potential differences is always zero. In the diagram, let I current flows in the circuit in the clockwise direction then using Kirchoff's laws (taking clockwise direction positive) +2 + Ir 1 + Ir 2 - 2 = 0 I(r 1 + r 2 ) = 0 or I = 0 as r 1 and r 2 are non zero.
solution_html:
Kirchoff's laws
First law: It states that the sum of all the currents meeting at a point called junction is always zero.
Second law: It states that in a closed loop the sum of all the potential differences is always zero.

In the diagram, let I current flows in the circuit in the clockwise direction then using Kirchoff's laws (taking clockwise direction positive)
+2 + Ir1 + Ir2 - 2 = 0
I(r1 + r2) = 0
or I = 0 as r1 and r2 are non zero.

question_html:
A storage battery of emf 8 V, internal resistance 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex}, is being charged by a 120 V d.c. source, using a 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor in series in the circuit. Calculate:

  1. the current in the circuit,
  2. the terminal voltage across the battery during charging, and
  3. chemical energy stored in the battery in 5 minutes.
question_text: A storage battery of emf 8 V, internal resistance 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , is being charged by a 120 V d.c. source, using a 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor in series in the circuit. Calculate: the current in the circuit, the terminal voltage across the battery during charging, and chemical energy stored in the battery in 5 minutes.
explanation: Total emf = 120 - 8 = 112 V Total resistance = 1 + 15 = 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Current, I = {tex}\frac{\text { Total emf }}{\text { Total resistance }}=\frac{112}{16}{/tex} = 7 A Terminal voltage during charging, V = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} + Ir = 8 + 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 15 V Chemical energy stored in the battery in 5 minutes = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} It = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} (5 {tex}\times{/tex} 60) = 16800 J
solution: Total emf = 120 - 8 = 112 V Total resistance = 1 + 15 = 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Current, I = {tex}\frac{\text { Total emf }}{\text { Total resistance }}=\frac{112}{16}{/tex} = 7 A Terminal voltage during charging, V = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} + Ir = 8 + 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 15 V Chemical energy stored in the battery in 5 minutes = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} It = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} (5 {tex}\times{/tex} 60) = 16800 J
solution_html:
Total emf = 120 - 8 = 112 V
Total resistance = 1 + 15 = 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex}

  1. Current, I = {tex}\frac{\text { Total emf }}{\text { Total resistance }}=\frac{112}{16}{/tex} = 7 A
  2. Terminal voltage during charging,
    V = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} + Ir = 8 + 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 15 V
  3. Chemical energy stored in the battery in 5 minutes
    = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex}It = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} (5 {tex}\times{/tex} 60) = 16800 J

subject: Physics
question_html:
Define the conductivity of a conductor and state its SI unit. State and explain the variation of conductivity of

  1. good conductor
  2. ionic conductor with temperature.
question_text: Define the conductivity of a conductor and state its SI unit. State and explain the variation of conductivity of good conductor ionic conductor with temperature.
explanation: The reciprocal of resistivity ( {tex}\rho{/tex} ) of a material is called its conductivity ( {tex}\sigma{/tex} ), i.e., {tex}\sigma=\frac{1}{\rho}{/tex} S.I. unit of conductivity is mho m -1 (or {tex}\Omega^{-1}{/tex} m -1 ). Conductivity of a metallic conductor {tex}\sigma=\frac{1}{\rho}=\frac{n e^{2} \tau}{m}{/tex} . With rise of temperature, the collision of electrons with fixed lattice ions/atoms increases so that relaxation time (T) decreases. Consequently, the conductivity of metals decreases with rise of temperature. Figure represents the variation of conductivity of metal with temperature. Initially the variation of conductivity with temperature is linear and then it is non-linear. Conductivity of ionic conductor increases with increase of temperature because with increase of temperature, the ionic bonds break releasing positive and negative ions which are charge carriers.
solution: The reciprocal of resistivity ( {tex}\rho{/tex} ) of a material is called its conductivity ( {tex}\sigma{/tex} ), i.e., {tex}\sigma=\frac{1}{\rho}{/tex} S.I. unit of conductivity is mho m -1 (or {tex}\Omega^{-1}{/tex} m -1 ). Conductivity of a metallic conductor {tex}\sigma=\frac{1}{\rho}=\frac{n e^{2} \tau}{m}{/tex} . With rise of temperature, the collision of electrons with fixed lattice ions/atoms increases so that relaxation time (T) decreases. Consequently, the conductivity of metals decreases with rise of temperature. Figure represents the variation of conductivity of metal with temperature. Initially the variation of conductivity with temperature is linear and then it is non-linear. Conductivity of ionic conductor increases with increase of temperature because with increase of temperature, the ionic bonds break releasing positive and negative ions which are charge carriers.
solution_html:
The reciprocal of resistivity ({tex}\rho{/tex}) of a material is called its conductivity ({tex}\sigma{/tex}), i.e.,
{tex}\sigma=\frac{1}{\rho}{/tex}
S.I. unit of conductivity is mho m-1 (or {tex}\Omega^{-1}{/tex} m-1).

  1. Conductivity of a metallic conductor {tex}\sigma=\frac{1}{\rho}=\frac{n e^{2} \tau}{m}{/tex}.
    With rise of temperature, the collision of electrons with fixed lattice ions/atoms increases so that relaxation time (T) decreases. Consequently, the conductivity of metals decreases with rise of temperature. Figure represents the variation of conductivity of metal with temperature. Initially the variation of conductivity with temperature is linear and then it is non-linear.
  2. Conductivity of ionic conductor increases with increase of temperature because with increase of temperature, the ionic bonds break releasing positive and negative ions which are charge carriers.

subject: Physics
question_html: Define resistivity of a conductor. Plot a graph showing the variation of resistivity with temperature for a metallic conductor. How does one explain such a behaviour, using the mathematical expression of the resistivity of a material.
question_text: Define resistivity of a conductor. Plot a graph showing the variation of resistivity with temperature for a metallic conductor. How does one explain such a behaviour, using the mathematical expression of the resistivity of a material.
explanation: The resistivity of a conductor is defined as the resistance offered by the material per unit length for a unit cross-section. It is denoted by the symbol {tex}\rho{/tex} . The resistivity of a metallic conductor is approximately given by {tex}\rho{/tex} T = {tex}\rho_0{/tex} [1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} (T - T 0 )] ...(i) where {tex}\rho{/tex} T is the resistivity at a temperature T and {tex}\rho_0{/tex} is the same at a reference temperature T 0 . {tex}\alpha{/tex} is called the temperature co-efficient of resistivity.
solution: The resistivity of a conductor is defined as the resistance offered by the material per unit length for a unit cross-section. It is denoted by the symbol {tex}\rho{/tex} . The resistivity of a metallic conductor is approximately given by {tex}\rho{/tex} T = {tex}\rho_0{/tex} [1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} (T - T 0 )] ...(i) where {tex}\rho{/tex} T is the resistivity at a temperature T and {tex}\rho_0{/tex} is the same at a reference temperature T 0 . {tex}\alpha{/tex} is called the temperature co-efficient of resistivity.
solution_html:
The resistivity of a conductor is defined as the resistance offered by the material per unit length for a unit cross-section. It is denoted by the symbol {tex}\rho{/tex}.

 The resistivity of a metallic conductor is approximately given by
{tex}\rho{/tex}T ={tex}\rho_0{/tex}[1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex}(T - T0)] ...(i)
where {tex}\rho{/tex}T is the resistivity at a temperature T and {tex}\rho_0{/tex} is the same at a reference temperature T0. {tex}\alpha{/tex} is called the temperature co-efficient of resistivity.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A number of identical cells, n, each of emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex}, internal resistance r connected in series are charged by a d.c. source of emf {tex}\varepsilon'{/tex}, using a resistor R.

  1. Draw the circuit arrangement
  2. Deduce the expressions for
    1. the charging current and
    2. the potential difference across the combination of the cells.
question_text: A number of identical cells, n, each of emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} , internal resistance r connected in series are charged by a d.c. source of emf {tex}\varepsilon'{/tex} , using a resistor R. Draw the circuit arrangement Deduce the expressions for the charging current and the potential difference across the combination of the cells.
explanation: The circuit arrangement for charging n cells is shown below: Net emf = {tex}\varepsilon^\prime{/tex} - n {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} Total resistance = R + nr Charging current, I = {tex}\frac{\text { Net emf }}{\text { Total resistance }}=\frac{\varepsilon^{\prime} \ - \ n{\varepsilon}}{R \ + \ n r}{/tex} P.D. across each cell = B + Ir = {tex}\varepsilon \ + \ \frac{\varepsilon^{\prime} \ - \ n \varepsilon}{R \ + \ n}=\frac{\varepsilon R \ - \ \varepsilon^{\prime} r}{R \ + \ n}{/tex} P.D. across the series combination of n cells, V = n {tex}(\varepsilon \ + \ I r)=n\left(\frac{\varepsilon_{R} \ - \ \varepsilon^{\prime} r}{R \ + \ n}\right){/tex}
solution: The circuit arrangement for charging n cells is shown below: Net emf = {tex}\varepsilon^\prime{/tex} - n {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} Total resistance = R + nr Charging current, I = {tex}\frac{\text { Net emf }}{\text { Total resistance }}=\frac{\varepsilon^{\prime} \ - \ n{\varepsilon}}{R \ + \ n r}{/tex} P.D. across each cell = B + Ir = {tex}\varepsilon \ + \ \frac{\varepsilon^{\prime} \ - \ n \varepsilon}{R \ + \ n}=\frac{\varepsilon R \ - \ \varepsilon^{\prime} r}{R \ + \ n}{/tex} P.D. across the series combination of n cells, V = n {tex}(\varepsilon \ + \ I r)=n\left(\frac{\varepsilon_{R} \ - \ \varepsilon^{\prime} r}{R \ + \ n}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
1. The circuit arrangement for charging n cells is shown below:
  2. Net emf = {tex}\varepsilon^\prime{/tex} - n{tex}\varepsilon{/tex}
    Total resistance = R + nr
    Charging current, I = {tex}\frac{\text { Net emf }}{\text { Total resistance }}=\frac{\varepsilon^{\prime} \ - \ n{\varepsilon}}{R \ + \ n r}{/tex}
  3. P.D. across each cell = B + Ir
    = {tex}\varepsilon \ + \ \frac{\varepsilon^{\prime} \ - \ n \varepsilon}{R \ + \ n}=\frac{\varepsilon R \ - \ \varepsilon^{\prime} r}{R \ + \ n}{/tex}
    P.D. across the series combination of n cells,
    V = n{tex}(\varepsilon \ + \ I r)=n\left(\frac{\varepsilon_{R} \ - \ \varepsilon^{\prime} r}{R \ + \ n}\right){/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Calculate the temperature at which the resistance of a conductor becomes 20% more than its resistance at 27oC. The value of the temperature coefficient of resistance of the conductor is 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 K-1.
question_text: Calculate the temperature at which the resistance of a conductor becomes 20% more than its resistance at 27 o C. The value of the temperature coefficient of resistance of the conductor is 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 K -1 .
explanation: If R 1 is the resistance of the conductor at the temperature {tex}\theta_1{/tex} , then its resistance at the temperature {tex}\theta_2{/tex} is given by {tex}\mathrm{R}_{2}=\mathrm{R}_{1}\left[1+\alpha\left(\theta_{2}-\theta_{1}\right)\right]{/tex} or {tex}\theta_{2}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{R}_{2}-\mathrm{R}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{R}_{1} \alpha}+\theta_{1}{/tex} Here, {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 K -1 ; {tex}\theta _1{/tex} = 27 o C and {tex}\mathrm{R}_{2}=\mathrm{R}_{1} \times \frac{120}{100}=1 \cdot 2 \mathrm{R}_{1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\theta_{2}=\frac{\left(1 \cdot 2 \mathrm{R}_{1}-\mathrm{R}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{R}_{1} \times 2 \cdot 0 \times 10^{-4}}+27=\frac{0 \cdot 2}{2 \cdot 0 \times 10^{-4}}+27{/tex} = 1000 + 27 = 1027 o C
solution: If R 1 is the resistance of the conductor at the temperature {tex}\theta_1{/tex} , then its resistance at the temperature {tex}\theta_2{/tex} is given by {tex}\mathrm{R}_{2}=\mathrm{R}_{1}\left[1+\alpha\left(\theta_{2}-\theta_{1}\right)\right]{/tex} or {tex}\theta_{2}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{R}_{2}-\mathrm{R}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{R}_{1} \alpha}+\theta_{1}{/tex} Here, {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 K -1 ; {tex}\theta _1{/tex} = 27 o C and {tex}\mathrm{R}_{2}=\mathrm{R}_{1} \times \frac{120}{100}=1 \cdot 2 \mathrm{R}_{1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\theta_{2}=\frac{\left(1 \cdot 2 \mathrm{R}_{1}-\mathrm{R}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{R}_{1} \times 2 \cdot 0 \times 10^{-4}}+27=\frac{0 \cdot 2}{2 \cdot 0 \times 10^{-4}}+27{/tex} = 1000 + 27 = 1027 o C
solution_html:
If R1 is the resistance of the conductor at the temperature {tex}\theta_1{/tex}, then its resistance at the temperature {tex}\theta_2{/tex} is given by
{tex}\mathrm{R}_{2}=\mathrm{R}_{1}\left[1+\alpha\left(\theta_{2}-\theta_{1}\right)\right]{/tex}
or {tex}\theta_{2}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{R}_{2}-\mathrm{R}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{R}_{1} \alpha}+\theta_{1}{/tex}
Here, {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 K-1; {tex}\theta _1{/tex} = 27oC
and {tex}\mathrm{R}_{2}=\mathrm{R}_{1} \times \frac{120}{100}=1 \cdot 2 \mathrm{R}_{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\theta_{2}=\frac{\left(1 \cdot 2 \mathrm{R}_{1}-\mathrm{R}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{R}_{1} \times 2 \cdot 0 \times 10^{-4}}+27=\frac{0 \cdot 2}{2 \cdot 0 \times 10^{-4}}+27{/tex}
= 1000 + 27 = 1027oC

subject: Physics
question_html: Prove that the current density of a metallic conductor is directly proportional to the drift speed of electrons.
question_text: Prove that the current density of a metallic conductor is directly proportional to the drift speed of electrons.
explanation: Consider a conductor of length l and area of cross section A having n electrons per unit length, as shown in the figure. Volume of the conductor = Al Total number of electrons in the Conductor = Volume {tex}\times{/tex} electron density = Aln If C is the charge of an electron, then total charge contained in the conductor, Q = en Al Let the potential difference V is applied across the conductor. The resulting electric field in the conductor is given by {tex}E = \frac{V}{l}{/tex} Under the influence this field E, free electrons begin to drift in a direction opposite to that of the field. Time taken by electrons to cross over the conductor is {tex}t = \frac{l}{{{v_d}}}{/tex} Where v d is the drift velocity of electrons. Therefore, current through the conductor is given by {tex}I = \frac{Q}{t} = \frac{{enAl}}{{l/{v_d}}}{/tex} or {tex}I = neA{v_d} \Rightarrow \frac{I}{A} = {n_e}{v_d}{/tex} or J = n e v d {tex} \Rightarrow J \propto {v_d}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} n, e, A are all constant] Thus, current density is proportional to drift velocity.
solution: Consider a conductor of length l and area of cross section A having n electrons per unit length, as shown in the figure. Volume of the conductor = Al Total number of electrons in the Conductor = Volume {tex}\times{/tex} electron density = Aln If C is the charge of an electron, then total charge contained in the conductor, Q = en Al Let the potential difference V is applied across the conductor. The resulting electric field in the conductor is given by {tex}E = \frac{V}{l}{/tex} Under the influence this field E, free electrons begin to drift in a direction opposite to that of the field. Time taken by electrons to cross over the conductor is {tex}t = \frac{l}{{{v_d}}}{/tex} Where v d is the drift velocity of electrons. Therefore, current through the conductor is given by {tex}I = \frac{Q}{t} = \frac{{enAl}}{{l/{v_d}}}{/tex} or {tex}I = neA{v_d} \Rightarrow \frac{I}{A} = {n_e}{v_d}{/tex} or J = n e v d {tex} \Rightarrow J \propto {v_d}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} n, e, A are all constant] Thus, current density is proportional to drift velocity.
solution_html:
Consider a conductor of length l and area of cross section A having n electrons per unit length, as shown in the figure.
Volume of the conductor = Al
Total number of electrons in the Conductor = Volume {tex}\times{/tex}electron density = Aln
If C is the charge of an electron, then total charge contained in the conductor,
Q = en Al
Let the potential difference V is applied across the conductor. The resulting electric field in the conductor is given by
{tex}E = \frac{V}{l}{/tex}

Under the influence this field E, free electrons begin to drift in a direction opposite to that of the field. Time taken by electrons to cross over the conductor is
{tex}t = \frac{l}{{{v_d}}}{/tex}
Where vd is the drift velocity of electrons. Therefore, current through the conductor is given by
{tex}I = \frac{Q}{t} = \frac{{enAl}}{{l/{v_d}}}{/tex}
or {tex}I = neA{v_d} \Rightarrow \frac{I}{A} = {n_e}{v_d}{/tex} or J = nevd
{tex} \Rightarrow J \propto {v_d}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} n, e, A are all constant]
Thus, current density is proportional to drift velocity.

subject: Physics
question_html:
Explain, giving reasons, how the internal resistance of a cell changes in the following cases:

  1. When concentration of the electrolyte is increased.
  2. When area of the anode is decreased.
  3. When temperature of the electrolyte is increased.
question_text: Explain, giving reasons, how the internal resistance of a cell changes in the following cases: When concentration of the electrolyte is increased. When area of the anode is decreased. When temperature of the electrolyte is increased.
explanation: When concentration of the electrolyte is increased, internal resistance of the cell increases. Reason: in more concentrated electrolyte, interionic attractions increase and the movements of the ions become difficult. When area of the anode is decreased, internal resistance of the cell increases. Reason: Lesser area of the anode decreases its tendency to attract oppositely charged ions. When temperature of the electrolyte is increased, internal resistance decreases. Reason: Both interionic attractions and viscous forces decrease at higher temperature.
solution: When concentration of the electrolyte is increased, internal resistance of the cell increases. Reason: in more concentrated electrolyte, interionic attractions increase and the movements of the ions become difficult. When area of the anode is decreased, internal resistance of the cell increases. Reason: Lesser area of the anode decreases its tendency to attract oppositely charged ions. When temperature of the electrolyte is increased, internal resistance decreases. Reason: Both interionic attractions and viscous forces decrease at higher temperature.
solution_html:
1. When concentration of the electrolyte is increased, internal resistance of the cell increases.
    Reason: in more concentrated electrolyte, interionic attractions increase and the movements of the ions become difficult.
  2. When area of the anode is decreased, internal resistance of the cell increases.
    Reason: Lesser area of the anode decreases its tendency to attract oppositely charged ions.
  3. When temperature of the electrolyte is increased, internal resistance decreases.
    Reason: Both interionic attractions and viscous forces decrease at higher temperature.

subject: Physics
question_html: Two wires X, Y have the same resistivity, but their cross-sectional areas are in the ratio 2 : 3 and lengths in the ratio 1 : 2. They are first connected in series and then in parallel to a d.c. source. Find out the ratio of the drift speeds of the electrons in the two wires for the two cases.
question_text: Two wires X, Y have the same resistivity, but their cross-sectional areas are in the ratio 2 : 3 and lengths in the ratio 1 : 2. They are first connected in series and then in parallel to a d.c. source. Find out the ratio of the drift speeds of the electrons in the two wires for the two cases.
explanation: Current, I = enAv d In series, current I is constant. So, v d {tex}\propto \frac{1}{A}{/tex} {tex}\frac{v_{d}(X)}{v_{d}(Y)}=\frac{A_{Y}}{A_{X}}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} = 3 : 2 In parallel, V = IR = constant {tex}\therefore \quad I \propto \frac{1}{R}{/tex} But {tex}R \propto \frac{l}{A}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I \propto \frac{A}{l}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{I_{X}}{I_{Y}}=\frac{A_{X}}{A_{Y}} \cdot \frac{l_{Y}}{l_{X}}=\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{2}{1}=\frac{4}{3}{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac{I_{X}}{I_{Y}}=\frac{e n A_{X} v_{d}(X)}{e n A_{Y} v_{d}(Y)}=\frac{2 v_{d}(X)}{3 v_{d}(Y)}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac{2 v_{d}(X)}{3 v_{d}(Y)}=\frac{4}{3}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{v_{d}(X)}{v_{d}(Y)}=\frac{2}{1}{/tex} = 2 : 1
solution: Current, I = enAv d In series, current I is constant. So, v d {tex}\propto \frac{1}{A}{/tex} {tex}\frac{v_{d}(X)}{v_{d}(Y)}=\frac{A_{Y}}{A_{X}}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} = 3 : 2 In parallel, V = IR = constant {tex}\therefore \quad I \propto \frac{1}{R}{/tex} But {tex}R \propto \frac{l}{A}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I \propto \frac{A}{l}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{I_{X}}{I_{Y}}=\frac{A_{X}}{A_{Y}} \cdot \frac{l_{Y}}{l_{X}}=\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{2}{1}=\frac{4}{3}{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac{I_{X}}{I_{Y}}=\frac{e n A_{X} v_{d}(X)}{e n A_{Y} v_{d}(Y)}=\frac{2 v_{d}(X)}{3 v_{d}(Y)}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac{2 v_{d}(X)}{3 v_{d}(Y)}=\frac{4}{3}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{v_{d}(X)}{v_{d}(Y)}=\frac{2}{1}{/tex} = 2 : 1
solution_html:
Current, I = enAvd

  1. In series, current I is constant. So, vd {tex}\propto \frac{1}{A}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{v_{d}(X)}{v_{d}(Y)}=\frac{A_{Y}}{A_{X}}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} = 3 : 2
  2. In parallel, V = IR = constant
    {tex}\therefore \quad I \propto \frac{1}{R}{/tex}
    But {tex}R \propto \frac{l}{A}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad I \propto \frac{A}{l}{/tex}
    or {tex}\frac{I_{X}}{I_{Y}}=\frac{A_{X}}{A_{Y}} \cdot \frac{l_{Y}}{l_{X}}=\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{2}{1}=\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
    Also, {tex}\frac{I_{X}}{I_{Y}}=\frac{e n A_{X} v_{d}(X)}{e n A_{Y} v_{d}(Y)}=\frac{2 v_{d}(X)}{3 v_{d}(Y)}{/tex}
    Hence, {tex}\frac{2 v_{d}(X)}{3 v_{d}(Y)}=\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
    or {tex}\frac{v_{d}(X)}{v_{d}(Y)}=\frac{2}{1}{/tex} = 2 : 1

subject: Physics
question_html: Two cells of emfs {tex}\varepsilon_1{/tex}, and {tex}\varepsilon_2{/tex}, are connected together in two ways shown here. The balance points in a given potentiometer experiment for these two combinations of cells are found to be at 351.0 cm and 70.2 cm respectively. Calculate the ratio of the emfs of the two cells.
question_text: Two cells of emfs {tex}\varepsilon_1{/tex} , and {tex}\varepsilon_2{/tex} , are connected together in two ways shown here. The balance points in a given potentiometer experiment for these two combinations of cells are found to be at 351.0 cm and 70.2 cm respectively. Calculate the ratio of the emfs of the two cells.
explanation: {tex}\frac{E_1 + E_2}{E_1 - E_1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{351}{70.2}{/tex} = 5 E 1 + E 2 = 5E 1 - 5E 2 6E 2 = 5E 1 {tex}\frac{E_1}{E_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac32{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{E_1 + E_2}{E_1 - E_1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{351}{70.2}{/tex} = 5 E 1 + E 2 = 5E 1 - 5E 2 6E 2 = 5E 1 {tex}\frac{E_1}{E_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac32{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{E_1 + E_2}{E_1 - E_1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{351}{70.2}{/tex} = 5
E1 + E2 = 5E1 - 5E2
6E2 = 5E1
{tex}\frac{E_1}{E_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac32{/tex}

question_html:
1. Write the sequence of colours present in a carbon resistor having value of 62 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 {tex}\Omega \pm 20 \%{/tex}.
  2. Write the nature of the material having resistivity of the order of
    1. 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 {tex}\Omega m{/tex} and
    2. 1015 {tex}\Omega m{/tex} at 0oC
question_text: Write the sequence of colours present in a carbon resistor having value of 62 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 {tex}\Omega \pm 20 \%{/tex} . Write the nature of the material having resistivity of the order of 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 {tex}\Omega m{/tex} and 10 15 {tex}\Omega m{/tex} at 0 o C
explanation: According to colour code of carbon resistance, the colour of bands are blue and red respectively and corresponding to multiplier 10 4 , the colour of band is yellow. Finally, corresponding to tolerance of 20%, there is no colour band. Therefore, the resistance of {tex}62 \times 10^{4} \Omega \pm 20 \%{/tex} will be represented by the bands of colour blue, red and yellow. The resistivity of 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} m corresponds to that of copper. The resistivity of 10 15 {tex}\Omega m{/tex} corresponds to that of mica and rubber.
solution: According to colour code of carbon resistance, the colour of bands are blue and red respectively and corresponding to multiplier 10 4 , the colour of band is yellow. Finally, corresponding to tolerance of 20%, there is no colour band. Therefore, the resistance of {tex}62 \times 10^{4} \Omega \pm 20 \%{/tex} will be represented by the bands of colour blue, red and yellow. The resistivity of 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} m corresponds to that of copper. The resistivity of 10 15 {tex}\Omega m{/tex} corresponds to that of mica and rubber.
solution_html:
1. According to colour code of carbon resistance, the colour of bands are blue and red respectively and corresponding to multiplier 104, the colour of band is yellow. Finally, corresponding to tolerance of 20%, there is no colour band.
    Therefore, the resistance of {tex}62 \times 10^{4} \Omega \pm 20 \%{/tex} will be represented by the bands of colour blue, red and yellow.
    1. The resistivity of 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 {tex}\Omega{/tex}m corresponds to that of copper.
    2. The resistivity of 1015 {tex}\Omega m{/tex} corresponds to that of mica and rubber.

question_html: Three resistances P, Q, R each of 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and an unknown resistance S form the four arms of a Wheatstone's bridge circuit. When the resistance of 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is connected in parallel to S, the bridge gets balanced. What is the value of S?
question_text: Three resistances P, Q, R each of 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and an unknown resistance S form the four arms of a Wheatstone's bridge circuit. When the resistance of 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is connected in parallel to S, the bridge gets balanced. What is the value of S?

options.explanation: The fourth arm has resistances S and 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} in parallel with equivalent resistance = {tex}\frac{6 S}{6 \ + \ S} \Omega{/tex} For the balanced Wheatstone bridge, {tex}\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{6 S}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{2}{2}=\frac{2(6 \ + \ S)}{6 S}{/tex} or 3S = 6 + S or S = 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex}

explanation: The fourth arm has resistances S and 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} in parallel with equivalent resistance = {tex}\frac{6 S}{6 \ + \ S} \Omega{/tex} For the balanced Wheatstone bridge, {tex}\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{6 S}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{2}{2}=\frac{2(6 \ + \ S)}{6 S}{/tex} or 3S = 6 + S or S = 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex}

question_text: The ratio of masses of three wires is 1 : 2 : 3 and that of their lengths is 3 : 2 : 1. If the wires are made of same material, the ratio of their resistance will be:

options.explanation: Mass = Volume {tex}\times{/tex} density = AId or A = {tex}\frac{m}{i d}{/tex} {tex}\therefore R=\rho \frac{l}{A}=\rho \frac{l}{\frac m{ l d}}=\rho \frac{l^{2} d}{m}{/tex} i.e., {tex}R \propto \frac{l^{2}}{m}{/tex} Given m 1 : m 2 : m 3 = 1 : 2 : 3 and l : l : l = 3 : 2 : 1 {tex}\therefore R_{1}: R_{2} : R_{3}=\frac{3^{2}}{1}: \frac{2^{2}}{2}: \frac{1^{2}}{3}=9: 2: \frac{1}{3}{/tex} = 27 : 6 : 1

explanation: Mass = Volume {tex}\times{/tex} density = AId or A = {tex}\frac{m}{i d}{/tex} {tex}\therefore R=\rho \frac{l}{A}=\rho \frac{l}{\frac m{ l d}}=\rho \frac{l^{2} d}{m}{/tex} i.e., {tex}R \propto \frac{l^{2}}{m}{/tex} Given m 1 : m 2 : m 3 = 1 : 2 : 3 and l : l : l = 3 : 2 : 1 {tex}\therefore R_{1}: R_{2} : R_{3}=\frac{3^{2}}{1}: \frac{2^{2}}{2}: \frac{1^{2}}{3}=9: 2: \frac{1}{3}{/tex} = 27 : 6 : 1

question_html: Two cells E1 and E2 in the circuit shown in Figure. have e.m.f. of 5 V and 9 V and internal resistance of 0.3{tex}\Omega{/tex} and 1.2{tex}\Omega{/tex} respectively. Calculate the value of current through the resistance of 3{tex} \Omega{/tex}
question_text: Two cells E 1 and E 2 in the circuit shown in Figure. have e.m.f. of 5 V and 9 V and internal resistance of 0.3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 1.2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} respectively. Calculate the value of current through the resistance of 3 {tex} \Omega{/tex}
explanation: Let R' be the resistance of the parallel combination of R 1 and R 2 . Then, {tex}R^{\prime}=\frac{R_{1} \times R_{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}=\frac{6 \times 3}{6+3}=2 \Omega{/tex} E = E 2 - E 1 = 9 - 5 = 4V The current through the main circuit is given by {tex}I=\frac{E}{R+R^{\prime}+r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{4 \cdot 5+2+0 \cdot 3+1 \cdot 2}=0 \cdot 5{/tex} Hence, the current through the 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance {tex}=I \times \frac{R_{1}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}=0 \cdot 5 \times \frac{6}{6+3}=0 \cdot 33 A{/tex}
solution: Let R' be the resistance of the parallel combination of R 1 and R 2 . Then, {tex}R^{\prime}=\frac{R_{1} \times R_{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}=\frac{6 \times 3}{6+3}=2 \Omega{/tex} E = E 2 - E 1 = 9 - 5 = 4V The current through the main circuit is given by {tex}I=\frac{E}{R+R^{\prime}+r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{4 \cdot 5+2+0 \cdot 3+1 \cdot 2}=0 \cdot 5{/tex} Hence, the current through the 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance {tex}=I \times \frac{R_{1}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}=0 \cdot 5 \times \frac{6}{6+3}=0 \cdot 33 A{/tex}
solution_html:
Let R' be the resistance of the parallel combination of R1 and R2. Then,
{tex}R^{\prime}=\frac{R_{1} \times R_{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}=\frac{6 \times 3}{6+3}=2 \Omega{/tex}
E = E2 - E1 = 9 - 5 = 4V
The current through the main circuit is given by
{tex}I=\frac{E}{R+R^{\prime}+r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4}{4 \cdot 5+2+0 \cdot 3+1 \cdot 2}=0 \cdot 5{/tex}
Hence, the current through the 3{tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance
{tex}=I \times \frac{R_{1}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}=0 \cdot 5 \times \frac{6}{6+3}=0 \cdot 33 A{/tex}

question_html: The variation of potential difference V with length l in case of two potentiometers X and Y is as shown in the Figure. Which one of these two will you prefer for comparing e.m.fs. of two cells and why?
question_text: The variation of potential difference V with length l in case of two potentiometers X and Y is as shown in the Figure. Which one of these two will you prefer for comparing e.m.fs. of two cells and why?
explanation: A potentiometer is said to be more sensitive, if fall of potential per unit length along the wire i.e. potential gradient {tex}\left(\frac{d \mathrm{V}}{d l}\right){/tex} is small. The slope of V-l graph i.e. {tex}\frac{dV}{dl}{/tex} is smaller for the wire Y than that for the wire X. Therefore, potentiometer using the wire Y will be preferred for comparing e.m.fs. of the two cells.
solution: A potentiometer is said to be more sensitive, if fall of potential per unit length along the wire i.e. potential gradient {tex}\left(\frac{d \mathrm{V}}{d l}\right){/tex} is small. The slope of V-l graph i.e. {tex}\frac{dV}{dl}{/tex} is smaller for the wire Y than that for the wire X. Therefore, potentiometer using the wire Y will be preferred for comparing e.m.fs. of the two cells.
solution_html: A potentiometer is said to be more sensitive, if fall of potential per unit length along the wire i.e. potential gradient {tex}\left(\frac{d \mathrm{V}}{d l}\right){/tex} is small. The slope of V-l graph i.e. {tex}\frac{dV}{dl}{/tex} is smaller for the wire Y than that for the wire X. Therefore, potentiometer using the wire Y will be preferred for comparing e.m.fs. of the two cells.

explanation: Power, P = I 2 R {tex}\therefore \frac{\Delta P}{P} \times 100=2 \frac{\Delta I}{I} \times 100+\frac{\Delta R}{R} \times 100{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1% + 0 = 2%

question_html: 3 identical bulbs are connected in series and these together dissipate a power P. If now the bulbs are connected in parallel, then the power dissipated will be:
question_text: 3 identical bulbs are connected in series and these together dissipate a power P. If now the bulbs are connected in parallel, then the power dissipated will be:

options.explanation: When the bulbs are connected in series, P = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{R_{s}}=\frac{V^{2}}{3 R}{/tex} When the bulbs are connected in parallel, {tex}P=\frac{V^{2}}{R_{p}}=\frac{V^{2}}{\frac R 3}=3 \frac{V^{2}}{R}=3 \times 3 P{/tex} = 9P

explanation: When the bulbs are connected in series, P = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{R_{s}}=\frac{V^{2}}{3 R}{/tex} When the bulbs are connected in parallel, {tex}P=\frac{V^{2}}{R_{p}}=\frac{V^{2}}{\frac R 3}=3 \frac{V^{2}}{R}=3 \times 3 P{/tex} = 9P

question_html:
Two cells of emfs 1.5 V and 2.0 V and internal resistances 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 2{tex}\Omega{/tex} respectively are connected in parallel so as to send current in the same direction through an external resistance of 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex}.

  1. Draw the circuit diagram.
  2. Using Kirchhoff's laws, calculate :
    1. current through each branch of the circuit.
    2. p.d. across the 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance.
question_text: Two cells of emfs 1.5 V and 2.0 V and internal resistances 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} respectively are connected in parallel so as to send current in the same direction through an external resistance of 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . Draw the circuit diagram. Using Kirchhoff's laws, calculate : current through each branch of the circuit. p.d. across the 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance.
explanation: The circuit diagram is shown in Figure Let I 1 and I 2 be the currents as shown in Figure. Using Kirchhoff's second law for the loop AFCBA, we get 2I 2 - 1I 1 = {tex}\varepsilon_{2}-\varepsilon_{1}{/tex} = 2 - 1.5 or 2I 2 - I 1 =0.5 or For loop CFEDC, we have 1I 1 + 5(I 1 + I 2 ) = {tex}\varepsilon_{1}{/tex} = 1.5 or 5I 2 + 6I 2 = 1.5 Solving equations (i) and (ii), we get {tex}I_{1}=\frac{1}{34} \mathrm{~A}, \quad I_{2}=\frac{9}{34} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current through branch BA, {tex}I_{1}=\frac{1}{34} \mathbf{A}{/tex} Current through branch CF, {tex}I_{2}=\frac{9}{34} \mathbf{A}{/tex} Current through branch DE, I 1 + I 2 = {tex}\frac{10}{34} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} P.D. across the 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance = (I 1 + I 2 ) {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = {tex}\frac{10}{34} \times{/tex} 5 V = 1.47 V
solution: The circuit diagram is shown in Figure Let I 1 and I 2 be the currents as shown in Figure. Using Kirchhoff's second law for the loop AFCBA, we get 2I 2 - 1I 1 = {tex}\varepsilon_{2}-\varepsilon_{1}{/tex} = 2 - 1.5 or 2I 2 - I 1 =0.5 or For loop CFEDC, we have 1I 1 + 5(I 1 + I 2 ) = {tex}\varepsilon_{1}{/tex} = 1.5 or 5I 2 + 6I 2 = 1.5 Solving equations (i) and (ii), we get {tex}I_{1}=\frac{1}{34} \mathrm{~A}, \quad I_{2}=\frac{9}{34} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current through branch BA, {tex}I_{1}=\frac{1}{34} \mathbf{A}{/tex} Current through branch CF, {tex}I_{2}=\frac{9}{34} \mathbf{A}{/tex} Current through branch DE, I 1 + I 2 = {tex}\frac{10}{34} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} P.D. across the 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance = (I 1 + I 2 ) {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = {tex}\frac{10}{34} \times{/tex} 5 V = 1.47 V
solution_html:
1. The circuit diagram is shown in Figure
    1. Let I1 and I2 be the currents as shown in Figure. Using Kirchhoff's second law for the loop AFCBA, we get
      2I2 - 1I1 = {tex}\varepsilon_{2}-\varepsilon_{1}{/tex} = 2 - 1.5
      or 2I2 - I1 =0.5
      or
      For loop CFEDC, we have
      1I1 + 5(I1 + I2) = {tex}\varepsilon_{1}{/tex}= 1.5
      or 5I2 + 6I2 = 1.5
      Solving equations (i) and (ii), we get
      {tex}I_{1}=\frac{1}{34} \mathrm{~A}, \quad I_{2}=\frac{9}{34} \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
      {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current through branch BA,
      {tex}I_{1}=\frac{1}{34} \mathbf{A}{/tex}
      Current through branch CF,
      {tex}I_{2}=\frac{9}{34} \mathbf{A}{/tex}
      Current through branch DE,
      I1 + I2 = {tex}\frac{10}{34} \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
    2. P.D. across the 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance
      = (I1 + I2) {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = {tex}\frac{10}{34} \times{/tex} 5 V = 1.47 V

question_html: Define the terms resistivity and conductivity and state their SI units. Draw a graph showing the variation of resistivity with temperature for a typical semiconductor.
question_text: Define the terms resistivity and conductivity and state their SI units. Draw a graph showing the variation of resistivity with temperature for a typical semiconductor.
explanation: Resistivity: The resistance of a material of a conductor of unit lenght and unit area of cross section is called resistivity. Sl unit- Wm Conductivity: The reciprocal of resistivity of the material is called conductivity. It is represented by 'S'. SI unit: ohm/m
solution: Resistivity: The resistance of a material of a conductor of unit lenght and unit area of cross section is called resistivity. Sl unit- Wm Conductivity: The reciprocal of resistivity of the material is called conductivity. It is represented by 'S'. SI unit: ohm/m
solution_html:
Resistivity: The resistance of a material of a conductor of unit lenght and unit area of cross section is called resistivity.
Sl unit- Wm
Conductivity: The reciprocal of resistivity of the material is called conductivity.
It is represented by 'S'.
SI unit: ohm/m

question_html: A battery is charged at a potential of 15 V for 8 hours when the current flowing is 10 A. The battery on discharge supplies a current of 5 A for 15 hours. The mean terminal voltage during discharge is 14V. The watt-hour efficiency of the battery is:
question_text: A battery is charged at a potential of 15 V for 8 hours when the current flowing is 10 A. The battery on discharge supplies a current of 5 A for 15 hours. The mean terminal voltage during discharge is 14V. The watt-hour efficiency of the battery is:

options.explanation: Input energy when the battery is charged = VIt = 15 V {tex}\times{/tex} 10 A {tex}\times{/tex} 8 h = 1200 Wh The energy released when the battery is discharged = 14 V {tex}\times{/tex} 5 A {tex}\times{/tex} 15 h = 1050 Wh Watt-hour efficiency of the battery = {tex}\frac{\text { Energy output }}{\text { Energy input }}=\frac{1050}{1200}{/tex} = 0.875 = 87.5 %

explanation: Input energy when the battery is charged = VIt = 15 V {tex}\times{/tex} 10 A {tex}\times{/tex} 8 h = 1200 Wh The energy released when the battery is discharged = 14 V {tex}\times{/tex} 5 A {tex}\times{/tex} 15 h = 1050 Wh Watt-hour efficiency of the battery = {tex}\frac{\text { Energy output }}{\text { Energy input }}=\frac{1050}{1200}{/tex} = 0.875 = 87.5 %

question_text: In India, electricity is supplied for domestic use at 220 V. It is supplied at 110 V in the U.S.A. If the resistance of a 60 W bulb for use in India is R, the resistance of a 60 W bulb for use in the U.S.A. will be:

question_html: Two 120 V light bulbs, one of 25 W and other of 200 W were connected in series across a 240 V line. One bulb burnt out almost instantaneously. Which one was burnt and why?
question_text: Two 120 V light bulbs, one of 25 W and other of 200 W were connected in series across a 240 V line. One bulb burnt out almost instantaneously. Which one was burnt and why?
explanation: As R = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{P}{/tex} , so 25 watt bulb has more resistance. In the series circuit, same current flows through both the bulbs. The 25 W bulb develops more heat (H = I 2 Rt) and hence burns out almost instantaneously.
solution: As R = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{P}{/tex} , so 25 watt bulb has more resistance. In the series circuit, same current flows through both the bulbs. The 25 W bulb develops more heat (H = I 2 Rt) and hence burns out almost instantaneously.
solution_html: As R = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{P}{/tex}, so 25 watt bulb has more resistance. In the series circuit, same current flows through both the bulbs. The 25 W bulb develops more heat (H = I2Rt) and hence burns out almost instantaneously.

question_html: Eels are able to generate current with biological cells called electro-plaques. The electro-plaques in an eel are arranged in 100 rows, each row stretching horizontally along the body of the fish containing 5000 electro-plaques. The arrangement is suggestively shown below. Each electro-plaque has an emf of 0.15 V and internal resistance of 0.25 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. The water surrounding the eel completes a circuit between the head and its tail. If the water surrounding it has a resistance of 500 {tex}\Omega{/tex}, the current an eel can produce in water is about
question_text: Eels are able to generate current with biological cells called electro-plaques. The electro-plaques in an eel are arranged in 100 rows, each row stretching horizontally along the body of the fish containing 5000 electro-plaques. The arrangement is suggestively shown below. Each electro-plaque has an emf of 0.15 V and internal resistance of 0.25 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . The water surrounding the eel completes a circuit between the head and its tail. If the water surrounding it has a resistance of 500 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , the current an eel can produce in water is about

options.explanation: In each row, all the 5000 electro-plaques are connected in series. Net emf for each row = 0.15 {tex}\times{/tex} 5000 = 750 V Net resistance of each row = 0.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 5000 = 1250 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Now all 100 rows are connected in parallel. Net emf for the network = 750 V The equivalent resistance of 100 rows = {tex}\frac{1250}{100}=12.50 \Omega{/tex} External resistance = 500 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Current through 500 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance, I = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{R+r}=\frac{750}{500 \ + \ 12.5}{/tex} = 1.46 A = 1.5 A

explanation: In each row, all the 5000 electro-plaques are connected in series. Net emf for each row = 0.15 {tex}\times{/tex} 5000 = 750 V Net resistance of each row = 0.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 5000 = 1250 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Now all 100 rows are connected in parallel. Net emf for the network = 750 V The equivalent resistance of 100 rows = {tex}\frac{1250}{100}=12.50 \Omega{/tex} External resistance = 500 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Current through 500 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance, I = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{R+r}=\frac{750}{500 \ + \ 12.5}{/tex} = 1.46 A = 1.5 A

subject: Physics
question_html:
Under what condition is the heat produced in an electric circuit:

  1. directly proportional to the resistance of the circuit?
  2. inversely proportional to the resistance of the circuit?
question_text: Under what condition is the heat produced in an electric circuit: directly proportional to the resistance of the circuit? inversely proportional to the resistance of the circuit?
explanation: Heat produced is directly proportional to the resistance if current in the circuit is kept constant because H = I 2 Rt. Heat produced is inversely proportional to the resistance if voltage is kept constant because H = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{R} t{/tex}
solution: Heat produced is directly proportional to the resistance if current in the circuit is kept constant because H = I 2 Rt. Heat produced is inversely proportional to the resistance if voltage is kept constant because H = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{R} t{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Heat produced is directly proportional to the resistance if current in the circuit is kept constant because H = I2 Rt.
  2. Heat produced is inversely proportional to the resistance if voltage is kept constant because
    H = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{R} t{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: The circuit shown in the Figure contains a battery B, a rheostat Rh and identical lamps P and Q. What will happen to the brightness of the lamps, if the resistance through the rheostat is increased? Give reasons.
question_text: The circuit shown in the Figure contains a battery B , a rheostat Rh and identical lamps P and Q. What will happen to the brightness of the lamps, if the resistance through the rheostat is increased? Give reasons.
explanation: The sum of the potential drops across the lamps P and Q is constant (equal to the battery voltage). As the resistance through the rheostat is increased, the current in the whole circuit decreases. So the brightness of lamp P decreases. But the brightness of lamp Q increases because its resistance does not change while the p.d. across it increases due to the decrease in p.d. across P.
solution: The sum of the potential drops across the lamps P and Q is constant (equal to the battery voltage). As the resistance through the rheostat is increased, the current in the whole circuit decreases. So the brightness of lamp P decreases. But the brightness of lamp Q increases because its resistance does not change while the p.d. across it increases due to the decrease in p.d. across P.
solution_html: The sum of the potential drops across the lamps P and Q is constant (equal to the battery voltage). As the resistance through the rheostat is increased, the current in the whole circuit decreases. So the brightness of lamp P decreases. But the brightness of lamp Q increases because its resistance does not change while the p.d. across it increases due to the decrease in p.d. across P.

subject: Physics
question_html: What is meant by drift velocity of free electrons? Derive Ohm's law on the basis of |the theory of electron drift.
question_text: What is meant by drift velocity of free electrons? Derive Ohm's law on the basis of |the theory of electron drift.
explanation: Drift velocity is defined as the average velocity attained by the particles (electrons) of a given material due to an electric field that is proportional to current We consider a conductor of cross section area A and length I in which drift velocity of electron is V d Applied voltage is V and electric field E between conductor ending point. So, electric current , l = neAVd But Drift velocity {tex}v_d=\frac{e E \tau^{-}}{m}=\frac{e V \tau}{m l}{/tex} {tex}\left(\because E=\frac{V}{I}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore I=n e A\left[\frac{e V \tau}{m l}\right]=n e^2 \frac{A \tau}{m l} V{/tex} or {tex}\frac{V}{I}=\frac{m l}{n e^2 A \tau}{/tex} = constant = R Where R is resistance of conductor. {tex}\therefore V \propto I {/tex} and {tex}R=\left(\frac{m}{n e^2 \tau}\right) \frac{l}{A}{/tex} But {tex}R=\frac{\rho l}{A} {/tex} So {tex}\rho=\frac{m}{n e^2 \tau} ; 6=\frac{n e^2}{m} \tau{/tex}
solution: Drift velocity is defined as the average velocity attained by the particles (electrons) of a given material due to an electric field that is proportional to current We consider a conductor of cross section area A and length I in which drift velocity of electron is V d Applied voltage is V and electric field E between conductor ending point. So, electric current , l = neAVd But Drift velocity {tex}v_d=\frac{e E \tau^{-}}{m}=\frac{e V \tau}{m l}{/tex} {tex}\left(\because E=\frac{V}{I}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore I=n e A\left[\frac{e V \tau}{m l}\right]=n e^2 \frac{A \tau}{m l} V{/tex} or {tex}\frac{V}{I}=\frac{m l}{n e^2 A \tau}{/tex} = constant = R Where R is resistance of conductor. {tex}\therefore V \propto I {/tex} and {tex}R=\left(\frac{m}{n e^2 \tau}\right) \frac{l}{A}{/tex} But {tex}R=\frac{\rho l}{A} {/tex} So {tex}\rho=\frac{m}{n e^2 \tau} ; 6=\frac{n e^2}{m} \tau{/tex}
solution_html:
Drift velocity is defined as the average velocity attained by the particles (electrons) of a given material due to an electric field that is proportional to current
We consider a conductor of cross section area A and length I in which drift velocity of electron is Vd Applied voltage is V and electric field E between conductor ending point.
So, electric current , l = neAVd
But Drift velocity {tex}v_d=\frac{e E \tau^{-}}{m}=\frac{e V \tau}{m l}{/tex} {tex}\left(\because E=\frac{V}{I}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore I=n e A\left[\frac{e V \tau}{m l}\right]=n e^2 \frac{A \tau}{m l} V{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{V}{I}=\frac{m l}{n e^2 A \tau}{/tex} = constant = R
Where R is resistance of conductor.
{tex}\therefore V \propto I {/tex} and {tex}R=\left(\frac{m}{n e^2 \tau}\right) \frac{l}{A}{/tex}
But {tex}R=\frac{\rho l}{A} {/tex} So {tex}\rho=\frac{m}{n e^2 \tau} ; 6=\frac{n e^2}{m} \tau{/tex}

question_html: The resistance of each of the three wires, shown in figure, is 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. This combination of resistors is connected to a source of emf E. The ammeter shows a reading of 1A. Calculate the power dissipated in the circuit.
question_text: The resistance of each of the three wires, shown in figure, is 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . This combination of resistors is connected to a source of emf E. The ammeter shows a reading of 1A. Calculate the power dissipated in the circuit.
explanation: Total resistance between the points P and Q, R = {tex}\frac{4 \ \times \ 4}{4 \ + \ 4}{/tex} + 4 = 2 + 4 = 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Current in the circuit, I = 1 A Power dissipated in the circuit, P = I 2 R = 1 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 6 W
solution: Total resistance between the points P and Q, R = {tex}\frac{4 \ \times \ 4}{4 \ + \ 4}{/tex} + 4 = 2 + 4 = 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Current in the circuit, I = 1 A Power dissipated in the circuit, P = I 2 R = 1 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 6 W
solution_html:
Total resistance between the points P and Q,
R = {tex}\frac{4 \ \times \ 4}{4 \ + \ 4}{/tex} + 4 = 2 + 4 = 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
Current in the circuit, I = 1 A
Power dissipated in the circuit,
P = I2R = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 6 W

question_text: In a Wheatstone's bridge, all the four arms have equal resistance R. If the resistance of the galvanometer arm is also R, the equivalent resistance of the combination as seen by the battery is:

options.explanation: In the balanced condition, the resistance R of the galvanometer is ineffective. We now have (R + R) and (R + R) resistances in parallel. {tex}\therefore R_{\alpha}=\frac{2 R\ \times \ 2 R}{2 R \ + \ 2 R}{/tex} = R

question_text: In a part of the circuit shown in the figure, the rate of heat dissipation in 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor is 100 J/s. Calculate the heat dissipated in the 3 resistors in 10 seconds.
explanation: Let I 1 be the current through the series combination of R 1 and R 2 and I 2 be the current through R 3 . P.D. across (R 1 + R 2 ) = P.D. across R 3 {tex}\therefore{/tex} (4 + 2) I 1 = 3I 2 or I 2 = 2I 1 Rate of heat dissipation in 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor = {tex}I_{1}^{2} R_{1}=I_{1}^{2} \ \times{/tex} 4 = 100 Js -1 {tex}\therefore I_{1}=\sqrt{\frac{100}{4}}=\sqrt{25}{/tex} = 5 A and I 2 = 2 I 1 = 10 A Heat dissipated in 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor in 10 s = {tex}I_{2}^{2} R_{2} t{/tex} = (10) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 3000 J
solution: Let I 1 be the current through the series combination of R 1 and R 2 and I 2 be the current through R 3 . P.D. across (R 1 + R 2 ) = P.D. across R 3 {tex}\therefore{/tex} (4 + 2) I 1 = 3I 2 or I 2 = 2I 1 Rate of heat dissipation in 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor = {tex}I_{1}^{2} R_{1}=I_{1}^{2} \ \times{/tex} 4 = 100 Js -1 {tex}\therefore I_{1}=\sqrt{\frac{100}{4}}=\sqrt{25}{/tex} = 5 A and I 2 = 2 I 1 = 10 A Heat dissipated in 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor in 10 s = {tex}I_{2}^{2} R_{2} t{/tex} = (10) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 3000 J
solution_html:
Let I1 be the current through the series combination of R1 and R2 and I2 be the current through R3.
P.D. across (R1 + R2) = P.D. across R3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (4 + 2) I1 = 3I2 or I2 = 2I1
Rate of heat dissipation in 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor
= {tex}I_{1}^{2} R_{1}=I_{1}^{2} \ \times{/tex} 4 = 100 Js-1
{tex}\therefore I_{1}=\sqrt{\frac{100}{4}}=\sqrt{25}{/tex} = 5 A
and I2 = 2 I1 = 10 A
Heat dissipated in 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor in 10 s
= {tex}I_{2}^{2} R_{2} t{/tex} = (10)2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 3000 J

question_html: The voltage current variation of two metallic wires X and Y at constant temperature are shown in Fig. Assuming that the wires have the same length and the same diameter, explain which of the two wires will have larger resistivity.
question_text: The voltage current variation of two metallic wires X and Y at constant temperature are shown in Fig. Assuming that the wires have the same length and the same diameter, explain which of the two wires will have larger resistivity.
explanation: Slope of I-V line for wire X > Slope of I-V line for wire Y. {tex}\therefore{/tex} The conductance of wire X > Conductance of wire Y or Resistance of wire X < Resistance pf wire Y or {tex}\rho_{X} \cdot \frac{l}{A}<\rho_{Y} \cdot \frac{l}{A}{/tex} or {tex}\rho_{X}<\rho_{Y}{/tex} Thus wire Y has a larger resistivity.
solution: Slope of I-V line for wire X > Slope of I-V line for wire Y. {tex}\therefore{/tex} The conductance of wire X > Conductance of wire Y or Resistance of wire X < Resistance pf wire Y or {tex}\rho_{X} \cdot \frac{l}{A}<\rho_{Y} \cdot \frac{l}{A}{/tex} or {tex}\rho_{X}<\rho_{Y}{/tex} Thus wire Y has a larger resistivity.
solution_html:
Slope of I-V line for wire X > Slope of I-V line for wire Y.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The conductance of wire X > Conductance of wire Y
or Resistance of wire X < Resistance pf wire Y
or {tex}\rho_{X} \cdot \frac{l}{A}<\rho_{Y} \cdot \frac{l}{A}{/tex} or {tex}\rho_{X}<\rho_{Y}{/tex}
Thus wire Y has a larger resistivity.

question_html: The voltage-current graphs for two resistors of the same material and the same radii with lengths L1 and L2 are shown in Fig. If L1 > L2 state with reason, which of these graphs represents voltage-current change for L1.
question_text: The voltage-current graphs for two resistors of the same material and the same radii with lengths L 1 and L 2 are shown in Fig. If L 1 > L 2 state with reason, which of these graphs represents voltage-current change for L 1 .
explanation: As L 1 > L 2 , so R 1 > R 2 . Now the graph B has a higher slope ( {tex}\frac VI{/tex} ), so it corresponds to a greater resistance. Clearly, graph B represents voltage-current change for length L 1 .

question_html: A battery of emf 3 volt and internal resistance r is connected in series with a resistor of 55 {tex}\Omega{/tex} through an ammeter of resistance 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. The ammeter reads 50 mA. Draw the circuit diagram and calculate the value of r.
question_text: A battery of emf 3 volt and internal resistance r is connected in series with a resistor of 55 {tex}\Omega{/tex} through an ammeter of resistance 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . The ammeter reads 50 mA. Draw the circuit diagram and calculate the value of r.
explanation: Total resistance = 55 + 1 + r {tex}\Omega{/tex} = 56 + r {tex}\Omega{/tex} Current = 50 mA = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 A emf = 3 V Resistance = {tex}\frac{e m f}{\text { Current }}{/tex} 56 + r = {tex}\frac{3}{50 \ \times \ 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 60 r = 60 - 56 = 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution: Total resistance = 55 + 1 + r {tex}\Omega{/tex} = 56 + r {tex}\Omega{/tex} Current = 50 mA = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 A emf = 3 V Resistance = {tex}\frac{e m f}{\text { Current }}{/tex} 56 + r = {tex}\frac{3}{50 \ \times \ 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 60 r = 60 - 56 = 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
Total resistance
= 55 + 1 + r {tex}\Omega{/tex} = 56 + r{tex}\Omega{/tex}
Current
= 50 mA
= 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 A
emf = 3 V
Resistance = {tex}\frac{e m f}{\text { Current }}{/tex}
56 + r = {tex}\frac{3}{50 \ \times \ 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 60
r = 60 - 56 = 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Define the term resistivity of a conductor. Give its SI unit. Show that the resistance of a conductor is given by
R = {tex}\frac{m l}{n e^{2} \tau A}{/tex},
where the symbols have their usual meanings.
question_text: Define the term resistivity of a conductor. Give its SI unit. Show that the resistance of a conductor is given by R = {tex}\frac{m l}{n e^{2} \tau A}{/tex} , where the symbols have their usual meanings.
explanation: Resistivity is the resistance of a conductor of unit length and unit area of cross-section. In SI, the unit of resistivity is ohm meter ( {tex}\Omega{/tex} m). The drift velocity of free electron in a conductor is {tex}V_d=\frac{e V}{m \epsilon}{/tex} But I = V d enA Substituting for V d we get, {tex}I=\left(\frac{e v}{m l}\right) \rho n A=\frac{e^2 V I n A}{m l}{/tex} {tex}V=I\left(\frac{m l}{n e^2 t_A}\right){/tex} By Ohm's law V = IR {tex}\therefore I R=I\left(\frac{m l}{n e^2 T A}\right){/tex} {tex}R=\frac{m l}{n e^2 T A}{/tex} Also {tex}R=\frac{\rho l}{A}{/tex} Where {tex}\rho{/tex} is the resistivity of the material. {tex}\therefore \frac{\rho}{A}=\frac{m l}{n e^2 T A}{/tex}
solution: Resistivity is the resistance of a conductor of unit length and unit area of cross-section. In SI, the unit of resistivity is ohm meter ( {tex}\Omega{/tex} m). The drift velocity of free electron in a conductor is {tex}V_d=\frac{e V}{m \epsilon}{/tex} But I = V d enA Substituting for V d we get, {tex}I=\left(\frac{e v}{m l}\right) \rho n A=\frac{e^2 V I n A}{m l}{/tex} {tex}V=I\left(\frac{m l}{n e^2 t_A}\right){/tex} By Ohm's law V = IR {tex}\therefore I R=I\left(\frac{m l}{n e^2 T A}\right){/tex} {tex}R=\frac{m l}{n e^2 T A}{/tex} Also {tex}R=\frac{\rho l}{A}{/tex} Where {tex}\rho{/tex} is the resistivity of the material. {tex}\therefore \frac{\rho}{A}=\frac{m l}{n e^2 T A}{/tex}
solution_html:
Resistivity is the resistance of a conductor of unit length and unit area of cross-section. In SI, the unit of resistivity is ohm meter ({tex}\Omega{/tex} m).
The drift velocity of free electron in a conductor is
{tex}V_d=\frac{e V}{m \epsilon}{/tex}
But I = VdenA
Substituting for Vd we get,
{tex}I=\left(\frac{e v}{m l}\right) \rho n A=\frac{e^2 V I n A}{m l}{/tex}
{tex}V=I\left(\frac{m l}{n e^2 t_A}\right){/tex}
By Ohm's law V = IR
{tex}\therefore I R=I\left(\frac{m l}{n e^2 T A}\right){/tex}
{tex}R=\frac{m l}{n e^2 T A}{/tex}
Also {tex}R=\frac{\rho l}{A}{/tex}
Where {tex}\rho{/tex} is the resistivity of the material.
{tex}\therefore \frac{\rho}{A}=\frac{m l}{n e^2 T A}{/tex}
