subject: Physics
question_html: Establish the relation between drift velocity of electrons and the electric field applied to the conductor.
question_text: Establish the relation between drift velocity of electrons and the electric field applied to the conductor.
explanation: Suppose PQ is a conductor of length l and a potential difference V is applied across PQ as shown in the figure. i.e., its P end remains negative and Q end positive. Therefore an electric field is developed directing from Q to P and its intensity is given by E = {tex}\frac{V}{l}{/tex} ...(i) Each free electron of the conductor experiences a force (F) towards the end Q, {tex}\therefore{/tex} F = -eE If m be the mass of the electron, then its acceleration = {tex}\frac{\vec{F}}{m}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-e \vec{E}}{m}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} The average thermal velocity of electrons is zero. {tex}\therefore{/tex} u = 0 Therefore using first equation of motion (v = u + at), we have {tex}\overrightarrow{v_d}{/tex} = 0 + {tex}\left(\frac{-e \vec{E}}{m}\right) \tau{/tex} or {tex}\overrightarrow{v_d}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{-e \vec{E}}{m}\right) \tau{/tex} or {tex}\overrightarrow{v_d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-e \vec{E}}{m} \tau{/tex} ...(ii) Numerically we can write, {tex}\left|\overrightarrow{v_d}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{e E}{m} \tau{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} From equation (i), E = {tex}\frac{V}{l}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} v d = {tex}\frac{e \tau}{m} \frac{V}{l}{/tex} ...(iii)
solution: Suppose PQ is a conductor of length l and a potential difference V is applied across PQ as shown in the figure. i.e., its P end remains negative and Q end positive. Therefore an electric field is developed directing from Q to P and its intensity is given by E = {tex}\frac{V}{l}{/tex} ...(i) Each free electron of the conductor experiences a force (F) towards the end Q, {tex}\therefore{/tex} F = -eE If m be the mass of the electron, then its acceleration = {tex}\frac{\vec{F}}{m}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-e \vec{E}}{m}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} The average thermal velocity of electrons is zero. {tex}\therefore{/tex} u = 0 Therefore using first equation of motion (v = u + at), we have {tex}\overrightarrow{v_d}{/tex} = 0 + {tex}\left(\frac{-e \vec{E}}{m}\right) \tau{/tex} or {tex}\overrightarrow{v_d}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{-e \vec{E}}{m}\right) \tau{/tex} or {tex}\overrightarrow{v_d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-e \vec{E}}{m} \tau{/tex} ...(ii) Numerically we can write, {tex}\left|\overrightarrow{v_d}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{e E}{m} \tau{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} From equation (i), E = {tex}\frac{V}{l}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} v d = {tex}\frac{e \tau}{m} \frac{V}{l}{/tex} ...(iii)
solution_html:
Suppose PQ is a conductor of length l and a potential difference V is applied across PQ as shown in the figure. i.e., its P end remains negative and Q end positive.
Therefore an electric field is developed directing from Q to P and its intensity is given by E = {tex}\frac{V}{l}{/tex} ...(i)
Each free electron of the conductor experiences a force (F) towards the end Q,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} F = -eE

If m be the mass of the electron, then its acceleration
= {tex}\frac{\vec{F}}{m}{/tex}
or {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-e \vec{E}}{m}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} The average thermal velocity of electrons is zero.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} u = 0 Therefore using first equation of motion (v = u + at), we have
{tex}\overrightarrow{v_d}{/tex} = 0 + {tex}\left(\frac{-e \vec{E}}{m}\right) \tau{/tex}
or {tex}\overrightarrow{v_d}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{-e \vec{E}}{m}\right) \tau{/tex}
or {tex}\overrightarrow{v_d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-e \vec{E}}{m} \tau{/tex} ...(ii)
Numerically we can write,
{tex}\left|\overrightarrow{v_d}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{e E}{m} \tau{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} From equation (i), E = {tex}\frac{V}{l}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} vd = {tex}\frac{e \tau}{m} \frac{V}{l}{/tex} ...(iii)

subject: Physics
question_html: Calculate the amount of heat produced per second (in calories), when a bulb of 100 W - 220 V glows assuming that only 20% of electric energy is converted into light. J = 4.2 J cal-1.
question_text: Calculate the amount of heat produced per second (in calories), when a bulb of 100 W - 220 V glows assuming that only 20% of electric energy is converted into light. J = 4.2 J cal -1 .
explanation: Power of bulb, P = 100 W {tex}\therefore{/tex} Electric energy consumed per second = 100 J Amount of heat produced per second = 80% of 100 J = 80 J = {tex}\frac{80}{4.2}{/tex} cal = 19.05 cal
solution: Power of bulb, P = 100 W {tex}\therefore{/tex} Electric energy consumed per second = 100 J Amount of heat produced per second = 80% of 100 J = 80 J = {tex}\frac{80}{4.2}{/tex} cal = 19.05 cal
solution_html:
Power of bulb, P = 100 W
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Electric energy consumed per second = 100 J
Amount of heat produced per second
= 80% of 100 J = 80 J = {tex}\frac{80}{4.2}{/tex} cal = 19.05 cal

subject: Physics
question_html: Three bulbs 40 W, 60 W and 100 W are connected to 220 V mains. Which bulb will glow brightly, if they are connected in series?
question_text: Three bulbs 40 W, 60 W and 100 W are connected to 220 V mains. Which bulb will glow brightly, if they are connected in series?
explanation: In the series circuit, the same current flows through each bulb. But the 40 W bulb has the highest resistance (R = {tex}\frac {V^2}{P}{/tex} ). The 40 W bulb produces maximum heat per second (P = I 2 R), so it will glow more brightly than the other two bulbs.
solution: In the series circuit, the same current flows through each bulb. But the 40 W bulb has the highest resistance (R = {tex}\frac {V^2}{P}{/tex} ). The 40 W bulb produces maximum heat per second (P = I 2 R), so it will glow more brightly than the other two bulbs.
solution_html: In the series circuit, the same current flows through each bulb. But the 40 W bulb has the highest resistance (R = {tex}\frac {V^2}{P}{/tex}). The 40 W bulb produces maximum heat per second (P = I2R), so it will glow more brightly than the other two bulbs.

subject: Physics
question_html: Find the current drawn from a cell of emf 1 V and internal resistance {tex}\frac 23 \Omega{/tex} connected to the network given below.
question_text: Find the current drawn from a cell of emf 1 V and internal resistance {tex}\frac 23 \Omega{/tex} connected to the network given below.
explanation: The equivalent circuit is shown below. Resistance in arm AB = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Resistance in arm PQ = {tex}\frac{1 \ \times \ 1}{1 \ + \ 1}+\frac{1 \ \times \ 1}{1 \ + \ 1}=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}=1 \Omega{/tex} Resistance in arm DC = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} These three resistances are connected in parallel. Their equivalent resistance R is given by {tex}\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{1}=\frac{3}{1}{/tex} or {tex}R=\frac{1}{3} \Omega{/tex} Current drawn from the cell, I = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{R \ + \ r}=\frac{1 \mathrm{~V}}{\left(\frac{1}{3}+\frac{2}{3}\right) \Omega}{/tex} = 1 A
solution: The equivalent circuit is shown below. Resistance in arm AB = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Resistance in arm PQ = {tex}\frac{1 \ \times \ 1}{1 \ + \ 1}+\frac{1 \ \times \ 1}{1 \ + \ 1}=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}=1 \Omega{/tex} Resistance in arm DC = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} These three resistances are connected in parallel. Their equivalent resistance R is given by {tex}\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{1}=\frac{3}{1}{/tex} or {tex}R=\frac{1}{3} \Omega{/tex} Current drawn from the cell, I = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{R \ + \ r}=\frac{1 \mathrm{~V}}{\left(\frac{1}{3}+\frac{2}{3}\right) \Omega}{/tex} = 1 A
solution_html:
The equivalent circuit is shown below.

Resistance in arm AB = 1{tex}\Omega{/tex}
Resistance in arm PQ = {tex}\frac{1 \ \times \ 1}{1 \ + \ 1}+\frac{1 \ \times \ 1}{1 \ + \ 1}=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}=1 \Omega{/tex}
Resistance in arm DC = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
These three resistances are connected in parallel.
Their equivalent resistance R is given by
{tex}\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{1}=\frac{3}{1}{/tex} or {tex}R=\frac{1}{3} \Omega{/tex}
Current drawn from the cell,
I = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{R \ + \ r}=\frac{1 \mathrm{~V}}{\left(\frac{1}{3}+\frac{2}{3}\right) \Omega}{/tex} = 1 A

subject: Physics
question_html: A heater joined in series with a 60 W bulb is connected to the mains. If 60 W bulb is replaced by a 100 W bulb, will the rate of heat produced by the heater be more, less or remain the same?
question_text: A heater joined in series with a 60 W bulb is connected to the mains. If 60 W bulb is replaced by a 100 W bulb, will the rate of heat produced by the heater be more, less or remain the same?
explanation: The resistance of the 100 W bulb is less than that of 60 W bulb. When 60 W bulb is replaced by 100 W bulb, the resistance in the series circuit decreases and the current increases. Hence the rate of heat produced by the heater will be more.
solution: The resistance of the 100 W bulb is less than that of 60 W bulb. When 60 W bulb is replaced by 100 W bulb, the resistance in the series circuit decreases and the current increases. Hence the rate of heat produced by the heater will be more.
solution_html: The resistance of the 100 W bulb is less than that of 60 W bulb. When 60 W bulb is replaced by 100 W bulb, the resistance in the series circuit decreases and the current increases. Hence the rate of heat produced by the heater will be more.

subject: Physics
question_html: Which has a greater resistance1 kW electric heater or a 100 W filament bulb both marked for 220 V?
question_text: Which has a greater resistance1 kW electric heater or a 100 W filament bulb both marked for 220 V?
explanation: Since the power of the filament bulb is smaller as compared to the electric heater, the resistance of the filament bulb will be greater. Thus, a 100W filament bulb offers greater resistance.
solution: Since the power of the filament bulb is smaller as compared to the electric heater, the resistance of the filament bulb will be greater. Thus, a 100W filament bulb offers greater resistance.
solution_html:
Since the power of the filament bulb is smaller as compared to the electric heater, the resistance of the filament bulb will be greater.
Thus, a 100W filament bulb offers greater resistance.

subject: Physics
question_html: A 20 V battery of internal resistance 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is connected to three coils of 12 {tex}\Omega{/tex}, 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} in parallel, a resistor of 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and a reversed battery (emf = 8 V and internal resistance = 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex}), as shown in Figure. Calculate the current in each resistor and the terminal potential difference across each battery.
question_text: A 20 V battery of internal resistance 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is connected to three coils of 12 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} in parallel, a resistor of 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and a reversed battery (emf = 8 V and internal resistance = 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} ), as shown in Figure. Calculate the current in each resistor and the terminal potential difference across each battery.
explanation: The equivalent resistance {tex}R^{\prime}{/tex} of 12 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistances connected in parallel is given by {tex}\frac{1}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{12}+\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{4}=\frac{6}{12}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} or {tex}R^{\prime}=2 \Omega{/tex} Total resistance = 1 + 5 + 2 + 2 = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Net emf = 20 - 8 = 12 Current in the circuit, I = {tex}\frac{12}{10}{/tex} = 1.2 A So the current through each battery and 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor is 1.2 A. P.D. across the parallel combination of three resistors is {tex}V^{\prime}=I R^{\prime}{/tex} = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 2.4 V {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current in 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} coil = {tex}\frac{2.4}{12}{/tex} = 0.2 A Current in 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} coil = {tex}\frac{2.4}{6}{/tex} = 0.4 A Current in 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} coil = {tex}\frac{2.4}{4}{/tex} = 0.6 A. Terminal p.d. across 20 V battery, V = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} - Ir = 20 - 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 18.8 V Terminal p.d. across 8 V battery, {tex}V^{\prime}=\varepsilon^{\prime}+I r^{\prime}{/tex} = 8 + 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 10.4 V
solution: The equivalent resistance {tex}R^{\prime}{/tex} of 12 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistances connected in parallel is given by {tex}\frac{1}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{12}+\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{4}=\frac{6}{12}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} or {tex}R^{\prime}=2 \Omega{/tex} Total resistance = 1 + 5 + 2 + 2 = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Net emf = 20 - 8 = 12 Current in the circuit, I = {tex}\frac{12}{10}{/tex} = 1.2 A So the current through each battery and 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor is 1.2 A. P.D. across the parallel combination of three resistors is {tex}V^{\prime}=I R^{\prime}{/tex} = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 2.4 V {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current in 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} coil = {tex}\frac{2.4}{12}{/tex} = 0.2 A Current in 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} coil = {tex}\frac{2.4}{6}{/tex} = 0.4 A Current in 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} coil = {tex}\frac{2.4}{4}{/tex} = 0.6 A. Terminal p.d. across 20 V battery, V = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} - Ir = 20 - 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 18.8 V Terminal p.d. across 8 V battery, {tex}V^{\prime}=\varepsilon^{\prime}+I r^{\prime}{/tex} = 8 + 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 10.4 V
solution_html:
The equivalent resistance {tex}R^{\prime}{/tex} of 12 {tex}\Omega{/tex}, 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistances connected in parallel is given by
{tex}\frac{1}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{12}+\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{4}=\frac{6}{12}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
or {tex}R^{\prime}=2 \Omega{/tex}
Total resistance = 1 + 5 + 2 + 2 = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
Net emf = 20 - 8 = 12
Current in the circuit, I = {tex}\frac{12}{10}{/tex} = 1.2 A
So the current through each battery and 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor is 1.2 A.
P.D. across the parallel combination of three resistors is
{tex}V^{\prime}=I R^{\prime}{/tex} = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 2.4 V
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Current in 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} coil = {tex}\frac{2.4}{12}{/tex} = 0.2 A
Current in 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} coil = {tex}\frac{2.4}{6}{/tex} = 0.4 A
Current in 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} coil = {tex}\frac{2.4}{4}{/tex} = 0.6 A.
Terminal p.d. across 20 V battery,
V = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} - Ir = 20 - 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 18.8 V
Terminal p.d. across 8 V battery,
{tex}V^{\prime}=\varepsilon^{\prime}+I r^{\prime}{/tex} = 8 + 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 10.4 V

subject: Physics
question_html: A heater coil is rated 100 W, 200 V. It is cut into two identical parts. Both parts are connected together in parallel, to the same source of 200 V. Calculate the energy liberated per second in the new combination.
question_text: A heater coil is rated 100 W, 200 V. It is cut into two identical parts. Both parts are connected together in parallel, to the same source of 200 V. Calculate the energy liberated per second in the new combination.
explanation: The resistance of heater coil, R = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{200 \ \times \ 200}{100}=400 \Omega{/tex} The resistance of either half part = 200 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Equivalent resistance when both parts are connected in parallel, {tex}R^{\prime}=\frac{200 \ \times \ 200}{200 \ + \ 200}=100 \Omega{/tex} The energy liberated per second when the combination is connected to a source of 200 V = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{200 \ \times \ 200}{100}{/tex} = 400 J
solution: The resistance of heater coil, R = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{200 \ \times \ 200}{100}=400 \Omega{/tex} The resistance of either half part = 200 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Equivalent resistance when both parts are connected in parallel, {tex}R^{\prime}=\frac{200 \ \times \ 200}{200 \ + \ 200}=100 \Omega{/tex} The energy liberated per second when the combination is connected to a source of 200 V = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{200 \ \times \ 200}{100}{/tex} = 400 J
solution_html:
The resistance of heater coil,
R = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{200 \ \times \ 200}{100}=400 \Omega{/tex}
The resistance of either half part = 200 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
Equivalent resistance when both parts are connected in parallel,
{tex}R^{\prime}=\frac{200 \ \times \ 200}{200 \ + \ 200}=100 \Omega{/tex}
The energy liberated per second when the combination is connected to a source of 200 V
= {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{200 \ \times \ 200}{100}{/tex} = 400 J

question_html: Calculate the current shown by the ammeter A in the circuit shown in Figure.
question_text: Calculate the current shown by the ammeter A in the circuit shown in Figure.
explanation: The equivalent circuit is shown in Figure For the two 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistances connected in parallel, equivalent resistance = {tex}\frac{10 \ \times \ 10}{10 \ + \ 10}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} For two such combinations connected in series, equivalent resistance = 5 + 5 = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Now we have resistances of 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} connected in parallel, so, {tex}\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{5}+\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{5}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} or R = 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Also V = 10 V {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current, I = {tex}\frac{V}{R}=\frac{10}{2}{/tex} = 5 A
solution: The equivalent circuit is shown in Figure For the two 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistances connected in parallel, equivalent resistance = {tex}\frac{10 \ \times \ 10}{10 \ + \ 10}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} For two such combinations connected in series, equivalent resistance = 5 + 5 = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Now we have resistances of 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} connected in parallel, so, {tex}\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{5}+\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{5}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} or R = 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Also V = 10 V {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current, I = {tex}\frac{V}{R}=\frac{10}{2}{/tex} = 5 A
solution_html:
The equivalent circuit is shown in Figure

For the two 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistances connected in parallel, equivalent resistance = {tex}\frac{10 \ \times \ 10}{10 \ + \ 10}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
For two such combinations connected in series, equivalent resistance = 5 + 5 = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
Now we have resistances of 5{tex}\Omega{/tex}, 10{tex}\Omega{/tex} and 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} connected in parallel, so,
{tex}\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{5}+\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{5}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
or R = 2{tex}\Omega{/tex}
Also V = 10 V

subject: Physics
question_html: A toaster produces more heat than a light bulb when connected in parallel to the 220 V mains. Which has a greater resistance?
question_text: A toaster produces more heat than a light bulb when connected in parallel to the 220 V mains. Which has a greater resistance?
explanation: The amount of heat produced by an electric current in time t is given by H = IRt = {tex}\frac{V^{2} t}{R}{/tex} i.e., {tex}H \propto \frac{1}{R}{/tex} For the parallel combination, potential difference V is same. As toaster produces more heat, so light bulb should have a greater resistance.
solution: The amount of heat produced by an electric current in time t is given by H = IRt = {tex}\frac{V^{2} t}{R}{/tex} i.e., {tex}H \propto \frac{1}{R}{/tex} For the parallel combination, potential difference V is same. As toaster produces more heat, so light bulb should have a greater resistance.
solution_html:
The amount of heat produced by an electric current in time t is given by
H = IRt = {tex}\frac{V^{2} t}{R}{/tex} i.e., {tex}H \propto \frac{1}{R}{/tex}
For the parallel combination, potential difference V is same. As toaster produces more heat, so light bulb should have a greater resistance.

subject: Physics
question_html: State the condition in which terminal voltage across a secondary cell is equal to its emf.
question_text: State the condition in which terminal voltage across a secondary cell is equal to its emf.
explanation: The potential difference between the terminals of a battery will equal the emf of the battery when there is no current in the battery. At this time, the potential drop across the internal resistance is zero. This only happens when there is no load placed on the battery that includes measuring the potential difference with a voltmeter. The terminal voltage will exceed the emf of the battery when the current is driven back through the battery, in at its positive terminal and out at its negative terminal.
solution: The potential difference between the terminals of a battery will equal the emf of the battery when there is no current in the battery. At this time, the potential drop across the internal resistance is zero. This only happens when there is no load placed on the battery that includes measuring the potential difference with a voltmeter. The terminal voltage will exceed the emf of the battery when the current is driven back through the battery, in at its positive terminal and out at its negative terminal.
solution_html: The potential difference between the terminals of a battery will equal the emf of the battery when there is no current in the battery. At this time, the potential drop across the internal resistance is zero. This only happens when there is no load placed on the battery that includes measuring the potential difference with a voltmeter. The terminal voltage will exceed the emf of the battery when the current is driven back through the battery, in at its positive terminal and out at its negative terminal.

question_html: Two cells of emfs 1.5 V and 2 V and internal resistances 2 {tex}\Omega {/tex} and 1 {tex}\Omega {/tex} respectively have their negative terminals joined by a wire of 6 {tex}\Omega {/tex} and positive terminals by a wire of 4 {tex}\Omega {/tex} resistance. A third resistance wire of 8 {tex}\Omega {/tex} connects the middle points of these wires. Draw the circuit diagram. Using Kirchhoff laws, find the potential difference at the end of this third wire.
question_text: Two cells of emfs 1.5 V and 2 V and internal resistances 2 {tex}\Omega {/tex} and 1 {tex}\Omega {/tex} respectively have their negative terminals joined by a wire of 6 {tex}\Omega {/tex} and positive terminals by a wire of 4 {tex}\Omega {/tex} resistance. A third resistance wire of 8 {tex}\Omega {/tex} connects the middle points of these wires. Draw the circuit diagram. Using Kirchhoff laws, find the potential difference at the end of this third wire.
explanation: As shown in Figure, the positive terminals of cells {tex}\varepsilon_{1} \text { and } \varepsilon_{2}{/tex} , are connected to the wire AE of resistance 4 {tex}\Omega {/tex} and negative terminals to the wire BD of resistance 6 {tex}\Omega {/tex} . The 8 {tex}\Omega {/tex} wire is connected between the middle points F and C of the wires AE and BD respectively. {tex}\therefore R_{1}=R_{2}=\frac{4}{2}=2 \Omega{/tex} and R 3 = R 4 = {tex}\frac{6}{2}=3 \Omega{/tex} The distribution of current in various branches is shown in Figure. Applying Kirchhoff's second law to the loop ABCFA, we get I 1 {tex}\times{/tex} r 1 + I 1 {tex}\times{/tex} R 1 + (I 1 + I 2 ) R + I 1 {tex}\times{/tex} R 3 = {tex}\varepsilon_{1}{/tex} I 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + (I 1 + I 2 ) {tex}\times{/tex} 8 + I 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 1.5 15I 1 + 8 I 2 = 1.5 ...(i) Applying Kirchhoff's second law to the loop CDEFC, we get I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} r 2 + I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} R 2 + (I 1 + I 2 ) {tex}\times{/tex} R + I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} R 4 = {tex}\varepsilon_{2}{/tex} , I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} l + I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + (I 1 + I 2 ) {tex}\times{/tex} 8 + I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 2 8I 1 + 14I 2 =2 or 4I 1 + 7I 2 = 1 ...(ii) On solving equations (i) and (ii), we get I 1 = {tex}\frac{5}{146} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} and I 2 = {tex}\frac{18}{146} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} Current through the 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance wire is I 1 + I 2 = {tex}\frac{5}{146}+\frac{18}{146}=\frac{23}{146} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} P.D. across the ends of 8 {tex}\Omega {/tex} resistance wire = {tex}\frac{23}{146} \times 8{/tex} = 1.26 A
solution: As shown in Figure, the positive terminals of cells {tex}\varepsilon_{1} \text { and } \varepsilon_{2}{/tex} , are connected to the wire AE of resistance 4 {tex}\Omega {/tex} and negative terminals to the wire BD of resistance 6 {tex}\Omega {/tex} . The 8 {tex}\Omega {/tex} wire is connected between the middle points F and C of the wires AE and BD respectively. {tex}\therefore R_{1}=R_{2}=\frac{4}{2}=2 \Omega{/tex} and R 3 = R 4 = {tex}\frac{6}{2}=3 \Omega{/tex} The distribution of current in various branches is shown in Figure. Applying Kirchhoff's second law to the loop ABCFA, we get I 1 {tex}\times{/tex} r 1 + I 1 {tex}\times{/tex} R 1 + (I 1 + I 2 ) R + I 1 {tex}\times{/tex} R 3 = {tex}\varepsilon_{1}{/tex} I 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + (I 1 + I 2 ) {tex}\times{/tex} 8 + I 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 1.5 15I 1 + 8 I 2 = 1.5 ...(i) Applying Kirchhoff's second law to the loop CDEFC, we get I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} r 2 + I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} R 2 + (I 1 + I 2 ) {tex}\times{/tex} R + I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} R 4 = {tex}\varepsilon_{2}{/tex} , I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} l + I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + (I 1 + I 2 ) {tex}\times{/tex} 8 + I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 2 8I 1 + 14I 2 =2 or 4I 1 + 7I 2 = 1 ...(ii) On solving equations (i) and (ii), we get I 1 = {tex}\frac{5}{146} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} and I 2 = {tex}\frac{18}{146} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} Current through the 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance wire is I 1 + I 2 = {tex}\frac{5}{146}+\frac{18}{146}=\frac{23}{146} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} P.D. across the ends of 8 {tex}\Omega {/tex} resistance wire = {tex}\frac{23}{146} \times 8{/tex} = 1.26 A
solution_html:
As shown in Figure, the positive terminals of cells {tex}\varepsilon_{1} \text { and } \varepsilon_{2}{/tex}, are connected to the wire AE of resistance 4 {tex}\Omega {/tex} and negative terminals to the wire BD of resistance 6 {tex}\Omega {/tex}. The 8 {tex}\Omega {/tex} wire is connected between the middle points F and C of the wires AE and BD respectively.
{tex}\therefore R_{1}=R_{2}=\frac{4}{2}=2 \Omega{/tex}
and R3 = R4 = {tex}\frac{6}{2}=3 \Omega{/tex}
The distribution of current in various branches is shown in Figure.

Applying Kirchhoff's second law to the loop ABCFA, we get
I1 {tex}\times{/tex} r1 + I1 {tex}\times{/tex} R1 + (I1 + I2) R + I1 {tex}\times{/tex} R3 = {tex}\varepsilon_{1}{/tex}
I1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + I2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + (I1 + I2) {tex}\times{/tex} 8 + I1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 1.5
15I1 + 8 I2 = 1.5 ...(i)
Applying Kirchhoff's second law to the loop CDEFC, we get
I2 {tex}\times{/tex} r2 + I2 {tex}\times{/tex} R2 + (I1 + I2) {tex}\times{/tex} R + I2 {tex}\times{/tex} R4 = {tex}\varepsilon_{2}{/tex},
I2 {tex}\times{/tex} l + I2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + (I1 + I2) {tex}\times{/tex} 8 + I2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 2
8I1 + 14I2 =2
or 4I1 + 7I2 = 1 ...(ii)
On solving equations (i) and (ii), we get
I1 = {tex}\frac{5}{146} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} and I2 = {tex}\frac{18}{146} \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
Current through the 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance wire is
I1 + I2 = {tex}\frac{5}{146}+\frac{18}{146}=\frac{23}{146} \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
P.D. across the ends of 8 {tex}\Omega {/tex} resistance wire
= {tex}\frac{23}{146} \times 8{/tex} = 1.26 A

question_html: Using Kirchhoff's laws in the electrical network shown in the figure, calculate the values of I1, I2 and I3.
question_text: Using Kirchhoff's laws in the electrical network shown in the figure, calculate the values of I 1 , I 2 and I 3 .
explanation: Applying Kirchhoff 's first law at the junction B, I 1 + I 2 = I 3 ...(i) Applying Kirchhoff's second law to loops ABEFA and BCDEB, we get, 2I 3 + 5l 1 = 12 ...(ii) - 2I 3 - 3I 2 = - 6 ...(iii) Solving equations (i), (ii) and (iii), we get {tex}I_{1}=\frac{48}{31} A, I_{2}=\frac{18}{31} A, I_{3}=\frac{66}{31} A{/tex}
solution: Applying Kirchhoff 's first law at the junction B, I 1 + I 2 = I 3 ...(i) Applying Kirchhoff's second law to loops ABEFA and BCDEB, we get, 2I 3 + 5l 1 = 12 ...(ii) - 2I 3 - 3I 2 = - 6 ...(iii) Solving equations (i), (ii) and (iii), we get {tex}I_{1}=\frac{48}{31} A, I_{2}=\frac{18}{31} A, I_{3}=\frac{66}{31} A{/tex}
solution_html:
Applying Kirchhoff 's first law at the junction B,
I1 + I2 = I3 ...(i)
Applying Kirchhoff's second law to loops ABEFA and BCDEB, we get,
2I3 + 5l1 = 12 ...(ii)
- 2I3 - 3I2 = - 6 ...(iii)
Solving equations (i), (ii) and (iii), we get
{tex}I_{1}=\frac{48}{31} A, I_{2}=\frac{18}{31} A, I_{3}=\frac{66}{31} A{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Define relaxation time of elections in a conductor. Explain how it varies with increase in temperature of a conductor. State the relation between resistivity and relaxation time.
question_text: Define relaxation time of elections in a conductor. Explain how it varies with increase in temperature of a conductor. State the relation between resistivity and relaxation time.
explanation: Relaxation time is the time interval between two successive collisions of electrons in a conductor, when current flows. As the temperature increases, the thermal energy of electrons increases. Thus they collide more frequently with the atoms and hence the average relaxation time which is the average time between successive collisions decreases. Thus temperature is inversely proportional to relaxation time. Electrical resistivity is inversely proportional to relaxation time.
solution: Relaxation time is the time interval between two successive collisions of electrons in a conductor, when current flows. As the temperature increases, the thermal energy of electrons increases. Thus they collide more frequently with the atoms and hence the average relaxation time which is the average time between successive collisions decreases. Thus temperature is inversely proportional to relaxation time. Electrical resistivity is inversely proportional to relaxation time.
solution_html:
Relaxation time is the time interval between two successive collisions of electrons in a conductor, when current flows. As the temperature increases, the thermal energy of electrons increases. Thus they collide more frequently with the atoms and hence the average relaxation time which is the average time between successive collisions decreases. Thus temperature is inversely proportional to relaxation time.
Electrical resistivity is inversely proportional to relaxation time.

question_html: If potential difference V applied across a conductor is increased to 2 V, how will the drift velocity of the electrons change?
question_text: If potential difference V applied across a conductor is increased to 2 V, how will the drift velocity of the electrons change?
explanation: The potential difference V applied across a conductor is increased to 2V with its temperature kept constant, free electrons in a conductor will become half of its previous value. If the temperature is kept constant, relaxation time - will remain constant, and e, m are also constants.
solution: The potential difference V applied across a conductor is increased to 2V with its temperature kept constant, free electrons in a conductor will become half of its previous value. If the temperature is kept constant, relaxation time - will remain constant, and e, m are also constants.
solution_html: The potential difference V applied across a conductor is increased to 2V with its temperature kept constant, free electrons in a conductor will become half of its previous value. If the temperature is kept constant, relaxation time - will remain constant, and e, m are also constants.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A conductor of length l is connected to a d.c. source of potential V. If the length of the conductor is tripled, by stretching it, keeping V
constant, explain how do the following factors vary in the conductor:

  1. Drift speed of electrons,
  2. Resistance and
  3. Resistivity.
question_text: A conductor of length l is connected to a d.c. source of potential V . If the length of the conductor is tripled, by stretching it, keeping V constant, explain how do the following factors vary in the conductor: Drift speed of electrons, Resistance and Resistivity.
explanation: Drift speed of electrons = {tex}\frac{V}{\text { nel } \rho}{/tex} Resistance of the conductor is given as {tex}\mathrm{R}=\rho \frac{\mathrm{l}}{\mathrm{A}}{/tex} When length is tripled area of cross-section is reduced to {tex}\frac{A}{3}{/tex} Hence, new resistance = 9R Thus, new resistance will be 9 times the original. Resistivity increases
solution: Drift speed of electrons = {tex}\frac{V}{\text { nel } \rho}{/tex} Resistance of the conductor is given as {tex}\mathrm{R}=\rho \frac{\mathrm{l}}{\mathrm{A}}{/tex} When length is tripled area of cross-section is reduced to {tex}\frac{A}{3}{/tex} Hence, new resistance = 9R Thus, new resistance will be 9 times the original. Resistivity increases
solution_html:
1. Drift speed of electrons = {tex}\frac{V}{\text { nel } \rho}{/tex}
  2. Resistance of the conductor is given as
    {tex}\mathrm{R}=\rho \frac{\mathrm{l}}{\mathrm{A}}{/tex}
    When length is tripled area of cross-section is reduced to {tex}\frac{A}{3}{/tex}
    Hence, new resistance = 9R
    Thus, new resistance will be 9 times the original.
  3. Resistivity increases

question_html: Resistivities of copper, silver and manganin are 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 {tex}\Omega{/tex}m, 1.0{tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 {tex}\Omega{/tex}m and 44 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 {tex}\Omega{/tex}m respectively. Which of these is the best conductor?
question_text: Resistivities of copper, silver and manganin are 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} m, 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} m and 44 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} m respectively. Which of these is the best conductor?
explanation: For given length and area of cross-section, the resistance of a material is directly proportional to its specific resistance. Since the specific resistance is least for silver, it is the best conductor of the three given materials.

subject: Physics
question_html: Using Kirchhoff's laws, determine the currents l1, I2 and I3 for the network shown in Figure.
question_text: Using Kirchhoff's laws, determine the currents l 1 , I 2 and I 3 for the network shown in Figure.
explanation: Traversing the upper and lower loops anticlockwise, we get 2I 1 + 6I 2 = 24 - 27 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2I 1 + 6I 2 = -3 ...(i) and 4I 3 - 6I 2 = 27 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4(I 1 - I 2 ) - 6I 2 = 27 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4I 1 - 10I 2 = 27 ...(ii) On solving (i) and (ii), we get I 1 = 3 A, I 2 = -1.5 A I 3 = I 1 - I 2 = 3 + 1.5 = 4.5 A
solution: Traversing the upper and lower loops anticlockwise, we get 2I 1 + 6I 2 = 24 - 27 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2I 1 + 6I 2 = -3 ...(i) and 4I 3 - 6I 2 = 27 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4(I 1 - I 2 ) - 6I 2 = 27 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4I 1 - 10I 2 = 27 ...(ii) On solving (i) and (ii), we get I 1 = 3 A, I 2 = -1.5 A I 3 = I 1 - I 2 = 3 + 1.5 = 4.5 A
solution_html:
Traversing the upper and lower loops anticlockwise, we get
2I1 + 6I2 = 24 - 27
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2I1 + 6I2 = -3 ...(i)
and 4I3 - 6I2 = 27
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4(I1 - I2) - 6I2 = 27
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4I1 - 10I2 = 27 ...(ii)
On solving (i) and (ii), we get
I1 = 3 A, I2 = -1.5 A
I3 = I1 - I2 = 3 + 1.5 = 4.5 A

subject: Physics
question_html:
Define internal resistance of a cell.
Prove that r = {tex}\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{V}-1\right) R{/tex},
where R is the external resistance used.
question_text: Define internal resistance of a cell. Prove that r = {tex}\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{V}-1\right) R{/tex} , where R is the external resistance used.
explanation: The internal resistance is defined as, "the resistance offered by the electrolyte and electrodes of the cell, when electric current flows through it." When the key is closed, the current flows through the circuit as shown in the figure. Now, the reading of the voltmeter is equal to terminal potential difference (V) of the cell. We can write E - V = Ir {tex}\Rightarrow r = \frac{E - V}{I}{/tex} ...(i) But from Ohm's law, I = {tex}\frac VR{/tex} Substituting the value of I in equation (i), we get r = {tex}\frac{\frac{E - V}{V}}R{/tex} ⇒ r = {tex}\frac{\frac{(E - V)}{V}}R{/tex} = {tex}(\frac{E}{V - 1}){/tex} R
solution: The internal resistance is defined as, "the resistance offered by the electrolyte and electrodes of the cell, when electric current flows through it." When the key is closed, the current flows through the circuit as shown in the figure. Now, the reading of the voltmeter is equal to terminal potential difference (V) of the cell. We can write E - V = Ir {tex}\Rightarrow r = \frac{E - V}{I}{/tex} ...(i) But from Ohm's law, I = {tex}\frac VR{/tex} Substituting the value of I in equation (i), we get r = {tex}\frac{\frac{E - V}{V}}R{/tex} ⇒ r = {tex}\frac{\frac{(E - V)}{V}}R{/tex} = {tex}(\frac{E}{V - 1}){/tex} R
solution_html:
The internal resistance is defined as, "the resistance offered by the electrolyte and electrodes of the cell, when electric current flows through it."
When the key is closed, the current flows through the circuit as shown in the figure. Now, the reading of the voltmeter is equal to terminal potential difference (V) of the cell.
We can write
E - V = Ir
{tex}\Rightarrow r = \frac{E - V}{I}{/tex} ...(i)
But from Ohm's law, I = {tex}\frac VR{/tex}
Substituting the value of I in equation (i), we get
r = {tex}\frac{\frac{E - V}{V}}R{/tex}
⇒ r = {tex}\frac{\frac{(E - V)}{V}}R{/tex}= {tex}(\frac{E}{V - 1}){/tex}R

subject: Physics
question_html:
A wire of 10 ohm resistance is stretched to thrice its original length. What will be its

  1. new resistivity, and
  2. new resistance?
question_text: A wire of 10 ohm resistance is stretched to thrice its original length. What will be its new resistivity, and new resistance?
explanation: Resistivity {tex}\rho{/tex} remains unchanged because it is the property of the material of the wire. In both cases, volume of wire is same. So V = A'l' = Al or {tex}\frac{A^{\prime}}{A}=\frac{l}{l^{\prime}}=\frac{l}{3 l}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} [ {tex}\because l^{\prime}{/tex} = l + 2l = 3l] {tex}\therefore \frac{R^{\prime}}{R}=\frac{\rho \frac{l^{\prime}}{A^{\prime}}}{\rho \frac{l}{A}}=\frac{l^{\prime}}{l} \times \frac{A}{A^{\prime}}=\frac{3}{1} \times \frac{3}{1}{/tex} = 9 Hence R' = 9R = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 90 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution: Resistivity {tex}\rho{/tex} remains unchanged because it is the property of the material of the wire. In both cases, volume of wire is same. So V = A'l' = Al or {tex}\frac{A^{\prime}}{A}=\frac{l}{l^{\prime}}=\frac{l}{3 l}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} [ {tex}\because l^{\prime}{/tex} = l + 2l = 3l] {tex}\therefore \frac{R^{\prime}}{R}=\frac{\rho \frac{l^{\prime}}{A^{\prime}}}{\rho \frac{l}{A}}=\frac{l^{\prime}}{l} \times \frac{A}{A^{\prime}}=\frac{3}{1} \times \frac{3}{1}{/tex} = 9 Hence R' = 9R = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 90 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Resistivity {tex}\rho{/tex} remains unchanged because it is the property of the material of the wire.
  2. In both cases, volume of wire is same. So
    V = A'l' = Al
    or {tex}\frac{A^{\prime}}{A}=\frac{l}{l^{\prime}}=\frac{l}{3 l}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} [{tex}\because l^{\prime}{/tex} = l + 2l = 3l]
    {tex}\therefore \frac{R^{\prime}}{R}=\frac{\rho \frac{l^{\prime}}{A^{\prime}}}{\rho \frac{l}{A}}=\frac{l^{\prime}}{l} \times \frac{A}{A^{\prime}}=\frac{3}{1} \times \frac{3}{1}{/tex} = 9
    Hence R' = 9R = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 90 {tex}\Omega{/tex}

question_html: How many electrons flow per second through an electric bulb rated 220 V, 100 W?
question_text: How many electrons flow per second through an electric bulb rated 220 V, 100 W?
explanation: Here, V = 220 V, P = 100 W, t = 1 s I = {tex}\frac{P}{V}=\frac{100}{220}=\frac{5}{11} A{/tex} But I = {tex}\frac{q}{t}=\frac{n e}{t}{/tex} or n = {tex} \frac{It}{e}=\frac{(\frac 5{11}) \times 1}{1.6 \ \times \ 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 2.84 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 18
solution: Here, V = 220 V, P = 100 W, t = 1 s I = {tex}\frac{P}{V}=\frac{100}{220}=\frac{5}{11} A{/tex} But I = {tex}\frac{q}{t}=\frac{n e}{t}{/tex} or n = {tex} \frac{It}{e}=\frac{(\frac 5{11}) \times 1}{1.6 \ \times \ 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 2.84 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 18
solution_html:
Here, V = 220 V, P = 100 W, t = 1 s
I = {tex}\frac{P}{V}=\frac{100}{220}=\frac{5}{11} A{/tex}
But I = {tex}\frac{q}{t}=\frac{n e}{t}{/tex} or n = {tex} \frac{It}{e}=\frac{(\frac 5{11}) \times 1}{1.6 \ \times \ 10^{-19}}{/tex}
= 2.84 {tex}\times{/tex} 1018

subject: Physics
question_html: Two wires A and B are of same metal, have the same area of cross-section and have their lengths in the ratio 2 : 1. What will be the ratio of currents flowing through them respectively when the same potential difference is applied across the length of each of them?
question_text: Two wires A and B are of same metal, have the same area of cross-section and have their lengths in the ratio 2 : 1. What will be the ratio of currents flowing through them respectively when the same potential difference is applied across the length of each of them?
explanation: Current, I = {tex}\frac{V}{R}=\frac{V A}{\rho l}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} R = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A}{/tex} ] For the two wires p, A and V are same, therefore {tex}I \propto \frac{1}{l}{/tex} Hence {tex}\frac{I_{A}}{I_{B}}=\frac{l_{B}}{l_{A}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 1:2 [ {tex}\because{/tex} l A : l B = 2 : 1]
solution: Current, I = {tex}\frac{V}{R}=\frac{V A}{\rho l}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} R = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A}{/tex} ] For the two wires p, A and V are same, therefore {tex}I \propto \frac{1}{l}{/tex} Hence {tex}\frac{I_{A}}{I_{B}}=\frac{l_{B}}{l_{A}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 1:2 [ {tex}\because{/tex} l A : l B = 2 : 1]
solution_html:
Current, I = {tex}\frac{V}{R}=\frac{V A}{\rho l}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} R = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A}{/tex}]
For the two wires p, A and V are same, therefore
{tex}I \propto \frac{1}{l}{/tex}
Hence {tex}\frac{I_{A}}{I_{B}}=\frac{l_{B}}{l_{A}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 1:2 [{tex}\because{/tex} lA : lB = 2 : 1]

subject: Physics
question_html: Find the resistivity of a conductor in which a current density of 2.5 Am-2 is found to exist, when an electric field of 15 Vm-1 is applied on it.
question_text: Find the resistivity of a conductor in which a current density of 2.5 Am -2 is found to exist, when an electric field of 15 Vm -1 is applied on it.
explanation: Here j = 2.5 Am -2 , E = 15 Vm -1 Resistivity, {tex}\rho=\frac{R A}{l}=\frac{V}{I} \cdot \frac{A}{l}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac V l}{\frac I A}=\frac{E}{j}=\frac{15}{2.5}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} m
solution: Here j = 2.5 Am -2 , E = 15 Vm -1 Resistivity, {tex}\rho=\frac{R A}{l}=\frac{V}{I} \cdot \frac{A}{l}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac V l}{\frac I A}=\frac{E}{j}=\frac{15}{2.5}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} m
solution_html:
Here j = 2.5 Am-2, E = 15 Vm-1
Resistivity, {tex}\rho=\frac{R A}{l}=\frac{V}{I} \cdot \frac{A}{l}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac V l}{\frac I A}=\frac{E}{j}=\frac{15}{2.5}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex}m

subject: Physics
question_html: The circuit diagram shown in the figure has two cells {tex}\varepsilon_1{/tex} and {tex}\varepsilon_2{/tex} with EMFs 4 V and 2 V respectively, each one having an internal resistance of 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. The external resistance R is of 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. Find the magnitude and direction of currents flowing through the two cells.
question_text: The circuit diagram shown in the figure has two cells {tex}\varepsilon_1{/tex} and {tex}\varepsilon_2{/tex} with EMFs 4 V and 2 V respectively, each one having an internal resistance of 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . The external resistance R is of 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . Find the magnitude and direction of currents flowing through the two cells.
explanation: Applying Kirchhoff's second law to loop 1, we get I 1 r 1 - I 2 r 2 = {tex}\varepsilon_{1}-\varepsilon_{2}{/tex} or 2I 1 - 2 I 2 = 4 - 2 or I 1 - I 2 = 1 ...(i) Similarly, from loop 2, we get I 2 r 2 + (I 2 + I 2 ) R = {tex}\varepsilon_2{/tex} or 2 I 2 + 8 (I 1 + I 2 ) = 2 or 4 I 1 + 5 I 2 = 1 ...(ii) On solving equations (i) and (ii), we get I 1 = {tex}\frac{2}{3} A{/tex} (From -ve to +ve terminal inside {tex}\varepsilon_1{/tex} ) and I 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{3} A{/tex} (From +ve to -ve terminal inside {tex}\varepsilon_2{/tex} )
solution: Applying Kirchhoff's second law to loop 1, we get I 1 r 1 - I 2 r 2 = {tex}\varepsilon_{1}-\varepsilon_{2}{/tex} or 2I 1 - 2 I 2 = 4 - 2 or I 1 - I 2 = 1 ...(i) Similarly, from loop 2, we get I 2 r 2 + (I 2 + I 2 ) R = {tex}\varepsilon_2{/tex} or 2 I 2 + 8 (I 1 + I 2 ) = 2 or 4 I 1 + 5 I 2 = 1 ...(ii) On solving equations (i) and (ii), we get I 1 = {tex}\frac{2}{3} A{/tex} (From -ve to +ve terminal inside {tex}\varepsilon_1{/tex} ) and I 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{3} A{/tex} (From +ve to -ve terminal inside {tex}\varepsilon_2{/tex} )
solution_html:
Applying Kirchhoff's second law to loop 1, we get
I1r1 - I2r2 = {tex}\varepsilon_{1}-\varepsilon_{2}{/tex}
or 2I1 - 2 I2 = 4 - 2
or I1 - I2 = 1 ...(i)
Similarly, from loop 2, we get
I2r2 + (I2 + I2) R = {tex}\varepsilon_2{/tex}
or 2 I2 + 8 (I1 + I2) = 2
or 4 I1 + 5 I2 = 1 ...(ii)
On solving equations (i) and (ii), we get
I1 = {tex}\frac{2}{3} A{/tex} (From -ve to +ve terminal inside {tex}\varepsilon_1{/tex})
and I2 = {tex}\frac{1}{3} A{/tex} (From +ve to -ve terminal inside {tex}\varepsilon_2{/tex})

subject: Physics
question_html: Define the electrical resistivity of a material. How it is related to electrical conductivity? Of the factors, length, area of cross-section, nature of material and temperature which ones control the resistivity value of conductor?
question_text: Define the electrical resistivity of a material. How it is related to electrical conductivity? Of the factors, length, area of cross-section, nature of material and temperature which ones control the resistivity value of conductor?
explanation: Resistivity is defined as the measure of resistance to electrical conduction for a given size of material. For a particular material at specified temperature, resistivity is the electrical resistance per unit length and per unit of cross-sectional area. Resistivity is the inverse of conductivity. The resistivity of a material depends upon the material and temperature of the material and is independent of the length and area of cross-section.
solution: Resistivity is defined as the measure of resistance to electrical conduction for a given size of material. For a particular material at specified temperature, resistivity is the electrical resistance per unit length and per unit of cross-sectional area. Resistivity is the inverse of conductivity. The resistivity of a material depends upon the material and temperature of the material and is independent of the length and area of cross-section.
solution_html:
Resistivity is defined as the measure of resistance to electrical conduction for a given size of material.
For a particular material at specified temperature, resistivity is the electrical resistance per unit length and per unit of cross-sectional area.
Resistivity is the inverse of conductivity.
The resistivity of a material depends upon the material and temperature of the material and is independent of the length and area of cross-section.

subject: Physics
question_html: Two resistors of 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} are connected in parallel to a constant d.c. voltage. In which case more heat is produced?
question_text: Two resistors of 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} are connected in parallel to a constant d.c. voltage. In which case more heat is produced?
explanation: Heat produced, H = {tex}\frac{V^2t}{R}{/tex} or {tex}H \propto \frac1R{/tex} Thus more heat is produced in 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor than that of 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor.
solution: Heat produced, H = {tex}\frac{V^2t}{R}{/tex} or {tex}H \propto \frac1R{/tex} Thus more heat is produced in 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor than that of 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor.
solution_html:
Heat produced, H = {tex}\frac{V^2t}{R}{/tex} or {tex}H \propto \frac1R{/tex}
Thus more heat is produced in 2{tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor than that of 4{tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor.

subject: Physics
question_html: Figure shows a cell of emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} and internal resistance r, connected to a voltmeter V and a variable resistance R Deduce the relation- ship among V, {tex}\varepsilon{/tex}, R and r. How will V vary when R is reduced.
question_text: Figure shows a cell of emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} and internal resistance r, connected to a voltmeter V and a variable resistance R Deduce the relation- ship among V, {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} , R and r. How will V vary when R is reduced.
explanation: The equivalent resistance first i.e. Internal resistance and External resistance. Net emf = E Net resistance = R + r Net Current = {tex}\frac{E}{R}{/tex} + r V = {tex}{I}{R}{/tex} V = {tex}\frac{ER}{R+r}{/tex} In order to verify the variation of the voltage as a function of External resistance, take R as common from both numerator and denominator V = {tex}\frac{E}{1+r/R}{/tex} As R is reduced, V is decreased.
solution: The equivalent resistance first i.e. Internal resistance and External resistance. Net emf = E Net resistance = R + r Net Current = {tex}\frac{E}{R}{/tex} + r V = {tex}{I}{R}{/tex} V = {tex}\frac{ER}{R+r}{/tex} In order to verify the variation of the voltage as a function of External resistance, take R as common from both numerator and denominator V = {tex}\frac{E}{1+r/R}{/tex} As R is reduced, V is decreased.
solution_html:
The equivalent resistance first i.e. Internal resistance and External resistance.
Net emf = E
Net resistance = R + r
Net Current = {tex}\frac{E}{R}{/tex} + r
V = {tex}{I}{R}{/tex}
V = {tex}\frac{ER}{R+r}{/tex}
In order to verify the variation of the voltage as a function of External resistance, take R as common from both numerator and denominator
V = {tex}\frac{E}{1+r/R}{/tex}
As R is reduced, V is decreased.

question_text: The maximum power rating of a 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor is 2.0 kW. (That is, this is the maximum power the resistor can dissipate (as heat) without melting or changing in some other undesirable way). Would you connect this resistor directly across a 300 V d.c. source of negligible internal resistance? Explain your answer.
explanation: Maximum power rating of the given 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor, {tex}P^\prime{/tex} = 2.0 kW When connected to 300 V d.c. supply, the power consumption or rate of production of heat would be P = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{R}=\frac{300 \ \times \ 300}{20}{/tex} W = 4500 W = 4.5 kW This power consumption exceeds the maximum power rating of the resistor. Hence the 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor must not be connected directly across the 300 V d.c. source. For doing so, a small resistance of 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} should be connected in series with it.
solution: Maximum power rating of the given 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor, {tex}P^\prime{/tex} = 2.0 kW When connected to 300 V d.c. supply, the power consumption or rate of production of heat would be P = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{R}=\frac{300 \ \times \ 300}{20}{/tex} W = 4500 W = 4.5 kW This power consumption exceeds the maximum power rating of the resistor. Hence the 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor must not be connected directly across the 300 V d.c. source. For doing so, a small resistance of 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} should be connected in series with it.
solution_html:
Maximum power rating of the given 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor,
{tex}P^\prime{/tex} = 2.0 kW
When connected to 300 V d.c. supply, the power consumption or rate of production of heat would be
P = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{R}=\frac{300 \ \times \ 300}{20}{/tex} W = 4500 W = 4.5 kW
This power consumption exceeds the maximum power rating of the resistor. Hence the 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor must not be connected directly across the 300 V d.c. source. For doing so, a small resistance of 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} should be connected in series with it.

subject: Physics
question_html: Two wires A and B of the same material and having the same length, have their cross-sectional areas in the ratio 1: 4. What would be the ratio of heat produced in these wires when same voltage is applied across each?
question_text: Two wires A and B of the same material and having the same length, have their cross-sectional areas in the ratio 1: 4. What would be the ratio of heat produced in these wires when same voltage is applied across each?
explanation: R A = {tex}\frac{\rho l}{A}, R_{B}=\frac{\rho l}{4 A} {/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{R_{A}}{R_{B}}{/tex} = 4 Ratio of heats produced in the two wires will be {tex}\frac{P_{A}}{P_{B}}=\frac{V^{2} / R_{A}}{V^{2} / R_{B}}=\frac{R_{B}}{R_{A}}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} = 1 : 4
solution: R A = {tex}\frac{\rho l}{A}, R_{B}=\frac{\rho l}{4 A} {/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{R_{A}}{R_{B}}{/tex} = 4 Ratio of heats produced in the two wires will be {tex}\frac{P_{A}}{P_{B}}=\frac{V^{2} / R_{A}}{V^{2} / R_{B}}=\frac{R_{B}}{R_{A}}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} = 1 : 4
solution_html:
RA = {tex}\frac{\rho l}{A}, R_{B}=\frac{\rho l}{4 A} {/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{R_{A}}{R_{B}}{/tex} = 4
Ratio of heats produced in the two wires will be
{tex}\frac{P_{A}}{P_{B}}=\frac{V^{2} / R_{A}}{V^{2} / R_{B}}=\frac{R_{B}}{R_{A}}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} = 1 : 4

question_html: A copper wire of diameter 1.0 mm carries a current of 0.2 A. Copper has 8.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1028 atoms per cubic meter. Find the drift velocity of electrons, assuming that one charge carrier of 1.6  {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C is associated with each atom of the metal.
question_text: A copper wire of diameter 1.0 mm carries a current of 0.2 A. Copper has 8.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 28 atoms per cubic meter. Find the drift velocity of electrons, assuming that one charge carrier of 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 C is associated with each atom of the metal.
explanation: Here, D = 1.0 mm = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −3 m, I = 0.2A, n = 8.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 28 m −3 , e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −19 C v d = {tex}\frac{I}{n A e}=\frac{I}{n\left(\pi D^{2} / 4\right) e}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.2}{\left(8.4 \ \times \ 10^{28}\right) \times \frac{22}{7} \ \times \ \frac{\left(10^{-3}\right)^{2}}{4} \ \times \ 1.6 \ \times \ 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 1.895 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 ms -1
solution: Here, D = 1.0 mm = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −3 m, I = 0.2A, n = 8.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 28 m −3 , e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −19 C v d = {tex}\frac{I}{n A e}=\frac{I}{n\left(\pi D^{2} / 4\right) e}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.2}{\left(8.4 \ \times \ 10^{28}\right) \times \frac{22}{7} \ \times \ \frac{\left(10^{-3}\right)^{2}}{4} \ \times \ 1.6 \ \times \ 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 1.895 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 ms -1
solution_html:
Here, D = 1.0 mm = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−3m,
I = 0.2A, n = 8.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1028m−3, e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−19C
vd = {tex}\frac{I}{n A e}=\frac{I}{n\left(\pi D^{2} / 4\right) e}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{0.2}{\left(8.4 \ \times \ 10^{28}\right) \times \frac{22}{7} \ \times \ \frac{\left(10^{-3}\right)^{2}}{4} \ \times \ 1.6 \ \times \ 10^{-19}}{/tex}
= 1.895 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 ms-1

subject: Physics
question_html: Mobilities of electrons and holes in a sample of intrinsic germanium at room temperature are 0.54 m2V-1s-1 and 0.18 m2V-1s-1 respectively. If the electron and hole densities are equal to 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1019m-3, calculate the germanium conductivity.
question_text: Mobilities of electrons and holes in a sample of intrinsic germanium at room temperature are 0.54 m 2 V -1 s -1 and 0.18 m 2 V -1 s -1 respectively. If the electron and hole densities are equal to 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 19 m -3 , calculate the germanium conductivity.
explanation: Here {tex}\mu_e{/tex} = 0.54 m 2 V -1 s -1 , {tex}\mu_h{/tex} = 0.18 m 2 V -1 s -1 , n = p = 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 19 m -3 Conductivity, {tex}\sigma{/tex} = e(n {tex}\mu_e{/tex} + p {tex}\mu_h{/tex} ) = en ( {tex}\mu_e{/tex} + {tex}\mu_h{/tex} ) = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 19 (0.54 + 0.18) = 4.147 Sm -1
solution: Here {tex}\mu_e{/tex} = 0.54 m 2 V -1 s -1 , {tex}\mu_h{/tex} = 0.18 m 2 V -1 s -1 , n = p = 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 19 m -3 Conductivity, {tex}\sigma{/tex} = e(n {tex}\mu_e{/tex} + p {tex}\mu_h{/tex} ) = en ( {tex}\mu_e{/tex} + {tex}\mu_h{/tex} ) = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 19 (0.54 + 0.18) = 4.147 Sm -1
solution_html:
Here {tex}\mu_e{/tex} = 0.54 m2V-1s-1, {tex}\mu_h{/tex} = 0.18 m2V-1s-1,
n = p = 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1019 m-3
Conductivity,
{tex}\sigma{/tex} = e(n{tex}\mu_e{/tex} + p{tex}\mu_h{/tex}) = en ({tex}\mu_e{/tex} + {tex}\mu_h{/tex})
= 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1019 (0.54 + 0.18)
= 4.147 Sm-1

subject: Physics
question_html: Write dimensional formula of electrical conductivity.
question_text: Write dimensional formula of electrical conductivity.
explanation: Electrical conductivity = {tex}\frac{1}{\text { Resistivity }}=\frac{\text { Length }}{\text { Resistance } \ \times \ \text { Area }}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} [Electrical conductivity] = {tex}\frac{L}{M L^{2} T^{-3} A^{-2} L^{2}}{/tex} = [M -1 L -3 T 3 A 2 ]
solution: Electrical conductivity = {tex}\frac{1}{\text { Resistivity }}=\frac{\text { Length }}{\text { Resistance } \ \times \ \text { Area }}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} [Electrical conductivity] = {tex}\frac{L}{M L^{2} T^{-3} A^{-2} L^{2}}{/tex} = [M -1 L -3 T 3 A 2 ]
solution_html:
Electrical conductivity = {tex}\frac{1}{\text { Resistivity }}=\frac{\text { Length }}{\text { Resistance } \ \times \ \text { Area }}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Electrical conductivity] = {tex}\frac{L}{M L^{2} T^{-3} A^{-2} L^{2}}{/tex} = [M-1L-3T3A2]

subject: Physics
question_html:
A potential difference V is applied to a conductor of length l, diameter D. How are the electric field E, the drift velocity vd and the resistance R affected when

  1. V is doubled
  2. l is doubled
  3. D is doubled?
question_text: A potential difference V is applied to a conductor of length l, diameter D. How are the electric field E, the drift velocity v d and the resistance R affected when V is doubled l is doubled D is doubled?
explanation: Electric field, E = {tex}\frac{V}{l}{/tex} Drift velocity, {tex}v_{d}=\frac{e E}{m} \tau=\frac{e V}{m l} \tau{/tex} Resistance, R = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A}=\frac{4 \rho l}{\pi D^{2}}{/tex} When V is doubled, E becomes double, v d becomes double and R remains unchanged. When l is doubled, E becomes half, v d becomes half and R becomes double. When D is doubled, E remains unchanged, v d is also unchanged and R becomes one-fourth.
solution: Electric field, E = {tex}\frac{V}{l}{/tex} Drift velocity, {tex}v_{d}=\frac{e E}{m} \tau=\frac{e V}{m l} \tau{/tex} Resistance, R = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A}=\frac{4 \rho l}{\pi D^{2}}{/tex} When V is doubled, E becomes double, v d becomes double and R remains unchanged. When l is doubled, E becomes half, v d becomes half and R becomes double. When D is doubled, E remains unchanged, v d is also unchanged and R becomes one-fourth.
solution_html:
Electric field,
E = {tex}\frac{V}{l}{/tex}
Drift velocity, {tex}v_{d}=\frac{e E}{m} \tau=\frac{e V}{m l} \tau{/tex}
Resistance, R = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A}=\frac{4 \rho l}{\pi D^{2}}{/tex}

  1. When V is doubled, E becomes double, vd becomes double and R remains unchanged.
  2. When l is doubled, E becomes half, vd becomes half and R becomes double.
  3. When D is doubled, E remains unchanged, vd is also unchanged and R becomes one-fourth.

subject: Physics
question_html: If the galvanometer in the circuit (Figure) reads zero, find the value of resistor R. The 12 V source has negligible internal resistance. If cool air is blown across the wire-wound resistance, what effect will be noticed and why?
question_text: If the galvanometer in the circuit (Figure) reads zero, find the value of resistor R. The 12 V source has negligible internal resistance. If cool air is blown across the wire-wound resistance, what effect will be noticed and why?
explanation: As the galvanometer shows no deflection, so the p.d. across R is 2 V. If I is the current in the circuit, then IR = 2 volt or I = {tex}\frac{2}{R}{/tex} But I = {tex}\frac{12}{R \ + \ 10^{4}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{2}{R}=\frac{12}{R \ + \ 10^{4}}{/tex} On solving, R = 2000 {tex}\Omega{/tex} = 2 k {tex}\Omega{/tex} When cool air is blown across the wire wound resistor, its resistance decreases. The current in the circuit increases. The voltage across R increases. The galvanometer will show some deflection.
solution: As the galvanometer shows no deflection, so the p.d. across R is 2 V. If I is the current in the circuit, then IR = 2 volt or I = {tex}\frac{2}{R}{/tex} But I = {tex}\frac{12}{R \ + \ 10^{4}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{2}{R}=\frac{12}{R \ + \ 10^{4}}{/tex} On solving, R = 2000 {tex}\Omega{/tex} = 2 k {tex}\Omega{/tex} When cool air is blown across the wire wound resistor, its resistance decreases. The current in the circuit increases. The voltage across R increases. The galvanometer will show some deflection.
solution_html:
As the galvanometer shows no deflection, so the p.d. across R is 2 V. If I is the current in the circuit, then
IR = 2 volt or I = {tex}\frac{2}{R}{/tex}
But I = {tex}\frac{12}{R \ + \ 10^{4}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{2}{R}=\frac{12}{R \ + \ 10^{4}}{/tex}
On solving, R = 2000 {tex}\Omega{/tex} = 2 k{tex}\Omega{/tex}
When cool air is blown across the wire wound resistor, its resistance decreases. The current in the circuit increases. The voltage across R increases. The galvanometer will show some deflection.

subject: Physics
question_html:
n identical cells each of emf and internal resistance {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} are connected in series to resistor R.

  1. Deduce an expression for the internal resistance r of one cell in terms of the current I flowing through the circuit.
  2. How does the internal resistance of the cell vary with temperature?
question_text: n identical cells each of emf and internal resistance {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} are connected in series to resistor R . Deduce an expression for the internal resistance r of one cell in terms of the current I flowing through the circuit. How does the internal resistance of the cell vary with temperature?
explanation: Figure shows n identical cells connected in series to a resistor R. As the internal resistances of all the n cells are connected in series, so total internal resistance = nr Total resistance in the circuit = R + nr Total emf of the cells = nB Current in the circuit = I Using Ohm's law, Total resistance = {tex}\frac{\text { Total emf }}{\text { Current in the circuit }}{/tex} R + nr = {tex}\frac{n \varepsilon}{I}{/tex} or nr = {tex}\frac{n \varepsilon}{I}{/tex} - R or r = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{I}-\frac{R}{n}{/tex} The internal resistance of a cell decreases with the increase of temperature.
solution: Figure shows n identical cells connected in series to a resistor R. As the internal resistances of all the n cells are connected in series, so total internal resistance = nr Total resistance in the circuit = R + nr Total emf of the cells = nB Current in the circuit = I Using Ohm's law, Total resistance = {tex}\frac{\text { Total emf }}{\text { Current in the circuit }}{/tex} R + nr = {tex}\frac{n \varepsilon}{I}{/tex} or nr = {tex}\frac{n \varepsilon}{I}{/tex} - R or r = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{I}-\frac{R}{n}{/tex} The internal resistance of a cell decreases with the increase of temperature.
solution_html:
1. Figure shows n identical cells connected in series to a resistor R.

    As the internal resistances of all the n cells are connected in series, so total internal resistance = nr
    Total resistance in the circuit = R + nr
    Total emf of the cells = nB
    Current in the circuit = I
    Using Ohm's law,
    Total resistance = {tex}\frac{\text { Total emf }}{\text { Current in the circuit }}{/tex}
    R + nr = {tex}\frac{n \varepsilon}{I}{/tex} or nr = {tex}\frac{n \varepsilon}{I}{/tex} - R
    or r = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{I}-\frac{R}{n}{/tex}
  2. The internal resistance of a cell decreases with the increase of temperature.

question_html:
An electric heater and an electric bulb are rated 500 W, 220 V and 100 W, 220 V respectively. Both are connected in series to a 220 V d.c. mains. Calculate the power consumed by

  1. the heater and
  2. electric bulb.
question_text: An electric heater and an electric bulb are rated 500 W, 220 V and 100 W, 220 V respectively. Both are connected in series to a 220 V d.c. mains. Calculate the power consumed by the heater and electric bulb.
explanation: {tex}P=\frac{V^2}{R} {/tex} or {tex}R=\frac{V^2}{P}{/tex} For heter, Resistane {tex}R_h=\frac{(220)^2}{500}=96.8 \Omega{/tex} For bulb resistance {tex}R_1=\frac{(220)^2}{100}=484 \Omega{/tex} Current in the circuit when both are connected in series {tex}I=\frac{V}{R_L+R_h}=\frac{220}{484+96.8}=0.38{/tex} Power consumed by heater = I 2 Ph = (0.38) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96.8 = 13.98 W Power consumed by bulb = I 2 R L = (0.38)2 {tex}\times{/tex} 484 = 69.89 W
solution: {tex}P=\frac{V^2}{R} {/tex} or {tex}R=\frac{V^2}{P}{/tex} For heter, Resistane {tex}R_h=\frac{(220)^2}{500}=96.8 \Omega{/tex} For bulb resistance {tex}R_1=\frac{(220)^2}{100}=484 \Omega{/tex} Current in the circuit when both are connected in series {tex}I=\frac{V}{R_L+R_h}=\frac{220}{484+96.8}=0.38{/tex} Power consumed by heater = I 2 Ph = (0.38) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96.8 = 13.98 W Power consumed by bulb = I 2 R L = (0.38)2 {tex}\times{/tex} 484 = 69.89 W
solution_html:
{tex}P=\frac{V^2}{R} {/tex} or {tex}R=\frac{V^2}{P}{/tex}
For heter, Resistane
{tex}R_h=\frac{(220)^2}{500}=96.8 \Omega{/tex}
For bulb resistance {tex}R_1=\frac{(220)^2}{100}=484 \Omega{/tex}
Current in the circuit when both are connected in series
{tex}I=\frac{V}{R_L+R_h}=\frac{220}{484+96.8}=0.38{/tex}

  1. Power consumed by heater
    = I2Ph = (0.38)2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96.8 = 13.98 W
  2. Power consumed by bulb
    = I2RL = (0.38)2 {tex}\times{/tex} 484 = 69.89 W

question_html:
Two cells of emfs 3 V and 4 V and ihtemal resistances 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} respectively are connected in parallel so as to send current in the same direction through an external resistance of 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex}.

  1. Draw the circuit diagram.
  2. Using Kirchhoff s laws, calculate
    1. the current through each branch of the circuit,
    2. p.d across the 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance.
question_text: Two cells of emfs 3 V and 4 V and ihtemal resistances 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} respectively are connected in parallel so as to send current in the same direction through an external resistance of 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . Draw the circuit diagram. Using Kirchhoff s laws, calculate the current through each branch of the circuit, p.d across the 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance.
explanation: Let the current are I 1 , I 2 and I 3 I 3 = I 1 + I 2 ...(i) In loop ACDF, I 3 (5) + I 1 (2) = 4 ...(ii) In loop BCDE, I 3 (5) + I 1 (1) = 3 ...(iii) From equation (i) and (ii) and (iii) I 1 = {tex}\frac{9}{17}{/tex} I 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{17}{/tex} I 3 = {tex}\frac{10}{17}{/tex} VCD = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} I 3 = {tex}\frac{50}{17}{/tex} = 2.94.
solution: Let the current are I 1 , I 2 and I 3 I 3 = I 1 + I 2 ...(i) In loop ACDF, I 3 (5) + I 1 (2) = 4 ...(ii) In loop BCDE, I 3 (5) + I 1 (1) = 3 ...(iii) From equation (i) and (ii) and (iii) I 1 = {tex}\frac{9}{17}{/tex} I 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{17}{/tex} I 3 = {tex}\frac{10}{17}{/tex} VCD = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} I 3 = {tex}\frac{50}{17}{/tex} = 2.94.
solution_html:
2. Let the current are I1, I2 and I3
    I3 = I1 + I2 ...(i)
    In loop ACDF,
    I3(5) + I1(2) = 4 ...(ii)
    In loop BCDE,
    I3(5) + I1(1) = 3 ...(iii)
    From equation (i) and (ii) and (iii)
    I1 = {tex}\frac{9}{17}{/tex}
    I2 = {tex}\frac{1}{17}{/tex}
    I3 = {tex}\frac{10}{17}{/tex}
    VCD = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} I3 = {tex}\frac{50}{17}{/tex} = 2.94.

subject: Physics
question_html: A constant voltage is applied between the two ends of a uniform metallic wire. Heat Q is developed in it. If another wire, double the radius and twice the length is used, how much heat will be developed in it?
question_text: A constant voltage is applied between the two ends of a uniform metallic wire. Heat Q is developed in it. If another wire, double the radius and twice the length is used, how much heat will be developed in it?
explanation: Q = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{R} t=V^{2} \cdot \frac{\pi r^{2}}{\rho l} \cdot t{/tex} {tex}Q^{\prime}=V^{2} \cdot \frac{\pi(2 r)^{2}}{\rho \cdot 2 l} \cdot t{/tex} = 2Q
solution: Q = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{R} t=V^{2} \cdot \frac{\pi r^{2}}{\rho l} \cdot t{/tex} {tex}Q^{\prime}=V^{2} \cdot \frac{\pi(2 r)^{2}}{\rho \cdot 2 l} \cdot t{/tex} = 2Q
solution_html:
Q = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{R} t=V^{2} \cdot \frac{\pi r^{2}}{\rho l} \cdot t{/tex}
{tex}Q^{\prime}=V^{2} \cdot \frac{\pi(2 r)^{2}}{\rho \cdot 2 l} \cdot t{/tex} = 2Q

subject: Physics
question_html: A copper wire has a resistance of 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and an area of cross-section 1 mm2. A potential difference of 10 V exists across the wire. Calculate the drift speed of electrons if the number of electrons per cubic metre in copper is 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1028 electrons
question_text: A copper wire has a resistance of 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and an area of cross-section 1 mm 2 . A potential difference of 10 V exists across the wire. Calculate the drift speed of electrons if the number of electrons per cubic metre in copper is 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 28 electrons
explanation: Here R = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , A = 1 mm 2 = 10 -6 m 2 , V = 10 V, n = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 28 electrons/m 3 Now I = enAv d {tex}\therefore \frac{V}{R}{/tex} = en A v d or v d = {tex}\frac{V}{e n A R}=\frac{10}{1.6 \ \times \ 10^{-19} \ \times \ 8 \ \times \ 10^{28} \ \times \ 10^{-6} \ \times \ 10}{/tex} = 0.078 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 ms -1 = 0.078 mm s -1
solution: Here R = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , A = 1 mm 2 = 10 -6 m 2 , V = 10 V, n = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 28 electrons/m 3 Now I = enAv d {tex}\therefore \frac{V}{R}{/tex} = en A v d or v d = {tex}\frac{V}{e n A R}=\frac{10}{1.6 \ \times \ 10^{-19} \ \times \ 8 \ \times \ 10^{28} \ \times \ 10^{-6} \ \times \ 10}{/tex} = 0.078 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 ms -1 = 0.078 mm s -1
solution_html:
Here R = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex}, A = 1 mm2 = 10-6m2, V = 10 V, n = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1028 electrons/m3
Now I = enAvd
{tex}\therefore \frac{V}{R}{/tex} = en A vd
or vd = {tex}\frac{V}{e n A R}=\frac{10}{1.6 \ \times \ 10^{-19} \ \times \ 8 \ \times \ 10^{28} \ \times \ 10^{-6} \ \times \ 10}{/tex}
= 0.078 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 ms-1 = 0.078 mm s-1

subject: Physics
question_html: A generator is supplying power to a factory by cables of resistance 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. If the generator is generating 50 kW power at 5000 V, what is the power received by the factory?
question_text: A generator is supplying power to a factory by cables of resistance 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . If the generator is generating 50 kW power at 5000 V, what is the power received by the factory?
explanation: P = 50 kW = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 W = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 W, V = 5000 V current supplied by generator I = {tex}\frac{P}{V}=\frac{5 \ \times \ 10^{4}}{5000}{/tex} = 10A power wasted as during transmission by cable P = l 2 R = (10) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 2000 W = 2 kW power received by factory = P − {tex}l^\prime{/tex} = 50 − 2 = 48 kW
solution: P = 50 kW = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 W = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 W, V = 5000 V current supplied by generator I = {tex}\frac{P}{V}=\frac{5 \ \times \ 10^{4}}{5000}{/tex} = 10A power wasted as during transmission by cable P = l 2 R = (10) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 2000 W = 2 kW power received by factory = P − {tex}l^\prime{/tex} = 50 − 2 = 48 kW
solution_html:
P = 50 kW = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 W = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 W, V = 5000 V
current supplied by generator I = {tex}\frac{P}{V}=\frac{5 \ \times \ 10^{4}}{5000}{/tex} = 10A
power wasted as during transmission by cable P = l2R = (10)2 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 2000 W = 2 kW
power received by factory = P − {tex}l^\prime{/tex} = 50 − 2 = 48 kW

question_html: Resistivities of copper, constantan and silver are 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 {tex}\Omega{/tex} m, 39.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} m and 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} m, respectively. Which has the best conductivity?
question_text: Resistivities of copper, constantan and silver are 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} m, 39.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} m and 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} m, respectively. Which has the best conductivity?
explanation: Conductivity = {tex}\frac{1}{\text { Resistivity }}{/tex} As silver has the lowest resistivity, so it has the best conductivity.
solution: Conductivity = {tex}\frac{1}{\text { Resistivity }}{/tex} As silver has the lowest resistivity, so it has the best conductivity.
solution_html:
Conductivity = {tex}\frac{1}{\text { Resistivity }}{/tex}
As silver has the lowest resistivity, so it has the best conductivity.

subject: Physics
question_html: Two identical cells, each of emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} and internal resistance r are connected in parallel to an external resistance R. Find the expression for the total current flowing in the circuit.
question_text: Two identical cells, each of emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} and internal resistance r are connected in parallel to an external resistance R. Find the expression for the total current flowing in the circuit.
explanation: Suppose 2 cells each of emf ε and internal resistance r be connected in parallel between points A and B, Let R be the external resistance. Since 2 internal resistance are connected in parallel, their equivalent resistance R’ is given by {tex}\frac{1}{R'} = \frac{1}{r} + \frac{1}{r }{/tex} Or R' = {tex}\frac r2{/tex} Total resistance in the circuit is R total = R + R' = {tex}\frac{R + r}2{/tex} Total emf of parallel combination = emf due to single cell = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} The current in the circuit is {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}+\frac{r}{2}=\frac{2 \varepsilon}{2 R+r}{/tex}
solution: Suppose 2 cells each of emf ε and internal resistance r be connected in parallel between points A and B, Let R be the external resistance. Since 2 internal resistance are connected in parallel, their equivalent resistance R’ is given by {tex}\frac{1}{R'} = \frac{1}{r} + \frac{1}{r }{/tex} Or R' = {tex}\frac r2{/tex} Total resistance in the circuit is R total = R + R' = {tex}\frac{R + r}2{/tex} Total emf of parallel combination = emf due to single cell = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} The current in the circuit is {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}+\frac{r}{2}=\frac{2 \varepsilon}{2 R+r}{/tex}
solution_html:
Suppose 2 cells each of emf ε and internal resistance r be connected in parallel between points A and B, Let R be the external resistance.

Since 2 internal resistance are connected in parallel, their equivalent resistance R’ is given by
{tex}\frac{1}{R'} = \frac{1}{r} + \frac{1}{r }{/tex}
Or R' = {tex}\frac r2{/tex}
Total resistance in the circuit is
Rtotal = R + R' = {tex}\frac{R + r}2{/tex}
Total emf of parallel combination = emf due to single cell = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex}
The current in the circuit is
{tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}+\frac{r}{2}=\frac{2 \varepsilon}{2 R+r}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Two wires of equal cross-sectional area, one of copper and other of manganin have the same resistance. Which one will be longer?
question_text: Two wires of equal cross-sectional area, one of copper and other of manganin have the same resistance. Which one will be longer?
explanation: As R = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A}{/tex} {tex} \therefore l=\frac{R A}{\rho}{/tex} For both wires R and A are same and {tex}\rho_{\text {copper }}<\rho_{\text {manganin }}{/tex} {tex}\therefore l_{\text {copper }}>l_{\text {manganin }}{/tex} i.e., copper wire is longer than manganin wire.
solution: As R = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A}{/tex} {tex} \therefore l=\frac{R A}{\rho}{/tex} For both wires R and A are same and {tex}\rho_{\text {copper }}<\rho_{\text {manganin }}{/tex} {tex}\therefore l_{\text {copper }}>l_{\text {manganin }}{/tex} i.e., copper wire is longer than manganin wire.
solution_html:
As R = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A}{/tex}
{tex} \therefore l=\frac{R A}{\rho}{/tex}
For both wires R and A are same and
{tex}\rho_{\text {copper }}<\rho_{\text {manganin }}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore l_{\text {copper }}>l_{\text {manganin }}{/tex}
i.e., copper wire is longer than manganin wire.

subject: Physics
question_html: Two identical cells, whether joined together in series or in parallel give the same current, when connected to an external resistance of 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. Find the internal resistance of each cell.
question_text: Two identical cells, whether joined together in series or in parallel give the same current, when connected to an external resistance of 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . Find the internal resistance of each cell.
explanation: When the cells are connected in series, (Figure), the current in the circuit is I s = {tex}\frac{2 E}{1 \ + \ 2 r}{/tex} When the cells are connected in parallel (Figure), the current in the circuit is I s = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{1 \ + \ \frac{r \ \times \ r}{r \ + \ r}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 e}{2 \ + \ r}{/tex} Given I s = I p {tex}\therefore \frac{2 \varepsilon}{1 \ + \ 2 r}=\frac{2 \varepsilon}{2 \ + \ r}{/tex} or 1 + 2r = 2 + r or r = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution: When the cells are connected in series, (Figure), the current in the circuit is I s = {tex}\frac{2 E}{1 \ + \ 2 r}{/tex} When the cells are connected in parallel (Figure), the current in the circuit is I s = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{1 \ + \ \frac{r \ \times \ r}{r \ + \ r}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 e}{2 \ + \ r}{/tex} Given I s = I p {tex}\therefore \frac{2 \varepsilon}{1 \ + \ 2 r}=\frac{2 \varepsilon}{2 \ + \ r}{/tex} or 1 + 2r = 2 + r or r = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
When the cells are connected in series, (Figure), the current in the circuit is
Is = {tex}\frac{2 E}{1 \ + \ 2 r}{/tex}

When the cells are connected in parallel (Figure), the current in the circuit is

Is = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{1 \ + \ \frac{r \ \times \ r}{r \ + \ r}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 e}{2 \ + \ r}{/tex}
Given Is = Ip
{tex}\therefore \frac{2 \varepsilon}{1 \ + \ 2 r}=\frac{2 \varepsilon}{2 \ + \ r}{/tex}
or 1 + 2r = 2 + r or r = 1{tex}\Omega{/tex}

question_html: Of copper and nichrome, which one has possibly larger value of temperature coefficient of resistance?
question_text: Of copper and nichrome, which one has possibly larger value of temperature coefficient of resistance?
explanation: Nichrome being an alloy has more resistance as compared to copper which is metal (as the resistivity of the alloy is more than metal). That is why nichrome wire gets heated first.
solution: Nichrome being an alloy has more resistance as compared to copper which is metal (as the resistivity of the alloy is more than metal). That is why nichrome wire gets heated first.
