subject: Physics
question_html: How is the conductivity of an electrolyte affected by the increase of temperature?
question_text: How is the conductivity of an electrolyte affected by the increase of temperature?
explanation: When we increase the temperature, the kinetic energy of the ions increases and they move faster i.e. they conduct their bearing charge faster and thus result in increased conductivity. So, with increase in temperature, the conductivity of electrolytic conductors increases.
solution: When we increase the temperature, the kinetic energy of the ions increases and they move faster i.e. they conduct their bearing charge faster and thus result in increased conductivity. So, with increase in temperature, the conductivity of electrolytic conductors increases.
solution_html: When we increase the temperature, the kinetic energy of the ions increases and they move faster i.e. they conduct their bearing charge faster and thus result in increased conductivity. So, with increase in temperature, the conductivity of electrolytic conductors increases.

question_html: A metallic wire of length 1 m is stretched to double its length. Calculate the ratio of its initial and final resistances assuming that there is no change in its density on stretching.
question_text: A metallic wire of length 1 m is stretched to double its length. Calculate the ratio of its initial and final resistances assuming that there is no change in its density on stretching.
explanation: R = {tex}\frac {\rho L}A{/tex} On stretching it to double light = LA = (2L)A' = A' = {tex}\frac A2{/tex} R' {tex}=\frac{\rho(2 L)}{(\frac A 2)}{/tex} = {tex}4 \frac{\rho L}{A}{/tex} = 4R R' = 4R {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{R}{R^\prime}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
solution: R = {tex}\frac {\rho L}A{/tex} On stretching it to double light = LA = (2L)A' = A' = {tex}\frac A2{/tex} R' {tex}=\frac{\rho(2 L)}{(\frac A 2)}{/tex} = {tex}4 \frac{\rho L}{A}{/tex} = 4R R' = 4R {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{R}{R^\prime}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
R = {tex}\frac {\rho L}A{/tex}
On stretching it to double light
= LA = (2L)A'
= A' = {tex}\frac A2{/tex}
R' {tex}=\frac{\rho(2 L)}{(\frac A 2)}{/tex} = {tex}4 \frac{\rho L}{A}{/tex} = 4R
R' = 4R
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{R}{R^\prime}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: An electric bulb is marked 100 W, 230 V. If the supply voltage drops to 115 V. What is the heat and light energy produced by the bulb in 20 min? Calculate the current flowing through it.
question_text: An electric bulb is marked 100 W, 230 V. If the supply voltage drops to 115 V. What is the heat and light energy produced by the bulb in 20 min? Calculate the current flowing through it.
explanation: If the resistance of the bulb be R, then Rate of production of heat and light energy, P = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{R}{/tex} {tex}\therefore R=\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{230 \ \times \ 230}{100}=529 \Omega{/tex} When the voltage drops to {tex}V^\prime{/tex} = 115 V, the total heat and light energy produced by the bulb in 20 min will be H = {tex}P \times t=\frac{V^{\prime 2}}{R} \times t{/tex} = {tex}\frac{115 \ \times \ 115}{529} \times 20 \times 60{/tex} = 30,000 J = 30 kJ Current, I = {tex}\frac{V^{\prime}}{R}=\frac{115}{529}=\frac{5}{23} \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
solution: If the resistance of the bulb be R, then Rate of production of heat and light energy, P = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{R}{/tex} {tex}\therefore R=\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{230 \ \times \ 230}{100}=529 \Omega{/tex} When the voltage drops to {tex}V^\prime{/tex} = 115 V, the total heat and light energy produced by the bulb in 20 min will be H = {tex}P \times t=\frac{V^{\prime 2}}{R} \times t{/tex} = {tex}\frac{115 \ \times \ 115}{529} \times 20 \times 60{/tex} = 30,000 J = 30 kJ Current, I = {tex}\frac{V^{\prime}}{R}=\frac{115}{529}=\frac{5}{23} \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
solution_html:
If the resistance of the bulb be R, then Rate of production of heat and light energy,
P = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{R}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore R=\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{230 \ \times \ 230}{100}=529 \Omega{/tex}
When the voltage drops to {tex}V^\prime{/tex} = 115 V, the total heat and light energy produced by the bulb in 20 min will be
H = {tex}P \times t=\frac{V^{\prime 2}}{R} \times t{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{115 \ \times \ 115}{529} \times 20 \times 60{/tex} = 30,000 J = 30 kJ
Current, I = {tex}\frac{V^{\prime}}{R}=\frac{115}{529}=\frac{5}{23} \mathrm{~A}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: You are given a primary and a secondary cell of the same emf. From which cell will you be able to draw larger current and why?
question_text: You are given a primary and a secondary cell of the same emf. From which cell will you be able to draw larger current and why?
explanation: The internal resistance of a primary cell is more than that of a secondary cell, so a secondary cell will provide a larger current.
solution: The internal resistance of a primary cell is more than that of a secondary cell, so a secondary cell will provide a larger current.
solution_html: The internal resistance of a primary cell is more than that of a secondary cell, so a secondary cell will provide a larger current.

subject: Physics
question_html: An arc lamp operates at 80 V, 10 A. Suggest a method to use it with a 240 V d.c. source. Calculate the value of the electric component required for this purpose.
question_text: An arc lamp operates at 80 V, 10 A. Suggest a method to use it with a 240 V d.c. source. Calculate the value of the electric component required for this purpose.
explanation: The resistance of the arc lamp is R = {tex}\frac{V}{I}=\frac{80}{10}{/tex} = 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} In order to use an arc lamp with a source of 240 V, a resistance R' should be connected in series with it so that current through the circuit does not exceed 10 A. Then, I (R + R') = V or 10(8 + R') = 240 or R' = 24 - 8 = 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution: The resistance of the arc lamp is R = {tex}\frac{V}{I}=\frac{80}{10}{/tex} = 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} In order to use an arc lamp with a source of 240 V, a resistance R' should be connected in series with it so that current through the circuit does not exceed 10 A. Then, I (R + R') = V or 10(8 + R') = 240 or R' = 24 - 8 = 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
The resistance of the arc lamp is
R = {tex}\frac{V}{I}=\frac{80}{10}{/tex} = 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
In order to use an arc lamp with a source of 240 V, a resistance R' should be connected in series with it so that current through the circuit does not exceed 10 A.
Then,
I (R + R') = V or 10(8 + R') = 240
or R' = 24 - 8 = 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex}

he resistances in the two cases are

subject: Physics
question_html: Define the term resistance. Give physical explanation of the opposition offered by a conductor to the flow of current through it.
question_text: Define the term resistance. Give physical explanation of the opposition offered by a conductor to the flow of current through it.
explanation: Resistance is defined as the property of a conductor to resist the flow of charges through it. The resistance of conductor is numerically given as the ratio of potential difference across its length to the current flowing through it. Large the number of collisions per second, smaller is the relaxation time t, and large will be the resistivity (density= mne 2 t) The number of collisions that the electrons make with the atoms/ions depends on the arrangement of atoms or ions in a conductor. So the resistance depends on the nature of the material (copper, silver, etc) of the conductor. The resistance of a conductor depends on its length. A long wire offers more resistance than short wire because three will be more collisions in the longer wire. The resistance of conductor depends on its area of cross-section. A thick wire offers less resistance than a thin wire because, in a thick wire, more area of cross-section is available for the flow of electrons.
solution: Resistance is defined as the property of a conductor to resist the flow of charges through it. The resistance of conductor is numerically given as the ratio of potential difference across its length to the current flowing through it. Large the number of collisions per second, smaller is the relaxation time t, and large will be the resistivity (density= mne 2 t) The number of collisions that the electrons make with the atoms/ions depends on the arrangement of atoms or ions in a conductor. So the resistance depends on the nature of the material (copper, silver, etc) of the conductor. The resistance of a conductor depends on its length. A long wire offers more resistance than short wire because three will be more collisions in the longer wire. The resistance of conductor depends on its area of cross-section. A thick wire offers less resistance than a thin wire because, in a thick wire, more area of cross-section is available for the flow of electrons.
solution_html:
Resistance is defined as the property of a conductor to resist the flow of charges through it. The resistance of conductor is numerically given as the ratio of potential difference across its length to the current flowing through it.
Large the number of collisions per second, smaller is the relaxation time t, and large will be the resistivity (density= mne2t)
The number of collisions that the electrons make with the atoms/ions depends on the arrangement of atoms or ions in a conductor. So the resistance depends on the nature of the material (copper, silver, etc) of the conductor.
The resistance of a conductor depends on its length. A long wire offers more resistance than short wire because three will be more collisions in the longer wire.
The resistance of conductor depends on its area of cross-section. A thick wire offers less resistance than a thin wire because, in a thick wire, more area of cross-section is available for the flow of electrons.

subject: Physics
question_html: Define emf of a cell. Show that the voltage drop across a resistor connected in parallel with a cell is different from the emf of the cell.
question_text: Define emf of a cell. Show that the voltage drop across a resistor connected in parallel with a cell is different from the emf of the cell.
explanation: The electromotive force (e) or e.m.f. is the energy provided by a cell or battery per coulomb of charge passing through it. The electromotive force is measured in volts (V) . Consider a cell of emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} and internal resistance r connected to an external resistance R, as shown in Fig. Suppose a constant current I flows through this circuit. By definition of emf, {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = Work done by the cell in carrying a unit charge along the closed-circuit = Work done in carrying a unit charge from A to B against external resistance R + Work done in carrying a unit charge from B to A against internal resistance r Or {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = V + V' V = IR and V' = Ir {tex}\therefore \varepsilon{/tex} = IR + r Hence the current in the circuit is I = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{R+r}{/tex} Voltage across external resistance R given by V = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon \; r}{R+r}{/tex} From the above expression, we can see that voltage across resistor is different from the emf of the battery.
solution: The electromotive force (e) or e.m.f. is the energy provided by a cell or battery per coulomb of charge passing through it. The electromotive force is measured in volts (V) . Consider a cell of emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} and internal resistance r connected to an external resistance R, as shown in Fig. Suppose a constant current I flows through this circuit. By definition of emf, {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = Work done by the cell in carrying a unit charge along the closed-circuit = Work done in carrying a unit charge from A to B against external resistance R + Work done in carrying a unit charge from B to A against internal resistance r Or {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = V + V' V = IR and V' = Ir {tex}\therefore \varepsilon{/tex} = IR + r Hence the current in the circuit is I = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{R+r}{/tex} Voltage across external resistance R given by V = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon \; r}{R+r}{/tex} From the above expression, we can see that voltage across resistor is different from the emf of the battery.
solution_html:
The electromotive force (e) or e.m.f. is the energy provided by a cell or battery per coulomb of charge passing through it. The electromotive force is measured in volts (V).
Consider a cell of emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} and internal resistance r connected to an external resistance R, as shown in Fig. Suppose a constant current I flows through this circuit.
By definition of emf,
{tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = Work done by the cell in carrying a unit charge along the closed-circuit
= Work done in carrying a unit charge from A to B against external resistance R + Work done in carrying a unit charge from B to A against internal resistance r
Or {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = V + V'
V = IR and V' = Ir
{tex}\therefore \varepsilon{/tex} = IR + r
Hence the current in the circuit is
I = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{R+r}{/tex}
Voltage across external resistance R given by
V = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon \; r}{R+r}{/tex}
From the above expression, we can see that voltage across resistor is different from the emf of the battery.

subject: Physics
question_html: Define the terms electric energy and electric power. Give their units.
question_text: Define the terms electric energy and electric power. Give their units.
explanation: Electric energy: The total work done by the source of emf in maintaining an electric current in a circuit for a given time is called electric energy consumed in the circuit. Its SI unit is joule. Electric power: The rate at which work is done by a source of emf in maintaining an electric current through a circuit is called electric power of the circuit. Its SI unit is watt.
solution: Electric energy: The total work done by the source of emf in maintaining an electric current in a circuit for a given time is called electric energy consumed in the circuit. Its SI unit is joule. Electric power: The rate at which work is done by a source of emf in maintaining an electric current through a circuit is called electric power of the circuit. Its SI unit is watt.
solution_html:
Electric energy: The total work done by the source of emf in maintaining an electric current in a circuit for a given time is called electric energy consumed in the circuit. Its SI unit is joule.
Electric power: The rate at which work is done by a source of emf in maintaining an electric current through a circuit is called electric power of the circuit. Its SI unit is watt.

question_html: Find the potential difference between points A and B i.e., (VA - VB) in the network shown in Figure.

solution_html:
Current through each branch = {tex}\frac 22{/tex} = 1 A
VC - VA = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 2 V
VC - VB = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 3 V
VA - VB = (VC - VB) - (VC - VA) = 3 - 2 = 1 V

subject: Physics
question_html: A battery consists of 12 cells in series, each having an emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} and internal resistor r. Some of the cells in the battery are connected with wrong polarity. This battery is connected to another source of emf 2 E and internal resistance 2 {tex}\varepsilon{/tex}. An ammeter in the circuit reads 3 A when battery and the source aid each other and 2 A in the same direction when they oppose each other. Find how many cells in the battery are connected with wrong polarity.
question_text: A battery consists of 12 cells in series, each having an emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} and internal resistor r. Some of the cells in the battery are connected with wrong polarity. This battery is connected to another source of emf 2 E and internal resistance 2 {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} . An ammeter in the circuit reads 3 A when battery and the source aid each other and 2 A in the same direction when they oppose each other. Find how many cells in the battery are connected with wrong polarity.
explanation: Suppose n cells are connected wrongly in the battery. Then (12 - n) cells give forward emf and n cells give reverse emf. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Effective emf of the battery = (12 - n) {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} - n {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = (12 - 2n) {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} Total resistance of the circuit with battery and source in both cases = 12r + 2r = 14r Currents in the two cases must be proportional to the emfs in the two cases {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{(12 \ - \ 2 n) \varepsilon \ + \ 2 \varepsilon}{(12 \ - \ 2 n) \varepsilon \ - \ 2 \varepsilon}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{(14-2 n) \varepsilon}{(10-2 n) \varepsilon}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{7 \ - \ n}{5 \ - \ n}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} or 14 - 2n = 15 - 3n {tex}\therefore{/tex} n = 1 i.e., one cell has been connected with wrong polarity in the battery.
solution: Suppose n cells are connected wrongly in the battery. Then (12 - n) cells give forward emf and n cells give reverse emf. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Effective emf of the battery = (12 - n) {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} - n {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = (12 - 2n) {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} Total resistance of the circuit with battery and source in both cases = 12r + 2r = 14r Currents in the two cases must be proportional to the emfs in the two cases {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{(12 \ - \ 2 n) \varepsilon \ + \ 2 \varepsilon}{(12 \ - \ 2 n) \varepsilon \ - \ 2 \varepsilon}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{(14-2 n) \varepsilon}{(10-2 n) \varepsilon}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{7 \ - \ n}{5 \ - \ n}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} or 14 - 2n = 15 - 3n {tex}\therefore{/tex} n = 1 i.e., one cell has been connected with wrong polarity in the battery.
solution_html:
Suppose n cells are connected wrongly in the battery. Then (12 - n) cells give forward emf and n cells give reverse emf.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Effective emf of the battery
= (12 - n) {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} - n {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = (12 - 2n){tex}\varepsilon{/tex}
Total resistance of the circuit with battery and source in both cases
= 12r + 2r = 14r
Currents in the two cases must be proportional to the emfs in the two cases
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{(12 \ - \ 2 n) \varepsilon \ + \ 2 \varepsilon}{(12 \ - \ 2 n) \varepsilon \ - \ 2 \varepsilon}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{(14-2 n) \varepsilon}{(10-2 n) \varepsilon}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{7 \ - \ n}{5 \ - \ n}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
or 14 - 2n = 15 - 3n
{tex}\therefore{/tex} n = 1
i.e., one cell has been connected with wrong polarity in the battery.

subject: Physics
question_html: Derive an expression for the heat produced in a resistor R when voltage drop across it is V.
question_text: Derive an expression for the heat produced in a resistor R when voltage drop across it is V.
explanation: When an electric charge Q moves against a p.d. V, the amount of work done is given by W = Q {tex}\times{/tex} V ...(i) We know, current, I = Qt Q = I {tex}\times{/tex} t ...(ii) By ohm's law, VI = R V = I {tex}\times{/tex} R ...(iii) Putting eqs. (ii) and (iii) in eq. (i), W = I {tex}\times{/tex} t {tex}\times{/tex} I {tex}\times{/tex} R W = I 2 Rt Assuming that all the electrical work done is converted into heat energy, we get Heat produced, H = I 2 Rt joules This relation is known as Joule's law of heating.
solution: When an electric charge Q moves against a p.d. V, the amount of work done is given by W = Q {tex}\times{/tex} V ...(i) We know, current, I = Qt Q = I {tex}\times{/tex} t ...(ii) By ohm's law, VI = R V = I {tex}\times{/tex} R ...(iii) Putting eqs. (ii) and (iii) in eq. (i), W = I {tex}\times{/tex} t {tex}\times{/tex} I {tex}\times{/tex} R W = I 2 Rt Assuming that all the electrical work done is converted into heat energy, we get Heat produced, H = I 2 Rt joules This relation is known as Joule's law of heating.
solution_html:
When an electric charge Q moves against a p.d. V, the amount of work done is given by
W = Q {tex}\times{/tex} V ...(i)
We know, current, I = Qt
Q = I {tex}\times{/tex} t ...(ii)
By ohm's law, VI = R
V = I {tex}\times{/tex} R ...(iii)
Putting eqs. (ii) and (iii) in eq. (i),
W = I {tex}\times{/tex} t {tex}\times{/tex} I {tex}\times{/tex} R
W = I2Rt
Assuming that all the electrical work done is converted into heat energy, we get
Heat produced, H = I2Rt joules
This relation is known as Joule's law of heating.

explanation: Here we need first to calculate the equivalent resistance i.e., internal resistance and external resistance. Total resistance = R + r Net emf = E Net Current = I = {tex}\frac{E}{R+r}{/tex} Applying Kirchoffs Voltage law, E = IR + Ir {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E = I(R + r) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{E}{R+r}{/tex}
solution: Here we need first to calculate the equivalent resistance i.e., internal resistance and external resistance. Net emf = E

Applying Kirchoffs Voltage law,
E = IR + Ir

subject: Physics
question_html: An electric bulb rated for 500 W at 100 V is used in circuit having a 200 V supply. Calculate the resistance R that must be put in series with the bulb, so that the bulb delivers 500 W.
question_text: An electric bulb rated for 500 W at 100 V is used in circuit having a 200 V supply. Calculate the resistance R that must be put in series with the bulb, so that the bulb delivers 500 W.
explanation: The resistance of the bulb, R = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{100 \ \times \ 100}{500}=20 \Omega{/tex} Current through the bulb, I = {tex}\frac{V}{R}=\frac{100}{20}{/tex} = 5A For the same power dissipation, the current through the bulb must be 5 A. When the bulb is connected to 200 V supply, the safe resistance of the circuit should be {tex}R^{\prime}=\frac{V^{\prime}}{I}=\frac{200}{5}=40 \Omega{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Resistance required to be put in series with the bulb is {tex}R^\prime{/tex} - R = 40 - 20 = 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution: The resistance of the bulb, R = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{100 \ \times \ 100}{500}=20 \Omega{/tex} Current through the bulb, I = {tex}\frac{V}{R}=\frac{100}{20}{/tex} = 5A For the same power dissipation, the current through the bulb must be 5 A. When the bulb is connected to 200 V supply, the safe resistance of the circuit should be {tex}R^{\prime}=\frac{V^{\prime}}{I}=\frac{200}{5}=40 \Omega{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Resistance required to be put in series with the bulb is {tex}R^\prime{/tex} - R = 40 - 20 = 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
The resistance of the bulb,
R = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{100 \ \times \ 100}{500}=20 \Omega{/tex}
Current through the bulb,
I = {tex}\frac{V}{R}=\frac{100}{20}{/tex} = 5A
For the same power dissipation, the current through the bulb must be 5 A.
When the bulb is connected to 200 V supply, the safe resistance of the circuit should be
{tex}R^{\prime}=\frac{V^{\prime}}{I}=\frac{200}{5}=40 \Omega{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Resistance required to be put in series with the bulb is
{tex}R^\prime{/tex} - R = 40 - 20 = 20{tex}\Omega{/tex}

question_html: 100 W, 220 V bulb is connected to 110 V source. Calculate the power consumed by the bulb.
question_text: 100 W, 220 V bulb is connected to 110 V source. Calculate the power consumed by the bulb.
explanation: Power of the bulb, P = 100 W Operating voltage V = 200V Resistance of the bulb, R = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{(220)^{2}}{100}{/tex} = 484 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Actual operating voltage V 1 = 110V Therefore, power consumed by the bulb, {tex}P_{1} = \frac{\left(V_{1}\right)^{2}}{R}=\frac{(110)^{2}}{484}{/tex} = 25 W
solution: Power of the bulb, P = 100 W Operating voltage V = 200V Resistance of the bulb, R = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{(220)^{2}}{100}{/tex} = 484 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Actual operating voltage V 1 = 110V Therefore, power consumed by the bulb, {tex}P_{1} = \frac{\left(V_{1}\right)^{2}}{R}=\frac{(110)^{2}}{484}{/tex} = 25 W
solution_html:
Power of the bulb, P = 100 W
Operating voltage V = 200V
Resistance of the bulb, R = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{(220)^{2}}{100}{/tex} = 484 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
Actual operating voltage V1 = 110V
Therefore, power consumed by the bulb,
{tex}P_{1} = \frac{\left(V_{1}\right)^{2}}{R}=\frac{(110)^{2}}{484}{/tex} = 25 W

subject: Physics
question_html:
In a house having 220 V line, the following appliances are working:

  1. a 60 W bulb
  2. a 1000 Wheater
  3. a 40 W radio.

Calculate:

  1. the current drawn by heater and
  2. the current passing through the fuse line.
question_text: In a house having 220 V line, the following appliances are working: a 60 W bulb a 1000 Wheater a 40 W radio. Calculate: the current drawn by heater and the current passing through the fuse line.
explanation: Current drawn by heater = {tex}\frac{P_{1}}{V}=\frac{1000}{220}=\frac{50}{11} \mathbf{A}{/tex} Current drawn by bulb = {tex}\frac{P_{2}}{V}=\frac{60}{220}=\frac{3}{11} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} Current drawn by radio = {tex}\frac{P_{3}}{V}=\frac{40}{220}=\frac{2}{11} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} Current passing through fuse for the line = {tex}\frac{50}{11}+\frac{3}{11}+\frac{2}{11}{/tex} = 5A
solution: Current drawn by heater = {tex}\frac{P_{1}}{V}=\frac{1000}{220}=\frac{50}{11} \mathbf{A}{/tex} Current drawn by bulb = {tex}\frac{P_{2}}{V}=\frac{60}{220}=\frac{3}{11} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} Current drawn by radio = {tex}\frac{P_{3}}{V}=\frac{40}{220}=\frac{2}{11} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} Current passing through fuse for the line = {tex}\frac{50}{11}+\frac{3}{11}+\frac{2}{11}{/tex} = 5A
solution_html:
1. Current drawn by heater = {tex}\frac{P_{1}}{V}=\frac{1000}{220}=\frac{50}{11} \mathbf{A}{/tex}
    Current drawn by bulb = {tex}\frac{P_{2}}{V}=\frac{60}{220}=\frac{3}{11} \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
    Current drawn by radio = {tex}\frac{P_{3}}{V}=\frac{40}{220}=\frac{2}{11} \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
  2. Current passing through fuse for the line = {tex}\frac{50}{11}+\frac{3}{11}+\frac{2}{11}{/tex} = 5A

subject: Physics
question_html: A 30 V storage battery is being charged by 120 V d.c. supply. A resistor has been connected in series with the battery to limit the charging current to 15 A. Find the rate at which energy is dissipated in the resistor. If the total heat produced could be made available for heating water, how long would it take to bring 1 kg of water from 15°C to the boiling point? Specific heat of water = 1 cal g-1 °C-1 and 1 cal = 4.2 J.
question_text: A 30 V storage battery is being charged by 120 V d.c. supply. A resistor has been connected in series with the battery to limit the charging current to 15 A. Find the rate at which energy is dissipated in the resistor. If the total heat produced could be made available for heating water, how long would it take to bring 1 kg of water from 15°C to the boiling point? Specific heat of water = 1 cal g -1 °C -1 and 1 cal = 4.2 J.
explanation: Charging current, I = {tex}\frac{120 \ - \ 30}{R}{/tex} = 15 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Series resistor, R = {tex}\frac{90}{15}=6 \Omega{/tex} Rate of energy dissipation in the resistor, P = I 2 R = (15) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 1350 Js -1 Heat produced in resistor in time t = Heat absorbed by water 1350 {tex}\times{/tex} t = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 4200 {tex}\times{/tex} (100 - 15) or t = {tex}\frac{4200 \ \times \ 85}{1350}{/tex} = 264.4 s
solution: Charging current, I = {tex}\frac{120 \ - \ 30}{R}{/tex} = 15 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Series resistor, R = {tex}\frac{90}{15}=6 \Omega{/tex} Rate of energy dissipation in the resistor, P = I 2 R = (15) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 1350 Js -1 Heat produced in resistor in time t = Heat absorbed by water 1350 {tex}\times{/tex} t = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 4200 {tex}\times{/tex} (100 - 15) or t = {tex}\frac{4200 \ \times \ 85}{1350}{/tex} = 264.4 s
solution_html:
Charging current, I = {tex}\frac{120 \ - \ 30}{R}{/tex} = 15
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Series resistor, R = {tex}\frac{90}{15}=6 \Omega{/tex}
Rate of energy dissipation in the resistor,
P = I2R = (15)2 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 1350 Js-1
Heat produced in resistor in time t = Heat absorbed by water
1350 {tex}\times{/tex} t = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 4200 {tex}\times{/tex} (100 - 15)
or t = {tex}\frac{4200 \ \times \ 85}{1350}{/tex} = 264.4 s

subject: Physics
question_html: A battery of emf 2 V and internal resistance 0.1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is being charged by a current of 5 A. What will be the direction of current inside the battery? What is the potential difference between the terminals of the battery?
question_text: A battery of emf 2 V and internal resistance 0.1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is being charged by a current of 5 A. What will be the direction of current inside the battery? What is the potential difference between the terminals of the battery?
explanation: During charging of a battery, the current inside the battery flows from the positive to the negative terminal of the battery, so the terminal p.d. is greater than the emf. Thus V = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} + Ir = 2 + 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 = 2.5 V
solution: During charging of a battery, the current inside the battery flows from the positive to the negative terminal of the battery, so the terminal p.d. is greater than the emf. Thus V = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} + Ir = 2 + 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 = 2.5 V
solution_html:
During charging of a battery, the current inside the battery flows from the positive to the negative terminal of the battery, so the terminal p.d. is greater than the emf.
Thus V = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} + Ir = 2 + 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 = 2.5 V
